However, these methods lead to a catastrophic decrease in recognition performance and visual quality when the encrypted videos are compressed. That is, the previous methods are not compression-friendly. To address these issues, in this paper, we propose the first compression-friendly encryption method for PPAR, called CFE-PPAR. In CFE-PPAR, videos encrypted with secret keys can be directly recognized by a video transformer, which uses parameters transformed by the same keys as those used for video encryption. In experiments, it is verified that CFE-PPAR outperforms previous methods on the UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets under Motion-JPEG and H.264 compression.

</details>


### [27] [Adaptive Physical-Facial Representation Fusion via Subject-Invariant Cross-Modal Prompt Tuning for Video-Based Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05694)
*Xiwen Luo,Jia Li,Rencheng Song,Yu Liu,Juan Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cross-modal prompt-tuning framework for video-based emotion recognition that converts rPPG signals into time-frequency representations to generate modality-complementary prompts for a frozen Vision Transformer, supplemented by a Decoupled Shared-Specific Adapter (DSSA) to separate subject-shared and subject-specific components, improving cross-subject generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address noise and inter-subject variability in rPPG, preserve pretrained facial representations, and enhance cross-subject generalization in multimodal emotion recognition.

Method: Convert rPPG waveforms to time-frequency representations (TFRs) to produce modality-complementary prompts that modulate facial tokens within a frozen ViT. Introduce a Decoupled Shared-Specific Adapter (DSSA) at each ViT layer to explicitly separate subject-shared and subject-specific components. Evaluate on MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP datasets.

Result: The proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines in both recognition accuracy and generalization on MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP.

Conclusion: Prompt-based cross-modal interaction with DSSA preserves pretrained facial representations while enhancing cross-subject generalization for video-based emotion recognition.

Abstract: Emotion recognition from facial videos enables non-contact inference of human emotional states. Although facial expressions are widely used cues, they cannot fully reflect intrinsic affective states. Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) provides complementary physiological information, but it is highly susceptible to noise and inter-subject variability, limiting generalization to unseen individuals. Existing multimodal methods combine facial and rPPG features, yet their fusion strategies often disrupt pretrained facial representations and lack explicit mechanisms to suppress subject-specific variations. To address these issues, we propose a subject-invariant cross-modal prompt-tuning framework for video-based emotion recognition. Specifically, rPPG waveforms are transformed into noise-robust time-frequency representations (TFRs), from which modality-complementary prompts are generated to modulate facial tokens within a frozen Vision Transformer (ViT). This design enables effective cross-modal interaction while preserving the generalizable facial representations learned by the pretrained backbone. In addition, we introduce a decoupled shared-specific adapter (DSSA) into each ViT layer to explicitly separate subject-shared and subject-specific components, thereby improving cross-subject generalization. Experiments on the MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines in both recognition accuracy and generalization ability, highlighting its effectiveness for video-based emotion recognition.

</details>


### [28] [Closing the Loop: Unified 3D Scene Generation and Immersive Interaction via LLM-RL Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05711)
*Anh H. Vo,Sungyo Lee,Phil-Joong Kim,Soo-Mi Choi,Yong-Guk Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified framework integrating language-driven 3D scene generation with immersive interaction. Uses LLMs to build scene structures, RL to optimize layouts under constraints, deployed in VR for HRI-in-the-loop, achieving state-of-the-art on ALFRED and improved immersion and task efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes the separation between scene generation and user interaction in prior work, which hampers adaptability and immersion in interactive multimedia systems; a closed-loop framework can align generated content with human perception and usability.

Method: 1) Use LLMs to generate structured scene representations from natural language instructions. 2) Optimize spatial layouts via reinforcement learning under geometric and semantic constraints. 3) Deploy environments in VR to enable HRI-in-the-loop feedback and alignment with user perception.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on task-based scene generation on the ALFRED benchmark. Qualitative results and user studies report improved immersion, interaction quality, and task efficiency.

Conclusion: Closed-loop integration of generation and interaction enhances responsiveness, adaptability, and realism in multimedia systems, enabling more effective next-generation immersive experiences.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly improved language-driven 3D content generation, but most existing approaches still treat scene generation and user interaction as separate processes, limiting the adaptability and immersive potential of interactive multimedia systems. This paper presents a unified framework that closes the loop between language-driven 3D scene generation and immersive user interaction. Given natural language instructions, the system first constructs structured scene representations using LLMs, and then optimizes spatial layouts via reinforcement learning under geometric and semantic constraints. The generated environments are deployed in a virtual reality setting to facilitate HRI-in-the-loop, where user interactions provide continuous feedback to align generated content with human perception and usability. By tightly coupling generation and interaction, the proposed framework enables more responsive, adaptive, and realistic multimedia experiences. Experiments on the ALFRED benchmark demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in task-based scene generation. Furthermore, qualitative results and user studies show consistent improvements in immersion, interaction quality, and task efficiency, highlighting the importance of closed-loop integration of generation and interaction for next-generation multimedia systems. Our project page can be found at https://proj-showcase.github.io/h3ds/.

</details>


### [29] [EgoEMG: A Multimodal Egocentric Dataset with Bilateral EMG and Vision for Hand Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05712)
*Ziheng Xi,Jiayi Yu,Yitao Wang,Yanbo Duan,Jianjiang Feng,Jie Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoEMG introduces a synchronized, multimodal dataset for bimanual hand pose estimation, combining sEMG, IMU, egocentric RGB, external RGB-D, and mocap data across 41 participants and 60 gestures. It defines EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, and EMG+vision fusion benchmarks, with baselines including EMGFormer and vision backbones, plus a residual fusion model that improves over vision-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the lack of synchronized multimodal data for hand pose estimation by leveraging EMG's fine-grained finger articulation and vision's global configuration, enabling robust hand tracking even under occlusion or poor lighting.

Method: Data collection includes bilateral sEMG (16 channels total, 8 per wrist) at 2 kHz, 120 Hz IMU, egocentric wide-angle RGB video, external RGB-D video, and mocap-derived wrist articulation angles from 41 participants performing 60 gestures (30 single-hand, 30 bimanual). Over 10 hours. Benchmark with three tasks (EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, EMG+vision fusion) targeting joint-angle prediction, using cross-gesture, cross-user, and combined generalization splits. Baselines: EMGFormer for EMG-to-pose; ResNet/ViT backbones for vision-to-pose; a residual fusion architecture.

Result: EMG+vision fusion surpasses vision-only baselines in the proposed fusion task; EMGFormer provides a strong EMG-to-pose baseline; a residual fusion model improves performance over lightweight vision-only baselines.

Conclusion: EgoEMG establishes a foundational multimodal dataset and benchmark for joint EMG-vision hand pose estimation, enabling future research into robust, occlusion-robust hand tracking across users and gestures.

Abstract: Surface electromyography (sEMG) records muscle activity during hand movement and can be decoded to recover detailed hand articulation. EMG and egocentric vision are complementary for hand sensing: EMG captures fine-grained finger articulation even under occlusion and poor lighting, while vision provides global hand configuration. However, no existing dataset synchronizes both modalities. We present EgoEMG, a multimodal egocentric dataset for bimanual hand pose estimation. EgoEMG includes bilateral wristband EMG with 16 total channels (8 per wrist) sampled at 2 kHz, 120 Hz IMU, egocentric wide-angle RGB video, external RGB-D video, and mocap-derived hand motion with wrist articulation angles. The dataset covers 41 participants performing 60 gesture classes, including 30 single-hand gestures and 30 bimanual gestures, totaling more than 10 hours of recording. We also introduce a benchmark with three tasks -- EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, and EMG+vision fusion -- under a shared joint-angle prediction target and common generalization split axes (cross-gesture, cross-user, and combined). As baselines, we evaluate EMGFormer for EMG-to-pose and generic ResNet/ViT backbones for vision-to-pose. We further study a residual fusion architecture that improves over matched lightweight vision-only baselines. Together, EgoEMG and its benchmark establish a foundation for future research on multimodal hand pose estimation with EMG and vision.

</details>


### [30] [TriRelVLA: Triadic Relational Structure for Generalizable Embodied Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05714)
*Hanyu Zhou,Chuanhao Ma,Gim Hee Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriRelVLA proposes a triadic relational VLA framework for generalizable embodied manipulation by constructing explicit object-hand-task relations, a task-grounded relational graph with cross-attention and a graph transformer, and a relation-conditioned action generation via a bottleneck feeding an LLM; achieves cross-scene/object/task generalization and real-world fine-tuning dataset gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the generalization gap in VLA models caused by entangled appearance representations; manipulation actions depend on object-hand-task relational structure; need explicit relational primitives and structured reasoning to decouple action from appearance.

Method: Three components: (1) construct explicit object-hand-task triadic relational primitives from multimodal inputs; (2) build a task-grounded relational graph where task-guided cross-attention forms nodes and a relation-aware graph transformer models interactions; (3) perform relation-conditioned action generation by compressing relational structure into a bottleneck and projecting into an LLM for action prediction; includes a real-world robotic dataset for fine-tuning.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows improved performance on fine-tuned tasks and clear gains in cross-scene, cross-object, and cross-task generalization; real-world dataset enables effective adaptation.

Conclusion: TriRelVLA reduces reliance on appearance statistics, enabling transfer across scenes/objects/tasks; the triadic relational bottleneck and LLM-conditioned generation provide robust generalization for embodied manipulation; dataset supports practical deployment.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models perform well on training-seen robotic tasks but struggle to generalize to unseen scenes and objects. A key limitation lies in their implicit visual representations, which entangle object appearance, background, and scene layout. This makes policies sensitive to visual variations. Prior work improves transferability through structured intermediate representations that objectify visual content. However, these representations mainly capture scene semantics instead of action-relevant relations. As a result, action prediction remains tied to appearance statistics. We observe that manipulation actions depend on the object-hand-task relational structure, which governs interactions among task requirements, robot states, and object properties. Based on this observation, we propose TriRelVLA, a triadic relational VLA framework for generalizable embodied manipulation. Our approach consists of three components: 1) We construct explicit object-hand-task triadic representations from multimodal inputs as relational primitives. 2) We build a task-grounded relational graph. Task-guided cross-attention forms nodes, and a relation-aware graph transformer models interactions among them. 3) We perform relation-conditioned action generation. The relational structure is compressed into a bottleneck space and projected into the LLM for action prediction. This triadic relational bottleneck reduces reliance on appearance statistics and enables transfer across scenes, objects, and task compositions. We further introduce a real-world robotic dataset for fine-tuning. Experiments show strong performance on fine-tuned tasks and clear gains in cross-scene, cross-object, and cross-task generalization.

</details>


### [31] [$\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net: Controlled Posterior Bridge Learning for Multi-Task Dense Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05722)
*Meihua Zhou,Li Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel multi-task dense prediction framework (B^3-Net) that explicitly models reliability of cross-task evidence to form a posterior bridge and redistribute it boundedly, reducing negative transfer and improving accuracy across tasks and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing decoder-side interactions fuse evidence implicitly via similarity without accounting for task- and location-dependent reliability, leading to contamination of shared representations.

Method: Decompose interaction into three components: Precision Field Estimator for patch-level evidence precision; Posterior Bridge Operator for precision-weighted heteroscedastic fusion; Contractive Dispatch Operator for bounded redistribution to task branches.

Result: On NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, Cityscapes, B^3-Net achieves competitive or superior trade-offs versus CNN-, Transformer-, diffusion-, Mamba-, and bridge-feature-based methods; backbone-matched comparisons and ablations indicate gains arise from controlled posterior bridge learning rather than backbone capacity or decoder scale.

Conclusion: Explicit reliability-aware posterior bridging yields more robust shared representations and reduces negative transfer in multi-task dense prediction.

Abstract: Multi-task dense prediction solves complementary pixel-level tasks in a unified model, such as semantic segmentation, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, and edge detection. Existing decoder-side interactions use attention, prompts, routing, diffusion, Mamba, or bridge features to exchange task evidence, but most of them organize this evidence implicitly. They usually fuse task features by similarity or affinity, without explicitly modeling that evidence reliability varies across tasks and spatial locations. As a result, unreliable evidence may contaminate the shared representation and intensify negative transfer. We propose $\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net, a controlled posterior bridge learning framework for multi-task dense prediction. Our method decomposes decoder-side interaction into reliability estimation, posterior bridge construction, and bounded redistribution. The Precision Field Estimator estimates patch-wise evidence precision from task-reference alignment and local variation. The Posterior Bridge Operator builds a precision-weighted posterior bridge through heteroscedastic evidence fusion, yielding a shared state more reliable than uniform or heuristic mixtures. The Contractive Dispatch Operator redistributes the bridge to each task branch through a bounded update, reducing uncontrolled feature injection. Experiments on NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, and Cityscapes show that $\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net achieves competitive or superior trade-offs over representative CNN-, Transformer-, diffusion-, Mamba-, and bridge-feature-based methods. Backbone-matched comparisons and extensive analyses further verify that the gains arise from controlled posterior bridge learning rather than backbone capacity or decoder scale.

</details>


### [32] [EA-WM: Event-Aware Generative World Model with Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06192)
*Zhaoyang Yang,Yurun Jin,Lizhe Qi,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EA-WM is a diffusion-based world model that injects actions into the visual domain via Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields and uses event-aware fusion to model dynamics, achieving state-of-the-art results on WorldArena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world-action models underutilize action signals to guide video synthesis and often prioritize policy learning over accurate forward visual dynamics; there is a need to preserve robot geometry and fine-grained interaction dynamics in generated rollouts.

Method: Propose EA-WM, which projects actions and kinematic states into the target camera view as Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields. Introduce event-aware bidirectional fusion blocks to modulate cross-branch attention, capturing object state changes and interaction dynamics.

Result: On the WorldArena benchmark, EA-WM achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming baselines by a significant margin.

Conclusion: EA-WM closes the loop between kinematic control and visual perception, improving geometric fidelity and interaction dynamics in generated rollouts and demonstrating strong performance on a comprehensive robotic world-model benchmark.

Abstract: Pretrained video diffusion models provide powerful spatiotemporal generative priors, making them a natural foundation for robotic world models. While recent world-action models jointly optimize future videos and actions, they predominantly treat video generation as an auxiliary representation for policy learning. Consequently, they insufficiently explore the inverse problem: leveraging action signals to guide video synthesis, thereby often failing to preserve precise robot spatial geometry and fine-grained robot-object interaction dynamics in the generated rollouts. To bridge this gap, we present EA-WM, an Event-Aware Generative World Model that effectively closes the loop between kinematic control and visual perception. Rather than injecting joint or end-effector actions as abstract, low-dimensional tokens, EA-WM projects actions and kinematic states directly into the target camera view as Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields. To fully exploit this geometrically grounded representation, we introduce event-aware bidirectional fusion blocks that modulate cross-branch attention, capturing object state changes and interaction dynamics. Evaluated on the comprehensive WorldArena benchmark, EA-WM achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing baselines by a significant margin.

</details>


### [33] [Ray-Aware Pointer Memory with Adaptive Updates for Streaming 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05749)
*Feifei Li,Qi Song,Chi Zhang,Rui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ray-aware memory with adaptive retain-or-replace updates improves online dense 3D reconstruction by jointly modeling 3D location and viewing direction, leading to more stable long-term memory and pose accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense streaming 3D reconstruction struggles with appearance-driven memory fusion, causing redundancy and drift under viewpoint changes. A memory that jointly encodes geometry and viewing direction can better distinguish informative observations and maintain bounded memory.

Method: Introduce a ray-aware pointer memory where each pointer stores 3D position, ray direction, and feature embedding. Replace fusion-based memory compression with a retain-or-replace strategy. Jointly reason about spatial proximity and ray-direction discrepancy to distinguish local redundancy, novelty, and loop revisits. Trigger pose refinement upon loop detection to enforce global consistency.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows improved long-term reconstruction stability and camera pose accuracy with bounded memory growth and efficient streaming inference across tests.

Conclusion: The ray-aware memory framework provides a scalable, drift-resistant approach to online 3D reconstruction from image streams, combining geometry-aware memory with adaptive updates and loop-aware refinement.

Abstract: Dense 3D reconstruction from continuous image streams requires both accurate geometric aggregation and stable long-term memory management. Recent feed-forward reconstruction frameworks integrate observations through persistent memory representations, yet most rely primarily on appearance-based similarity when updating memory. Such appearance-driven integration often leads to redundant accumulation of observations and unstable geometry when viewpoint changes occur. In this work, we propose a ray-aware pointer memory for streaming 3D reconstruction that explicitly models both spatial location and viewing direction within a unified memory representation. Each memory pointer stores its 3D position, associated ray direction, and feature embedding, allowing the system to reason jointly about geometric proximity and viewpoint consistency. Based on this representation, we introduce an adaptive pointer update strategy that replaces traditional fusion-based memory compression with a retain-or-replace mechanism. Instead of averaging nearby observations, the system selectively retains informative pointers while discarding redundant ones, preserving distinctive geometric structures while maintaining bounded memory growth. Furthermore, the joint reasoning over spatial distance and ray-direction discrepancy enables the system to distinguish between local redundancy, novel observations, and potential loop revisits in a unified manner. When loop candidates are detected, pose refinement is triggered to enforce global geometric consistency across the reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed ray-aware memory design significantly improves long-term reconstruction stability and camera pose accuracy while maintaining efficient streaming inference. Our approach provides a principled framework for scalable and drift-resistant online 3D reconstruction from image streams.

</details>


### [34] [Jointly Learning Structured Representations and Stabilized Affinity for Human Motion Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05753)
*Xianghan Meng,Zhiyuan Huang,Zhengyu Tong,Chun-Guang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Temporal Deep Self-expressive Subspace Clustering (TDSC) for HMS, combining structured self-expressive representations with temporal constraints, a coding-rate regularizer to avoid collapse, and a momentum-based affinity stabilization, achieving robust segmentation across varied features on five HMS datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HMS features often violate the Union-of-Subspaces (UoS) assumption, causing subspace-clustering-based segmentation to underperform; there is a need for temporally coherent representations and stable affinities.

Method: Alternating optimization of structured frame representations and self-expressive coefficients; coding-rate maximization regularizer to enforce a UoS-like distribution and avoid representation collapse; temporal constraints to encourage temporally adjacent frames to share labels; temporal momentum averaging to stabilize affinity evolution; reparameterization for efficient optimization.

Result: Extensive experiments on five HMS datasets using both conventional HoG and modern deep features (CLIP, DINOv2) show the proposed method is effective and robust across feature types, validating the approach.

Conclusion: TDSC provides an efficient and effective HMS framework that integrates structured representations, regularized self-expression, and temporal stabilization, yielding improved segmentation performance across diverse datasets and feature modalities.

Abstract: Human Motion Segmentation (HMS), which aims to partition a video into non-overlapping segments corresponding to different human motions, has recently attracted increasing research attention. Existing HMS approaches are predominantly based on subspace clustering, which are grounded on the assumption that the distribution of high-dimensional temporal features well aligns with a Union-of-Subspaces (UoS). For videos in the real world, however, the raw frame-level features often violate the UoS assumption and yield unsatisfactory segmentation performance. To address this issue, we propose an efficient and effective approach for HMS, named Temporal Deep Self-expressive subspace Clustering (TDSC), which jointly learns temporally consistent structured representations and stabilized affinity for accurate and robust HMS. Specifically, in TDSC, we alternately learn structured representations of the input frame features and self-expressive coefficients via a properly regularized self-expressive model, in which a coding-rate maximization regularizer is incorporated to avoid representation collapse and conform the learned representations to span a desired UoS distribution, and meanwhile, temporal constraints are incorporated to promote temporally adjacent frames to be partitioned into the same groups. Moreover, we develop a temporal momentum averaging mechanism to stabilize affinity evolution and design a reparameterization strategy to enable efficient optimization. We conduct extensive experiments on five benchmark HMS datasets using both conventional (HoG) and up-to-date deep features (i.e., CLIP, DINOv2) to validate the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [35] [iTRIALSPACE: Programmable Virtual Lesion Trials for Controlled Evaluation of Lung CT Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05761)
*Fakrul Islam Tushar,Umme Hafsa Momy,Joseph Y. Lo,Geoffrey D. Rubin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: iTRIALSPACE enables controlled, auditable virtual lesion trials for lung CT models by composing real CTs and lesion profiles via a four-stage pipeline; demonstrates strong transfer to real data and reveals insights unattainable with static benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static retrospective benchmarks entangle factors like lesion size, anatomy, and acquisition context, hindering understanding of what drives model accuracy. A controlled evaluation framework is needed for falsifiable testing and comparability across methods.

Method: A four-stage pipeline: (1) multidataset nodule profiling; (2) explicit trial specification; (3) anatomy-aware mask insertion; (4) ControlNet-conditioned CT synthesis. Builds on a unified 54-attribute nodule-profile dataset (13,140 nodules from seven public CT sources) instantiated as 13 trial modes. Evaluated in a 55,469-sample Virtual Lesion Study across three medical VLMs, four spatial-guidance conditions, and three clinical tasks.

Result: Synthetic substrate stays within the real-to-real FID baseline; synthetic performance rankings correlate strongly with real data (ρ = 0.93, p < 1e-15). Controlled modes reveal insights not seen in fixed benchmarks, including shortcut-driven size prediction collapse under lobe-equalized sampling and host-to-donor variance ratios of 8.9x and 3.3x in twin-cross analysis.

Conclusion: iTRIALSPACE provides an auditable evaluation infrastructure for controlled, falsifiable testing beyond static retrospective benchmarks and enables systematic disentanglement of factors shaping model performance.

Abstract: We introduce iTRIALSPACE, a programmable evaluation framework for controlled assessment of lung CT models. Standard benchmarks are static retrospective collections that entangle lesion size, lobe prevalence, anatomy, and acquisition context, making it difficult to determine what structurally drives model accuracy. iTRIALSPACE addresses this limitation by composing real clinical CTs and lesion profiles into controlled virtual lesion trials through a four-stage pipeline: multidataset nodule profiling, explicit trial specification, anatomy-aware mask insertion, and ControlNet-conditioned CT synthesis. The framework is built on a unified 54-attribute nodule-profile dataset spanning 13,140 annotated nodules from seven public CT sources and instantiated as 13 trial modes. We evaluate iTRIALSPACE in a 55,469-sample Virtual Lesion Study spanning three medical VLMs, four spatialguidance conditions, and three clinical tasks. Across all 13 modes, the synthetic substrate remains within the real-to-real FID baseline, and synthetic performance rankings transfer strongly to real clinical data ($ρ$ = 0.93, p < 10$^{-15}$). Controlled trial modes expose findings unavailable to fixed-distribution benchmarks, including shortcut-driven size prediction collapse under lobe-equalized sampling and hostto-donor variance ratios of 8.9x and 3.3x in twin-cross analysis. These results position iTRIALSPACE as an auditable evaluation infrastructure for controlled, falsifiable testing beyond static retrospective benchmarks.

</details>


### [36] [Reconstruction or Semantics? What Makes a Latent Space Useful for Robotic World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06388)
*Nilaksh,Saurav Jha,Artem Zholus,Sarath Chandar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Semantic latent spaces outperform reconstruction encoders for action-conditioned LDM world models; on BridgeV2, V-JEPA 2.1, Web-DINO, and SigLIP 2 provide stronger policy performance and latent quality than reconstruction encoders like VAE and Cosmos, though still achieving pixel fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine which latent encodings best support world-model-based policy evaluation in action-conditioned diffusion models, moving beyond pixel-reconstruction objectives to representation-aligned semantics.

Method: Systematic evaluation of six encoders (three reconstruction and three semantic) for training action-conditioned diffusion-world models. Encoders include reconstruction (VAE, Cosmos, and a third) and semantic (V-JEPA 2.1, Web-DINO, SigLIP 2). Fixed evaluation protocol on BridgeV2; examine high-dimensional representations with/without dimension compression; assess via three axes: visual fidelity, planning/policy performance, and latent representation quality.

Result: Semantic encoders generally outperform on planning/policy and latent quality across model scales, despite reconstruction encoders achieving strong pixel-level metrics. V-JEPA 2.1 shows strongest policy performance; Web-DINO and SigLIP 2 also perform well on policy and latent quality. Visual fidelity alone is not sufficient for world-model selection; semantic latent spaces provide a stronger foundation for policy-relevant robotics diffusion models.

Conclusion: Adopt semantic latent spaces as the preferred foundation for action-conditioned diffusion world models in robotics; visual fidelity alone cannot guide model selection; consider dimension compression and three-axis evaluation when comparing encoders.

Abstract: World model-based policy evaluation is a practical proxy for testing real-world robot control by rolling out candidate actions in action-conditioned video diffusion models. As these models increasingly adopt latent diffusion modeling (LDM), choosing the right latent space becomes critical. While the status quo uses autoencoding latent spaces like VAEs that are primarily trained for pixel reconstruction, recent work suggests benefits from pretrained encoders with representation-aligned semantic latent spaces. We systematically evaluate these latent spaces for action-conditioned LDM by comparing six reconstruction and semantic encoders to train world model variants under a fixed protocol on BridgeV2 dataset, and show effective world model training in high-dimensional representation spaces with and without dimension compression. We then propose three axes to assess robotic world model performance: visual fidelity, planning and downstream policy performance, and latent representation quality. Our results show visual fidelity alone is insufficient for world model selection. While reconstruction encoders like VAE and Cosmos achieve strong pixel-level scores, semantic encoders such as V-JEPA 2.1 (strongest overall on policy), Web-DINO, and SigLIP 2 generally excel across the other two axes at all model scales. Our study advocates semantic latent space as stronger foundation for policy-relevant robotics diffusion world models.

</details>


### [37] [X-OmniClaw Technical Report: A Unified Mobile Agent for Multimodal Understanding and Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05765)
*Xiaoming Ren,Ru Zhen,Chao Li,Yang Song,Qiuxia Hou,Yanhao Zhang,Peng Liu,Qi Qi,Quanlong Zheng,Qi Wu,Zhenyi Liao,Binqiang Pan,Haobo Ji,Haonan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: X-OmniClaw is a unified mobile agent architecture for Android that fuses multimodal perception, memory, and action. It includes Omni Perception (multimodal ingress and temporal alignment to structured intents), Omni Memory (runtime + long-term memory from local data for personalized interactions), and Omni Action (hybrid grounding using XML metadata and vision, with Behavior Cloning and Trajectory Replay to build reusable skills). Demonstrations suggest improved interaction efficiency and task reliability, offering an architectural blueprint for mobile-native personal assistants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a rising need for mobile-native personal agents that can perform complex, intuitive interactions with high contextual awareness. Building on OpenClaw-inspired ideas, the work aims to unify perception, memory, and action within the Android ecosystem to enable context-aware, personalized task execution on-device.

Method: A three-layer architecture: (1) Omni Perception with a unified multimodal ingress pipeline that incorporates UI states, real-world visuals, and speech, plus a temporal alignment module that decomposes data into structured multimodal intents; (2) Omni Memory that combines runtime working memory for task continuity with distilled long-term personal memory from local data to support personalized interactions; (3) Omni Action with a hybrid grounding strategy that fuses structural XML metadata and visual perception, plus Behavior Cloning and Trajectory Replay to capture user navigation as reusable skills for direct-access execution.

Result: Demonstrations across multiple scenarios show enhanced interaction efficiency and task reliability, indicating that the X-OmniClaw blueprint is practically viable and scalable as the next generation of mobile-native personal assistants.

Conclusion: X-OmniClaw provides a cohesive architectural blueprint for integrating perception, memory, and action in mobile agents, enabling context-aware, personalized interactions and robust task execution on mobile devices.

Abstract: Inspired by the development of OpenClaw, there is a growing demand for mobile-based personal agents capable of handling complex and intuitive interactions. In this technical report, we introduce X-OmniClaw, a unified mobile agent designed for multimodal understanding and interaction in the Android ecosystem. This unified architecture of perception, memory, and action enables the agent to handle complex mobile tasks with high contextual awareness. Specifically, Omni Perception provides a unified multimodal ingress pipeline that integrates UI states, real-world visual contexts, and speech inputs, leveraging a temporal alignment module to decompose raw data into structured multimodal intent representations. Omni Memory leverages multimodal memory optimization to enhance personalized intelligence by integrating runtime working memory for task continuity with long-term personal memory distilled from local data, enabling highly context-aware and personalized interactions. Finally, Omni Action employs a hybrid grounding strategy that combines structural XML metadata with visual perception for robust interaction. Through Behavior Cloning and Trajectory Replay, the system captures user navigation as reusable skills, enabling precise direct-access execution. Demonstrations across diverse scenarios show that X-OmniClaw effectively enhances interaction efficiency and task reliability, providing a practical architectural blueprint for the next generation of mobile-native personal assistants.

</details>


### [38] [Steering Visual Generation in Unified Multimodal Models with Understanding Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05781)
*Zeyu Liu,Zanlin Ni,Yang Yue,Cheng Da,Huan Yang,Di Zhang,Kun Gai,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UNO is a lightweight post-training framework that aligns understanding objectives with generative models by adding captioning (semantic abstraction) and visual regression (structural details) as supervisory signals to enable gradient flow from understanding to generation; it improves generation in image creation/editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art multimodal models decouple understanding and generation, which hinders mutual improvement; restoring synergy could boost generation by leveraging semantic/structural cues.

Method: Understanding-Oriented Post-Training (UNO): adds understanding-focused objectives (captioning and visual regression) as auxiliary tasks during post-training to provide direct supervision from understanding to generation; fosters gradient flow between components.

Result: Empirical evaluation on image generation and editing tasks demonstrates that incorporating understanding enhances generation performance, indicating understanding can catalyze generative capabilities.

Conclusion: Understanding can serve as a productive catalyst for generation in multimodal models; UNO offers a lightweight, effective mechanism to foster synergy between understanding and generation.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models are envisioned to bridge the gap between understanding and generation. Yet, to achieve competitive performance, state-of-the-art models adopt largely decoupled understanding and generation components. This design, while effective for individual tasks, weakens the connection required for mutual enhancement, leaving the potential synergy empirically uncertain. We propose to explicitly restore this synergy by introducing Understanding-Oriented Post-Training (UNO), a lightweight framework that treats understanding not only as a distinct task, but also a direct supervisory signal to steer generative representations. By incorporating objectives that encode semantic abstraction (captioning) and structural details (visual regression), we enable effective gradient flow from understanding to generation. Extensive experiments on image generation and editing demonstrate that understanding can serve as an effective catalyst for generation.

</details>


### [39] [Na-IRSTD: Enhancing Infrared Small Target Detection via Native-Resolution Feature Selection and Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05804)
*Qian Xu,Chi Zhang,Qiming Zhang,Xi Li,Haojuan Yuan,Mingjin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Na-IRSTD: a native-resolution infrared small target detection framework with token reduction/selection, preserving subtle target cues and achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the loss of small target cues and localization accuracy caused by downsampling and feature downscaling in conventional IRSTD methods within cluttered infrared backgrounds.

Method: Propose native-resolution feature extraction and fusion to preserve fine target details, plus a token reduction and selection strategy that identifies high-accuracy, high-confidence target patches while reducing native-resolution tokens to manage computational load.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate robustness and effectiveness of the token reduction/selection strategy, with Na-IRSTD achieving state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

Conclusion: Preserving native-resolution representations improves localization of small infrared targets; the token reduction approach yields efficient computation without sacrificing accuracy, enabling SOTA performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

</details>


### [40] [CXR-ContraBench: Benchmarking Negated-Option Attraction in Medical VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05810)
*Zhengru Fang,Yanan Ma,Yu Guo,Senkang Hu,Yixian Zhang,Hangcheng Cao,Wenbo Ding,Yuguang Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Benchmark reveals negated-option attraction and polarity-confusion in chest X-ray QA; a no-retraining verifier (QCCV-Neg) restores high accuracy, exposing hidden inference-time failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify and quantify polarity-confusion (negation bias) in medical vision-language models on presence/absence questions in chest X-ray data; assess clinical risk of negated answers.

Method: Introduce CXR-ContraBench benchmark spanning internal ReXVQA and external OpenI/CheXpert protocols; focus on present-finding questions (risk if 'No X' is chosen despite evidence) and absent-finding questions; evaluate direct presence probe accuracy, training-split protocol performance, apply chain-of-thought prompting, and apply QCCV-Neg verifier without retraining; provide source code.

Result: Direct presence accuracy: MedGemma 31.49%, Qwen2.5-VL 30.21%. In matched CheXpert training-split protocol, >62% of presence questions elicit negated selections. Chain-of-thought reduces some reversals but not eliminative; may amplify absence contradictions. QCCV-Neg repairs the polarity-confused subset, boosting accuracy to 96.60% (MedGemma) and 95.32% (Qwen2.5-VL).

Conclusion: Standard accuracy can mask clinically meaningful polarity failures; negation-aware verification and benchmarking reveal and mitigate such errors; code and benchmark available.

Abstract: When a chest X-ray shows consolidation but the question asks which finding is present, a medical vision-language model may answer "No consolidation." This is more than an incorrect choice: it is a polarity reversal that emits a clinical statement contradicting the image. We study this failure as negated-option attraction, where a model is drawn to a negated answer option even when it conflicts with both the visual evidence and the question. We introduce CXR-ContraBench (Chest X-Ray Contradiction Benchmark), a diagnostic benchmark spanning internal ReXVQA slices and external OpenI and CheXpert protocols. The benchmark centers on present-finding questions, where selecting "No X" despite visible X creates the main clinical risk, and uses absent-finding questions as secondary tests of whether models copy negated wording. Across CheXpert protocols, the failure is substantial and persistent. On a strict direct presence probe, MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL reach only 31.49% and 30.21% accuracy, respectively; on a matched 135,754-record CheXpert training-split protocol, both models select negated options on over 62% of presence questions. Chain-of-thought prompting reduces some presence-side reversals but does not eliminate them and can amplify absence-side contradictions. Finally, QCCV-Neg (Question-Conditioned Consistency Verifier for Negation) deterministically repairs the measured polarity-confused subset without retraining, raising MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL to 96.60% and 95.32% accuracy on the direct presence probe. These results show that standard accuracy can hide a clinically meaningful inference-time polarity failure. Source code and benchmark construction scripts are available at https://github.com/fangzr/cxr-contrabench-code.

</details>


### [41] [ChartZero: Synthetic Priors Enable Zero Shot Chart Data Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05820)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Yasir Mahmud,Sujan Kumar Saha,Mark Tehranipoor,Farimah Farahmandi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ChartZero enables zero-shot chart data extraction trained solely on synthetic simple functions, leveraging a Global Orthogonal Instance (GOI) loss and Vision-Language Model (VLM) guided legend matching, plus a new end-to-end reconstruction metric and benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end chart digitization is severely bottlenecked by stylistic diversity, annotation scarcity, curve fragmentation, brittle semantic associations, and non-holistic evaluation protocols.

Method: Train exclusively on synthetic mathematical functions; introduce Global Orthogonal Instance (GOI) loss to reduce curve fragmentation; replace brittle spatial heuristics with open-vocabulary VLM-guided legend matching; propose a dedicated end-to-end reconstruction metric and benchmark for evaluation.

Result: Empirical evaluations indicate ChartZero substantially improves generalized plot digitization without real-world supervision; code and dataset will be released upon acceptance.

Conclusion: Synthetic-data training with GOI loss and VLM-guided legend matching enables robust zero-shot chart data extraction and establishes a new end-to-end benchmark for chart digitization.

Abstract: Automated data extraction from line charts remains fundamentally bottlenecked by extreme stylistic diversity and a severe scarcity of comprehensively annotated, real-world datasets. Current end-to-end pipelines depend heavily on costly manual annotations, crippling their ability to generalize across arbitrary aesthetics and grid layouts. Furthermore, existing models suffer from two critical failure modes during reconstruction. First, extracting thin, intersecting curves frequently causes structural fragmentation and the erasure of fine visual details, as standard architectures struggle against complex backgrounds. Second, semantic association is notoriously error-prone; current pipelines rely on rigid spatial heuristics that easily break down against the unpredictable legend placements of in-the-wild charts. Finally, measuring true progress is hindered by evaluation protocols that assess isolated sub-tasks rather than holistic, end-to-end data reconstruction. To address these foundational issues, we introduce ChartZero, a parsing framework that leverages synthetic priors to enable robust zero-shot chart data extraction. By training exclusively on a purely synthetic dataset of simple mathematical functions, our model completely bypasses the real-world annotation bottleneck. We overcome curve fragmentation via a novel Global Orthogonal Instance (GOI) loss, and replace brittle spatial rules with an open-vocabulary, Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided legend matching strategy. Accompanied by a new metric and benchmark specifically designed for full end-to-end reconstruction, our evaluations demonstrate that ChartZero significantly advances generalized plot digitization without requiring real-world supervision. Code and dataset will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [42] [Unifying Scientific Communication: Fine-Grained Correspondence Across Scientific Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05831)
*Megha Mariam K. M,Vineeth N. Balasubramanian,C. V. Jawahar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces the Multimodal Conference Dataset (MCD), a benchmark linking research papers, presentation videos, explanatory videos, and slides from the same works to study cross-format correspondences; evaluates embedding-based and vision-language models; reveals strengths and limitations and releases resources for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific communication is increasingly multimodal, but cross-format links among text, visuals, and speech are missing, hindering unified analysis and exploration of research content.

Method: Construct MCD by aggregating matched components across papers, slides, and videos; evaluate a range of embedding-based and vision-language models to discover fine-grained cross-format correspondences and analyze their表现 in alignment tasks.

Result: Vision-language models show robustness in cross-modal retrieval but struggle with fine-grained alignment; embedding-based models capture text-visual correspondences well yet struggle with symbolic content like equations, which form distinct clusters; the study establishes the first systematic benchmark for this task and highlights key directions for future research.

Conclusion: Current models have complementary strengths; improving fine-grained, cross-format alignment, especially for symbolic content, is a priority; the authors provide resources to enable reproducibility and further research in multimodal scientific understanding.

Abstract: The communication of scientific knowledge has become increasingly multimodal, spanning text, visuals, and speech through materials such as research papers, slides, and recorded presentations. These different representations collectively convey a study's reasoning, results, and insights, offering complementary perspectives that enrich understanding. However, despite their shared purpose, such materials are rarely connected in a structured way. The absence of explicit links across formats makes it difficult to trace how concepts, visuals, and explanations correspond, limiting unified exploration and analysis of research content. To address this gap, we introduce the Multimodal Conference Dataset (MCD), the first benchmark that integrates research papers, presentation videos, explanatory videos, and slides from the same works. We evaluate a range of embedding-based and vision-language models to assess their ability to discover fine-grained cross-format correspondences, establishing the first systematic benchmark for this task. Our results show that vision-language models are robust but struggle with fine-grained alignment, while embedding-based models capture text-visual correspondences well but equations and symbolic content form distinct clusters in the embedding space. These findings highlight both the strengths and limitations of current approaches and point to key directions for future research in multimodal scientific understanding. To ensure reproducibility, we release the resources for MCD at https://github.com/meghamariamkm2002/MCD

</details>


### [43] [VideoRouter: Query-Adaptive Dual Routing for Efficient Long-Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05848)
*Kuanwei Lin,Wenhao Zhang,Ge Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoRouter introduces a query-adaptive dual-router on InternVL to allocate visual-token budgets across frames, enabling aggressive compression on less relevant frames while preserving critical evidence; demonstrates up to 67.9% token reduction across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long video sequences yield very long visual-token sequences, causing high memory and latency; fixed or weakly query-aware compression fails when evidence is unevenly distributed in time.

Method: Dual-router framework: Semantic Router selects an allocation policy (broad temporal coverage vs adaptive high-resolution preservation); Image Router scores frame relevance using early LLM layers; budgeted evidence allocation with aggressive compression on low-relevance frames and preserved detail on key frames. Training uses Video-QTR-10K for allocation-policy supervision and Video-FLR-200K for frame-relevance supervision. Implemented on InternVL; evaluated on VideoMME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench.

Result: Consistent improvements over the InternVL baseline under comparable or lower budgets; up to 67.9% token reduction.

Conclusion: Query-adaptive budgeted evidence allocation via a dual-router approach effectively improves efficiency of large video multimodal models while preserving critical information; validated across multiple benchmarks with supervised datasets.

Abstract: Video large multimodal models increasingly face a scalability bottleneck: long videos produce excessively long visual-token sequences, which sharply increase memory and latency during inference. While existing compression methods are effective in specific settings, most are either weakly query-aware or apply a fixed compression policy across frames, proving suboptimal when visual evidence is unevenly distributed over time. To address this, we present VideoRouter, a query-adaptive dual-router framework built on InternVL for budgeted evidence allocation. The Semantic Router predicts the dominant allocation policy, choosing between broad temporal coverage and adaptive high-resolution preservation, while the Image Router uses early LLM layers to score frame relevance. This enables aggressive compression on less relevant frames while preserving detail on critical evidence frames. To train both routers, we build Video-QTR-10K for allocation-policy supervision and Video-FLR-200K for frame-relevance supervision. Experiments on VideoMME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench show that VideoRouter consistently improves over the InternVL baseline under comparable or lower budgets, achieving up to a 67.9% token reduction.

</details>


### [44] [Align3D-AD: Cross-Modal Feature Alignment and Dual-Prompt Learning for Zero-shot 3D Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05850)
*Letian Bai,Xuanming Cao,Juan Du,Chengyu Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage zero-shot 3D anomaly detection method Align3D-AD that aligns 3D rendering features to RGB semantics and uses dual-prompt contrastive learning; achieves leading performance on multiple datasets, addressing the RGB-encoder domain gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot 3D anomaly detection relies on geometry-centric multi-view projections processed by RGB-pretrained encoders, causing a domain gap between rendered features and semantic RGB representations. The work aims to bridge this gap by cross-modally aligning rendering features to RGB semantics and leveraging RGB guidance.

Method: Stage 1: Cross-modal feature alignment that maps rendering features into the RGB semantic space, plus a semantic consistency reweighting strategy to emphasize regions with strong semantic coherence. Stage 2: Modality-aware prompt learning with dual-prompt contrastive alignment, assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features and applying contrastive learning to enhance prompts.

Result: Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, Real3D-AD show Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods in both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, indicating strong generalization and robustness.

Conclusion: Align3D-AD provides a unified, effective framework for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection by semantically aligning 3D renderings with RGB guidance and using modality-specific prompts with contrastive alignment. Code and datasets will be released on acceptance.

Abstract: Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection aims to identify anomalies without access to training data from target categories. However, existing methods mainly rely on projecting 3D observations into multi-view representations that primarily capture geometric cues rather than realistic visual semantics and process them with vision encoders pretrained on RGB data, leading to a significant domain gap between the encoder and the projected representations. To address this issue, we propose Align3D-AD, a unified two-stage framework that leverages the RGB modality from auxiliary categories as cross-modal guidance for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection. First, we introduce a cross-modal feature alignment paradigm that maps rendering features into the RGB semantic space. Unlike prior works that implicitly rely on pretrained encoders, our method enables direct semantic transfer from RGB observations. A semantic consistency reweighting strategy is further introduced to refine feature alignment by reweighting local regions according to holistic semantic consistency. Second, we propose a modality-aware prompt learning framework with dual-prompt contrastive alignment. By assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features, our method captures complementary semantics across modalities, while the contrastive alignment further enhances prompt representations to improve discriminability. Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, and Real3D-AD demonstrate that Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods under both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, highlighting its generalization capability and robustness. Code and the dataset will be made available once our paper is accepted.

</details>


### [45] [InkDiffuser: High-Fidelity One-shot Chinese Calligraphy via Differentiable Morphological Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05865)
*Kunchong Shi,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InkDiffuser is a diffusion-based framework for one-shot Chinese calligraphy synthesis that improves stroke rendering and ink realism via (1) high-frequency content fusion and (2) a Differentiable Ink Structure (DIS) loss, enabling explicit ink-trace morphology learning from a single reference glyph and outperforming prior few-shot methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Chinese calligraphy generation methods suffer from poor stroke rendering and unrealistic ink morphology, limiting visual fidelity and artistic fluidity. There is a need for high-fidelity rendering and realistic ink behavior in one-shot/few-shot settings.

Method: Introduce two core components: (1) a high-frequency enhancement mechanism to fuse high-frequency representations for more accurate font structure during content extraction; (2) a differentiable ink structure (DIS) loss that integrates differentiable morphological operations into the diffusion process to learn explicit decomposition of ink-trace structures for fine-grained stroke contour refinement.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple styles and complex characters show that InkDiffuser generates superior calligraphy fonts with realistic ink rendering from a single reference glyph and outperforms existing few-shot font generation methods in structural consistency, detail fidelity, and visual authenticity.

Conclusion: InkDiffuser delivers significantly improved visual realism for one-shot Chinese calligraphy synthesis by combining high-frequency content fusion with a differentiable ink-structure loss, enabling precise stroke contour refinement; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Current Chinese calligraphy generation methods suffer from poor stroke rendering and unrealistic ink morphology, resulting in outputs with limited visual fidelity and artistic fluidity. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{InkDiffuser}, a diffusion-based generative framework for one-shot Chinese calligraphy synthesis. To guarantee high-fidelity rendering, we introduce two core contributions: a high-frequency enhancement mechanism and a Differentiable Ink Structure (DIS) loss that explicitly regularizes ink morphology. Inspired by the observation that high-frequency information in individual samples typically carries contour details, we enhance content extraction by explicitly fusing high-frequency representations for more accurate font structure. Furthermore, we propose a differentiable ink structure loss that integrates differentiable morphological operations into the diffusion process. By allowing the model to learn an explicit decomposition of ink-trace structures, DIS facilitates fine-grained refinement of stroke contours and delivers significantly improved visual realism in the generated calligraphy. Extensive experiments on various calligraphic styles and complex characters demonstrate that InkDiffuser can generate superior calligraphy fonts with realistic ink rendering effects from only a single reference glyph and outperform existing few-shot font generation approaches in structural consistency, detail fidelity, and visual authenticity. The code is available at the following address: https://github.com/JingVIPLab/InkDiffuser.

</details>


### [46] [Training-Free Dense Hand Contact Estimation with Multi-Modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05886)
*Daniel Sungho Jung,Kyoung Mu Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Zero-shot, training-free dense hand contact estimation via ContactPrompt using MLLMs; encodes 3D hand geometry with part segmentation and vertex-grid; multi-stage reasoning bridges semantics and geometry; claims state-of-the-art without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense hand contact estimation requires both high-level semantics and fine-grained geometry. MLLMs struggle with explicit 3D geometry and vertex-level contact; a training-free method to leverage MLLMs is desirable.

Method: Introduce detailed hand-part segmentation and a part-wise vertex-grid representation for structured local geometry; develop a multi-stage structured contact reasoning with part conditioning to connect global semantics with fine-grained geometry; training-free/zero-shot approach leveraging MLLMs.

Result: Claims to surpass previous supervised methods trained on large-scale dense contact datasets in a zero-shot setting; plans to release code.

Conclusion: ContactPrompt demonstrates a viable pathway to leverage MLLMs for dense hand contact estimation by combining structured geometric encoding with staged reasoning, enabling strong zero-shot performance and suggesting broader potential for geometry–semantics fusion with LLMs.

Abstract: Dense hand contact estimation requires both high-level semantic understanding and fine-grained geometric reasoning of human interaction to accurately localize contact regions. Recently, multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in understanding visual semantics, enabled by vision-language priors learned from large-scale data. However, leveraging MLLMs for dense hand contact estimation remains underexplored. There are two major challenges in applying MLLMs to dense hand contact estimation. First, encoding explicit 3D hand geometry is difficult, as MLLMs primarily operate on vision and language modalities. Second, capturing fine-grained vertex-level contact remains challenging, as MLLMs tend to focus on high-level semantics rather than detailed geometric reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose ContactPrompt, a training-free and zero-shot approach for dense hand contact estimation using MLLMs. To effectively encode 3D hand geometry, we introduce a detailed hand-part segmentation and a part-wise vertex-grid representation that provides structured, localized geometric information. To enable accurate and efficient dense contact prediction, we develop a multi-stage structured contact reasoning with part conditioning, progressively bridging global semantics and fine-grained geometry. Therefore, our method effectively leverages the reasoning capabilities of MLLMs while enabling precise dense hand contact estimation. Surprisingly, the proposed approach outperforms previous supervised methods trained on large-scale dense contact datasets without requiring any training. The codes will be released.

</details>


### [47] [DBMSolver: A Training-free Diffusion Bridge Sampler for High-Quality Image-to-Image Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05889)
*Sankarshana Venugopal,Mohammad Mostafavi,Jonghyun Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DBMSolver is a training-free exponential-integrator sampler for diffusion bridge models that speeds up I2I sampling by up to 5x while improving image quality, demonstrated across inpainting, stylization, and semantics-to-image tasks up to 256x256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slow sampling in diffusion-based image-to-image translation using Diffusion Bridge Models (DBMs) motivates a training-free, efficient solver that preserves or enhances quality, enabling real-world deployment.

Method: Leverages the semi-linear structure of DBM-associated SDE/ODE and applies exponential integrators to obtain first- and second-order solutions without training. This yields faster sampling with maintained or improved fidelity.

Result: NFEs reduced by up to 5x; FID improved (e.g., 53% reduction at 20 NFEs compared to 2nd-order baseline); achieves state-of-the-art efficiency-quality tradeoffs across inpainting, stylization, and semantics-to-image tasks at resolutions up to 256x256; code released.

Conclusion: A training-free, exponential-integrator-based sampler (DBMSolver) delivers substantial speedups and quality gains for diffusion-based I2I with DBMs, advancing practical applicability.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image-to-image (I2I) translation excels in high-fidelity generation but suffers from slow sampling in state-of-the-art Diffusion Bridge Models (DBMs), often requiring dozens of function evaluations (NFEs). We introduce DBMSolver, a training-free sampler that exploits the semi-linear structure of DBM's underlying SDE and ODE via exponential integrators, yielding highly-efficient 1st- and 2nd-order solutions. This reduces NFEs by up to 5x while boosting quality (e.g., FID drops 53% on DIODE at 20 NFEs vs. 2nd-order baseline). Experiments on inpainting, stylization, and semantics-to-image tasks across resolutions up to 256x256 show DBMSolver sets new SOTA efficiency-quality tradeoffs, enabling real-world applicability. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/snumprlab/dbmsolver.

</details>


### [48] [MTL-MAD: Multi-Task Learners are Effective Medical Anomaly Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05891)
*Bogdan Alexandru Bercean,Florinel Alin Croitoru,Vlad Hondru,Ciprian Mihai Ceausescu,Andreea Iuliana Ionescu,Radu Tudor Ionescu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task self-supervised learning framework with a Mixture-of-Experts learns from scratch multiple proxy tasks to model normal anatomy, achieving state-of-the-art anomaly detection and interpretable anomaly maps on BMAD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomalies are rare during training; single pretext/self-supervised tasks and reliance on large pre-trained models hinder robust anomaly detection. From-scratch multi-task MoE aims to build a robust normal representation by integrating diverse signals.

Method: Train multiple self-supervised and pseudo-labeling tasks jointly in a Mixture-of-Experts architecture. The joint model yields task-specific experts; anomaly scores are derived from how well the multi-task learner solves each task during inference, enabling anomaly scoring and interpretability.

Result: On the BMAD benchmark across modalities, the multi-task learner outperforms state-of-the-art competitors and provides interpretable anomaly maps.

Conclusion: Multi-task MoE-based self-supervised learning is effective for unsupervised anomaly detection in medical imaging and can produce clinically useful explanations; potential for broader applicability and exploration of additional tasks.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in medical images is a challenging task, since anomalies are not typically available during training. Recent methods leverage a single pretext task coupled with a large-scale pre-trained model to reach state-of-the-art performance. Instead, we propose to learn multiple self-supervised and pseudo-labeling tasks from scratch, using a joint model based on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). By carefully integrating multiple proxy tasks, the joint model effectively learns a robust representation of normal anatomical structures, so that anomaly scores can be derived based on how well the multi-task learner (MTL) solves each task during inference. We perform comprehensive experiments on BMAD, a recent benchmark that comprises a broad range of medical image modalities. The empirical results indicate that our multi-task learner is an effective anomaly detector, outperforming all state-of-the-art competitors on BMAD. Moreover, our model produces interpretable anomaly maps, potentially helping physicians in providing more accurate diagnoses.

</details>


### [49] [Detecting AI-Generated Videos with Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05895)
*Minsuk Jang,Yujin Yang,Heeseon Kim,Minseok Son,Younghun Kim,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAST uses a spike-driven temporal branch with a frozen semantic encoder to detect AI-generated videos, achieving strong cross-generator generalization (93.14% mean accuracy across 10 unseen generators on GenVideo) and matching or surpassing ANN baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-generated videos exhibit cross-generator variability in artifacts and timescales; existing detectors struggle to generalize across generators. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer sparse, event-driven residual processing that aligns with temporal and edge-localized artifacts.

Method: Introduce MAST, a detector that processes multi-channel temporal residuals through a spike-driven temporal branch while keeping a frozen semantic encoder for stable features. Leverage cross-generator training/evaluation on GenVideo and compare to ANN-based detectors.

Result: On GenVideo, MAST achieves 93.14% mean accuracy across 10 unseen generators under strict cross-generator evaluation, matching or surpassing strong ANN-based detectors.

Conclusion: Spike-driven temporal processing with SNNs provides robust cross-generator generalization for AI-generated video detection and complements semantic encoding, illustrating practical applicability of SNNs in this domain.

Abstract: Modern AI-generated videos are photorealistic at the single-frame level, leaving inter-frame dynamics as the main remaining axis for detection. Existing detectors typically handle this temporal evidence in three ways: feeding the full frame sequence to a generic temporal backbone, reducing one dominant temporal cue to fixed video-level descriptors, or comparing temporal features to real-video statistics through a detection metric. These strategies degrade sharply under cross-generator evaluation, where artifact type and timescale vary across generators. On caption-paired benchmark, GenVidBench, we identify two signatures that prior detectors do not jointly exploit: AI-generated videos exhibit smoother frame-to-frame temporal residuals at the pixel level, and more compact trajectories in the semantic feature space, indicating a temporal smoothness gap at both levels. We further observe that, when raw video is fed into a Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), fake clips elicit firing predominantly at object and motion boundaries, unlike real clips, suggesting that the SNN responds to temporal artifacts localized at edges. These cues are sparse, asynchronous, and concentrated at moments of change, which makes SNNs a natural choice for this task: their event-driven, sparsely-activated dynamics align with the structure of the residual signal in a way that dense ANN backbones do not. Building on this observation, we propose MAST, a detector that processes multi-channel temporal residuals with a spike-driven temporal branch alongside a frozen semantic encoder for cross-generator generalization. On the GenVideo benchmark, MAST achieves 93.14\% mean accuracy across 10 unseen generators under strict cross-generator evaluation, matching or surpassing the strongest ANN-based detectors and demonstrating the practical applicability of SNNs to AI-generated video detection.

</details>


### [50] [Understanding Cross-Language Transfer Improvements in Low-Resource HTR: The Role of Sequence Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05900)
*Sana Al-azzawi,Chang Liu,Nudrat Habib,Elisa Barney,Marcus Liwicki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: In low-resource cross-language Arabic-script HTR, CRNNs with sequence modeling outperform CNN-only + CTC; shared visual features alone do not explain transfer gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether cross-language transfer in low-resource Arabic-script HTR is primarily due to shared visual (CNN) representations or sequence-level dependencies captured by CRNN architectures.

Method: Controlled architectural study comparing CNN-only models with CTC decoding versus CRNN models, under identical single-script and multi-script training regimes across KHATT (Arabic), NUST-UHWR (Urdu), and PHTD (Persian) datasets with low-resource settings K ∈ {100, 500, 1000}.

Result: CRNN models achieve better transfer under multi-script training, especially in the most data-constrained settings; CNN-only models show limited or unstable improvements. Delta CER improvements correlate with sequence-level modeling rather than shared CNN features.

Conclusion: Contextual (sequence-level) modeling is crucial for effective cross-language transfer in low-resource Arabic-script HTR; relying on shared visual representations alone is insufficient. Findings likely extend to other low-resource language scenarios.

Abstract: Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) for Arabic-script languages benefits from cross-language joint training under low-resource conditions, particularly when using CRNN-based models that combine convolutional encoders with sequence modeling. However, it remains unclear whether these improvements are better explained by shared visual representations or sequence-level dependencies. In this work, we conduct a controlled architectural study of line-level Arabic-script HTR, comparing CNN-only models with CTC decoding and CRNN models under identical single-script and multi-script training regimes. Experiments are performed on Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR), and Persian (PHTD) datasets under low-resource settings (K in {100, 500, 1000}). Our results show a clear divergence in transfer behavior: while CNN-only models exhibit limited or unstable improvements, CRNN models achieve better performance under multi-script training, particularly in the most data-constrained regimes. Focusing on transfer improvements (delta CER) rather than absolute performance, we find that cross-language improvements are associated with sequence-level modeling, while sharing visual representations learned by the CNN encoder, corresponding to similarities in character shapes across scripts, alone appears to be insufficient. This finding suggests that contextual modeling plays an important role in enabling effective transfer in low-resource scenarios, and that similar behavior may extend to other low-resource language settings.

</details>


### [51] [Architecture-agnostic Lipschitz-constant Bayesian header and its application to resolve semantically proximal classification errors with vision transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05908)
*Frederik Schäfer,Luis Mandl,Lars Kälber,Tim Ricken*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LipB-ViT: an architecture-agnostic, bi-Lipschitz-constrained Bayesian ViT that calibrates uncertainty and detects label noise. It uses spectral normalization on both mean and log-variance of variational weights, Monte Carlo sampling, and an adaptive fusion of feature proximity with predictive uncertainty to identify corrupted labels, outperforming kNN-based methods but with higher compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Label noise, especially semantically proximal and structured errors, severely harms generalization of supervised models. There is a need for an architecture-agnostic method that calibrates predictive uncertainty and reliably detects corrupted labels while remaining compatible with pre-trained backbones.

Method: Incorporates a bi-Lipschitz constraint into Bayesian ViTs by applying spectral normalization to both the mean and the log-variance of variational weights. Uses Monte Carlo sampling for uncertainty estimates, and introduces an adaptive arithmetic-mean fusion that combines feature-space proximity with predictive uncertainty to detect label noise. Proposes a new metric that jointly captures uncertainty and confidence across misclassification rates. Designed to be plug-and-play with pre-trained backbones and consistent hyperparameters.

Result: Demonstrates higher recall in detecting corrupted labels than state-of-the-art kNN-based methods (recall > 0.93 at 15% semantically mislabeled labels). Shows robustness to both structured (adversarial) and unstructured noise at inference, under high-noise and attack scenarios. Provides a second metric for joint dataset-quality and label-noise quantification. Despite increased computation from Monte Carlo sampling, it maintains compatibility with existing backbones and hyperparameters.

Conclusion: LipB-ViT offers an architecture-agnostic, robust approach to detect label noise and calibrate uncertainty, with practical plug-and-play deployment and meaningful metrics for dataset-quality assessment, suitable for high-stakes environments where annotation reliability is variable.

Abstract: Label noise remains a critical bottleneck for the generalization of supervised deep learning models, particularly when errors are structured rather than random. Standard robust training methods often fail in the presence of such semantically proximal classification errors. This work presents an architecture-agnostic Lipschitz-constant Bayesian header that can be integrated into feature extractors such as vision transformers, yielding the bi-Lipschitz-constrained Bayesian Vision Transformer (LipB-ViT). In contrast to conventional Bayesian layers, our approach enforces spectral normalization on both the mean and log-variance of the variational weights, which promotes calibrated predictive uncertainty and mitigates noise amplification.
