Through a comprehensive experimental evaluation, we show that modern pretrained vision transformers violate the locality assumption and exhibit a strong intra-object leakage, in which each part encodes information from the whole object, a visual metonymy that compromises the faithfulness of attention-based interpretable-by-design methods for part-based reasoning, ultimately rendering them uninterpretable. In addition, we establish an upper bound using a two-stage approach that prevents leakage by design. We then show that this inherently disentangled feature extraction improves attribute-driven part discovery on a variety of tasks, confirming the practical impact of intra-object leakage. Our results uncover a neglected issue affecting the interpretability of part-based representations, such as those in CBMs relying on part-centric concepts, highlighting that two-stage approaches offer a promising way to mitigate it.

</details>


### [77] [Dynamic Pondering Sparsity-aware Mixture-of-Experts Transformer for Event Stream based Visual Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06112)
*Shiao Wang,Xiao Wang,Duoqing Yang,Wenhao Zhang,Bo Jiang,Lin Zhu,Yonghong Tian,Bin Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An event-sparsity-aware tracking framework that uses multi-density event regions in a three-stage ViT, aided by a sparsity-aware Mixture-of-Experts and dynamic inference-depth adaptation for robust, efficient event-based tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB trackers struggle under low illumination and fast motion; event cameras offer high dynamic range and temporal resolution, but existing trackers ignore event sparsity and rely on fixed temporal windows, limiting adaptability to varying motion dynamics.

Method: Proposes a three-stage Vision Transformer backbone that progressively injects sparse, medium-density, and dense event search regions for hierarchical multi-density feature learning; introduces a sparsity-aware Mixture-of-Experts to specialize on different sparsity patterns; implements a dynamic pondering strategy to adapt inference depth to tracking difficulty.

Result: Extensive experiments on FE240hz, COESOT, and EventVOT show favorable trade-offs between tracking accuracy and computational efficiency; code will be released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: The framework effectively models event-density variations across scales, enables adaptive, efficient tracking across diverse motion conditions, and demonstrates robust performance on multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Despite significant progress, RGB-based trackers remain vulnerable to challenging imaging conditions, such as low illumination and fast motion. Event cameras offer a promising alternative by asynchronously capturing pixel-wise brightness changes, providing high dynamic range and high temporal resolution. However, existing event-based trackers often neglect the intrinsic spatial sparsity and temporal density of event data, while relying on a single fixed temporal-window sampling strategy that is suboptimal under varying motion dynamics. In this paper, we propose an event sparsity-aware tracking framework that explicitly models event-density variations across multiple temporal scales. Specifically, the proposed framework progressively injects sparse, medium-density, and dense event search regions into a three-stage Vision Transformer backbone, enabling hierarchical multi-density feature learning. Furthermore, we introduce a sparsity-aware Mixture-of-Experts module to encourage expert specialization under different sparsity patterns, and design a dynamic pondering strategy to adaptively adjust the inference depth according to tracking difficulty. Extensive experiments on FE240hz, COESOT, and EventVOT demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves a favorable trade-off between tracking accuracy and computational efficiency. The source code will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenEvTracking.

</details>


### [78] [Pest-Thinker: Learning to Think and Reason like Entomologists via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06121)
*Xueheng Li,Yu Wang,Tao Hu,Ji Huang,Ke Cao,Qize Yang,Rui Li,Jie Zhang,Chengjun Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pest-Thinker introduces a knowledge-driven RL framework for multimodal LLMs to reason about fine-grained pest morphology. It builds two HD pest benchmarks (QFSD, AgriInsect), synthesizes Chain-of-Thought trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, and applies Group Relative Policy Optimization with a feature reward guided by an LLM-as-a-Judge, achieving notable improvements in in-domain and out-of-domain morphological understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pest recognition faces high inter-species complexity, intra-species variability, and scarce expert-annotated data. Current Multimodal LLMs struggle with fine-grained pest morphology. The work aims to enable structured visual reasoning and robust generalization for pest analysis in agriculture.

Method: 1) Construct two high-definition pest benchmarks (QFSD, AgriInsect) with diverse species and expert-annotated morphological traits. 2) Synthesize Chain-of-Thought reasoning trajectories and perform Supervised Fine-Tuning to teach the model to recognize pest-specific visual cues. 3) Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel feature reward to guide attention to observable morphological evidence. 4) Use an LLM-as-a-Judge strategy to assess the evidence quality. 

Result: Extensive experiments show Pest-Thinker substantially improves both in-domain and out-of-domain morphological understanding, approaching expert-level visual reasoning for pest analysis. The datasets and source code are slated for release upon acceptance.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that integrating knowledge-driven RL with CoT learning and LLM-guided evaluation can enhance fine-grained morphological reasoning in MLLMs for agricultural pests, and provides two new benchmarks to support future research.

Abstract: Pest-induced crop losses pose a major threat to global food security and sustainable agricultural development. While recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for visual understanding and smart agriculture, their direct application to pest recognition remains limited due to the domain's unique challenges such as high inter-species complexity, intra-species variability, and the scarcity of expert-annotated data. In this work, we introduce Pest-Thinker, a knowledge-driven reinforcement learning (RL) framework that enables MLLMs to reason over fine-grained pest morphology. We first construct two high-definition pest benchmarks, QFSD and AgriInsect, comprising diverse species and expert-annotated morphological traits. Leveraging these datasets, we synthesize Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning trajectories to facilitate structured learning of pest-specific visual cues through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Subsequently, we employ Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel feature reward that guides the model to focus on observable morphological evidence, assessed by an LLM-as-a-Judge strategy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Pest-Thinker substantially improves both in-domain and out-of-domain morphological understanding, marking a step toward expert-level visual reasoning for intelligent agricultural pest analysis. The datasets and source code are available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [79] [Continuous Expert Assembly: Instance-Conditioned Low-Rank Residuals for All-in-One Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06127)
*Haisen He,Xiangyu Zou,SongLin Dong,Heng Li,Yihong Gong,Zhiheng Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Continuous Expert Assembly (CEA) introduces a token-wise dynamic parameterization for all-in-one image restoration using a Cross-Attention Hyper-Adapter to synthesize per-token, low-rank routing bases and residual directions, enabling dense, per-token updates without prompts or fixed expert banks, and achieving improved restoration on spatially varying degradations with efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world degradations are unknown, spatially non-uniform, and compositional. A single model must adapt to diverse local corruption patterns without access to test-time degradation labels, which challenges global conditioning and static expert routing.

Method: A Lightweight Cross-Attention Hyper-Adapter probes intermediate spatial features to generate instance-conditioned low-rank routing bases and residual directions. Each spatial token assembles its own residual update via dense signed dot-product affinities over the rank-wise components, avoiding external prompts, static expert banks, and Top-k selection. The approach admits a linear-attention interpretation for transparency.

Result: On AIO-3, AIO-5, and CDD-11, CEA yields higher average restoration quality than strong prompt-, descriptor-, and expert-based baselines, with the largest gains on spatially varying and compositional degradations, while maintaining favorable parameter count, FLOPs, and runtime.

Conclusion: CEA provides a scalable, interpretable, and efficient per-token adaptation mechanism for all-in-one image restoration, addressing the limitations of global conditioning and static expert routing.

Abstract: Real-world image degradation is often unknown, spatially non-uniform, and compositional, requiring all-in-one restoration models to adapt a single set of weights to diverse local corruption patterns without test-time degradation labels. Existing methods typically modulate a shared backbone with global prompts or degradation descriptors, or route features through predefined expert pools. However, compact global conditioning can bottleneck localized degradation evidence, while static expert routing may produce homogeneous updates or rely on unstable sparse assignments. We propose \textbf{Continuous Expert Assembly} (CEA), a token-wise dynamic parameterization framework for all-in-one image restoration. CEA employs a lightweight \textbf{Cross-Attention Hyper-Adapter} to probe intermediate spatial features and synthesize instance-conditioned low-rank routing bases and residual directions. Each spatial token then assembles its own residual update via dense signed dot-product affinities over the generated rank-wise components, avoiding external prompts, static expert banks, and discrete Top-
  selection. The resulting assembly rule also admits a linear-attention perspective, making its dense token-wise routing behavior transparent. Experiments on AIO-3, AIO-5, and CDD-11 show that CEA improves average restoration quality over strong prompt-, descriptor-, and expert-based baselines, with the clearest gains on spatially varying and compositional degradations, while maintaining favorable parameter, FLOP, and runtime efficiency.

</details>


### [80] [Autoregressive Visual Generation Needs a Prologue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06137)
*Bowen Zheng,Weijian Luo,Guang Yang,Colin Zhang,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prologue introduces a small set of prologue tokens prepended to visual tokens in autoregressive image generation, trained solely with AR cross-entropy to decouple generation from reconstruction. This improves generation metrics (gFID, rFID) on ImageNet 256x256 without hurting reconstruction and reveals emergent semantic structure in the prologue tokens, suggesting a two-representation approach for better generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the reconstruction-generation trade-off in AR image generation by decoupling the generative representation from reconstruction tokens, enabling the AR model to optimize the true distribution for generation without compromising reconstruction quality.

Method: Add a short sequence of learnable prologue tokens before the visual token sequence. Train prologues with AR cross-entropy while keeping visual tokens dedicated to reconstruction. Formalize the setup via an ELBO perspective.

Result: Prologue-Base reduces gFID from 21.01 to 10.75 on ImageNet 256x256 without guidance and preserves reconstruction well; Prologue-Large achieves rFID 0.99 and gFID 1.46 using a standard AR model with no extra semantic supervision. Prologues exhibit emergent semantic structure (16-token linear probe yields 35.88% Top-1). Fixed prologues preserve high-level semantic layout during resampling.

Conclusion: Separating a learned generative representation via prologue tokens can boost generation quality while maintaining the original reconstruction representation, pointing to a new direction in AR-image generation.

Abstract: In this work, we propose Prologue, an approach to bridging the reconstruction-generation gap in autoregressive (AR) image generation. Instead of modifying visual tokens to satisfy both reconstruction and generation, Prologue generates a small set of prologue tokens prepended to the visual token sequence. These prologue tokens are trained exclusively with the AR cross-entropy (CE) loss, while visual tokens remain dedicated to reconstruction. This decoupled design lets us optimize generation through the AR model's true distribution without affecting reconstruction quality, which we further formalize from an ELBO perspective. On ImageNet 256x256, Prologue-Base reduces gFID from 21.01 to 10.75 without classifier-free guidance while keeping reconstruction almost unchanged; Prologue-Large reaches a competitive rFID of 0.99 and gFID of 1.46 using a standard AR model without auxiliary semantic supervision. Interestingly, driven only by AR gradients, prologue tokens exhibit emergent semantic structure: linear probing on 16 prologue tokens reaches 35.88% Top-1, far above the 23.71% of the first 16 tokens from a standard tokenizer; resampling with fixed prologue tokens preserves a similar high-level semantic layout. Our results suggest a new direction: generation quality can be improved by introducing a separate learned generative representation while leaving the original representation intact.

</details>


### [81] [AI-Generated Images: What Humans and Machines See When They Look at the Same Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06143)
*Silvia Poletti,Justin Ilyes,Marcel Hasenbalg,David Fischinger,Martin Boyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A study on making detectors for AI-generated images more explainable to humans by integrating 16 XAI methods, training on a large synthetic dataset (AIText2Image), and evaluating explanations via a human survey with 100 participants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the rising risk of online disinformation via AI-generated images and the need for transparent, human-understandable explanations of detectors' predictions.

Method: Develops a suite of detectors with diverse architectures and fine-tuning strategies trained on AIText2Image; evaluates on state-of-the-art text-to-image generators; integrates 16 XAI methods into the detection framework; refines and evaluates visual explanations with a novel human-centric approach using textual and visual responses collected from a 100-participant survey.

Result: Offers insights into visual-language cues that support fake image detection and assesses the clarity and human-alignment of XAI methods, measuring how well XAI outputs match human preferences.

Conclusion: The framework advances human-centered evaluation of XAI in AI-generated image detection, informing the design of detectors and explanations that better align with user expectations and improve transparency in disinformation countermeasures.

Abstract: The misuse of generative AI in online disinformation campaigns highlights the urgent need for transparent and explainable detection systems. In this work, we investigate how detectors for AI-generated images can be more effective in providing human-understandable explanations for their predictions. To this end, we develop a suite of detectors with various architectures and fine-tuning strategies, trained on our large-scale photorealistic fake image dataset, AIText2Image, and assess their performance on state-of-the-art text-to-image AI generators. We integrate 16 different explainable AI (XAI) methods into our detection framework, and the visual explanations are comprehensively refined and evaluated through a novel approach that prioritizes human understanding of AI-generated images, using both textual and visual responses collected from a survey of 100 participants. This framework offers insights into visual-language cues in fake image detection and into the clarity of XAI methods from a human perspective, measuring the alignment of XAI outputs with human preferences.

</details>


### [82] [Learning Discrete Autoregressive Priors with Wasserstein Gradient Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06148)
*Bowen Zheng,Yihong Luo,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage discrete image tokenizer training preserves reconstruction but misaligns with an autoregressive prior. Introducing a distribution-level prior-matching signal via Wasserstein gradient flow (wAR-Tok) improves AR predictability and generation quality without sacrificing reconstruction, yielding better AR loss and FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fix the mismatch between tokenizer learning and the downstream autoregressive prior, which leaves prior-consistency outside the tokenizer objective and harms left-to-right token prediction.

Method: Decompose learning with Tripartite Variational Consistency (TVC) into conditional-likelihood consistency, prior consistency, and posterior consistency. Add a distribution-level prior-matching signal during tokenizer training using Wasserstein-gradient-flow updates; for hard tokens, implement a token-level contrast between an auxiliary AR model and the target AR prior using only forward passes (no backprop through AR models).

Result: The wAR-Tok tokenizer reduces autoregressive (AR) loss and improves generation FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet while maintaining comparable reconstruction quality to baselines.

Conclusion: Aligning the tokenizer with the downstream AR prior via a prior-consistency signal improves autoregressive generation without sacrificing reconstruction. The approach is general to discrete tokenizers and demonstrates practical gains in standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Discrete image tokenizers are commonly trained in two stages: first for reconstruction, and then with a prior model fitted to the frozen token sequences. This decoupling leaves the tokenizer unaware of the model that will later generate its tokens. As a result, the learned tokens may preserve image information well but still be difficult for an autoregressive (AR) prior to predict from left to right. We analyze this mismatch using Tripartite Variational Consistency (TVC), which decomposes latent-variable learning into three consistency conditions: conditional-likelihood consistency, prior consistency, and posterior consistency. TVC shows that two-stage training preserves the reconstruction side but leaves prior consistency outside the tokenizer objective: the overall token distribution is fixed before the AR prior participates in training. Motivated by this view, we add a distribution-level prior-matching signal during tokenizer training, while keeping the reconstruction objective unchanged. We optimize this signal with a Wasserstein-gradient-flow update. For hard categorical tokens, the update reduces to a token-level contrast between an auxiliary AR model that tracks the tokenizer's current token distribution and the target AR prior. It requires only forward passes through the two AR models and does not backpropagate through either of them. The resulting tokenizer, wAR-Tok, reduces AR loss and improves generation FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet at comparable reconstruction quality.

</details>


### [83] [Beyond Forgetting in Continual Medical Image Segmentation: A Comprehensive Benchmark Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06160)
*Bomin Wang,Hangqi Zhou,Yibo Gao,Xiahai Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Defines three clinically motivated continual learning scenarios for medical image segmentation (Domain-CL, Class-CL, Organ-CL) and a comprehensive evaluation framework; through extensive experiments, replay-based methods excel in balancing stability/plasticity, while parameter-isolation reduces forgetting at the cost of larger models; forward generalizability remains underexplored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation in clinical environments encounters evolving domains, targets, and tasks. There is a need for standardized continual learning benchmarks that assess not only accuracy and forgetting but also plasticity, generalization, efficiency, and practical replay burdens.

Method: Propose three clinically motivated CL scenarios (Domain-CL, Class-CL, Organ-CL). Build an evaluation framework measuring performance, forgetting, plasticity, forward generalizability, parameter efficiency, and replay burden. Conduct extensive experiments with representative CL methods to compare trade-offs.

Result: Replay-based methods provide the best overall balance between stability and plasticity. Parameter-isolation approaches effectively reduce forgetting but require larger models. Forward generalizability is significantly understudied. No single method satisfies all requirements simultaneously.

Conclusion: This benchmark study establishes a standardized framework and highlights key trade-offs and gaps in continual medical image segmentation, guiding future research toward improving forward generalization and exploring hybrid approaches across learning paradigms.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) is essential for deploying medical image segmentation models in clinical environments where imaging domains, anatomical targets, and diagnostic tasks evolve over time. However, continual segmentation still faces three main challenges. First, the scenarios for this task remain insufficiently standardized for real-world clinical settings. Second, existing research has been primarily focused on mitigating forgetting, overlooking the other essential properties such as plasticity. Third, a benchmark work with comprehensive evaluation on existing methods is stll desirable. To address these gaps, we present such benchmark study of continual medical image segmentation. We first define three clinically motivated scenarios, namely Domain-CL, Class-CL, and Organ-CL, to respectively capture the cross-center domain shift, the incremental anatomical structure segmentation, and the cross-organ segmentation. We then introduce an evaluation framework that measures not only general performance and forgetting, but also plasticity, forward generalizability, parameter efficiency, and replay burden. The results, from extensive experiments with representative CL methods, showed that it was still challenging to develop a model that could satisfy all the requirements simultaneously. Nevertheless, these studies also suggested that the replay-based methods achieve the best overall balance between stability and plasticity, the parameter-isolation methods should be effective at reducing forgetting, though at the cost of increased model size, and the forward generalizability remain a significantly understudied aspect of this research field. Finally, we discuss related learning paradigms and outline future directions for continual medical image segmentation.

</details>


### [84] [DynT2I-Eval: A Dynamic Evaluation Framework for Text-to-Image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06170)
*Juntong Wang,Jiarui Wang,Huiyu Duan,Lewei Li,Guangtao Zhai,Xiongkuo Min*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DynT2I-Eval presents a fully automated dynamic evaluation framework for text-to-image models, generating fresh prompts from structured semantic spaces and scoring via prompt-conditioned pairwise comparisons with a Bayesian online leaderboard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate overfitting and benchmark contamination from fixed prompt sets by continuously refreshing prompts; enable robust, evolving evaluation as models and prompts change.

Method: Decompose long-form prompts into controllable dimensions (subject, logical constraint, environment, composition) to build a structured visual semantic space. Generate fresh prompts through task-specific spaces with difficulty-aware sampling; unify outputs into prompt-conditioned pairwise comparisons; use a dynamic scheduler, micro-batch aggregation, and weighted Bayesian updates to maintain a stable online leaderboard under changing prompts and model entries.

Result: Demonstrates that continually refreshed prompts reduce prompt-set tuning effects, with simulations and ablations showing the ranking framework balances cold-start convergence, late-entry discovery, and long-run fidelity.

Conclusion: Dynamic, prompt-stream based evaluation yields robust, fair benchmarking for T2I models and stabilizes leaderboards under evolving prompts and models.

Abstract: Existing text-to-image (T2I) benchmarks largely rely on fixed prompt sets, leaving them vulnerable to overfitting and benchmark contamination once publicly released and repeatedly reused. In this work, we propose DynT2I-Eval, a fully automated dynamic evaluation framework for T2I models. It constructs a structured visual semantic space from long-form descriptions, decomposing prompts into controllable dimensions (e.g., subject, logical constraint, environment, and composition). This enables the continuous generation of fresh prompts via task-specific spaces and difficulty-aware sampling. DynT2I-Eval evaluates model performance across text alignment, perceptual quality, and aesthetics. Heterogeneous outputs are unified into prompt-conditioned pairwise comparisons, allowing a dynamic scheduler, micro-batch aggregation, and weighted Bayesian updates to maintain a stable online leaderboard despite changing prompt distributions and model injection. Experiments with independently sampled prompt streams demonstrate that continually refreshed prompts provide a robust evaluation protocol, reducing the impact of prompt-set-specific tuning. Simulations and ablations further confirm that the proposed ranking framework achieves a strong balance among cold-start convergence, late-entry discovery, and long-run ranking fidelity.

</details>


### [85] [Retina-RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Vision-Language Modeling for Joint Retinal Diagnosis and Clinical Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06173)
*Abdelrahman Zaian,Sheethal Bhat,Mohamed Abdalkader,Andreas Maier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A modular Retina-RAG framework combines a retinal classifier with a LoRA-adapted vision-language model and a retrieval-augmented generator to perform DR severity grading, macular edema detection, and clinically structured report generation on a consumer GPU; achieves strong metrics and efficiency benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of clinically structured reporting and reliance on image-level classification in retinal screening by creating a modular, low-cost system that jointly handles grading, pathology detection, and report synthesis with knowledge integration.

Method: Decoupled architecture: a high-performance retinal classifier paired with a parameter-efficient vision-language model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct) adapted via LoRA; a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module injects curated ophthalmic knowledge and structured classifier outputs at inference to improve consistency and reduce hallucinations.

Result: DR grading F1=0.731; ME detection F1=0.948; outperforms zero-shot Qwen (0.096, 0.732) and MMed-RAG (0.541, 0.641); report generation ROUGE-L=0.429; SBERT similarity=0.884; runs on a single consumer-grade GPU.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that clinically structured retinal AI can be achieved with modest compute resources, offering a practical path toward deployable, multi-task ophthalmic AI systems with standardized reporting.

Abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of preventable blindness among working-age adults worldwide, yet most automated screening systems are limited to image-level classification and lack clinically structured reporting. We propose Retina-RAG, a low-cost modular framework that jointly performs DR severity grading, macular edema (ME) detection, and report generation. The architecture decouples a high-performance retinal classifier and a parameter-efficient vision-language model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct) adapted via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), enabling flexible component integration. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module injects curated ophthalmic knowledge together with structured classifier outputs at inference time to improve diagnostic consistency and reduce hallucinations. Retina-RAG achieves an F1-score of 0.731 for DR grading and 0.948 for ME detection, substantially outperforming zero-shot Qwen (0.096, 0.732) and MMed-RAG (0.541, 0.641) on a retinal disease detection dataset with captions. For report generation, Retina-RAG attains ROUGE-L 0.429 and SBERT similarity 0.884, exceeding all baselines. The full framework operates on a single consumer-grade GPU, demonstrating that clinically structured retinal AI can be achieved with modest computational resources.

</details>


### [86] [SuperFace: Preference-Aligned Facial Expression Estimation Beyond Pseudo Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06179)
*Zejian Kang,Xuanyang Xu,Wentao Yang,Kai Zheng,Yuanchen Fei,Hongyuan Zou,Hui Shan,Shuo Yang,Xiangru Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SuperFace uses human preference feedback to refine ARKit blendshape predictions, outperforming pseudo-label supervision from Live Link Face.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth ARKit coefficients are unreliable due to noisy pseudo labels; perceptual fidelity is the key objective.

Method: Initialize from Live Link Face estimates; optimize via human preferences on rendered expressions, aligning predictions with perceptual judgments rather than fixed pseudo labels.

Result: Demonstrates higher expression fidelity than Live Link Face supervision in experiments.

Conclusion: Preference-driven optimization is effective for semantic facial action prediction and improves perceptual realism.

Abstract: Accurate facial estimation is crucial for realistic digital human animation, and ARKit blendshape coefficients offer an interpretable representation by mapping facial motions to semantic animation controls. However, learning high-quality ARKit coefficient prediction remains limited by the absence of reliable ground-truth supervision. Existing methods typically rely on capture software such as Live Link Face to provide pseudo labels, which may contain noisy activations, biased coefficient magnitudes, and missing or inaccurate facial actions. Consequently, models trained with supervised learning tend to reproduce imperfect pseudo labels rather than optimize for perceptual expression fidelity. In this paper, we propose SuperFace, a preference-driven framework that moves ARKit facial expression estimation from pseudo-label imitation toward human-aligned perceptual optimization. Instead of treating software-estimated coefficients as fixed ground truth, SuperFace uses them only as an initialization and further improves coefficient prediction through human preference feedback on rendered facial expressions. By aligning the model with perceptual judgments rather than numerical pseudo labels, SuperFace enables more visually faithful and expressive facial animation. Experiments show that SuperFace improves expression fidelity over Live Link Face supervision, demonstrating the effectiveness of preference-driven optimization for semantic facial action prediction.

</details>


### [87] [Taming the Entropy Cliff: Variable Codebook Size Quantization for Autoregressive Visual Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06207)
*Bowen Zheng,Weijian Luo,Guang Yang,Colin Zhang,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dynamic, per-position codebooks (VCQ) overcome an entropy cliff in fixed-codebook tokenizers by growing codebook size along the sequence, yielding large gains in image generation quality and revealing a coarse-to-fine semantic hierarchy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constant-codebook designs waste capacity: the per-position conditional entropy decays quickly so most tokens become predictable after a few positions, turning many tokens into a memorization problem and limiting reconstruction quality. This entropy cliff is tight for images but not for language. A reallocation of capacity across the sequence should improve modeling efficiency.

Method: Introduce Variable Codebook Size Quantization (VCQ): codebook size K_t grows monotonically along the sequence from K_min=2 to K_max, while keeping the same loss, parameter count, and autoregressive training procedure. Applied to an autoregressive Transformer with vanilla next-token prediction.

Result: Base VCQ reduces gFID from 27.98 to 14.80 on ImageNet 256x256 using a baseline AR setup. Scaling up achieves gFID 1.71 with 684M AR parameters, without extra training tricks. A linear probe on the first 10 tokens yields 43.8% top-1 accuracy vs 27.1% with uniform codebooks, indicating a coarse-to-fine semantic hierarchy.

Conclusion: Capacity should be distributed and organized across sequence positions, not merely maximized overall. The Entropy Cliff explains limitations of constant-codebook designs; VCQ enables a coarse-to-fine representation and strong image modeling without extra training techniques, differing from language where entropy per position remains below codebook capacity.

Abstract: Most discrete visual tokenizers rely on a default design: every position in the sequence shares the same codebook. Researchers try to scale the codebook size $K$ to get better reconstruction performance. Such a constant-codebook design hits a fundamental information-theoretic limit. We observe that the per-position conditional entropy of the training set decays so quickly along the sequence that, after a few positions, the conditional distribution becomes essentially deterministic. On ImageNet with $K=16384$, this happens within only 2 out of 256 positions, turning the remaining 254 into a memorization problem. We call this phenomenon the Entropy Cliff and formalize it with a simple expression: $t^{*} = \lceil \log_2 N / \log_2 K \rceil$. Interestingly, this phenomenon is not observed in language, as its natural structure keeps the effective entropy per position well below the codebook capacity. To address this, we propose Variable Codebook Size Quantization (VCQ), where the codebook size $K_t$ grows monotonically along the sequence from $K_{\min}=2$ to $K_{\max}$, leaving the loss function, parameter count, and AR training procedure unchanged. With a vanilla autoregressive Transformer and standard next-token prediction, a base version of VCQ reduces gFID w/o CFG from 27.98 to 14.80 on ImageNet $256\times256$ over the baseline. Scaled up, it reaches gFID 1.71 with 684M autoregressive parameters, without any extra training techniques such as semantic regularization or causal alignment. The extreme information bottleneck at $K_{\min}=2$ naturally induces a coarse-to-fine semantic hierarchy: a linear probe on only the first 10 tokens reaches 43.8% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, compared to 27.1% for uniform codebooks. Ultimately, these results show that what matters is not only the total capacity of the codebook, but also how that capacity is distributed and organized.

</details>


### [88] [Differentiable Adaptive 4D Structured Illumination for Joint Capture of Shape and Reflectance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06214)
*Huakeng Ding,Yaowen Chen,Kun Zhou,Hongzhi Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A differentiable framework for adaptive 4D illumination that jointly optimizes shape (depth) and reflectance capture using a single camera and structured light, guided by a histogram-based uncertainty model to select illumination; iterative measurement updates; final fine-tuning reconstruction. Depth competitive; reflectance comparable to photos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient, high-quality simultaneous acquisition of an object's geometry and appearance by actively selecting illumination to reduce depth uncertainty, using a unified spatial-angular structured light setup and differentiable optimization.

Method: A differentiable pipeline that adaptively selects illumination conditions based on a pixel-level probability (histogram) model for depth and reflectance. New structured illumination measurements update per-pixel uncertainty; a loss ties illumination choices to uncertainty reduction; final fine-tuning minimizes discrepancy between measurements and simulated data to recover depth maps and BRDF-like reflectance maps.

Result: Depth results are competitive with state-of-the-art methods; reflectance results are comparable to photographs when validated against ground truth images.

Conclusion: The framework effectively combines adaptive, differentiable illumination strategy with a joint depth and reflectance reconstruction, demonstrating robustness across objects with varied shapes and appearances.

Abstract: We present a differentiable framework to adaptively compute 4D illumination conditions with respect to an object, for efficient, high-quality simultaneous acquisition of its shape and reflectance, with a unified spatial-angular structured light and a single camera. Using a simple histogram-based pixel-level probability model for depth and reflectance, we differentiably link the next illumination condition(s) with a loss that encourages the reduction in depth uncertainty. As new structured illumination is cast, corresponding image measurements are used to update the uncertainty at each pixel. Finally, a fine-tuning-based approach reconstructs the depth map and reflectance parameter maps, by minimizing the differences between all physical measurements and their simulated counterparts. The effectiveness of our framework is demonstrated on physical objects with wide variations in shape and appearance. Our depth results compare favorably with state-of-the-art techniques, while our reflectance results are comparable when validated against photographs.

</details>


### [89] [Look Beyond Saliency: Low-Attention Guided Dual Encoding for Video Semantic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06229)
*Faisal Aljehrai,Mohammed A. Alkhrashi,Alreem Almuhrij,Sarah Abuhimed,Noorh Aldossary,Abdullah Aldwyish,Raied Aljadaany,Huda Alamri,Muhammad Kamran J Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Inverse Attention Embedding to capture overlooked background regions, boosting video semantic search recall in crowded scenes without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foreground-biased visual encoders often neglect contextual background cues in densely crowded scenes, impairing semantic search performance.

Method: Proposes Inverse Attention Embedding (IAE) that explicitly encodes overlooked regions and combines IAE with traditional visual embeddings to improve semantic retrieval without additional training.

Result: Initial experiments and ablation studies show notable improvements in recall for video semantic search in crowded environments compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: Inverse Attention Embedding complements standard visual embeddings to enhance semantic retrieval in crowded videos, achieving gains without additional training.

Abstract: Video semantic search in densely crowded scenes remains a challenging task due to visual encoders tendency to prioritize salient foreground regions while neglecting contextually important, background areas. We propose an Inverse Attention Embedding mechanism that explicitly captures and highlights these overlooked regions. By combining inverse attention embeddings with traditional visual embeddings, our method significantly enhances semantic retrieval performance without additional training. Initial experiments and ablation studies demonstrate promising improvements over existing approaches in recall for video semantic search in crowded environments.

</details>


### [90] [Spark3R: Asymmetric Token Reduction Makes Fast Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06270)
*Zecheng Tang,Jiaye Fu,Qiankun Gao,Haijie Li,Yanmin Wu,Jiaqi Zhang,Siwei Ma,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Spark3R is a training-free acceleration framework that decouples query-token and key-value-token compression in Vision-Transformer–based feed-forward 3D reconstruction, delivering up to 28x speedups on 1,000-frame videos with competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling feed-forward 3D reconstruction with global attention to long video sequences is hindered by quadratic attention costs; uniform token-merge methods neglect the distinct roles of query vs key-value tokens in 3D reconstruction.

Method: Decouple compression by using intra-group merging for query tokens (view-specific geometric requests) and lightweight pruning for key-value tokens (shared scene context); dynamically adjust key-value reduction across layers; plug-and-play without retraining into pretrained models (VGGT, π^3, Depth-Anything-3).

Result: Up to 28× speedup on 1,000-frame inputs with competitive reconstruction quality; adaptable across multiple FF3D models.

Conclusion: Recognizing the functional roles of token types enables a more effective, training-free acceleration approach for vision-transformer-based 3D reconstruction, preserving quality while substantially improving efficiency.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models based on Vision Transformers can directly estimate scene geometry and camera poses from a small set of input images, but scaling them to video inputs with hundreds or thousands of frames remains challenging due to the quadratic cost of global attention layers. Recent token-merging methods accelerate these models by compressing the token sequence within the global attention layers, but they apply a uniform reduction to query tokens and key-value tokens, ignoring their functionally distinct roles in 3D reconstruction. In this work, we identify a key property of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models: query tokens encode view-specific geometric requests and are sensitive to compression, while key-value tokens represent shared scene context and tolerate aggressive compression. Guided by this insight, we propose Spark3R, a training-free acceleration framework that decouples the compression of query tokens and key-value tokens by assigning distinct reduction factors, with intra-group token merging applied to query tokens and lightweight token pruning to key-value tokens. Additionally, Spark3R adaptively adjusts the key-value reduction factor across layers, further improving the quality-efficiency trade-off. As a plug-and-play framework requiring no retraining, Spark3R integrates directly into multiple pretrained feed-forward 3D reconstruction models, including VGGT, $π^3$, and Depth-Anything-3, and achieves up to $28\times$ speedup on 1,000-frame inputs while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality.

</details>


### [91] [On-Orbit Real-Time Wildfire Detection Under On-Board Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06273)
*Matthias Rötzer,Veronika Pörtge,Martin Ickerott,Jayendra Praveen Kumar Chorapalli,Dimitri Scheftelowitsch,Max Bereczky,Dmitry Rashkovetsky,Sai Manoj Appalla,Julia Gottfriedsen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A nine-satellite MWIR wildfire-detection system achieves sub-1 MB models with fast inference; DenseMAE SSL pretraining yields state-of-the-art AP/Fire-F1 under strict latency and size constraints, outperforming supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting fires in uncalibrated MWIR imagery (200 m GSD) is challenged by sub-pixel fire signatures and extreme class imbalance; current MODIS/VIIRS thresholding pipelines are not optimal under onboard latency/size constraints, necessitating lightweight, robust representations.

Method: Compare lightweight dense representation learning approaches (DenseMAE and DenseMAE+EMA distillation) trained on a proprietary nine-satellite MWIR dataset; evaluate with linear probing and full-distribution pixel-level AP; deploy SSL-pretrained models on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX with TensorRT FP16; measure latency (ms/batch) and model size (MB); contrast against a supervised baseline under identical constraints; end-to-end alert pipeline from satellite overpass to fire event communication.

Result: Fastest SSL-pretrained model: 0.640 test AP and 0.69 Fire-F1 with 65.34 ms latency per batch and a 0.52 MB engine. Best configuration: 0.699 AP and 0.744 Fire-F1 under 1 MB. Both outperform a supervised baseline (0.650 AP) under comparable resource constraints.

Conclusion: DenseMAE pretraining enables compact downstream models that approach the latency-accuracy Pareto frontier for on-orbit wildfire detection in MWIR imagery, providing improved performance under tight size and latency budgets and outperforming supervised baselines.

Abstract: We present a deployed system for on-orbit wildfire detection aboard a nine-satellite commercial thermal infrared constellation, operating under demanding joint constraints: sub-megabyte model footprint, sub-150 ms per-batch TensorRT FP16 inference on an NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, and an end-to-end alert pipeline targeting under 10 minutes from satellite overpass to fire event communication. The system operates on uncalibrated mid-wave infrared (MWIR) single-band imagery at 200 m ground sampling distance, where fires frequently appear as sub-pixel or single-pixel thermal anomalies under extreme class imbalance -- challenges not addressed by the contextual thermal-thresholding pipelines (MODIS, VIIRS) that currently dominate operational fire monitoring. We present an empirical study of lightweight dense representation learning for this regime using a proprietary nine-satellite MWIR dataset. We compare dense masked autoencoding (DenseMAE) and a hybrid DenseMAE+EMA (exponential moving average) distillation variant, and evaluate representations via linear probing and full-distribution pixel-level average precision (AP) under extreme class imbalance. DenseMAE pretraining enables compact downstream models on the latency-accuracy Pareto frontier: our fastest SSL-pretrained model achieves 0.640 test AP and 0.69 event-level Fire-F1 with 65.34 ms latency per batch and a 0.52 MB engine, without pruning or compression. The best configuration reaches 0.699 AP and 0.744 Fire-F1 below 1 MB, outperforming a supervised baseline (0.650 AP) under comparable constraints.

</details>


### [92] [Eulerian Motion Guidance: Robust Image Animation via Bidirectional Geometric Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06280)
*Thong Nguyen,Khoi M. Le,Cong-Duy Nguyen,Luu Anh Tuan,See-Kiong Ng,Chunyan Miao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based image animation using adjacent-frame Eulerian supervision with bidirectional geometric consistency to enable parallel training, reduce drift, and improve temporal coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome drift artifacts and inefficiencies of Lagrangian, initial-frame optical-flow guidance by adopting local, bounded-error supervision that supports parallel training.

Method: Replace global, initial-frame optical flow with adjacent-frame Eulerian motion fields describing short temporal hops. Introduce Bidirectional Geometric Consistency (forward-backward cycle check) to identify and mask occluded regions, preventing incorrect warps and drift. Enable parallelized training with bounded-error supervision.

Result: Faster training, improved temporal coherence, and reduced dynamic artifacts compared with reference-based baselines across experiments.

Conclusion: Local adjacent-frame supervision with Bidirectional Geometric Consistency yields a scalable and robust framework for diffusion-based animation, mitigating drift and artifacts while preserving coherence.

Abstract: Recent advancements in image animation have utilized diffusion models to breathe life into static images. However, existing controllable frameworks typically rely on Lagrangian motion guidance, where optical flow is estimated relative to the initial frame. This paper revisits the same optical-flow primitive through a more local supervision design: we use adjacent-frame Eulerian motion fields to guide generation, where the motion signal always describes a short temporal hop. This shift enables parallelized training and provides bounded-error supervision throughout the generation process. To mitigate the drift artifacts common in adjacent frame generation, we introduce a Bidirectional Geometric Consistency mechanism, which computes a forward-backward cycle check to mathematically identify and mask occluded regions, preventing the model from learning incorrect warping objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach accelerates training, preserves temporal coherence, and reduces dynamic artifacts compared to reference-based baselines.

</details>


### [93] [Render, Don't Decode: Weight-Space World Models with Latent Structural Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06298)
*Roussel Desmond Nzoyem,Mauro Comi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NOVA encodes world dynamics in the parameters of a coordinate-based implicit neural representation (INR), enabling decoder-free rendering, compact models, zero-shot super-resolution, and disentangled editing of content and dynamics with strong forecasting on consumer hardware (~40M params).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video-based world models rely on opaque pixel latent spaces with heavy decoders, which are computationally expensive and hard to interpret. A compact, interpretable, and editable representation of state would improve efficiency, portability, and controllability of learned dynamics.

Method: Represent the system state as the weights and biases of an auxiliary coordinate-based INR. The INR is analytically rendered (no decoder bottleneck). The model can be distilled into a context-dependent video generator via an action-matching objective. Remarkably, disentanglement of background, foreground, and motion emerges without auxiliary losses, enabling content/dynamics editing. Demonstrations show strong controllable forecasting on challenging datasets on a single consumer GPU (~40M parameters).

Result: NOVA achieves strong controllable forecasting with a compact model that runs on consumer hardware, supports zero-shot super-resolution, and yields interpretable disentanglement of scene components without extra loss terms.

Conclusion: Structured representations like INRs can advance latent dynamics understanding and enable immersive, customizable virtual experiences by providing a compact, interpretable, and editable world model that avoids decoder bottlenecks.

Abstract: Training world models on vast quantities of unlabelled videos is a critical step toward fully autonomous intelligence. However, the prevailing paradigm of encoding raw pixels into opaque latent spaces and relying on heavy decoders for reconstruction leaves these models computationally expensive and uninterpretable. We address this problem by introducing NOVA, a world modelling framework that represents the system state as the weights and biases of an auxiliary coordinate-based implicit neural representation (INR). This structured representation is analytically rendered, which eliminates the decoder bottleneck while conferring compactness, portability, and zero-shot super-resolution. Furthermore, like most latent action models, NOVA can be distilled into a context-dependent video generator via an action-matching objective. Surprisingly, without resorting to auxiliary losses or adversarial objectives, NOVA can disentangle structural scene components such as background, foreground, and inter-frame motion, enabling users to edit either content or dynamics without compromising the other. We validate our framework on several challenging datasets, achieving strong controllable forecasting while operating on a single consumer GPU at $\sim$40M parameters. Ultimately, structured representations like INRs not only enhance our understanding of latent dynamics but also pave the way for immersive and customisable virtual experiences.

</details>


### [94] [NavOne: One-Step Global Planning for Vision-Language Navigation on Top-Down Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06317)
*Dijia Zhan,Jinyi Li,Chenxi Zheng,Shaoyu Huang,Yong Li,Jie Tang,Xuemiao Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A top-down, one-shot global path-planning VLN framework (TD-VLN) with NavOne on a multi-modal top-down map, achieving state-of-the-art results among map-based VLN methods and large planning speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses error accumulation and inefficiency in egocentric, step-by-step VLN; also overcomes discrete bottlenecks of map-based approaches that rely on memory graphs or discrete path proposals.

Method: TD-VLN formulates navigation as a one-step global path planning problem on pre-built top-down maps. NavOne, a unified end-to-end framework, predicts dense path probabilities over multi-modal maps in a single forward pass. Key components include a Top-Down Map Fuser for joint multi-modal map representation and extended Attention Residuals for spatial-aware depth mixing.

Result: NavOne achieves state-of-the-art performance among map-based VLN methods on the R2R-TopDown dataset, with a planning-stage speedup of 8x over existing map-based baselines and 80x over egocentric methods.

Conclusion: Global planning on pre-built top-down maps is effective for efficient VLN. NavOne enables fast, dense path planning with continuous spatial reasoning, surpassing map-based baselines and offering substantial speed gains for real-time navigation.

Abstract: Existing Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) methods typically adopt an egocentric, step-by-step paradigm, which struggles with error accumulation and limits efficiency. While recent approaches attempt to leverage pre-built environment maps, they often rely on incrementally updating memory graphs or scoring discrete path proposals, which restricts continuous spatial reasoning and creates discrete bottlenecks. We propose Top-Down VLN (TD-VLN), reformulating navigation as a one-step global path planning problem on pre-built top-down maps, supported by our newly constructed R2R-TopDown dataset. To solve this, we introduce NavOne, a unified framework that directly predicts dense path probabilities over multi-modal maps in a single end-to-end forward pass. NavOne features a Top-Down Map Fuser for joint multi-modal map representation, and extends Attention Residuals for spatial-aware depth mixing. Extensive experiments on R2R-TopDown show that NavOne achieves state-of-the-art performance among map-based VLN methods, with a planning-stage speedup of 8x over existing map-based baselines and 80x over egocentric methods, enabling highly efficient global navigation.

</details>


### [95] [TinyBayes: Closed-Form Bayesian Inference via Jacobi Prior for Real-Time Image Classification on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06333)
*Shouvik Sardar,Sourish Das*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TinyBayes is a mobile edge-friendly framework that combines a CV pipeline (YOLOv8-Nano + MobileNetV3-Small) with a closed-form Jacobi-prior Bayesian classifier (Jacobi-DMR) for cocoa disease detection, achieving 78.7% accuracy on Amini Cocoa dataset with a small model (~9.5 MB) and end-to-end CPU latency <150 ms, demonstrating best accuracy–size–speed trade-offs among baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge deployment of plant-disease detection requires small, fast models with uncertainty quantification, suitable for resource-constrained settings. Existing edge systems are end-to-end DL without uncertainty and Bayesian methods target hardware inference rather than agriculture, creating a gap this work aims to fill.

Method: A pipeline with YOLOv8-Nano for lesion localization and MobileNetV3-Small for feature extraction feeds into Jacobi prior-based Bayesian classifier (Jacobi-DMR). The Jacobi prior enables closed-form, non-iterative estimators via projection; the Jacobi-DMR classifier adds 13.5 KB, keeping total size at 9.5 MB; inference is end-to-end on CPU (<150 ms per image). Benchmarks compare against RF, SVM, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net, XGBoost, and Jacobi-GP.

Result: Jacobi-DMR offers the best trade-off between accuracy, model size, and inference speed among the tested classifiers, achieving 78.7% accuracy on the Amini Cocoa Contamination Challenge dataset. The approach is proven to be asymptotically equivalent and consistent, asymptotically normal, with a bias correction. All data and code are released.

Conclusion: TinyBayes fills the gap for uncertainty-aware, edge-deployable plant-disease detection in agriculture. By integrating a lightweight CV backbone with a closed-form Bayesian classifier, it delivers competitive accuracy with minimal resource usage and provable statistical properties, making it suitable for deployment in West African cocoa farming contexts.

Abstract: Cocoa (Theobroma cacao) is a critical cash crop for millions of smallholder farmers in West Africa, where Cocoa Swollen Shoot Virus Disease (CSSVD) and anthracnose cause devastating yield losses. Automated disease detection from leaf images is essential for early intervention, yet deploying such systems in resource-constrained settings demands models that are small, fast, and require no internet connectivity. Existing edge-deployable plant disease systems rely on end-to-end deep learning without uncertainty quantification, while Bayesian methods for edge devices focus on hardware-level inference architectures rather than agricultural applications. We bridge this gap with TinyBayes, the first framework to combine a closed-form Bayesian classifier with a mobile-grade computer vision pipeline for crop disease detection. Our pipeline uses YOLOv8-Nano (5.9 MB) for lesion localisation, MobileNetV3-Small (3.5 MB) for feature extraction, and the Jacobi prior; a Bayesian method that provides a closed form non-iterative estimators via projection, for the classification. The Jacobi-DMR (Distributed Multinomial Regression) classifier adds only 13.5 KB to the pipeline, bringing the total model size within 9.5 MB, while achieving 78.7% accuracy on the Amini Cocoa Contamination Challenge dataset and enabling end-to-end CPU inference under 150 ms per image. We benchmark against seven classifiers including Random Forest, SVM, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net, XGBoost, and Jacobi-GP, and demonstrate that the Jacobi-DMR offers the best trade-off between accuracy, model size, and inference speed for edge deployment. We have proved the asymptotic equivalence and consistency, asymptotic normality and the bias correction of Jacobi-DMR. All data and codes are available here: https://github.com/shouvik-sardar/TinyBayes

</details>


### [96] [Earth-o1: A Grid-free Observation-native Atmospheric World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06337)
*Junchao Gong,Kaiyi Xu,Wangxu Wei,Siwei Tu,Jingyi Xu,Zili Liu,Hang Fan,Zhiwang Zhou,Tao Han,Yi Xiao,Xinyu Gu,Zhangrui Li,Wenlong Zhang,Hao Chen,Xiaokang Yang,Yaqiang Wang,Lijing Cheng,Pierre Gentine,Wanli Ouyang,Feng Zhang,Zhe-Min Tan,Bowen Zhou,Fenghua Ling,Ben Fei,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Earth-o1 is an observation-native, grid-free atmospheric model that learns continuous 3D dynamics directly from ungridded sensor data, enabling real-time forecasting and cross-sensor inference with hindcast skill comparable to the IFS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome grid-based bottlenecks and limited exploitation of raw multimodal Earth observation data by traditional data assimilation and fixed grids; to develop a scalable, data-driven alternative for atmospheric modeling and digital twins.

Method: Train a continuous, grid-free dynamical model that ingests ungridded, multimodal observations from diverse sensors to learn the 3D evolution of the atmosphere. The model autonomously advances the state in space and time without explicit numerical solvers, enabling real-time forecasting and cross-sensor inference.

Result: Hindcast surface forecast skill comparable to the operational Integrated Forecasting System (IFS). Demonstrates direct, real-time forecasting and cross-sensor inference without traditional solvers, indicating competitive fidelity with established physical frameworks.

Conclusion: Continuous, observation-driven world models constitute a new class of fully observation-native geophysical simulators capable of matching traditional framework fidelity and enabling scalable digital twins of the Earth.

Abstract: Despite the unprecedented volume of multimodal data provided by modern Earth observation systems, our ability to model atmospheric dynamics remains constrained. Traditional modeling frameworks force heterogeneous measurements into predefined spatial grids, inherently limiting the full exploitation of raw sensor data and creating severe computational bottlenecks. Here we present Earth-o1, an observation-native atmospheric world model that overcomes these structural limitations. Rather than relying on conventional atmospheric dynamical modeling systems or traditional data assimilation, Earth-o1 directly learns the continuous, three-dimensional physical evolution of the Earth system from ungridded observational data. By integrating diverse sensor inputs into a unified, grid-free dynamical field, the model autonomously advances the atmospheric state in space and time. We show that this fundamentally distinct paradigm enables direct, real-time forecasting and cross-sensor inference without the overhead of explicit numerical solvers. In hindcast evaluations, Earth-o1 achieves surface forecast skill comparable to the operational Integrated Forecasting System (IFS). These results establish that continuous, observation-driven world models -- a new class of fully observation-native geophysical simulators -- can match the fidelity of established physical frameworks, providing a scalable data-driven foundation for a digital twin of the Earth.

</details>


### [97] [SwiftI2V: Efficient High-Resolution Image-to-Video Generation via Conditional Segment-wise Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06356)
*YaoYang Liu,Yuechen Zhang,Wenbo Li,Yufei Zhao,Rui Liu,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SwiftI2V is a two-stage, segment-wise, input-conditioned high-resolution image-to-video framework. It first generates a low-resolution motion reference to reduce token costs, then performs a strongly image-conditioned 2K synthesis guided by the motion. It introduces Conditional Segment-wise Generation (CSG) and bidirectional segment interaction to improve coherence and input fidelity. It achieves comparable performance to end-to-end baselines with ~202x GPU-time reduction on VBench-I2V at 2K, enabling practical 2K I2V on modest GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution (2K) image-to-video generation is computationally demanding. End-to-end models have prohibitive memory and latency; post-hoc super-resolution can hallucinate details and drift from input-local structures since SR is not conditioned on the input image. A scalable, input-faithful approach is needed.

Method: A two-stage design: (1) generate a low-resolution motion reference to reduce token costs and simplify modeling; (2) perform high-resolution (2K) synthesis conditioned on the motion to recover input-faithful details with controlled overhead. Introduces Conditional Segment-wise Generation (CSG) to synthesize videos segment-by-segment with a bounded per-step token budget, and uses bidirectional contextual interaction within each segment to improve cross-segment coherence and input fidelity.

Result: On VBench-I2V at 2K, SwiftI2V achieves performance comparable to end-to-end baselines while reducing total GPU-time by 202x. It enables practical 2K I2V generation on a single datacenter GPU (e.g., H800) or consumer GPU (e.g., RTX 4090).

Conclusion: SwiftI2V provides an efficient, scalable pathway for high-resolution I2V by decoupling motion modeling from high-res synthesis, leveraging segment-wise generation with bounded tokens and bidirectional intra-segment interaction to maintain fidelity. It delivers competitive results with dramatically reduced compute, making 2K I2V practical on common hardware.

Abstract: High-resolution image-to-video (I2V) generation aims to synthesize realistic temporal dynamics while preserving fine-grained appearance details of the input image. At 2K resolution, it becomes extremely challenging, and existing solutions suffer from various weaknesses: 1) end-to-end models are often prohibitively expensive in memory and latency; 2) cascading low-resolution generation with a generic video super-resolution tends to hallucinate details and drift from input-specific local structures, since the super-resolution stage is not explicitly conditioned on the input image. To this end, we propose SwiftI2V, an efficient framework tailored for high-resolution I2V. Following the widely used two-stage design, it addresses the efficiency--fidelity dilemma by first generating a low-resolution motion reference to reduce token costs and ease the modeling burden, then performing a strongly image-conditioned 2K synthesis guided by the motion to recover input-faithful details with controlled overhead. Specifically, to make generation more scalable, SwiftI2V introduces Conditional Segment-wise Generation (CSG) to synthesize videos segment-by-segment with a bounded per-step token budget, and adopts bidirectional contextual interaction within each segment to improve cross-segment coherence and input fidelity. On VBench-I2V at 2K resolution, SwiftI2V achieves performance comparable to end-to-end baselines while reducing total GPU-time by 202x. Particularly, it enables practical 2K I2V generation on a single datacenter GPU (e.g., H800) or consumer GPU (e.g., RTX 4090).

</details>


### [98] [eXplaining to Learn (eX2L): Regularization Using Contrastive Visual Explanation Pairs for Distribution Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06368)
*Paulo Mario P. Medina,Jose Marie Antonio Miñoza,Sebastian C. Ibañez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: eX2L is an interpretable framework that decorrelates confounding features from a classifier by penalizing Grad-CAM map similarity with a concurrently trained confounder, achieving strong results on a hard benchmark and suggesting group-level domain invariance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inconsistent distribution-shift robustness and lack of interpretability in existing methods, which often fail to beat ERM and struggle with spurious correlations.

Method: Train a primary label classifier and a confounder classifier in parallel; apply a penalty that minimizes similarity between Grad-CAM activation maps from the two classifiers, thereby decoupling label and nuisance attributes at the group level.

Result: On the Spawrious Many-to-Many Hard Challenge benchmark, eX2L attains AA 82.24% ±3.87% and WGA 66.31% ±8.73%, surpassing SOTA by 5.49% in AA and 10.90% in WGA.

Conclusion: Explicitly decoupling label and nuisance attributes via Grad-CAM similarity penalties can achieve functional domain invariance with competitive performance and improved worst-group robustness.

Abstract: Despite extensive research into mitigating distribution shifts, many existing algorithms yield inconsistent performance, often failing to outperform baseline Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) across diverse scenarios. Furthermore, high algorithmic complexity frequently limits interpretability and offers only an indirect means of addressing spurious correlations. We propose eXplaining to Learn (eX2L): an interpretable, explanation-based framework that decorrelates confounding features from a classifier's latent representations during training. eX2L achieves this by penalizing the similarity between Grad-CAM activation maps generated by a primary label classifier and those from a concurrently trained confounder classifier. On the rigorous Spawrious Many-to-Many Hard Challenge benchmark, eX2L achieves an average accuracy (AA) of 82.24% +/- 3.87% and a worst-group accuracy (WGA) of 66.31% +/- 8.73%, outperforming the current state-of-the-art (SOTA) by 5.49% and 10.90%, respectively. Beyond its competitive performance, eX2L demonstrates that functional domain invariance can be achieved by explicitly decoupling label and nuisance attributes at the group level.

</details>


### [99] [Continuous-Time Distribution Matching for Few-Step Diffusion Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06376)
*Tao Liu,Hao Yan,Mengting Chen,Taihang Hu,Zhengrong Yue,Zihao Pan,Jinsong Lan,Xiaoyong Zhu,Ming-Ming Cheng,Bo Zheng,Yaxing Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Continuous-Time Distribution Matching (CDM) to replace discrete DMD in diffusion models by continuous-time distribution alignment and off-trajectory latent matching, achieving competitive few-step visual fidelity without auxiliary modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete anchoring in DMD and the mode-seeking nature of the reverse KL converge to artifacts and over-smoothing; prior solutions rely on complex auxiliary components (GANs, reward models) for fidelity.

Method: Two continuous-time designs: (1) a dynamic continuous schedule with random length that enforces distribution matching at arbitrary points along sampling trajectories; (2) a continuous-time alignment objective for active off-trajectory matching on latents extrapolated by the student’s velocity field to improve generalization and preserve details.

Result: Empirical results on SD3-Medium and Longcat-Image show CDM achieves highly competitive visual fidelity for few-step generation without extra auxiliary objectives; code available at the authors’ repository.

Conclusion: CDM demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of continuous-time distribution matching, improving generalization and preserving fine visuals in few-step diffusion without ancillary modules.

Abstract: Step distillation has become a leading technique for accelerating diffusion models, among which Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and Consistency Distillation are two representative paradigms. While consistency methods enforce self-consistency along the full PF-ODE trajectory to steer it toward the clean data manifold, vanilla DMD relies on sparse supervision at a few predefined discrete timesteps. This restricted discrete-time formulation and mode-seeking nature of the reverse KL divergence tends to exhibit visual artifacts and over-smoothed outputs, often necessitating complex auxiliary modules -- such as GANs or reward models -- to restore visual fidelity. In this work, we introduce Continuous-Time Distribution Matching (CDM), migrating the DMD framework from discrete anchoring to continuous optimization for the first time. CDM achieves this through two continuous-time designs. First, we replace the fixed discrete schedule with a dynamic continuous schedule of random length, so that distribution matching is enforced at arbitrary points along sampling trajectories rather than only at a few fixed anchors. Second, we propose a continuous-time alignment objective that performs active off-trajectory matching on latents extrapolated via the student's velocity field, improving generalization and preserving fine visual details. Extensive experiments on different architectures, including SD3-Medium and Longcat-Image, demonstrate that CDM provides highly competitive visual fidelity for few-step image generation without relying on complex auxiliary objectives. Code is available at https://github.com/byliutao/cdm.

</details>


### [100] [Empirical Evidence for Simply Connected Decision Regions in Image Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06380)
*Arjhun Swaminathan,Mete Akgün*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Probes whether decision regions in deep nets are simply connected by constructing surfaces bounded by loops within regions using quad-mesh filling and Coons patches; finds empirical evidence of simple connectedness across modern image classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond path connectivity to the stronger topological property of simple connectedness for neural decision regions, informing robustness and interpretability of DNNs.

Method: Iterative quad-mesh filling to create a label-preserving surface bounded by a loop entirely inside a decision region; relate the constructed surface to Coons patches to quantify deviation from a canonical geometric interpolation of the loop.

Result: Empirical evidence suggests decision regions are simply connected; loops within a region can be contracted without leaving the region; the surface construction provides a practical, topology-aware analysis tool across models.

Conclusion: Decision regions in DNNs are not only path-connected but simply connected; the proposed quad-mesh/Coons-patch approach offers a concrete methodology to study the topology of decision regions and has implications for understanding model robustness and decision boundary structure.

Abstract: Understanding the topology of decision regions is central to explaining the inner workings of deep neural networks. Prior empirical work has provided evidence that these regions are path connected. We study a stronger topological question: whether closed loops inside a decision region can be contracted without leaving that region. To this end, we propose an iterative quad-mesh filling procedure that constructs a finite-resolution label-preserving surface bounded by a given loop and lying entirely within the same decision region. We further connect this construction to natural Coons patches in order to quantify its deviation from a canonical geometric interpolation of the loop. By evaluating our method across several modern image-classification models, we provide empirical evidence supporting the hypothesis that decision regions in deep neural networks are not only path connected, but also simply connected.

</details>


### [101] [FREPix: Frequency-Heterogeneous Flow Matching for Pixel-Space Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06421)
*Mingfeng Lin,Jiakun Chen,Liang Han,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FREPix introduces a frequency-heterogeneous flow-matching framework for pixel-space image generation that decomposes generation into low- and high-frequency components with separate transport paths, a factorized predictor, and a frequency-aware objective, achieving competitive 256×256 and 512×512 results on ImageNet, with strong low-NFE performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-space diffusion often treats all frequencies uniformly, overlooking distinct learning dynamics of low- and high-frequency components and making coarse-to-fine generation implicit rather than explicit. A frequency-aware design aims to improve quality and efficiency by explicitly modeling frequency bands.

Method: Decompose images into low- and high-frequency components, assign separate transport paths for each band, predict them with a factorized network, and train with a frequency-aware objective. This makes coarse-to-fine generation an explicit design principle in pixel-space diffusion (flow matching).

Result: On ImageNet class-to-image generation, FREPix achieves 1.91 FID at 256×256 and 2.38 FID at 512×512, with particularly strong performance in the low-NFE regime, among pixel-space generation models.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that explicit frequency-heterogeneous design can improve efficiency and control in pixel-space diffusion by providing distinct pathways for low and high frequencies. It achieves competitive results and invites further exploration of frequency-aware objectives and architectures in pixel-space generative modeling.

Abstract: Pixel-space diffusion has re-emerged as a promising alternative to latent-space generation because it avoids the representation bottleneck introduced by VAEs. Yet most existing methods still treat image generation as a frequency-homogeneous process, overlooking the distinct roles and learning dynamics of low- and high-frequency components. To address this, we propose FREPix, a FREquency-heterogeneous flow matching framework for Pixel-space image generation. FREPix explicitly decomposes generation into low- and high-frequency components, assigns them separate transport paths, predicts them with a factorized network, and trains them with a frequency-aware objective. In this way, coarse-to-fine generation becomes an explicit design principle rather than an implicit behavior. On ImageNet class-to-image generation, FREPix achieves competitive results among pixel-space generation models, reaching 1.91 FID at $256\times256$ and 2.38 FID at $512\times512$, with particularly strong behavior in the low-NFE regime.

</details>


### [102] [GeoStack: A Framework for Quasi-Abelian Knowledge Composition in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06477)
*Pranav Mantini,Shishir K. Shah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoStack provides a modular, geometry-constrained adapter framework to compose independently trained domain experts into a single Vision-Language Model with constant-time inference, mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enabling scalable, long-term knowledge integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address catastrophic forgetting when continually integrating domain-specific knowledge into Vision-Language Models, enabling scalable long-term knowledge composition without retraining from scratch.

Method: Introduce GeoStack, a modular framework of domain adapters trained independently. Impose geometric and structural constraints on the adapter manifold to preserve base model knowledge. Prove a weight-folding property that achieves O(1) inference time regardless of the number of integrated experts.

Result: Empirical results in multi-domain adaptation and class-incremental learning show efficient knowledge composition and substantial reduction of forgetting, with constant-time inference across varying numbers of experts.

Conclusion: GeoStack enables scalable, memory-efficient, and forgetting-resistant knowledge assembly for VLMs and is practical for long-term continual learning; code is available.

Abstract: We address the challenge of knowledge composition in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), where accumulating expertise across multiple domains or tasks typically leads to catastrophic forgetting. We introduce GeoStack (Geometric Stacking), a modular framework that allows independently trained domain experts to be composed into a unified model. By imposing geometric and structural constraints on the adapter manifold, GeoStack ensures the foundational knowledge of the base model is preserved. Furthermore, we mathematically demonstrate a weight-folding property that achieves constant-time inference complexity ($O(1)$), regardless of the number of integrated experts.
