Experimental results across multi-domain adaptation and class-incremental learning show that GeoStack provides an efficient mechanism for long-term knowledge composition while significantly mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Code is available at https://github.com/QuantitativeImagingLaboratory/GeoStack.

</details>


### [103] [3D MRI Image Pretraining via Controllable 2D Slice Navigation Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06487)
*Yu Wang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes action-conditioned self-supervision for MRI by converting volumes into controllable 2D slice sequences and training a tokenizer plus latent dynamics model to predict feature evolution; outperforms static-volume baselines on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of static-volume pretraining by leveraging intrinsic supervision from controllable navigation, aiming to better capture 3D anatomical and spatial structure in MRI.

Method: Render 3D MRI volumes into dense video-action sequences via continuous variation of slice position, orientation, and scale. Use an action-conditioned objective where a tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts latent feature evolution under actions. Compare against static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions.

Result: Pretraining with controllable slice navigation provides a useful complementary signal, yielding improvements on representative anatomical and spatial downstream tasks compared to baselines and ablations.

Conclusion: Controllable MRI slice navigation is a valuable pretraining interface for learning anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections, suggesting avenues to integrate controllable SSL signals with standard MRI pretraining pipelines.

Abstract: Self-supervised pretraining has become the mainstream approach for learning MRI representations from unlabeled scans. However, most existing objectives still treat each scan primarily as static aggregations of slices, patches or volumes. We ask whether there exists an intrinsic form of self-supervision signal that is different from reconstructing the masked patches, through transforming the 3D volumes into controllable 2D rendered sequences: by rendering slices at continuous positions, orientations, and scales, a 3D volume can be converted into dense video-action sequences whose controls are the action trajectories. We study this formulation with an action-conditioned pretraining objective, where a tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts the evolution of latent features. Across representative anatomical and spatial downstream tasks, the proposed pretraining is evaluated against standard static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions. These results suggest that controllable MRI slice navigation provides a useful complementary pretraining interface for learning anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections.

</details>


### [104] [MARBLE: Multi-Aspect Reward Balance for Diffusion RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06507)
*Canyu Zhao,Hao Chen,Yunze Tong,Yu Qiao,Jiacheng Li,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MARBLE uses gradient-space optimization with per-reward independent advantage estimators and a quadratic program to fuse multi-reward signals into a single update, avoiding manual reward weighting; introduces amortized backward-cost and EMA-based stabilization; shows multi-dimensional gains on SD3.5 with five rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aligning diffusion models with human preferences requires optimizing multiple criteria simultaneously. Naive weighted-sum aggregation causes sample-level mismatch and dilutes supervision, hindering joint optimization across rewards.

Method: Maintain independent advantage estimators for each reward, compute per-reward policy gradients, and harmonize them into a single update via a Quadratic Program. Propose an amortized formulation that leverages the affine structure of the DiffusionNFT loss to reduce per-step cost, and apply EMA smoothing on balancing coefficients to stabilize updates.

Result: On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all five reward dimensions simultaneously; the worst-aligned reward gradient becomes consistently positive in most mini-batches (instead of negative under weighted sums in ~80% of cases); training runs at 0.97x the baseline speed.

Conclusion: MARBLE provides a unified, efficient framework for multi-reward optimization in diffusion-model alignment, overcoming limitations of naive weighted-sum approaches. It offers better gradient compatibility across rewards and maintains near-baseline training efficiency, with potential applicability to other multi-objective RL settings.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning has become the dominant approach for aligning diffusion models with human preferences. However, assessing images is intrinsically a multi-dimensional task, and multiple evaluation criteria need to be optimized simultaneously. Existing practice deal with multiple rewards by training one specialist model per reward, optimizing a weighted-sum reward $R(x)=\sum_k w_k R_k(x)$, or sequentially fine-tuning with a hand-crafted stage schedule. These approaches either fail to produce a unified model that can be jointly trained on all rewards or necessitates heavy manually tuned sequential training. We find that the failure stems from using a naive weighted-sum reward aggregation. This approach suffers from a sample-level mismatch because most rollouts are specialist samples, highly informative for certain reward dimensions but irrelevant for others; consequently, weighted summation dilutes their supervision. To address this issue, we propose MARBLE (Multi-Aspect Reward BaLancE), a gradient-space optimization framework that maintains independent advantage estimators for each reward, computes per-reward policy gradients, and harmonizes them into a single update direction without manually-tuned reward weighting, by solving a Quadratic Programming problem. We further propose an amortized formulation that exploits the affine structure of the loss used in DiffusionNFT, to reduce the per-step cost from K+1 backward passes to near single-reward baseline cost, together with EMA smoothing on the balancing coefficients to stabilize updates against transient single-batch fluctuations. On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all five reward dimensions simultaneously, turns the worst-aligned reward's gradient cosine from negative under weighted summation in 80% of mini-batches to consistently positive, and runs at 0.97X the training speed of baseline training.

</details>


### [105] [FreeSpec: Training-Free Long Video Generation via Singular-Spectrum Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06509)
*Fangda Chen,Shanshan Zhao,Longrong Yang,Chuanfu Xu,Zhigang Luo,Long Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FreeSpec is a training-free spectral reconstruction framework for long-video generation that uses SVD to separate global (low-rank) and local (high-rank) features, enabling spectrum-level fusion to maintain long-range consistency while preserving spatial details and temporal dynamics, improving temporal dynamics without sacrificing visual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending video diffusion models from short to long videos often causes content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothing. Existing training-free global/local decompositions rely on predefined criteria to separate appearance and dynamics, which break when appearance and motion are tightly coupled (e.g., camera motion, sequential motion). A singular-spectrum view shows that larger self-attention windows concentrate spectral energy into a few low-rank directions, preserving coarse structure but dampening high-rank spatial details and motion-rich temporal variations.

Method: FreeSpec applies singular value decomposition to decompose global and local features. The global branch serves as low-rank spectral guidance, while the local branch acts as a high-rank reconstruction basis. This spectrum-level fusion avoids rigid feature partitioning and preserves long-range consistency alongside richer spatial details and temporal dynamics, all without training.

Result: Empirical evaluations on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video show FreeSpec improves long-video generation, particularly temporal dynamics, while maintaining strong visual quality and temporal consistency. A project demo is provided.

Conclusion: Spectral reconstruction via SVD enables effective long-video generation by mitigating spectral concentration induced by large attention windows, achieving better balance between global coherence and local detail without training.

Abstract: Video diffusion models perform well in short-video synthesis, but their training-free extension to long videos often suffers from content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothed dynamics. Existing methods improve temporal consistency by combining a global branch with a local branch, but they often further decompose appearance consistency and temporal dynamics within each branch using predefined criteria. This assignment is unreliable when appearance and action progression are tightly coupled, such as in camera motion and sequential motion. We analyze the video temporal extension issue from a singular-spectrum perspective and show that enlarged self-attention windows induce spectral concentration: spectral energy becomes dominated by a few low-rank singular directions, preserving coarse structure but suppressing high-rank spatial details and motion-rich temporal variations. To mitigate this problem, we propose FreeSpec, a training-free spectral reconstruction framework for long-video generation. FreeSpec decomposes global and local features with singular value decomposition, and uses the global branch as low-rank spectral guidance and the local branch as a high-rank reconstruction basis. This spectrum-level fusion avoids the rigid feature partitioning of previous decomposition rules, preserving long-range consistency while better retaining spatial details and temporal dynamics. Experiments on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video demonstrate that FreeSpec improves long-video generation, especially for temporal dynamics, while maintaining strong visual quality and temporal consistency. Project demo: https://fdchen24.github.io/FreeSpec-Website/.

</details>


### [106] [DCR: Counterfactual Attractor Guidance for Rare Compositional Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06512)
*Taewon Kang,Matthias Zwicker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free method (DCR) suppresses default completion bias in diffusion models to improve rare compositional prompts by constructing a counterfactual attractor and applying projection-based repulsion during sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models tend to default-complete toward high-frequency semantics, causing valid but underrepresented compositions (e.g., snowy beach, rainbow at night) to collapse to common alternatives. There is a need to explicitly model and counteract this competing tendency without retraining.

Method: DCR builds a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare compositional factor while preserving surrounding semantics, defining a counterfactual drift as the discrepancy between target and attractor trajectories, and applying a projection-based repulsion that removes guidance components aligned with this drift. This operates entirely within the standard diffusion sampling process without retraining or architectural changes.

Result: Experiments on rare compositional prompts show improved compositional fidelity while maintaining visual quality. The approach also reveals and counteracts intrinsic model biases, offering a new perspective on controllable generation beyond explicit constraint enforcement.

Conclusion: DCR provides a training-free mechanism to mitigate default completion bias in diffusion models, enabling more faithful rare-composition generation and exposing underlying biases for further study.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate realistic visual content, yet often fail to produce rare but plausible compositions. When prompted with combinations that are valid but underrepresented in training data, such as a snowy beach or a rainbow at night, the generation process frequently collapses toward more common alternatives. We identify this failure mode as default completion bias, where denoising trajectories are implicitly attracted toward high-frequency semantic configurations. Existing guidance mechanisms do not explicitly model this competing tendency and therefore struggle to prevent such collapse. We introduce Default Completion Repulsion (DCR), a training-free framework that explicitly models and suppresses default completion behavior. DCR constructs a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare compositional factor while preserving surrounding semantics, inducing an alternative denoising trajectory reflecting the model's preferred completion. We define the discrepancy between target and attractor trajectories as a counterfactual drift, and propose a projection-based repulsion mechanism that removes guidance components aligned with this drift direction. This suppresses undesired frequent completions while preserving other semantic components. DCR operates entirely within the standard diffusion sampling process without retraining or architectural modification. Experiments on rare compositional prompts show that DCR improves compositional fidelity while maintaining visual quality. Our analysis further shows that the framework exposes and counteracts intrinsic model biases, offering a new perspective on controllable generation beyond explicit constraint enforcement.

</details>


### [107] [Sparkle: Realizing Lively Instruction-Guided Video Background Replacement via Decoupled Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06535)
*Ziyun Zeng,Yiqi Lin,Guoqiang Liang,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sparkle provides a large-scale, quality-controlled dataset (≈140K video pairs) for background replacement, with decoupled foreground/background guidance and strict filtering, plus Sparkle-Bench benchmarks. It achieves superior performance over prior baselines on OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench, and is fully open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of high-quality, temporally consistent background replacement data. Existing datasets often yield static, unnatural backgrounds and fail to capture foreground-background interactions, hindering model performance in creative tasks like film/advertising.

Method: A scalable pipeline that decouples foreground and background guidance generation with strict quality filtering. Construction of Sparkle (≈140K video pairs across five background-change themes) and Sparkle-Bench as a large evaluation benchmark for background replacement.

Result: Models trained on Sparkle achieve substantially better performance than existing baselines on both OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench.

Conclusion: The dataset, benchmark, and model are fully open-sourced at the project URL, filling a key data quality gap and enabling improved background replacement research and applications.

Abstract: In recent years, open-source efforts like Senorita-2M have propelled video editing toward natural language instruction. However, current publicly available datasets predominantly focus on local editing or style transfer, which largely preserve the original scene structure and are easier to scale. In contrast, Background Replacement, a task central to creative applications such as film production and advertising, requires synthesizing entirely new, temporally consistent scenes while maintaining accurate foreground-background interactions, making large-scale data generation significantly more challenging. Consequently, this complex task remains largely underexplored due to a scarcity of high-quality training data. This gap is evident in poorly performing state-of-the-art models, e.g., Kiwi-Edit, because the primary open-source dataset that contains this task, i.e., OpenVE-3M, frequently produces static, unnatural backgrounds. In this paper, we trace this quality degradation to a lack of precise background guidance during data synthesis. Accordingly, we design a scalable pipeline that generates foreground and background guidance in a decoupled manner with strict quality filtering. Building on this pipeline, we introduce Sparkle, a dataset of ~140K video pairs spanning five common background-change themes, alongside Sparkle-Bench, the largest evaluation benchmark tailored for background replacement to date. Experiments demonstrate that our dataset and the model trained on it achieve substantially better performance than all existing baselines on both OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench. Our proposed dataset, benchmark, and model are fully open-sourced at https://showlab.github.io/Sparkle/.

</details>


### [108] [MedHorizon: Towards Long-context Medical Video Understanding in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06537)
*Bodong Du,Bowen Liu,Yang Yu,Xinpeng Ding,Zhiheng Wu,Shuning Wang,Shuo Nie,Naiming Liu,Qifeng Chen,Yangqiu Song,Xiaomeng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedHorizon is a challenging long-context medical video benchmark with sparse evidence that requires retrieval before reasoning over full clinical procedures; current MLLMs achieve only 41.1% accuracy, highlighting major bottlenecks in evidence retrieval and procedural reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in evaluating long, full-procedure medical video understanding where decisive evidence is sparse, temporally punctate, and context-dependent, and where retrieval of evidence prior to reasoning is necessary.

Method: Curated MedHorizon dataset containing 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures and 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questions. Evaluates general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs. Evidence frames average only 0.166% of frames, necessitating search over noisy procedural streams.

Result: Best-performing model achieves 41.1% accuracy, significantly below robust full-procedure understanding. Analyses identify bottlenecks in evidence retrieval, clinical interpretation, weak procedural reasoning, attention drift due to redundancy, and insufficient coverage via generic sampling strategies.

Conclusion: MedHorizon provides a rigorous testbed for models that must retrieve sparse evidence and perform multi-hop reasoning over entire clinical workflows, revealing key directions to improve evidence retrieval, procedural reasoning, and attention mechanisms in long medical videos.

Abstract: Medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced image understanding and short-video analysis, but real clinical review often requires full-procedure video understanding. Unlike general long videos, medical procedures contain highly redundant anatomical views, while decisive evidence is temporally sparse, spatially subtle, and context dependent. Existing benchmarks often assume this evidence has already been localized through images, short clips, or pre-segmented videos, leaving the retrieval-before-reasoning problem under-tested. We introduce MedHorizon, an in-the-wild benchmark for long-context medical video understanding. MedHorizon preserves 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures and provides 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questionsthat jointly evaluate sparse evidence understanding and multi-hop clinical reasoning. Its evidence is extremely sparse, with only 0.166% evidence frames on average, requiring models to search noisy procedural streams before interpreting and aggregating findings. We evaluate representative general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs. The best model reaches only 41.1% accuracy, showing that current systems remain far from robust full-procedure understanding. Further analysis yields four key findings: performance does not scale reliably with more frames, evidence retrieval and clinical interpretation remain primary bottlenecks; these bottlenecks are rooted in weak procedural reasoning and attention drift under redundancy, and generic sampling methods only partially balances local detail with global coverage. MedHorizon provides a rigorous testbed for MLLMs that retrieve sparse evidence and reason over complete clinical workflows.

</details>


### [109] [Solving Minimal Problems Without Matrix Inversion Using FFT-Based Interpolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06572)
*Haidong Wu,Snehal Bhayani,Janne Heikkilä*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sampling-based, matrix-inversion-free solver for camera-geometry minimal problems using sparse hidden-variable resultants; determinant polynomial reconstructed via inverse FFT from sampled evaluations; remaining unknowns recovered through rank-1 deficiency and Cramer's rule, with gcd-based robustness to noise; strong numerical stability and competitive runtimes for small-scale problems, offering a practical alternative to Gröbner-basics and traditional resultants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Minimal problems in camera geometry are traditionally solved using Gröbner-basis or resultant methods that rely on symbolic expansions and online matrix inversions, which can be numerically fragile and computationally expensive. A matrix-inversion-free, numerically stable, sampling-based approach is desirable to improve robustness and efficiency, especially for small-scale problems.

Method: Construct solvers via sparse hidden-variable resultants. The determinant polynomial in the hidden variable is reconstructed from sampled evaluations using inverse fast Fourier transform interpolation, avoiding symbolic expansion. Solving the polynomial yields the hidden variable; remaining unknowns are recovered by identifying rank-1 deficient submatrices and applying Cramer's rule. A gcd-based criterion is used to robustly select submatrices under noise.

Result: Empirical evaluations on diverse minimal problems show strong numerical stability and competitive runtimes, particularly for small-scale problems. The method provides a practical alternative to traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers and demonstrates robust performance under typical noise conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed sampling-based, inversion-free approach offers a robust, efficient alternative for solving minimal problems in camera geometry, combining sparse hidden-variable resultants with FFT-based determinant reconstruction. It holds promise as a practical solver for small-scale polynomial systems in vision and potentially broader polynomial-system contexts.

Abstract: Estimating camera geometry typically involves solving minimal problems formulated as systems of multivariate polynomial equations, which often pose computational challenges when using existing Gröbner-basis or resultant-based methods due to matrix inversion needed in the online solver. Here we propose a sampling-based, matrix inversion-free method that constructs the solvers using sparse hidden-variable resultants. The determinant polynomial in the hidden variable is efficiently reconstructed via inverse fast Fourier transform interpolation from sampled evaluations, avoiding symbolic expansion. Solving this polynomial yields the hidden variable, and the remaining unknowns are recovered by identifying rank-1 deficient submatrices and applying Cramer's rule. A greatest common divisor-based criterion ensures robust submatrix identification under noise. Experiments on diverse minimal problems demonstrate that the proposed solver achieves strong numerical stability and competitive runtime, particularly for small-scale problems, providing a practical alternative to traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers.

</details>


### [110] [DINORANKCLIP: DINOv3 Distillation and Injection for Vision-Language Pretraining with High-Order Ranking Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06592)
*Shuyang Jiang,Nan Yu,Yiming Zhang,Zenghui Ding,Zhenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DINORANKCLIP introduces dual improvements for CLIP: (1) a frozen DINOv3 teacher integrated into a dual-branch lightweight student with multi-scale fusion and attention-based refinements to preserve cross-modal alignment, and (2) a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model that captures pairwise and tuple-wise transitions. It outperforms CLIP and RANKCLIP, especially on fine-grained and out-of-distribution tasks, with R*=3 being the optimal order under the studied benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP suffers from (i) the symmetric InfoNCE loss that discards relative ordering among in-batch negatives, and (ii) global pooling that creates a semantic bottleneck insensitive to fine-grained local structure. RANKCLIP partially addresses the first issue but remains first-order and does not fix the second. The paper aims to address both deficiencies jointly to improve cross-modal alignment and local structure sensitivity.

Method: 1) Cross-modal trunk augmented with a frozen DINOv3 teacher via a dual-branch lightweight student and a multi-scale fusion module that uses channel-spatial attention, a self-attention refiner, and a conflict-aware gate to retain first-order cross-modal alignment. 2) A high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model where per-position utilities are augmented with attention-parameterized pairwise and tuple-wise transition terms; the model subsumes CLIP (zero-order) and RANKCLIP (first-order) as special cases, with an optimal order of R*=3 on the benchmarks.

Result: Empirical study includes an order sweep, a Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, a four-node Modality-Gap analysis, and six-variant Fusion ablations. Training is 72 hours on a single eight-GPU H100 node using Conceptual Captions 3M. DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute, with the largest relative gains on fine-grained and out-of-distribution evaluations.

Conclusion: Jointly addressing the two CLIP weaknesses through a frozen-teacher augmented architecture and a high-order ranking objective yields consistent performance gains, especially in fine-grained and OOD scenarios. The proposed R*=3 order indicates the utility of higher-order interactions in cross-modal alignment, while maintaining computational feasibility within current hardware budgets.

Abstract: Contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) suffers from two structural weaknesses: the symmetric InfoNCE loss discards the relative ordering among unmatched in-batch pairs, and global pooling collapses the visual representation into a semantic bottleneck that is poorly sensitive to fine-grained local structure. RANKCLIP partially addresses the first issue with a list-wise Plackett-Luce ranking-consistency loss, but its model is strictly first-order and inherits the second weakness untouched. We propose DINORANKCLIP, a pretraining framework that addresses both jointly. Our principal contribution is injecting a frozen DINOv3 teacher into the contrastive trunk through a dual-branch lightweight student and a multi-scale fusion module with channel-spatial attention, a self-attention refiner, and a conflict-aware gate that preserves the cross-modal alignment up to first order. Complementarily, we introduce a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model in which the per-position utility is augmented with attention-parameterised pairwise and tuple-wise transition terms; the family contains CLIP and RANKCLIP as nested zero-order and first-order special cases, and the optimal order on every benchmark is $R^*=3$. The full empirical study -- order sweep, Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, four-node Modality-Gap analysis, six-variant Fusion ablation -- fits in 72 hours on a single eight-GPU H100 node and trains entirely on Conceptual Captions 3M. DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute, with the largest relative gains on the fine-grained and out-of-distribution evaluations that most directly stress local structural reasoning.

</details>


### [111] [DPM++: Dynamic Masked Metric Learning for Occluded Person Re-identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06637)
*Lei Tan,Yingshi Luan,Pincong Zou,Pingyang Dai,Liujuan Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes DPM++, a dynamic masked metric learning framework for occluded person re-identification that uses input-adaptive masking in a classifier-prototype space, with CLIP-based two-stage supervision, a saliency-guided patch transfer to generate realistic occlusions, and occlusion-aware pairing/mask-guided optimization; achieves state-of-the-art results on occluded and holistic re-id benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion-induced mismatches between occluded samples and holistic identity representations hinder robust re-id; existing methods rely on pre-trained models and data augmentation but lack a unified framework for robust visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion patterns.

Method: Dynamic input-adaptive masking to select reliable identity subspaces; CLIP-based two-stage supervision transferring text-derived ID priors into classifier-prototype space; saliency-guided patch transfer for realistic occluded sample synthesis; occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization.

Result: Empirical results show DPM++ outperforms previous state-of-the-art on both occluded and holistic re-id benchmarks.

Conclusion: DPM++ provides a unified, robust framework for visibility-consistent matching under occlusion with improved stability and effectiveness, applicable to real-world occlusion scenarios.

Abstract: Although person re-identification has made impressive progress, occlusion caused by obstacles remains an unsettled issue in real applications. The difficulty lies in the mismatch between incomplete occluded samples and holistic identity representations. Severe occlusion removes discriminative body cues and introduces interference from background clutter and occluders, making global metric learning unreliable. Existing methods mainly rely on extra pre-trained models to estimate visible parts for alignment or construct occluded samples via data augmentation, but still lack a unified framework that learns robust visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion patterns. In this paper, we propose DPM++, a Dynamic Masked Metric Learning framework for occluded person re-identification. DPM++ learns an input-adaptive masked metric that dynamically selects reliable identity subspaces for each occluded instance, enabling matching to emphasize visibility-consistent evidence while suppressing unreliable components. Built upon the classifier-prototype space, DPM++ introduces a CLIP-based two-stage supervision scheme, where ID-level semantic priors are learned from the text branch and transferred into the classifier-prototype space for dynamic masked matching. To strengthen the masked metric, we introduce a saliency-guided patch transfer strategy to synthesize controllable and photo-realistic occluded samples during training. Exploiting real scene priors, this strategy exposes the model to realistic partial observations and provides richer supervision than random erasing. In addition, occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization improve the stability and effectiveness of the framework. Experiments on occluded and holistic person re-identification benchmarks show that DPM++ consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in both holistic and occlusion scenarios.

</details>


### [112] [Are We Making Progress in Multimodal Domain Generalization? A Comprehensive Benchmark Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06643)
*Hao Dong,Hongzhao Li,Shupan Li,Muhammad Haris Khan,Eleni Chatzi,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified benchmark (MMDG-Bench) for Multimodal Domain Generalization; standardizes evaluation across datasets, modalities, and tasks, revealing limited gains and robustness issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inconsistent evaluation protocols across MMDG studies hinder reliable assessment of progress; need comprehensive benchmark addressing real-world challenges like corruption and missing modalities.

Method: MMDG-Bench standardizes six datasets across action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, sentiment analysis; six modality combinations; nine representative methods; multiple evaluation settings including corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, OOD detection; 7,402 trained networks across 95 cross-domain tasks.

Result: Under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods yield marginal gains over ERM; no single method dominates; substantial gap to upper-bound performance; trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform bimodal; all methods degrade under corruption and missing modalities, with trustworthiness affected.

Conclusion: Standardized benchmarking is essential; current MMDG progress is incremental and not robust; MMDG-Bench enables fair, comprehensive evaluation and reveals weaknesses in current approaches.

Abstract: Despite the growing popularity of Multimodal Domain Generalization (MMDG) for enhancing model robustness, it remains unclear whether reported performance gains reflect genuine algorithmic progress or are artifacts of inconsistent evaluation protocols. Current research is fragmented, with studies varying significantly across datasets, modality configurations, and experimental settings. Furthermore, existing benchmarks focus predominantly on action recognition, often neglecting critical real-world challenges such as input corruptions, missing modalities, and model trustworthiness. This lack of standardization obscures a reliable assessment of the field's advancement. To address this issue, we introduce MMDG-Bench, the first unified and comprehensive benchmark for MMDG, which standardizes evaluation across six datasets spanning three diverse tasks: action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, and sentiment analysis. MMDG-Bench encompasses six modality combinations, nine representative methods, and multiple evaluation settings. Beyond standard accuracy, it systematically assesses corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, and out-of-distribution detection. With 7, 402 neural networks trained in total across 95 unique cross-domain tasks, MMDG-Bench yields five key findings: (1) under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods offer only marginal improvements over ERM baseline; (2) no single method consistently outperforms others across datasets or modality combinations; (3) a substantial gap to upper-bound performance persists, indicating that MMDG remains far from solved; (4) trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform the strongest bimodal configurations; and (5) all evaluated methods exhibit significant degradation under corruption and missing-modality scenarios, with some methods further compromising model trustworthiness.

</details>


### [113] [Relit-LiVE: Relight Video by Jointly Learning Environment Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06658)
*Weiqing Xiao,Hong Li,Xiuyu Yang,Houyuan Chen,Wenyi Li,Tianqi Liu,Shaocong Xu,Chongjie Ye,Hao Zhao,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Relit-LiVE presents a pose-agnostic video relighting framework that uses raw reference images and joint environment-map prediction within a single diffusion process to achieve physically consistent, temporally stable relighting, outperforming prior methods on synthetic and real data and enabling downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intrinsic decomposition-based video relighting is unreliable in real-world footage, leading to distorted appearances, broken materials, and temporal artifacts; a method that preserves geometric-illumination consistency without known per-frame camera poses is highly desirable.

Method: Introduce raw reference images into the rendering pipeline to recover critical scene cues lost in intrinsic decompositions. Propose an environment video prediction formulation that jointly generates relit videos and per-frame environment maps aligned to each camera viewpoint within a single diffusion process, enforcing geometry–illumination alignment and supporting dynamic lighting and camera motion.

Result: Extensive experiments show Relit-LiVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks, with strong physical consistency, temporal stability, and applicability to downstream tasks such as scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming relighting.

Conclusion: Relit-LiVE provides a physically consistent, temporally stable video relighting framework that is robust to unknown per-frame camera poses, and enables a broad set of downstream applications while advancing neural rendering research in relighting.

Abstract: Recent advances have shown that large-scale video diffusion models can be repurposed as neural renderers by first decomposing videos into intrinsic scene representations and then performing forward rendering under novel illumination. While promising, this paradigm fundamentally relies on accurate intrinsic decomposition, which remains highly unreliable for real-world videos and often leads to distorted appearances, broken materials, and accumulated temporal artifacts during relighting. In this work, we present Relit-LiVE, a novel video relighting framework that produces physically consistent, temporally stable results without requiring prior knowledge of camera pose. Our key insight is to explicitly introduce raw reference images into the rendering process, enabling the model to recover critical scene cues that are inevitably lost or corrupted in intrinsic representations. Furthermore, we propose a novel environment video prediction formulation that simultaneously generates relit videos and per-frame environment maps aligned with each camera viewpoint in a single diffusion process. This joint prediction enforces strong geometric-illumination alignment and naturally supports dynamic lighting and camera motion, significantly improving physical consistency in video relighting while easing the requirement of known per-frame camera pose. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Relit-LiVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods across synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Beyond relighting, our framework naturally supports a wide range of downstream applications, including scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming video relighting. The Project is available at https://github.com/zhuxing0/Relit-LiVE.

</details>


### [114] [BAMI: Training-Free Bias Mitigation in GUI Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06664)
*Borui Zhang,Bo Zhang,Bo Wang,Wenzhao Zheng,Yuhao Cheng,Liang Tang,Yiqiang Yan,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A bias-aware GUI grounding method (BAMI) with coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection mitigates precision and ambiguity biases identified by MPD attribution, achieving training-free improvements on GUI benchmarks (e.g., ScreenSpot-Pro) and robustness across configurations; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI grounding models struggle with high image resolution causing precision bias and complex interface elements causing ambiguity bias. There is a need for bias-aware, training-free improvements to improve task accuracy on challenging benchmarks.

Method: Use Masked Prediction Distribution (MPD) attribution to diagnose errors. Introduce Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI) with two manipulations: (1) coarse-to-fine focus to address precision bias, (2) candidate selection to address ambiguity bias. Evaluated in a training-free regime on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark, applied to TianXi-Action-7B baseline, with ablation studies.

Result: BAMI enhances GUI grounding accuracy; on ScreenSpot-Pro, TianXi-Action-7B improves from 51.9% to 57.8%. Ablation studies show robustness across parameter configurations.

Conclusion: BAMI effectively mitigates both precision and ambiguity biases in GUI grounding without retraining, and demonstrates stable, generalized improvements across models and settings; code available at the referenced repository.

Abstract: GUI grounding is a critical capability for enabling GUI agents to execute tasks such as clicking and dragging. However, in complex scenarios like the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark, existing models often suffer from suboptimal performance. Utilizing the proposed \textbf{Masked Prediction Distribution (MPD)} attribution method, we identify that the primary sources of errors are twofold: high image resolution (leading to precision bias) and intricate interface elements (resulting in ambiguity bias). To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI)}, which incorporates two key manipulations, coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection, to effectively mitigate these biases. Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that BAMI significantly enhances the accuracy of various GUI grounding models in a training-free setting. For instance, applying our method to the TianXi-Action-7B model boosts its accuracy on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark from 51.9\% to 57.8\%. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the robustness of the BAMI approach across diverse parameter configurations, highlighting its stability and effectiveness. Code is available at https://github.com/Neur-IO/BAMI.

</details>


### [115] [ActCam: Zero-Shot Joint Camera and 3D Motion Control for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06667)
*Omar El Khalifi,Thomas Rossi,Oscar Fossey,Thibault Fouque,Ulysse Mizrahi,Philip Torr,Ivan Laptev,Fabio Pizzati,Baptiste Bellot-Gurlet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ActCam enables zero-shot joint control of actor motion and camera trajectory in video generation by conditioning a pretrained diffusion model on pose and scene depth, using a two-stage conditioning schedule, achieving improved camera adherence and motion fidelity without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide fine-grained, geometry-consistent control over both actor motion and camera viewpoint in video synthesis in a zero-shot setting.

Method: Leverage any pretrained image-to-video diffusion model with depth and pose conditioning; given source video and target camera motion, synthesize pose and depth conditioning that remain geometrically consistent; apply a two-phase sampling: early steps condition on both pose and sparse depth to enforce structure; later steps drop depth and rely on pose-only guidance for high-frequency detail.

Result: Demonstrates improved camera adherence and motion fidelity vs pose-only and other pose+camera methods; preferred in human evaluations, especially with large viewpoint changes; validates joint control without training.

Conclusion: Shows that camera-consistent conditioning and staged guidance enable effective joint camera and motion control in video generation without training, enabling flexible artistic control.

Abstract: For artistic applications, video generation requires fine-grained control over both performance and cinematography, i.e., the actor's motion and the camera trajectory. We present ActCam, a zero-shot method for video generation that jointly transfers character motion from a driving video into a new scene and enables per-frame control of intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters. ActCam builds on any pretrained image-to-video diffusion model that accepts conditioning in terms of scene depth and character pose. Given a source video with a moving character and a target camera motion, ActCam generates pose and depth conditions that remain geometrically consistent across frames. We then run a single sampling process with a two-phase conditioning schedule: early denoising steps condition on both pose and sparse depth to enforce scene structure, after which depth is dropped and pose-only guidance refines high-frequency details without over-constraining the generation. We evaluate ActCam on multiple benchmarks spanning diverse character motions and challenging viewpoint changes. We find that, compared to pose-only control and other pose and camera methods, ActCam improves camera adherence and motion fidelity, and is preferred in human evaluations, especially under large viewpoint changes. Our results highlight that careful camera-consistent conditioning and staged guidance can enable strong joint camera and motion control without training. Project page: https://elkhomar.github.io/actcam/.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [116] [Are Flat Minima an Illusion?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05209)
*Michael Timothy Bennett*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weakness, defined as the volume of completions consistent with the learned function, predicts generalisation and is reparameterisation-invariant; sharpness and simplicity are not robust causal factors. Large-batch generalisation gains vanish with more data; flat minima are not the answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the claim that flatness of minima or Hessian-based simplicity governs generalisation; demonstrate that reparameterisation can inflate Hessian without affecting predictions and propose a dataset-robust predictor.

Method: Empirical analysis on 100 networks with identical architectures trained on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST. Examine correlation of weakness, sharpness, and simplicity with generalisation across different training set sizes (n=2k to 60k). Head-to-head comparisons; relate findings to exchangeable demands and PAC-Bayes bounds.

Result: Weakness correlates positively with generalisation (MNIST ρ=+0.374, p=1.2e-4; Fashion-MNIST ρ=+0.384, p=8.15e-5). Sharpness anticorrelates (ρ=-0.226). Simplicity is predictive on Fashion-MNIST but not on MNIST (p=0.848). The large-batch generalisation advantage on MNIST shrinks with more data. Weakness shows reparameterisation invariance and dataset-independent predictive power, unlike flatness or simplicity.

Conclusion: Flat minima are not the driver of generalisation. Weakness—the function-space volume compatible with the learned mapping—emerges as a robust predictor and remains invariant under reparameterisations; dataset-dependent effects of simplicity suggest caution in attributing generalisation to model simplicity. PAC-Bayes bounds align with weakness due to their correlation with it.

Abstract: Neural networks that land in flat regions of the loss landscape tend to generalise better than those in sharp regions. Sharpness-Aware Minimisation exploits this to improve generalisation. But function-preserving reparameterisation can inflate the Hessian of any minimum by two orders of magnitude without changing a single prediction. If the geometry of weight space can be manufactured from nothing, it cannot be the cause of anything. In other words, flat is simple and simplicity depends on encoding. Here I show that the actual driver is weakness, the volume of completions compatible with the learned function in the learner's embodied language. Weakness is reparameterisation-invariant because it is defined over what the network \emph{does}, not how it is parameterised. I prove weakness is minimax-optimal under exchangeable demands, and that PAC-Bayes bounds work because they correlate with it. On MNIST, the large-batch generalisation advantage \emph{vanishes} as training data grows, from $+1.6\%$ at $n = 2{,}000$ to $+0.02\%$ at $n = 60{,}000$. A quantity whose predictive power depends on how much data you have is not a cause but a confounder. I run head-to-heads on 100 networks with identical architecture and training. For MNIST weakness predicts generalisation ($ρ= +0.374$, $p = 0.00012$), sharpness anticorrelates ($ρ= -0.226$) and simplicity predicts nothing ($p = 0.848$). For Fashion-MNIST ($ρ= +0.384$, $p = 8.15 \times 10^{-5}$), though simplicity is at least somewhat predictive there. Simplicity is dataset dependent, whereas weakness is invariant. Flat minima were never the answer.

</details>


### [117] [Nationwide EHR-Based Chronic Rhinosinusitis Prediction Using Demographic-Stratified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05213)
*Sicong Chang,Yidan Shen,Justina Varghese,Akshay R Prabhakar,Sebastian Guadarrama-Sistos-Vazquez,Jiefu Chen,Masayoshi Takashima,Omar G. Ahmed,Renjie Hu,Xin Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-year pre-diagnostic EHR data from All of Us used with a hybrid feature-selection pipeline to predict CRS; demographic-stratified models achieve AUC 0.846 and outperform the baseline by 0.0168.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CRS is a common, heterogeneous inflammatory disorder that is difficult to identify early due to overlapping symptoms and phenotypic heterogeneity; previous predictive studies are often single-institutional and lack generalizability, limiting population-level risk stratification.

Method: Utilized nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the All of Us program to predict CRS diagnosis using two years of pre-diagnostic history. Implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline combining prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking to reduce ~110,000 candidate codes to 100 interpretable features. Built demographic-stratified models across six adult sex-life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning.

Result: Overall AUC of 0.8461, representing an improvement of 0.0168 over the best baseline.

Conclusion: Routinely collected EHR data can support population-representative CRS risk stratification and enable earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

Abstract: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a common heterogeneous inflammatory disorder that causes substantial morbidity and healthcare costs. CRS is difficult to identify early from routine encounters, as symptom presentations overlap with common conditions such as allergic rhinitis, and heterogeneous phenotypes further obscure risk patterns. Prior predictive studies often rely on single-institutional cohorts , which reduce population-level generalizability. To overcome this, we leveraged nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the \textit{All of Us} Research Program to predict CRS diagnosis using two years of pre-diagnostic history. To address extreme feature sparsity and dimensionality in coded EHR data, we implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline that combines prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking, compressing approximately 110,000 candidate codes into 100 interpretable features. To capture demographic heterogeneity, we trained demographic stratified models across six adult sex and life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning. Our framework achieved an overall AUC of 0.8461, improving discrimination by 0.0168 over the best baseline. These results demonstrate that routinely collected EHR data may support population-representative CRS risk stratification and inform earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

</details>


### [118] [SAT: Sequential Agent Tuning for Coordinator Free Plug and Play Multi-LLM Training with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05216)
*Yi Xie,Yangyang Xu,Yi Fan,Bo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAT enables coordinator-free, sequential training of a team of smaller LLM agents; provides monotone improvement and plug-and-play invariance; empirically strong results on AIME benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate high cost of large LLMs; coordinate multiple smaller agents without central controller; tackle distribution shift during joint updates.

Method: Factorized policy; block-coordinate updates; sequence-aware on-policy advantage estimator conditioned on evolving team policy; per-agent KL trust regions; theoretical guarantees of monotone improvement and plug-and-play invariance.

Result: 3x4B agents (12B total) beat Qwen3-32B on AIME24/25 by 3.9% avg; swapping in two 8B agents yields +10.4% composite score; code and proofs released.

Conclusion: SAT offers scalable, stable decentralized coordination for multi-agent LLM ensembles; supports plug-and-play upgrades; validated empirically and theoretically.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) with a large number of parameters achieve strong performance but are often prohibitively expensive to deploy. Recent work explores using teams of smaller, more efficient LLMs that collectively match or even outperform a single large model. However, jointly updating multiple agents introduces compounding distribution shifts, making coordination and stability during training difficult. We address this by introducing Sequential Agent Tuning (SAT), a coordinator-free training paradigm. SAT represents the team as a factorized policy and employs block-coordinate updates over agents, enabling scalable, decentralized training without a central controller. Specifically, we develop a sequence-aware, on-policy advantage estimator that conditions on the evolving team policy, coupled with per-agent KL trust regions that isolate occupancy drift. Theoretically, this framework provides two critical guarantees. First, it ensures monotonic improvement, stabilizing the training process. Second, it establishes provable plug-and-play invariance: any agent can be upgraded to a stronger model without retraining the rest of the team, with a formal guarantee that the performance bound improves. Empirically, a team of three 4B agents (12B total) trained with SAT surpasses the much larger Qwen3-32B on AIME24/25 benchmarks by 3.9\% on average. We validate our plug-and-play theory by swapping in two 8B agents, which boosts the composite score by 10.4\%. We provide code and appendix of proof at https://github.com/Yydc/SAT-AAMAS

</details>


### [119] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks with Learnable Loss Balancing and Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05217)
*Reza Pirayeshshirazinezhad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive self-supervised PINN with a learnable blending neuron that balances physics and data loss, plus transfer learning for data-scarce scenarios; achieves <8% error on heat transfer with 87 CFD datapoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity in scientific ML and limitations of fixed/heuristic PINN weighting. A dynamic, uncertainty-aware balancing mechanism and transfer learning can improve stability and generalization without manual tuning.

Method: A self-supervised PINN with a learnable blending neuron that adaptively weights physics residuals and data loss based on their uncertainties; include transfer learning to reuse representations from related domains and adapt to new physical systems with limited data.

Result: Adaptive PINN attains error <8% on predicting heat transfer in liquid-metal miniature heat sinks using only 87 CFD datapoints, outperforming shallow networks, kernel methods, and physics-only baselines.

Conclusion: Offers a general, reproducible recipe for embedding physics adaptively into neural networks for data-scarce problems across domains (e.g., fluid dynamics, material modeling), leveraging uncertainty-driven blending and transfer learning.

Abstract: We propose a self-supervised physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework that adaptively balances physics-based and data-driven supervision for scientific machine learning under data scarcity. Unlike prior PINNs that rely on fixed or heuristic weighting of physics residuals and data loss, our approach introduces a learnable blending neuron that dynamically adjusts the relative contribution of each term based on their uncertainties. This mechanism enables stable training and improved generalization without manual tuning. To further enhance efficiency, we integrate a transfer learning strategy that reuses representations from related domains and adapts them to new physical systems with limited data. We validate the framework for the prediction of heat transfer in liquid-metal miniature heat sinks using only 87 CFD datapoints, where the adaptive PINN achieves an error <8%, outperforming shallow neural networks, kernel methods, and physics-only baselines. Our framework provides a general recipe for embedding physics adaptively into neural networks, offering a robust and reproducible approach for data-scarce problems across various scientific domains, including fluid dynamics and material modeling.

</details>


### [120] [Horizon-Constrained Rashomon Sets for Chaotic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05218)
*Gauri Kale,Rahul Vishwakarma,Holly Diamond,Ava Hedayatipour,Amin Rezaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Horizon-constrained Rashomon sets link predictive multiplicity to chaotic dynamics by showing the Rashomon set contracts exponentially with lead time at a rate set by the max Lyapunov exponent; introduces Lyapunov-weighted metrics and decision-aligned model selection to improve downstream utility in chaotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between predictive multiplicity and chaos by analyzing how predictive equivalence evolves under chaotic dynamics and how to select models for downstream tasks, rather than solely forecasting accuracy.

Method: Define horizon-constrained Rashomon sets; prove exponential contraction with lead time governed by the maximum Lyapunov exponent; introduce Lyapunov-weighted metrics for tighter disagreement bounds; develop decision-aligned selection algorithms that optimize downstream utility; evaluate on synthetic chaotic systems (Lorenz-96, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky) and real-world domains (wind power, traffic, weather).

Result: The effective Rashomon set contracts exponentially with lead time at a rate determined by the maximum Lyapunov exponent; Lyapunov-weighted metrics yield tighter predictive-disagreement bounds; decision-aligned model selection improves downstream decision quality by 18–34% while maintaining competitive predictive performance; first rigorous connection between chaos theory and predictive multiplicity.

Conclusion: Establishes a principled link between chaos theory and predictive multiplicity, offering guidance for deploying ML in safety-critical chaotic domains.

Abstract: Predictive multiplicity and chaotic dynamics represent two fundamental challenges in machine learning that have evolved independently despite their conceptual connections. We bridge this gap by introducing horizon-constrained Rashomon sets, a theoretical framework that characterizes how model multiplicity evolves with prediction horizon in chaotic systems. Unlike static prediction tasks where the Rashomon set remains fixed, chaos induces exponential divergence among initially similar models, fundamentally transforming the nature of predictive equivalence. We prove that the effective Rashomon set contracts exponentially with lead time at a rate determined by the maximum Lyapunov exponent and introduce Lyapunov-weighted metrics that provide tighter bounds on predictive disagreement. Leveraging these insights, we develop decision-aligned selection algorithms that choose among near-optimal models based on downstream utility rather than forecast accuracy alone. Extensive experiments on synthetic chaotic systems (Lorenz-96, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky) and real-world applications (wind power, traffic, weather) demonstrate that our framework improves decision quality by 18-34\% while maintaining competitive predictive performance. This work establishes the first rigorous connection between chaos theory and predictive multiplicity, providing principled guidance for deploying machine learning in safety-critical chaotic domains.

</details>


### [121] [MidSteer: Optimal Affine Framework for Steering Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05220)
*Tatiana Gaintseva,Andrew Stepanov,Ziquan Liu,Martin Benning,Gregory Slabaugh,Jiankang Deng,Ismail Elezi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a theoretical framework for concept steering, linking it to LEACE affine erasure, introducing LEACE-Switch and MidSteer, and showing empirical effectiveness across vision diffusion models and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a formal, principled theory of concept steering to complement its empirical successes and enable controlled, low-disturbance manipulations across models.

Method: Formalize the theory of concept steering; prove that standard concept erasure is a special case of LEACE (affine erasure); define LEACE-Switch with optimality conditions for affine solutions; introduce MidSteer (Minimal Disturbance concept Steering) as a more general affine framework relax­ing assumptions; validate across tasks, modalities, and architectures.

Result: Demonstrates that standard erasure is a special case of LEACE; LEACE-Switch has principled optimality under specific assumptions; MidSteer enables directed, minimal-disturbance transformations and performs favorably across tasks, modalities, and architectures including vision diffusion models and LLMs.

Conclusion: MidSteer provides a general, practically effective affine framework for concept manipulation, unifying previous approaches under a minimal-disturbance paradigm and establishing a theoretical foundation for future work in concept steering.

Abstract: Steering intermediate representations has emerged as a powerful strategy for controlling generative models, particularly in post-deployment alignment and safety settings. However, despite its empirical success, it currently lacks a comprehensive theoretical framework. In this paper, we bridge this gap by formalizing the theory of concept steering. First, we establish a link between steering and affine concept erasure, proving that the standard approach for removing unwanted behaviors is a special case of LEACE (a closed-form method for affine erasure). Next, we formulate a principled theoretical framework for concept switching, LEACE-Switch, and characterize the assumptions under which it provides an optimal affine solution. Building on this analysis, we then introduce MidSteer (Minimal Disturbance concept Steering), a more general affine framework for concept manipulation that relaxes these assumptions and enables directed, minimal-disturbance transformations. We demonstrate that MidSteer performs favorably across a range of tasks, modalities, and architectures, including vision diffusion models and large language models.

</details>


### [122] [Data-Driven Variational Basis Learning Beyond Neural Networks: A Non-Neural Framework for Adaptive Basis Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05221)
*Andrew Kiruluta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A non-neural, variational framework (DVBL) learns data-adaptive basis atoms jointly with coefficients (and optionally a latent evolution operator), yielding an interpretable dictionary learning approach with theoretical guarantees on minimizers, convergence, and identifiability, and compatible with manifold/dynamical regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical representations are analytically tractable but fixed and not tailored to modern high-dimensional data; neural networks are powerful but opaque and hard to analyze; DVBL aims to combine data-adaptivity with explicit, analyzable basis structures.

Method: DVBL treats basis atoms as optimization variables and jointly optimizes them with sample-specific coefficients, optionally together with a latent linear evolution operator. It uses a variational objective and analyzes an alternating minimization algorithm, proving existence of minimizers, descent properties, and conditions for coefficient recovery and basis identifiability; accommodates manifold and dynamical regularization without neural nets.

Result: The paper establishes theoretical foundations: existence of minimizers, blockwise descent of alternating minimization, coefficient recovery and basis identifiability conditions, and a framework for incorporating regularizations; it positions DVBL as a conceptual bridge between dictionary learning, spectral methods, Koopman operator theory, and deep learning.

Conclusion: DVBL provides a data-adaptive, interpretable alternative to neural networks for learning basis expansions, with rigorous analysis and clear connections to classical and dynamical systems methods; it offers a principled path toward principled, non-neural representations of high-dimensional data.

Abstract: Classical representation systems such as Fourier series, wavelets, and fixed dictionaries provide analytically tractable basis expansions, but they are not intrinsically adapted to the empirical structure of modern high-dimensional data. Neural networks overcome this limitation by learning features from data, yet they do so through layered nonlinear parameterizations that often sacrifice interpretability, explicit control over basis structure, and mathematical transparency. In this manuscript we develop a non-neural alternative that learns basis functions directly from data through variational optimization. The proposed framework, termed Data Driven Variational Basis Learning (DVBL), treats basis atoms as primary optimization variables and learns them jointly with sample-specific coefficients and, when appropriate, a latent linear evolution operator. This yields a data-adaptive basis expansion that remains explicit, interpretable, and amenable to rigorous analysis. We formulate the model, establish existence of minimizers, prove blockwise descent properties for an alternating minimization algorithm, give conditions for coefficient recovery and basis identifiability, and show how manifold and dynamical regularization can be integrated without invoking neural architectures. We also discuss the conceptual novelty of the framework relative to classical dictionary learning, spectral methods, Koopman operator methods, and deep representation learning.

</details>


### [123] [Adaptive Computation Depth via Learned Token Routing in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05222)
*Ahmed Abdelmuniem Abdalla Mohammed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSA introduces a per-token gate that selectively gates residual updates between transformer blocks, enabling dynamic, token-wise depth with minimal overhead; it reduces token-layer operations by 14-23% on char-level LM with negligible quality loss and can transfer to inference-time sparse execution for real speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard transformers apply uniform depth to all tokens regardless of contextual difficulty, leading to inefficiency. There is a need for dynamic, token-aware computation to reduce unnecessary processing while maintaining performance.

Method: A per-token gate implemented as a small two-layer MLP produces a continuous halting probability for each token, applied to residual updates between consecutive transformer blocks. This TSA gate is end-to-end differentiable, adds ~1.7% parameters, and does not require explicit depth regularization; even with λ=0, gradients encourage skipping ~20% of token-layer operations. The approach preserves the base architecture and enables inference-time sparse execution.

Result: On character-level language modeling (Tiny-Shakespeare, enwik8), TSA reduces token-layer operations by 14-23% with <0.5% loss in quality. At matched efficiency, TSA achieves 0.7% lower validation loss than early exit baselines. The learned routing also transfers to inference-time sparse execution, delivering real wall-clock speedups.

Conclusion: TSA demonstrates that dynamic, per-token depth can be learned with minimal architectural changes and overhead, yielding substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing performance, and enabling practical speedups via sparse execution at inference time.

Abstract: Standard transformer architectures apply the same number of layers to every token regardless of contextual difficulty. We present Token-Selective Attention (TSA), a learned per-token gate on residual updates between consecutive transformer blocks. Each gate is a lightweight two-layer multi-layer perceptron (MLP) that produces a continuous halting probability, making the mechanism end-to-end differentiable with 1.7% parameter overhead and no changes to the base architecture. Notably, TSA learns difficulty-proportional routing without any explicit depth pressure: even at $λ=0$ (no depth regularisation), the task-loss gradient alone drives the router to skip 20% of token-layer operations. On character-level language modeling, TSA saved 14-23% of token-layer operations (TLOps) across Tiny-Shakespeare and enwik8 at <0.5% quality loss. At matched efficiency, TSA achieved 0.7% lower validation loss than early exit, and the learned routing transfers directly to inference-time sparse execution for real wall-clock speedup.

</details>


### [124] [Structural Instability of Feature Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05223)
*Yunpeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometric framework for compositional steering in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) revealing an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold under a spherical dictionary, driven by Gaussian mean width; ReLU induces a drift in high-bias regimes causing interference growth during composition; validated on CLEVR-derived semantic features, showing faster collapse with hierarchical correlations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the theoretical gap in understanding how multiple latent features compose in nonlinear, overcomplete representations and how interference emerges when steering multiple semantic latents, moving beyond linear representations.

Method: Model activation space as a high-dimensional sparse cone manifold; derive asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold using Gaussian mean width (statistical dimension) of the signal cone under a spherical dictionary; analyze high-bias ReLU rectification causing variance fluctuations to drift and accumulate during composition (ratchet effect); empirical validation on structured semantic features from CLEVR, comparing to random baselines.

Result: Derived an asymptotic threshold for compositional collapse; demonstrated that ReLU-induced drift leads to systematic interference growth under composition; observed scaling trends consistent with theory; structured semantic features with hierarchical correlations accelerate the collapse relative to random baselines.

Conclusion: Geometric constraints bound the scalability of union-based steering in SAEs; interference cannot be ignored in nonlinear, overcomplete regimes, motivating composition mechanisms that explicitly manage non-linear interference beyond naive linear superposition.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for disentangling feature superposition in transformer-based architectures, enabling precise control via activation steering. However, the theoretical foundations of compositional steering -- the simultaneous activation of distinct semantic latents -- remain under-explored. The prevailing Linear Representation Hypothesis often abstracts away non-linear interference effects that arise in overcomplete dictionaries. We present a geometric framework for analyzing the instability of feature unions. Modeling the activation space as a high-dimensional sparse cone manifold, we derive an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold under a spherical dictionary model, characterized by the Gaussian mean width (statistical dimension) of the signal cone. We further show that, in the high-bias regime, ReLU rectification converts microscopic correlation-induced variance fluctuations into a systematic drift that accumulates under composition, yielding interference growth consistent with a ratchet effect. We validate the predicted scaling trends on structured semantic features extracted from CLEVR, where hierarchical correlations accelerate the transition relative to random baselines. Together, our results highlight geometric constraints on the scalability of union-based steering and motivate composition mechanisms that explicitly manage interference beyond naive linear superposition.

</details>


### [125] [Channel-Level Semantic Perturbations: Unlearnable Examples for Diverse Training Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05224)
*Bo Wang,Jia Ni,Mengnan Zhao,Zhan Qin,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic study of unlearnable examples under pretraining-finetuning (PF). Pretrained weights and shallow-layer freezing weaken existing UEs; semantic filtering explains this effect. Introduces Shallow Semantic Camouflage (SSC) to confine perturbations to semantically valid subspaces, preserving data unlearnability under PF, including shallow-layer freezing and semantic-focused pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlearnable examples aim to protect data used in model training, but privacy threats persist. The PF paradigm is widely used in practice, yet most UE evaluations focus on from-scratch training. Understanding UE behavior under PF and developing robust defenses are essential for privacy-preserving ML.

Method: Systematic analysis of UEs across diverse training paradigms, evidence-based explanation via semantic filtering: shallow layers preserve semantics under PF, reducing effectiveness of noise. Propose SSC, a hierarchical deception strategy that confines perturbations to a semantically valid subspace. Extensive experiments under challenging PF scenarios, including shallow-layer freezing and SF-Pretrain.

Result: Existing UEs are weakened when pretrained weights are loaded and frozen; SSC maintains data unlearnability under PF, including when shallow layers are frozen or subjected to semantic-focused pretraining.

Conclusion: The PF setting changes how UEs operate due to semantic preservation by pretrained weights. Limiting perturbations to semantically meaningful subspaces via SSC can circumvent this, bridging the gap in pretrain-based unlearnable learning and highlighting the need for PF-aware defenses.

Abstract: The unauthorized use of personal data in model training has emerged as a growing privacy threat. Unlearnable examples (UEs) address this issue by embedding imperceptible perturbations into benign examples to obstruct feature learning. However, existing studies mainly evaluate UEs under from-scratch training settings, leaving their behavior under the widely adopted pretraining-finetuning (PF) paradigm largely unexplored. In this work, we provide the first systematic investigation of unlearnable examples across diverse training paradigms. Our analysis reveals that loading and freezing pretrained weights significantly weakens the effectiveness of existing UEs methods. We further explain these findings through semantic filtering: while UEs tend to induce models to overfit non-semantic noise, thereby weakening their semantic extraction capabilities, under the PF paradigm, frozen shallow layers preserve data semantics, effectively filtering out distracting information like unlearnable noise. Guided by these insights, we propose a hierarchical deception strategy, Shallow Semantic Camouflage (SSC), that confines the generation process to a semantically valid subspace, aiming to bypass the semantic suppression introduced by pretrained weights. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently preserves data unlearnability even under challenging training paradigms, such as shallow-layer freezing and semantic-focused pretraining (SF-Pretrain), bridging the critical gap in pretrain-based unlearnable learning.

</details>


### [126] [MACS: Modality-Aware Capacity Scaling for Efficient Multimodal MoE Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05225)
*Bo Li,Chuan Wu,shaolin Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MACS provides training-free inference for MoE multimodal LLMs by using entropy-weighted visual-token loads and dynamic, input-driven capacity allocation, mitigating straggler effects in Expert Parallelism across variable modality compositions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce straggler-induced inefficiency in expert-parallel MoE multimodal LLMs, addressing information heterogeneity among visual tokens and modality-dynamics due to varying visual/textual ratios across tasks.

Method: Introduce two components: (1) Entropy-Weighted Load to quantify semantic value of visual tokens and (2) Dynamic Modality-Adaptive Capacity to allocate expert resources based on real-time modal composition; MACS operates in a training-free inference setting.

Result: MACS significantly outperforms existing methods on diverse multimodal benchmarks, demonstrating robust and efficient EP inference for MoE MLLMs.

Conclusion: MACS is a practical, training-free framework that effectively mitigates straggler effects in multimodal MoE models by accounting for information heterogeneity and modality dynamics.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts Multimodal Large Language Models (MoE MLLMs) suffer from a significant efficiency bottleneck during Expert Parallelism (EP) inference due to the straggler effect. This issue is worsened in the multimodal context, as existing token-count-based load balancing methods fail to address two unique challenges: (1) Information Heterogeneity, where numerous redundant visual tokens are treated equally to semantically critical ones, and (2) Modality Dynamics, where varying visual to text ratios across tasks lead to resource misallocation. To address these challenges, we propose MACS (Modality-Aware Capacity Scaling), a training-free inference framework. Specifically, MACS introduces an Entropy-Weighted Load mechanism to quantify the semantic value of visual tokens, addressing information heterogeneity. Additionally, the Dynamic Modality-Adaptive Capacity mechanism allocates expert resources based on the real-time modal composition of the input. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MACS significantly outperforms existing methods on various multimodal benchmarks, providing a novel and robust solution for the efficient deployment of MoE MLLMs in EP inference.

</details>


### [127] [Internalizing Outcome Supervision into Process Supervision: A New Paradigm for Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05226)
*Fei Ding,Yongkang Zhang,Runhao Liu,Yuhao Liao,Zijian Zeng,Sibo wang,Huiming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes reinforcement learning for reasoning that internalizes outcome supervision into process-level supervision, enabling the model to extract and reuse internal signals from failed trajectories to guide finer-grained policy updates under outcome-only feedback; introduces a training paradigm where internal supervision is continually generated and refined.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-based supervision is sparse and credit assignment for intermediate reasoning is hard. External process supervision is costly and scales poorly. Internalizing process signals aims to provide fine-grained learning signals without external supervision.
