Method: Identify, correct, and reuse failed reasoning trajectories to extract process-level learning signals; use these signals to guide policy updates under outcome-only supervision; develop a continual internal supervision loop where the model generates and refines its own internal feedback during RL.

Result: Conceptual framework and proposed training paradigm. The abstract outlines a theoretical approach and potential benefits but does not report empirical results.

Conclusion: Introduces a novel perspective and pathway for fine-grained credit assignment in RL for reasoning by internalizing supervision, distinguishing it from approaches relying on externally provided process supervision.

Abstract: The central challenge of reinforcement learning for reasoning lies not only in the sparsity of outcome-level supervision, but more fundamentally in how to transform feedback provided only at the end of a sequence into fine-grained learning signals that can guide intermediate reasoning steps. Existing approaches either rely on outcome-level rewards for sequence-level optimization, which makes precise credit assignment difficult, or depend on externally constructed process supervision, which is costly and difficult to scale sustainably. To address this, we propose a new perspective: reinforcement learning for reasoning can be understood as the problem of internalizing outcome supervision into process supervision. From this perspective, we introduce a supervision-internalization method for reinforcement learning for reasoning, enabling the model to automatically extract process-level learning signals through identifying, correcting, and reusing failed reasoning trajectories, thereby achieving finer-grained policy optimization under outcome-only supervision. We further abstract this idea into a new training paradigm, in which the model continually generates and refines its own internal process supervision during reinforcement learning, opening a new path for fine-grained credit assignment in reinforcement learning for reasoning that differs from externally provided process supervision.

</details>


### [128] [Rethinking Data Curation in LLM Training: Online Reweighting Offers Better Generalization than Offline Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05227)
*Wanru Zhao,Yihong Chen,Yuzhi Tang,Wentao Ma,Shengchao Hu,Shell Xu Hu,Alex Iacob,Abhinav Mehrotra,Nicholas D. Lane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADAPT reweights training samples online via per-sample loss weighting, turning data curation into an implicit curriculum without changing dataset size, improving cross-benchmark generalization under equal FLOPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline data curation (selection/mixing) is brittle and costly, often requiring re-running pipelines with model/task shifts and sacrificing data diversity; online reweighting aims to adapt data emphasis dynamically during training.

Method: ADAPT (Adaptive Data reweighting for Pretraining and FineTuning) dynamically assigns per-sample learning rates guided by similarity-based quality signals, adjusting weights during training without changing the number of samples, effectively acting as an implicit curriculum.

Result: Empirical results on instruction tuning and large-scale pretraining show ADAPT outperforming offline selection/mixing and prior online methods, achieving stronger cross-benchmark generalization under equal FLOPs.

Conclusion: Online data reweighting is a viable alternative to offline curation; ADAPT demonstrates robust generalization gains by progressively shifting focus during training while preserving data quantity.

Abstract: Data curation is a critical yet under-explored area in large language model (LLM) training. Existing methods, such as data selection and mixing, operate in an offline paradigm, detaching themselves from training. This separation introduces engineering overhead and makes the curation brittle: the entire pipeline must be re-run under model/task shifts. Moreover, offline methods alter data size through hard filtering or resampling, often sacrificing data diversity and harming generalization. We propose to rethink data curation as an online reweighting problem, where sample importance is dynamically adjusted during training via loss weighting rather than static pre-processing. Specifically, we introduce ADAPT (Adaptive Data reweighting for Pretraining and FineTuning), a dynamic online framework that reweights training samples with adaptive per-sample learning rates guided by similarity-based quality signals, without changing the number of training samples. Unlike offline methods that enforce a static data distribution, ADAPT acts as an implicit curriculum learner, progressively shifting focus from coarse-grained patterns to fine-grained semantic distinctions as the model evolves. Experiments on both instruction tuning and large-scale pretraining show that ADAPT consistently outperforms offline selection/mixing and prior online methods, achieving stronger cross-benchmark generalization under equal FLOPs.

</details>


### [129] [Evolutionary fine tuning of quantized convolution-based deep learning models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05228)
*Marcin Pietroń*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evolution strategy optimization is used to improve post-quantization accuracy beyond nearest-neighbor rounding by mutating a small subset of weights to alternate quantized states, improving CNN and autoencoder performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nearest-neighbor quantization often does not yield optimal accuracy after quantization; improving post-quantization performance is crucial for compact, real-time models.

Method: Apply evolution strategy to pretrained quantized models, iteratively mutate a small percentage of weights to different quantization states using a tailored set of operators and parameters.

Result: The ES-based approach fast improves accuracy of quantized models, with reported gains on popular architectures such as VGG and ResNet for image classification and detection, and additional experiments on autoencoders.

Conclusion: Appropriate ES operators and parameter choices can enhance quantized model accuracy, suggesting a viable post-quantization optimization pathway beyond standard nearest-neighbor rounding.

Abstract: Deep learning models are the most efficient models in many machine learning tasks. The main disadvantage when using them in IoT, mobile devices, independent autonomous or real-time systems is their complexity and memory size. Therefore, much research has concentrated on compression techniques of deep learning architectures. One of the most popular technique is quantization. In most of the works, the quantization is done based on the nearest neighbour quantization technique. This work focuses on improving the quantization efficiency in pretrained and quantized models. This approach has the potential to improve the final accuracy of quantized models. The main postulate of the work is that final quantization states of the network based on nearest neighbour rounding does not guarantee optimal accuracy. In the presented work, the evolution strategy is used as an optimization approach. The evolution in each iteration changes the values of the small percentage of weights. It shifts theirs values to different quantization states. The work shows that proposed evolution with an appropriate set of operators and parameters can fast improve the accuracy of the quantized models. The results are presented for popular architectures such as VGG and Resnet for image classification and detection. Additionally, simulations were carried out for the autoencoder architecture.

</details>


### [130] [Expert Routing for Communication-Efficient MoE via Finite Expert Banks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05278)
*Mohammad Reza Deylam Salehi,Ali Khalesi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Treats gating in sparse MoE as a stochastic channel; introduces a finite-bank MNIST setup with a discrete, data-dependent selection to estimate information measures; shows I(S;W) tracks the generalization gap; provides empirical I(X;T) estimation and a Blahut–Arimoto routine to trace accuracy–rate curves; frames I(X;T) and D(R_g) as design proxies for efficient routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable tractable information-theoretic analysis of resource-constrained MoE routing and connect information measures to generalization and efficiency in sparse MoEs.

Method: Construct a finite-bank MNIST setting using pretrained CNN experts with a discrete, data-dependent selection rule. Since the selected model is drawn from a finite candidate set, compute a closed-form discrete-entropy estimator for I(S;W) from the empirical posterior q(W|S). Sweep a data-dependence parameter α. Develop an empirical estimator of I(X;T) and apply a Blahut–Arimoto procedure to trace an accuracy–rate curve over the expert bank. Compare against a uniform union-bound baseline.

Result: I(S;W) monotonically tracks the generalization gap; Xu–Raginsky bound exhibits the expected looseness; the empirical I(X;T) estimator and the BA-based tracing reveal a practicable accuracy–rate characterization; the baseline provides a reference.

Conclusion: The framework yields a practical tool for analyzing resource-aware MoE inference and supports interpreting I(X;T) and D(R_g) as design proxies for efficient expert routing.

Abstract: Resource-efficient machine learning increasingly uses sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, where the gate acts as both a learning component and a routing interface controlling computation, communication, and accuracy. Motivated by finite-rate interpretations of MoE gating, we treat the gate as a stochastic channel and use $I(X;T)$ to quantify the routing information available to the selected expert. To make the associated information quantities tractable beyond synthetic examples, we develop a finite-bank MNIST construction using pretrained CNN experts and a discrete, data-dependent selection rule. Since the selected model belongs to a finite candidate set, the algorithmic mutual information $I(S;W)$ admits a closed-form discrete-entropy estimator from the empirical posterior $q(W|S)$. Sweeping a data-dependence parameter $α$, we observe that $\widehat I(S;W)$ monotonically tracks the generalization gap, while the Xu-Raginsky bound exhibits the expected looseness. We also compare with a uniform union-bound baseline and introduce an empirical estimator of $I(X;T)$ together with a Blahut-Arimoto procedure for tracing an accuracy-rate curve over the expert bank. The proposed framework provides a practical tool for analyzing resource-aware MoE inference systems and for interpreting $I(X;T)$ and $D(R_g)$ as design proxies for efficient expert routing.

</details>


### [131] [Forecasting Green Skill Demand in the Automotive Industry: Evidence from Online Job Postings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05280)
*Sabur Butt,Joshua N. Arrazola E.,Hector G. Ceballos,Patricia Caratozzolo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multilingual, ESCO-validated pipeline uses online job ads from Mexico's automotive sector to identify 274 green skills (4.22% of skills) and forecast demand with transformer-based time-series models, achieving strong accuracy and classifying skills by growth to inform workforce planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of systematic methods to identify and forecast green skills in labor markets, leveraging multilingual embeddings, ESCO mapping, and large-scale job postings to support data-driven workforce planning during the green transition.

Method: Two-stage workflow: (1) extract and align skills from 204,373 records across Indeed Mexico, OCC Mundial, and LinkedIn (July 2024–July 2025) using multilingual embeddings and ESCO validation to identify 274 unique green skills with 8,576 occurrences (4.22%); (2) evaluate 15 time-series forecasting models with rolling-origin evaluation; compare Transformer-based models (FEDformer, Reformer, Informer) against baselines; classify skills by absolute/relative growth into stable, emerging, high-impact categories.

Result: Transformer-based models achieved best performance (MAE ~2.5e-5; relative RMSE < 15). The current demand concentrates on operational sustainability; fastest growth seen in renewable energy, recycling, and hydrogen technologies. The workflow also yields a practical framework to categorize skill trajectories, supporting data-driven workforce planning in the green transition.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, scalable approach to measuring and forecasting green skill demand using online job postings and standardized skill taxonomy (ESCO). The approach can inform policy, industry planning, and scalable adaptation across sectors and regions, though generalizability and external validation remain areas for further work.

Abstract: The global transition toward sustainable economies is reshaping labor markets, yet systematic methods for identifying and forecasting green skills remain limited. This study presents a computational framework to measure and predict green skill demand using online job postings from Mexico's automotive industry, which contributes about 4% of national GDP. We compile a dataset of job advertisements from Indeed Mexico, OCC Mundial, and LinkedIn (July 2024 to July 2025), yielding 204,373 skill records. A two-stage pipeline combining multilingual embeddings and ESCO validation identifies 274 unique green skills across 8,576 occurrences (4.22% of all skills). We benchmark 15 time series forecasting models using a rolling origin evaluation. Transformer-based models, especially FEDformer, Reformer, and Informer, achieve the best performance, with MAE around 2.5e-5 and relative RMSE below 15. We further propose a framework to classify skills by absolute and relative growth, identifying stable, emerging, and high-impact competencies. Results show current demand is concentrated in operational sustainability practices, while the fastest-growing skills relate to renewable energy, recycling, and hydrogen technologies. This pipeline supports data-driven workforce planning in the green transition.

</details>


### [132] [Attribution-Guided Continual Learning for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05285)
*Yazheng Liu,Yuxuan Wan,Rui Xu,Xi Zhang,Sihong Xie,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An attribution-guided continual fine-tuning framework for LLMs that estimates per-parameter, per-layer task importance and uses it to modulate gradients, yielding better retention of old tasks with competitive performance on new tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning for LLMs; existing methods (replay, freezing, regularization) lack semantic awareness of parameter knowledge distribution, hindering identification of parameters to preserve vs update.

Method: Compute element-wise, task-specific parameter importance within each Transformer layer (attribution-guided). Use these scores to modulate gradients during fine-tuning so that important parameters for previous tasks receive smaller updates while less important ones remain plastic for new tasks.

Result: Empirical benchmarks show the proposed method consistently outperforms baselines, achieving improved retention of old tasks while maintaining competitive performance on newly learned tasks.

Conclusion: Attribution-guided parameter importance shaping gradients provides a semantically informed mechanism to mitigate forgetting in LLM continual learning, balancing stability and plasticity across tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often suffer from catastrophic forgetting in continual learning: after learning new tasks sequentially, they perform worse on earlier tasks. Existing methods mitigate catastrophic forgetting by data replay, parameter freezing, or regularization. However, these methods lack semantic awareness of internal knowledge distribution in LLMs. As a result, they cannot distinguish parameters that should be preserved or updated. We propose an attribution-guided continual fine-tuning framework for LLMs. Our method estimates task-specific, element-wise parameter importance in each Transformer layer and uses these scores to modulate gradients. Parameters important to previous tasks receive smaller updates, while less relevant ones remain plastic for learning new tasks. Experiments on continual learning benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms baselines, achieving better retention of old tasks while maintaining competitive performance on new tasks.

</details>


### [133] [Graph Normalization: Fast Binarizing Dynamics for Differentiable MWIS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05330)
*Laurent Guigues*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GN is a differentiable dynamical system for MWIS that converges to a binary MIS solution, linking to replicator dynamics and Motzkin–Straus, providing fast binarization and constrained optimization suitable for differentiable DL architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, differentiable engine for solving NP-hard MWIS and related combinatorial problems, enabling end-to-end learning and application to resource allocation, structured attention, pruning, and inference in graphs while offering convergence guarantees.

Method: Define Graph Normalization as a dynamical system on graphs that implements an exact Majorization–Minimization step to realize a fast quasi-Newton descent for the MWIS relaxed objective; prove convergence to a binary MIS indicator; establish an equivalence to nonlinear replicator dynamics; derive a weighted Motzkin–Straus-type correspondence; extend to the Assignment Problem with a Sinkhorn-like mechanism; demonstrate binarization and integration with Bregman–Sinkhorn solvers.

Result: GN converges to a binary MIS indicator and monotonically increases the MWIS objective; is equivalent to a replicator dynamics interpretation; yields a weighted Motzkin–Straus relationship; for Assignment, acts as a Sinkhorn-like binarization that generalizes to arbitrary constraint graphs; on real-world graphs up to 1M edges, GN finds near-optimal solutions within 1% of the best known in seconds on CPU; serves as a fast binarization engine for Bregman–Sinkhorn MWIS solvers.

Conclusion: GN provides a principled, differentiable framework for hard combinatorial decisions on graphs, with convergence guarantees and practical performance, enabling end-to-end learning of constrained optimization in AI and broad applications in DL architectures and beyond (structured sparse attention, pruning, Mixture-of-Experts, CV/biology/resource allocation).

Abstract: We introduce Graph Normalization (GN), a principled dynamical system on graphs that serves as a differentiable approximation engine for the NP-hard Maximum Weight Independent Set (MWIS) problem. MWIS encompasses many combinatorial challenges, including optimal assignment, scheduling, set packing, and MAP inference in discrete Markov Random Fields. Unlike Belief Propagation, we prove GN always converges to a binary indicator of a Maximum Independent Set. GN realizes a fast quasi-Newton descent through an exact Majorization-Minimization step, systematically improving the MWIS relaxed primal objective. We establish an equivalence between GN and the Replicator Dynamics of a nonlinear evolutionary game, where vertices compete for inclusion in an independent set. While a non-potential game, the GN game follows Fisher's Fundamental Theorem of Natural Selection, where the average fitness equals the MWIS primal objective and strictly increases. This connection leads to a weighted extension of the Motzkin-Straus theorem, showing MISes are in bijection with the local minima of a quadratic form over a tilted simplex. For the Assignment Problem, GN acts as a variant of the Sinkhorn algorithm that naturally converges to a hard assignment while generalizing to arbitrary constraint graphs. We demonstrate GN's performance as a fast binarization engine for the state-of-the-art Bregman-Sinkhorn relaxed MWIS solver. On real-world benchmarks with up to 1M edges, GN identifies solutions within 1% of the best known results in seconds on a CPU. GN opens new avenues for deep learning architectures requiring differentiable, "hard" decisions under constraints, with applications in structured sparse attention, dynamic network pruning, and Mixture-of-Experts. Beyond core AI, the GN framework enables end-to-end learning of constrained optimization in computer vision, computational biology, and resource allocation.

</details>


### [134] [Feature Starvation as Geometric Instability in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05341)
*Faris Chaudhry,Keisuke Yano,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive elastic net sparse autoencoders (AEN-SAEs) provide a differentiable, stable sparse coding framework that remedies feature starvation and shrinkage bias in overcomplete SAEs, with theoretical guarantees and empirical success on synthetic and LLM data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard l1-regularized SAEs suffer from feature starvation and shrinkage bias due to overcomplete dictionaries; the sparse coding map is unstable and misaligned with shallow encoders, motivating a differentiable, stable alternative.

Method: Introduce AEN-SAEs by adding an l2 structural term for strong convexity and Lipschitz stability, combined with adaptive l1 reweighting to reduce shrinkage bias and suppress spurious features. This yields a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and controlled polyhedral geometry, with fully differentiable training.

Result: Theoretical: AEN-SAEs yield a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and can recover the global feature support under mild assumptions. Empirical: across synthetic experiments and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B), they mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while preserving competitive reconstruction performance.

Conclusion: AEN-SAEs address the optimization-geometric pathology of overcomplete dictionaries, offering stable, interpretable representations without resorting to heuristics or non-differentiable masking, and show practical effectiveness on language-model-related tasks.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are used to disentangle the dense, polysemantic internal representations of large language models (LLMs) into interpretable, monosemantic concepts. However, standard $\ell_1$-regularized SAEs suffer from feature starvation (dead neurons) and shrinkage bias, often requiring computationally expensive heuristic resampling and nondifferentiable hard-masking methods to bypass these challenges. We argue that feature starvation is not merely an empirical artifact of poor data diversity, but a fundamental optimization-geometric pathology of overcomplete dictionaries: the $\ell_1$-induced sparse coding map is unstable and fundamentally misaligned with shallow, amortized encoders. To address this structural instability, we introduce adaptive elastic net SAEs (AEN-SAEs), a fully differentiable architecture grounded in classical sparse regression. AEN-SAEs combine an $\ell_2$ structural term that enforces strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive $\ell_1$ reweighting that eliminates shrinkage bias and suppresses spurious features, thereby jointly controlling the curvature and interaction structure of the induced polyhedral geometry. Theoretically, we show that AEN-SAEs yield a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and recover the global feature support under mild assumptions. Empirically, across synthetic settings and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B), AEN-SAEs mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while maintaining competitive reconstruction abilities.

</details>


### [135] [Balancing Stability and Plasticity in Sequentially Trained Early-Exiting Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05358)
*Alaa Zniber,Ouassim Karrakchou,Mounir Ghogho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two continual-learning-inspired methods for sequentially training exits in early-exit networks: (1) Elastic Weight Consolidation to protect weights critical to earlier exits, (2) Learning without Forgetting to preserve earlier exits' output distributions. Both aim to mitigate interference between newly added exits and existing ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevent interference and degradation of previously trained exits when adding new exit classifiers along a shared backbone, enabling faster, more accurate early exits under constrained computation.

Method: 1) EWC-style regularization that penalizes changes to weights important for earlier exits; 2) LwF-style distillation that preserves the outputs of earlier exits while the network learns new exits. These approaches address the stability-plasticity trade-off in continual learning.

Result: Empirical results on standard benchmarks show consistent improvement over typical sequential training, yielding higher early-exit accuracy and notable speedups at low computational budgets.

Conclusion: Framing sequential exit training as a continual-learning problem with EWC and LwF effectively mitigates forgetting, improving early-exit performance and enabling efficient computation under budget constraints.

Abstract: Early-exiting neural networks enable adaptive inference by allowing inputs to exit at intermediate classifiers, reducing computation for easy samples while maintaining high accuracy. In practice, exits can be trained sequentially by incrementally adding them to a shared backbone; however, this sequential training can cause newly introduced exits to interfere with previously learned ones, degrading the performance of earlier classifiers. We address this problem by retaining the knowledge embedded in existing exits while allowing new ones to specialize. We propose two alternative approaches that operate at different levels of the model. The first constrains learning by protecting parameters that are important for previously trained exits, while the second preserves the output distributions of earlier exits as the network adapts. These alternatives directly reflect the stability-plasticity trade-off studied in continual learning. Accordingly, we leverage \textit{Elastic Weight Consolidation} to constrain critical weights and \textit{Learning without Forgetting} to preserve output distributions. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that our approaches consistently improve early-exit performance, achieving higher accuracy over existing sequential training methods and significant performance speedups at low computational budgets.

</details>


### [136] [COPYCOP: Ownership Verification for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05360)
*Rahul Nandakumar,Deepayan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CopyCop is a GNN ownership verification framework that detects copycat relationships between GNNs, even when the attacker aligns embeddings via architecture/weight/transformation changes; it outperforms watermarking/fingerprinting and comes with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the risk that one GNN can be trained to imitate another's embeddings and then transformed to hide the linkage, undermining intellectual property protection for graph models.

Method: Introduce CopyCop algorithm that analyzes embeddings from two GNNs to determine independence vs. copycat origin, with theoretical guarantees; robust to adversarial transforms of embeddings and differing architectures/dimensions; evaluates across 14 datasets and 5 GNN architectures.

Result: CopyCop achieves high accuracy in identifying copied GNNs, remains robust against a broad class of embedding transformations, and maintains effectiveness across diverse datasets and model families; outperforms existing watermarking and fingerprinting approaches.

Conclusion: CopyCop provides a practical and theoretically grounded solution for graph ownership verification, demonstrating reliability and robustness in realistic adversarial settings; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Given two GNNs that output node embeddings, how can we determine if they were trained independently? An adversary could have trained one GNN specifically to mimic the other GNN's embeddings. To obscure this relationship between the GNNs, the adversarial GNN might then transform its output embeddings. The two GNNs could have different architectures, weights, and embedding dimensions, and the adversary can transform the embeddings. Despite these stringent conditions, our algorithm (named CopyCop) can identify such copycat GNNs, unlike existing watermarking and fingerprinting methods. We also provide theoretical guarantees for CopyCop. Finally, experiments on 14 datasets and 5 GNN architectures demonstrate that CopyCop is accurate and robust against a broad class of adversarial attacks and transformations. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CopyCop-Graph-Ownership-Verification-8143/README.md

</details>


### [137] [SPADE: Faster Drug Discovery by Learning from Sparse Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05370)
*Rahul Nandakumar,Ben Fauber,Deepayan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPADE is a ligand-selection algorithm for drug discovery that achieves high sample efficiency: on average ~40 tests find 10 high-quality ligands; in one-vs-one comparisons, SPADE outperforms deep learning and Bayesian optimization across more proteins with median improvements of 7-32% in sample efficiency; it is about 10x faster at scoring candidates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the very low pass rate (<5%) of candidate ligands in early drug discovery and the need to generalize to novel proteins with little prior data, by minimizing the number of tests required to discover high-quality ligands.

Method: Introduce SPADE, a novel approach that learns from sparse data and uses iterative ligand selection. The method emphasizes one-vs-one comparisons and aims to improve sample efficiency and scoring speed in ligand discovery.

Result: In one-vs-one experiments, SPADE achieves median improvements of 7–32% in sample efficiency on more proteins; requires ~40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands; scores candidate drugs about 10x faster than its closest competitor; dataset and code are available.

Conclusion: SPADE provides improved sample efficiency and faster scoring in ligand discovery under sparse data conditions, particularly advantageous for novel proteins; code and dataset are publicly available for replication.

Abstract: Drug discovery seeks molecules (ligands) that bind strongly and selectively to a target protein. However, fewer than 5% of candidate ligands pass the bar for even the early stages of drug discovery. Furthermore, we want methods that work for novel proteins for which we have no prior data. Starting from scratch, we have to iteratively select and test candidate ligands such that we find enough ligands of the desired quality in as few tests as possible. Our proposed algorithm, named SPADE, introduces a novel approach to ligand selection that requires only 40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands. In one-vs-one comparisons, SPADE outperforms deep learning and Bayesian optimization methods on more proteins, achieving median improvements of 7%-32% in sample efficiency. SPADE is also 10x faster than its closest competitor at scoring candidate drugs. Dataset and code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SPADE_Fast_Drug_Discovery_by_Learning_from_Sparse_Data-F028/README.md

</details>


### [138] [Neural Co-state Policies: Structuring Hidden States in Recurrent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05373)
*David Leeftink,Max Hinne,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recurrent policies in partial observability can be mapped to Pontryagin co-states; a PMP-based readout minimizes the Hamiltonian, and a co-state loss enforces this structure, yielding robust, interpretable control with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interpretability and robustness of memory-based policies under partial observability by linking hidden states to a principled optimal-control framework (PMP).

Method: Proves a formal mapping from standard recurrent architectures' latent representations to PMP co-states. Interprets the readout as Hamiltonian minimization. Introduces a PMP-derived co-state loss to align internal dynamics with PMP. Evaluates on partially observable DMControl tasks and tests robustness to sensor masking.

Result: Achieves comparable or improved performance relative to baselines on partially observable DMControl tasks and shows robustness to zero-shot sensor masking.

Conclusion: Framing RNNs as PMP-governed dynamical systems provides a principled approach for designing robust continuous-control policies and offers interpretable connections between internal states and optimal-control quantities.

Abstract: A key capability of intelligent agents is operating under partial observability: reasoning and acting effectively despite missing or incomplete state observations. While recurrent (memory-based) policies learned via reinforcement learning address this by encoding history into latent state representations, their internal dynamics remain uninterpretable black boxes. This paper establishes a formal link between these hidden states and the Pontryagin minimum principle (PMP) from optimal control. We demonstrate that for standard recurrent architectures, latent representations map directly to PMP co-states, which allows the readout layer to be interpreted as performing Hamiltonian minimization. Because standard reward maximization does not naturally discover this alignment, we introduce a PMP-derived co-state loss to explicitly structure the internal dynamics. Empirically, this approach matches or improves performance on partially observable DMControl tasks, and is robust against zero-shot out-of-distribution sensor masking. By framing recurrent networks as dynamic processes governed by the minimum principle, we provide a principled approach to designing robust continuous control policies.

</details>


### [139] [Conditional Diffusion Under Linear Constraints: Langevin Mixing and Information-Theoretic Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05387)
*Ahmad Aghapour,Erhan Bayraktar,Asaf Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We analyze zero-shot conditional sampling with diffusion models for linear inverse problems, show measurement-consistency alone can bias conditional samples, and derive a normal–tangent score decomposition with a dimension-free bound via conditional mutual information; propose a projected-Langevin initialization with guided reverse denoising, improving inpainting and super-resolution over a strong projection-based baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how to correctly sample from the conditional distribution over the unknown signal given partial measurements in diffusion-based, zero-shot inverse problems, and to quantify the limitations of projection-based consistency enforcement.

Method: Apply a normal–tangent decomposition of the score function under Gaussian noise: the observed-direction score is fixed by the measurement, while the tangent conditional score is unknown. Derive a bound on the sampling error in terms of a dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. Decompose total error into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch components. Propose an algorithm: projected-Langevin initialization followed by guided reverse denoising.

Result: Theoretical result: a dimension-free upper bound on the conditional-sampling error expressed via conditional mutual information; an information-theoretic decomposition into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch errors. Empirical result: the proposed method outperforms a strong projection-based baseline in inpainting and super-resolution tasks.

Conclusion: Measurement consistency alone does not fully determine the conditional distribution over feasible signals; the normal–tangent framework yields an actionable error bound and motivates a two-stage method (initialization plus guided reverse denoising) that improves zero-shot conditional sampling for linear inverse problems.

Abstract: We study zero-shot conditional sampling with pretrained diffusion models for linear inverse problems, including inpainting and super-resolution. In these problems, the observation determines only part of the unknown signal. The remaining degrees of freedom must be sampled according to the correct conditional data distribution. Existing projection-based samplers enforce measurement consistency by correcting the observed component during reverse diffusion. However, measurement consistency alone does not determine how probability mass should be distributed along the feasible set, and this can lead to biased conditional samples.
  We analyze this issue through a normal--tangent decomposition of the score function. For Gaussian noising, the observed-direction score is exactly determined by the measurement; only the tangent conditional score is unknown. We prove that the error from replacing this score by the unconditional tangent score is upper bounded by a dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. This gives an information-theoretic decomposition into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch errors. Motivated by the theory, we propose a projected-Langevin initialization followed by guided reverse denoising, which outperforms a strong projection-based baseline in inpainting and super-resolution experiments.

</details>


### [140] [Two-Stage Learned Decomposition for Scalable Routing on Multigraphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05389)
*Filip Rydin,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani,Balázs Kulcsár*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NEPF splits VRP policy into a node permutation stage and an edge selection stage for multigraphs, using a pre-encoding edge aggregation, a non-autoregressive edge-stage, and hierarchical RL to train jointly; it achieves state-of-the-art or competitive solution quality with significantly faster training and inference across six VRP variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural VRP methods are largely designed for Euclidean graphs or simple graphs and struggle with multigraphs where parallel edges encode diverse travel options; existing approaches face severe scalability issues. There is a need for scalable architectures that can decompose the policy and efficiently leverage multiple edge choices.

Method: Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF): decomposes routing into a node-permutation stage and an edge-selection stage. Introduces a pre-encoding edge aggregation to enable decomposition, a non-autoregressive edge-stage to improve speed, and a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework to train the two stages jointly. Evaluated on six VRP variants.

Result: NEPF matches or surpasses state-of-the-art solution quality across six VRP variants while offering significantly faster training and inference times.

Conclusion: NEPF demonstrates scalable, high-performing policy design for multigraph VRPs by factorizing the decision process into node and edge stages, enabled by edge aggregation and non-autoregressive edge decisions, with joint hierarchical RL training.

Abstract: Most neural methods for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) are limited to Euclidean settings or simple graphs. In this work, we instead consider multigraphs, where parallel edges represent distinct travel options with varying trade-offs (e.g., distance vs time). Few methods are designed for such formulations and those that do exist face major scalability issues. We mitigate these scalability issues via a Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) approach, which splits the routing policy into a node permutation stage and an edge selection stage. To enable the decomposition, we introduce a pre-encoding edge aggregation scheme and a non-autoregressive architecture for the edge stage, as well as a hierarchical reinforcement learning method to train the stages jointly. Our experiments across six VRP variants demonstrate that NEPF matches or outperforms the state-of-the-art in terms of solution quality, while being significantly faster in training and inference.

</details>


### [141] [Differentiable Parameter Optimization for DAEs with State-Dependent Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05395)
*Ion Matei,Maksym Zhenirovskyy,Anthony Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two gradient strategies for learning parameters in semi-explicit DAEs with state-dependent events: (i) automatic-differentiation-through-simulation (ADTS) that differentiates the algebraic solve via implicit function theorem and uses segmented differentiable integration across events; (ii) explicit discrete adjoint method that treats forward simulation as an event-split residual system and solves for Lagrange multipliers to obtain gradients. Both yield gradients for the forward-selected event path, valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings; comparison focuses on gradient interpretation, event-time handling, and implementation complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Differential-algebraic equations with state-dependent events arise in constrained systems with mode changes, impacts, or resets. Gradient-based parameter learning is difficult due to implicit algebraic variables, event-time dependency on parameters, and discontinuities from resets. A principled framework and practical gradient computation strategies are needed.

Method: Formulate learning as a constrained least-squares problem incorporating DAE dynamics, algebraic constraints, guards, and resets. Develop two complementary gradient computation strategies: (1) automatic-differentiation-through-simulation (ADTS): solve algebraic variables inside the vector field; differentiate the algebraic solve via the implicit-function theorem; handle events by segmented, differentiable integration. (2) explicit discrete adjoint: reinterpret forward simulation as an event-split residual system and compute gradients by solving for Lagrange multipliers associated with smooth segments and event residuals; treat residual terms as equality constraints rather than penalties.

Result: Provide gradients for the event path selected by the forward simulation and compare the two methods in terms of gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity. Both approaches are valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings.

Conclusion: The formulation clarifies that adjoint residuals are equality constraints, not penalties, and presents two complementary gradient strategies for semi-explicit DAEs with events. The choice between ADTS and discrete adjoint depends on interpretation, constraints, and implementation considerations, given the fixed-order and transversal-event assumptions.

Abstract: Differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) with state-dependent events arise in systems whose continuous dynamics are constrained by algebraic equations and interrupted by mode changes, switching logic, impacts, or state reinitializations. Gradient-based parameter learning for such systems is challenging because algebraic variables are implicitly defined, event times depend on the parameters, and reset maps introduce discontinuities. This paper studies differentiable parameter optimization for semi-explicit DAEs with events. We formulate the learning problem as a constrained least-squares problem with DAE dynamics, algebraic constraints, guard equations, and reset maps. We then develop two complementary gradient-computation strategies. The first is an automatic-differentiation-through-simulation method that solves algebraic variables inside the vector field, differentiates the algebraic solve using the implicit function theorem, and handles events through segmented differentiable integration. The second is an explicit discrete-adjoint method that represents the forward simulation as an event-split residual system and computes gradients by solving for the Lagrange multipliers of smooth-segment and event residuals. The formulation clarifies that residual terms in the adjoint method are equality constraints, not heuristic penalties. We compare the two approaches in terms of gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity. Both methods provide gradients for the event path selected by the forward simulation and are valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings.

</details>


### [142] [Information Theoretic Adversarial Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05415)
*Yiwei Zhang,Jeremiah Birrell,Reza Ebrahimi,Rouzbeh Behnia,Jason Pacheco,Elisa Bertino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WARDEN introduces a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs. It uses an f-divergence ambiguity set around the empirical distribution to reweight adversarial examples, leading to a log-sum-exp objective under KL divergence that emphasizes harder cases while keeping computational and utility costs comparable to existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts despite alignment efforts, and existing robust-training methods (CAT, CAPO, MixAT) are computationally expensive and hard to scale. A scalable, data-driven reweighting mechanism is needed to focus training on the hardest adversarial examples.

Method: Define an f-divergence ambiguity set around the empirical training distribution and optimize the worst-case adversarial loss within this divergence ball. Use a convex dual to obtain a log-sum-exp form (under KL divergence) with a dynamic parameter that controls reweighting strength. The approach yields an information-theoretic objective and remains comparable in cost to CAT/CAPO/MixAT baselines.

Result: WARDEN substantially reduces attack success rates across multiple LLMs and attack settings, while maintaining model utility. Computational costs are comparable to existing baselines (CAT, CAPO, MixAT), making it practical for scalable robust alignment.

Conclusion: WARDEN provides a practical, scalable distributionally robust training framework for LLM alignment that automatically emphasizes harder adversarial examples via an f-divergence ambiguity set, delivering robust performance with acceptable computational overhead.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to adversarial prompting despite advances in alignment and safety, often exhibiting harmful behaviors under novel attack strategies. While adversarial training can improve robustness, existing approaches are computationally expensive and difficult to scale. Recent continuous adversarial training methods, such as Continuous adversarial training (CAT) and Continuous Adversarial Preference Optimization (CAPO), address this challenge by leveraging gradient-based perturbations in the embedding space, enabling more efficient and expressive attacks. Building on this paradigm, we propose WARDEN, a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs that dynamically reweights adversarial examples through an f -divergence ambiguity set around the empirical training distribution. Our method optimizes the worst-case adversarial loss within a divergence ball around the empirical data distribution, automatically emphasizing harder adversarial examples. Using the convex dual formulation, the objective reduces to a log-sum-exp form under the KL divergence, with a dynamical parameter controlling the strength of reweighting. This study leads to a new class of information-theoretic objectives that significantly reduce attack success rates while maintaining model utility. Across multiple LLMs and attack settings, WARDEN substantially reduces attack success rates with computational and utility costs comparable to CAT-, CAPO-, and MixAT-based baselines, making it a practical approach for scalable robust alignment.

</details>


### [143] [Active Learning for Conditional Generative Compressed Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05435)
*Alexander DeLise,Nick Dexter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conditional generative compressed sensing: separating prompts for sensing and recovery; prompt-matched Christoffel sampling preserves the Christoffel complexity constant, while prompt mismatch incurs a compatibility penalty; prompts influence sampling distributions and recovery quality in subsampled Fourier imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how prompts affect both the design of the sensing distribution and the recovery model in a conditional generative CS framework, and to quantify these effects theoretically and empirically.

Method: Develop a conditional generative CS framework using prompt-conditioned generators (ReLU/Lipschitz). Distinguish two prompts: one shaping the sampling distribution, another defining the recovery model. Prove stable recovery bounds showing prompt-matched Christoffel sampling preserves the Christoffel complexity constant; derive a compatibility penalty for prompt mismatch. Validate with experiments using Stable Diffusion on subsampled Fourier measurements to observe prompt-induced changes in Christoffel sampling and recovery.

Result: Theoretical results establish stability bounds: with prompt-matched sampling, the Christoffel complexity constant remains as in existing near-optimal theory; prompt mismatch introduces an explicit penalty reducing recovery guarantees. Empirically, prompts reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and meaningfully affect image recovery performance.

Conclusion: Prompts should be treated as design variables with distinct roles in sensing, approximation, and recovery. Properly aligned prompts (sensing and recovery) yield stable recovery; misalignment incurs a penalty. This framework suggests optimizing prompts to balance sensing distribution and generative recovery for improved reconstruction.

Abstract: Generative compressed sensing uses the range of a pretrained generator as a nonlinear model for recovering structured signals from limited measurements. We study a conditional version of this problem for image recovery from subsampled Fourier measurements using prompt-conditioned generative models. Our framework separates two roles of conditioning: the prompt used to design the sampling distribution and the prompt used to define the recovery model. For ReLU and Lipschitz conditional generators, we prove stable recovery bounds showing that prompt-matched Christoffel sampling retains the same Christoffel complexity constant as existing near-optimal generative compressed sensing theory, while prompt mismatch incurs an explicit compatibility penalty. Experiments with Stable Diffusion show that prompts meaningfully reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and influence image recovery. Overall, our results suggest that prompts should be treated as design variables with distinct effects on sensing, approximation, and recovery.

</details>


### [144] [Adaptive Q-Chunking for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05544)
*Nandiraju Gireesh,Yuanliang Ju,He Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC) enables per-state selection of action chunk sizes in offline-to-online reinforcement learning by comparing chunk-wise advantages against a per-horizon baseline and discount normalization, yielding unbiased decisions when signals are weak and discriminative when a scale is advantageous. The approach includes theoretical guarantees and strong empirical gains on benchmarks and large-scale sequence-predictive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed chunk sizes in action chunking force a trade-off between reactive control (short chunks) and credit assignment over longer horizons (long chunks). Naive comparisons of learned chunk-values across scales suffer from discount-scale mismatch, biasing toward the shortest chunk and producing noise in low-signal regions.

Method: For each state, evaluate multiple chunk sizes k. Compute the advantage of each chunk relative to a per-horizon baseline and normalize by the discount factor, yielding a scale-aware selector. Choose the chunk with the largest normalized advantage. Train critics (potentially multiple) accordingly. Prove bounds on the selector’s noise immunity and on the value dominance of adaptive chunking over any fixed chunk size. Apply in offline-to-online RL and integrate with large-scale action-sequence predictors (VLA) to improve RoboCasa-GR1 tasks.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art offline and online success on OGBench and Robomimic. When used with large-scale VLA models predicting action sequences, it substantially boosts RoboCasa-GR1 performance.

Conclusion: Adaptive Q-Chunking provides a principled, theoretically grounded mechanism to adapt chunk size to the state, enabling better planning across varied contexts and yielding strong empirical gains. It generalizes to chunked RL settings and enhances large sequence-prediction models.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning with action chunking eliminates multi-step off-policy bias and enables temporally coherent exploration, but all existing methods use a fixed chunk size across every state. This is suboptimal: near contact events the agent needs short chunks for reactive control, while during free-space motion long chunks provide better credit assignment. The natural solution is to train critics for several chunk sizes and select the best one at each state, but naive comparison of learned critic values systematically collapses to the shortest chunk due to discount-scale mismatch, and degrades to noise in low-value states. We propose Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC), which resolves both failures by comparing the advantage of each chunk size relative to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by the discount factor. This criterion converts biased wrong answers into unbiased near-random choices when no genuine signal exists, and becomes discriminative when a particular scale enables better planning. We prove theoretical bounds on the advantage selector's noise immunity and on the value dominance of adaptive chunking over any fixed chunk size. We demonstrate that AQC achieves state-of-the-art offline and online success rates on OGBench and Robomimic, and can be applied to enhance the performance of large-scale VLA models that predict action sequences, significantly boosting performance on RoboCasa-GR1 tasks.

</details>


### [145] [On Semantic Loss Fine-Tuning Approach for Preventing Model Collapse in Causal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05438)
*Pratik Deshmukh,Atirek Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semantic loss with graph-based constraints and dynamic lambda scheduling mitigates catastrophic fine-tuning collapse in causal reasoning tasks, yielding stable, context-dependent reasoning and substantial gains over collapsed baselines across transitivity and d-separation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard fine-tuning of transformers on causal reasoning tasks often collapses to trivial solutions, misrepresenting model capabilities. A semantic loss aims to enforce genuine reasoning by embedding graph-based logical constraints.

Method: Introduce a semantic loss with graph-based logical constraints that encode causal relationships, combined with a dynamic lambda scheduling strategy to balance the semantic loss with the primary objective during training. Evaluated on transitivity and d-separation tasks using Gemma 270M across multiple variants, with extensive evaluation and adversarial testing.

Result: Semantic models achieve 70.4% accuracy on transitivity and 68.6% on d-separation, with stable, context-dependent predictions, representing a 42.7% improvement over collapsed baselines. Adversarial evaluation on 1,000 samples yields 67–70% accuracy for semantic models, while collapsed models fail catastrophically within 43–71%. Benchmarking on 200,000+ samples across five model variants supports the approach.

Conclusion: Semantic loss is essential for achieving stable causal reasoning in transformers, not optional, as demonstrated by comprehensive benchmarking and adversarial tests.

Abstract: Standard fine-tuning of transformer models on causal reasoning tasks leads to catastrophic model collapse, where models learn trivial solutions such as always predicting "Yes" or "No" regardless of input structure. We demonstrate that fine-tuning Gemma 270M on transitivity and d-separation tasks without semantic loss results in 100% collapse rate, with models achieving misleadingly high accuracy (73.9%) while learning no causal reasoning. We propose a semantic loss function with graph-based logical constraints and dynamic lambda scheduling that prevents this collapse. Our approach achieves 70.4% accuracy on transitivity tasks and 68.6% on d-separation tasks with stable, context-dependent predictions, representing a 42.7% improvement over collapsed baselines. Adversarial evaluation on 1,000 structural reasoning samples shows semantic models achieve 67-70% accuracy while collapsed models fail catastrophically at 43-71%. We validate our findings through comprehensive benchmarking on 200,000+ evaluation samples across five model variants, demonstrating that semantic loss is essential and not optional, for stable causal reasoning in transformers.

</details>


### [146] [Robustness of Graph Self-Supervised Learning to Real-World Noise: A Case Study on Text-Driven Biomedical Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05463)
*Othmane Kabal,Mounira Harzallah,Fabrice Guillet,Hideaki Takeda,Ryutaro Ichise*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework NATD-GSSL for evaluating GSSL on noisy, text-driven graphs; shows robustness varies by task and GNN, with practical guidance and up to 7% gains over LM baselines; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world GSSL uses automatically extracted knowledge graphs that are noisy; existing robustness studies rely on synthetic perturbations; need evaluation on text-driven graphs for unsupervised term typing.

Method: Propose NATD-GSSL integrating automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL; dual-graph protocol comparing noisy MedMentions-derived graph with clean UMLS reference; evaluate across pretext tasks, GNN architectures, and contrastive objectives.

Result: Relation reconstruction sensitive to noise and benefits from defined schemas; feature reconstruction robust; contrastive objectives less impacted by noise but depend on downstream task alignment; bidirectional relational message passing outperforms on noisy graphs, unidirectional on clean; up to 7% improvement over LM baselines.

Conclusion: NATD-GSSL offers practical guidance for deploying GSSL on real-world noisy graphs and highlights how graph construction, schema clarity, and GNN choice influence robustness; code and benchmarks provided to facilitate future research.

Abstract: Graph Self-Supervised Learning (GSSL) offers a powerful paradigm for learning graph representations without labeled data. However, existing work assumes clean, manually curated graphs. Recent advances in NLP enable the large-scale automatic extraction of knowledge graphs from text, opening new opportunities for GSSL while introducing substantial real-world noise. This type of noise remains largely unexplored, as prior robustness studies typically rely on synthetic perturbations. To address this gap, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of GSSL methods on text-driven graphs for unsupervised term typing. We introduce Noise-Aware Text-Driven Graph GSSL (NATD-GSSL), a unified framework that combines automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL. Our evaluation follows a dual-graph protocol that contrasts a noisy graph derived from MedMentions with a clean Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) reference graph, aligned through a shared gold standard. Our results reveal variability in robustness across both pretext tasks and Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures. Relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from well-defined schemas, whereas feature reconstruction is considerably more robust, achieving performance comparable to clean-graph settings. Contrastive objectives are generally less affected by noise but depend strongly on alignment with downstream tasks. GNN architecture also plays a critical role: bidirectional relational message-passing designs are better suited to noisy, text-driven graphs, while unidirectional relational ones perform best on clean graphs. Overall, NATD-GSSL provides practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs and achieves up to a 7\% improvement over pretrained language model baselines. All code and benchmarks are publicly available at https://github.com/OthmaneKabal/MC2GAE.

</details>


### [147] [A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Knowledge Graph Construction Methods and Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05476)
*Othmane Kabal,Mounira Harzallah,Fabrice Guillet,Hideaki Takeda,Ryutaro Ichise*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dual-purpose benchmark to evaluate GNNs on noisy text-derived graphs and the quality of graph construction methods, using a biomedical corpus with two automatically generated graphs and an expert reference graph, plus a standardized evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge graphs mined from text are noisy and fragmented, which confounds GNN performance. A need exists to separate model performance from graph quality and to assess construction methods in a reproducible benchmark.

Method: Construct two automatic graphs from different extraction methods on a single biomedical corpus, plus a high-quality expert-curated reference graph as an upper bound. Evaluate semi-supervised node classification and provide a standardized, extensible evaluation framework for integrating new graph extraction methods and learning models.

Result: The benchmark enables controlled comparison of graph construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness to graph quality, with the reference graph setting an upper bound for performance. It offers reproducible evaluation and extensibility for future methods and models.

Conclusion: The proposed design supports robust assessment of both GNNs and graph construction pipelines in biomedical knowledge graphs, promoting fair comparisons and reproducible research.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs automatically constructed from text are increasingly used in real-world applications. However, their inherent noise, fragmentation, and semantic inconsistencies significantly affect the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on downstream tasks. Assessing their performance and robustness remains difficult, as it is often unclear whether observed results stem from the learning model or from the quality of the constructed graph itself. In this work, we introduce a dual-purpose benchmark designed to jointly evaluate (i) the performance of GNNs on noisy, text-derived graphs and (ii) the effectiveness of graph construction methods on a downstream task. The benchmark is built in the biomedical domain from a single textual corpus and includes two automatically constructed graphs generated using different extraction methods, alongside a high-quality reference graph curated by experts that serves as an upper performance bound. This design enables controlled comparison of construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness through semi-supervised node classification. We further provide a standardized, reproducible, and extensible evaluation framework, facilitating the integration of new graph extraction methods and learning models.

</details>


### [148] [GRALIS: A Unified Canonical Framework for Linear Attribution Methods via Riesz Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05480)
*Raimondo Fanale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRALIS provides a unified, canonical framework for additive, linear, continuous attributions on L^2 that subsumes SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM, with seven formal guarantees; it excludes nonlinear functionals and offers strong completeness and multi-scale aggregation; preliminary validation on BreaKHis shows modest improvements and high consistency; a companion paper will expand empirical comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a formal, canonical representation theory for attribution methods in XAI, enabling direct comparison and provable guarantees across methods.

Method: Developed GRALIS: a representation-theoretic framework in which attribution functionals on L^2(Q, mu) are represented uniquely by (Q, w, Delta) per a Riesz-type theorem. Establish seven formal theorems (T1–T7) about completeness, convergence, interaction values, Hoeffding/ANOVA, Sobol generalization, and multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS). Introduce GRALIS-SIV via Mobius transform and a multi-scale aggregation with minimum-variance weights.

Result: Defines a broad class of additive, linear, continuous attributions that includes SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM; excludes nonlinear functionals (e.g., standard GradCAM, attention maps). Provides seven theoretical guarantees lacking in individual methods. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis (DenseNet-121) reports deletion AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL 0.762±0.109, sparsity 0.39; plans for extended comparisons in a companion paper.

Conclusion: GRALIS offers a rigorous, axiomatic framework for attributions with simultaneous guarantees on completeness, locality, interactions, and multi-scale aggregation, surpassing existing methods on most axioms; real-world utility will be further tested via extended experiments in a subsequent paper.

Abstract: The main XAI attribution methods for deep neural networks -- GradCAM, SHAP, LIME, Integrated Gradients -- operate on separate theoretical foundations and are not formally comparable. We present GRALIS (Gradient-Riesz Averaged Locally-Integrated Shapley), a mathematical framework establishing a representation theory for attributions: every additive, linear, and continuous attribution functional on L^2(Q,mu) admits a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta), proved necessary by the Riesz Representation Theorem. This class encompasses SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM, but excludes nonlinear functionals such as standard GradCAM or attention maps. Seven formal theorems provide simultaneous guarantees absent in any individual method: (T1) necessary canonical form; (T2) exact completeness; (T3) Monte Carlo convergence O(1/sqrt(m))+O(1/k); (T4) exact Shapley Interaction Values; (T5) Hoeffding ANOVA decomposition; (T6) Sobol sensitivity generalization; (T7) multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS) with minimum-variance weights. An algebraic appendix justifies the GRALIS-SIV correspondence via the Mobius transform without circularity. GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties vs. 2.5-6/14 for individual methods, including completeness, sensitivity, locality, order-k interactions and optimal multi-scale aggregation simultaneously. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis (1,187 histology images, DenseNet-121) reports deletion faithfulness AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL = 0.762+/-0.109 and sparsity index 0.39. Extended comparison with baseline XAI methods is planned for a companion paper.

</details>


### [149] [Approximate Next Policy Sampling: Replacing Conservative Target Policy Updates in Deep RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05481)
*Dillon Sandhu,Ronald Parr*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS) to align training data with the next policy, via Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API) and Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), enabling larger target policy updates with safety guarantees under stability criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the RL chicken-and-egg problem where value estimation must be accurate for the unknown, update-dependent state distribution; conservative updates limit policy change, so ANPS aims to adjust training distribution instead.

Method: Introduce SV-API: keep target policy fixed while a behavioral policy gathers experience and only commit to a new policy once convergence is achieved; derive safety guarantees based on stability; apply to PPO to obtain SV-PPO.

Result: SV-API applied to PPO yields SV-PPO, achieving equal or better performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks, while enabling substantially larger target policy updates.

Conclusion: ANPS is a viable alternative to conservative updates for safe RL; SV-API/SV-PPO demonstrate feasibility and effectiveness of training data distributions that approximate the next policy, with potential for broader applicability.

Abstract: We revisit a classic "chicken-and-egg" problem in reinforcement learning: to safely improve a policy, the value function must be accurate on the state-visitation distribution of the updated policy. That distribution over states is unknown and cannot be sampled for the purposes of training the value function. Conservative updates solve this problem, but at the cost of shrinking the policy update. This paper explores an alternative solution, Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS), which addresses the problem by modifying the training distribution rather than constraining the policy update. ANPS is satisfied if the distribution of the training data approximates that of the next policy. To demonstrate the feasibility and efficacy of ANPS, we introduce Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API). SV-API modifies the standard approximate policy iteration loop to hold the target policy fixed while an iteratively updated behavioral policy gathers relevant experience. It only commits to a new policy once a convergence criterion has been met. If certain stability criteria are met, the update is guaranteed to be safe; otherwise, it remains no less safe than standard approximate policy iteration. Applying SV-API to PPO yields Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), which matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates. These results demonstrate the viability of ANPS as a new solution to this classic challenge in RL.

</details>


### [150] [Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06246)
*Jan-Hendrik Ewering,Kathrin Flaßkamp,Niklas Wahlström,Thomas B. Schön,Thomas Seel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) to learn dynamics from discrete position data by embedding the Lagrange-d'Alembert structure into GP conditioning; enables data-efficient, physically consistent long-horizon predictions with uncertainty, demonstrated on synthetic and real-world systems including a hysteretic soft robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for data-efficient, physically consistent dynamical models that can learn from sparse position measurements without velocities, leveraging geometric structure to prevent energy drift and ensure stable long-term predictions.

Method: Define linear operators for GP conditioning derived from discrete forced Euler–Lagrange equations and variational discretization; preserve Lagrange–d'Alembert principle when no external forces; enable learning from position snapshots; handle external forces; provide uncertainty quantification.

Result: Demonstrates data efficiency and generalization; stable long-term predictions; uncertainty quantification; validated on synthetic and real-world experiments; includes a real-world soft robot with hysteresis.

Conclusion: LGPs successfully learn physically consistent dynamics from sparse positional data, with reliable uncertainty and long-horizon stability; potential for broader applications and extension to other geometric dynamical systems.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

</details>


### [151] [A Robust Foundation Model for Conservation Laws: Injecting Context into Flux Neural Operators via Recurrent Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05488)
*Taeyoung Kim,Joon-Hyuk Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Augments Flux Neural Operator with ViT-based context injection via a hypernetwork to adapt operator parameters from a short temporal context, enabling PDE-free inference and robust long-time predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve neural operators for conservation laws by enabling PDE-agnostic learning, enhancing robustness, generalization, and extrapolation to unseen fluxes.

Method: Hypernetwork architecture: a recurrent Vision Transformer encodes solution dynamics over a finite time window and outputs context-conditioned parameters for the Flux NO; integrates with the finite volume method; does not require explicit governing equations or PDE coefficients.

Result: Maintains Flux NO's robustness and long-time prediction advantages; achieves reliable numerical solutions across a broad class of conservative systems, including unseen fluxes; code released.

Conclusion: ViT-based context injection enhances PDE-agnostic neural operators, preserving numerical stability and accuracy while broadening applicability to conservation laws, with open-source implementation.

Abstract: We propose an architecture that augments the Flux Neural Operator (Flux NO), which combines the classical finite volume method (FVM) with neural operators, with ViT-based context injection. Our model is formulated as a hypernetwork: it extracts solution dynamics over a finite temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates the parameters of a context-conditioned neural operator. This enables the model to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit access to the governing equation or PDE coefficients. Experimentally, we show that the proposed method preserves the robustness, generalization ability, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, while delivering reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including previously unseen fluxes. Our code is available at https://github.com/xx257xx/CONTEXT_FLUX_NO.

</details>


### [152] [MEMOA: Massive Mixtures of Online Agents via Mean-Field Decentralized Nash Equilibria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05492)
*Xuwei Yang,David B. Emerson,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Anastasis Kratsios*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unique optimal decentralized policy for mean-field federated learning under a minimax worst-client objective; proves convergence to the centralized Nash policy in the large-population limit; introduces online weighting to improve ensemble predictions; empirical results show gains over greedy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability bottlenecks in federated learning by enabling autonomous agents that use only local state plus a mean-field summary, while ensuring fairness/robustness via a worst-case (worst-client) criterion.

Method: Derive a closed-form, unique decentralized policy under a minimax criterion focused on the weakest agent; analyze its asymptotic convergence to the Nash-optimal centralized policy as population grows; incorporate an online weighting mechanism to optimally mix client predictions and improve mean accuracy; validate theoretically and empirically.

Result: The policy is uniquely optimal under the worst-client regret; in the large-population limit it converges to the Nash-optimal centralized policy; the online weighting yields better mean predictions; experiments show the decentralized policy outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

Conclusion: A scalable decentralized mean-field approach can achieve near-centralized optimum as the population grows and can further boost ensemble performance via online weighting; the framework provides theoretical guarantees and practical benefits for large-scale federated learning.

Abstract: In the modern age of large-scale AI, federated learning has become an increasingly important tool for training large populations of AI agents; however, its computational and communication costs can rapidly fail to scale with the number of agents. This is precisely where decentralized agentic strategies shine: each agent acts autonomously, using only its own state together with a minimal summary of the ensemble, namely the mean-field. We derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form. Optimality is characterized through a worst-client/minimax criterion: minimizing the under-performer regret, namely the maximal online cost incurred by the weakest agent in the ensemble. We further prove that the resulting decentralized policy asymptotically converges, in the large-population limit, to the Nash-optimal centralized policy, whose direct computation is not scalable. We use an online weighting mechanism to optimize the server-computed mixture of client predictions, thereby improving the mean prediction in addition to the previously optimized weakest-client prediction. Numerical experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that our decentralized policy typically outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

</details>


### [153] [Shortcut Solutions Learned by Transformers Impair Continual Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05495)
*William T. Redman,Erik C. Johnson,Brian Robinson*

Main category: cs.LG
