TL;DR: Extends LEGO to continual learning to compare BERT (feedforward) and ALBERT (recurrent) Transformers on CL; finds BERT tends to shortcut solutions with poor transfer, while ALBERT forms a For loop-like strategy yielding better CL; both struggle with cross-experience compositional generalization; cross-experience data sharing helps ALBERT but not BERT; suggests architecture-induced inductive biases shape CL behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Transformer architectures influence continual learning and compositional generalization, by adapting the LEGO framework to continual settings and comparing BERT vs ALBERT.

Method: Introduce continual LEGO and evaluate feedforward (BERT) and recurrent (ALBERT) Transformers on CL tasks that require composing knowledge across experiences; examine transferability, shortcut vs loop-like solutions, and the impact of training strategies that merge data across experiences.

Result: BERT learns shortcut solutions that limit generalization and transfer; ALBERT appears to solve a For loop-like strategy, improving CL; both models fail on CL tasks requiring composition across experiences; ALBERT’s performance can be rescued by training strategies that combine data across experiences, BERT’s cannot; suggests ALBERT’s inductive bias is more suitable for CL.

Conclusion: The recurrent ALBERT architecture may have an inductive bias favorable for continual learning, motivating further study on how Transformer architecture shapes the computational solutions that emerge in CL tasks and how training regimes interact with these biases.

Abstract: Identifying and exploiting common features across domains is at the heart of the human ability to make analogies, and is believed to be crucial for the ability to continually learn. To do this successfully, general and flexible computational strategies must be developed. While the extent to which Transformer neural network models can perform compositional reasoning has been the subject of intensive recent investigation, little work has been done to systematically understand how well these models can leverage their representations to learn new, related experiences. To address this gap, we expand the previously developed Learning Equality and Group Operations (LEGO) framework to a continual learning (CL) setting ("continual LEGO"). Using this continual LEGO experimental paradigm, we study the capability of feedforward and recurrent Transformer models to perform CL. We find that BERT, a canonical feedforward Transformer model, learns shortcut solutions that limits its ability to generalize and prevents strong forward transfer to new experiences. In contrast, we find evidence supporting the hypothesis that ALBERT, a recurrent version of BERT, learns a For loop-esque solution, which leads to better CL performance. When applying BERT and ALBERT models to a CL setting that requires composition across experiences, we find that both model families fail. Our investigation suggests that ALBERT models can have their performance drop rescued by use of training strategies that combine data across experiences, but this is not true for BERT models, where a detrimental shortcut solution becomes entrenched with initial training. Our results demonstrate that the recurrent ALBERT model may have an inductive bias better suited for CL and motivate future investigation of the interplay between Transformer architecture and computational solutions that emerge in modern models and tasks.

</details>


### [154] [Online Localized Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05497)
*Yuheng Lai,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OLCP provides online, covariate-localized conformal prediction with online bandwidth selection (OLCP-Hedge), achieving valid long-run coverage with narrower sets under covariate heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online learning/time-series contexts violate exchangeability, making global conformal calibration inefficient. Localized, adaptive calibration is needed to respect covariate heterogeneity while preserving validity.

Method: OLCP combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization. OLCP-Hedge selects localization bandwidths via online expert aggregation using constrained online convex optimization.

Result: Both methods come with coverage guarantees. Simulations and real-data experiments show valid long-run coverage and narrower prediction sets compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Localization-aware online conformal prediction improves efficiency under covariate heterogeneity while maintaining validity; Hedge-based bandwidth selection mitigates sensitivity to the localization parameter and supports practical deployment.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a framework that provides valid uncertainty quantification for general models with exchangeable data. However, in the online learning and time-series settings, exchangeability is not satisfied. Existing online conformal methods, such as adaptive conformal inference (ACI), can achieve long-run validity, yet they remain inefficient under covariate heterogeneity because they rely on global calibration. We propose \emph{Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP)}, which combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization to better reflect heterogeneity. To reduce sensitivity to the localization bandwidth, we further develop \emph{OLCP-Hedge}, which performs bandwidth selection as an online expert aggregation problem using a constrained online convex optimization framework. Importantly, we provide coverage guarantees for both algorithms and demonstrate through simulations and real-data experiments that the proposed methods attain valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing baselines.

</details>


### [155] [Non-Myopic Active Feature Acquisition via Pathwise Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05511)
*Linus Aronsson,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NM-PPG: a non-myopic pathwise policy gradient method for active feature acquisition (AFA) using continuous relaxation and straight-through rollout to optimize feature collection trajectories end-to-end, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature acquisition is costly; treating AFA as a POMDP enables sequential decisions. A non-myopic, low-variance gradient method is needed to optimize long-horizon acquisition policies and align training with deployment.

Method: Introduce NM-PPG, a non-myopic pathwise policy gradient method with a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process to enable pathwise gradients, coupled with a straight-through rollout that uses hard acquisitions in the forward pass but soft relaxation during backward pass. Regularization via entropy and staged temperature sharpening stabilizes training.

Result: Experimental results on synthetic and real-world data show NM-PPG achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed NM-PPG framework provides effective end-to-end optimization for AFA with reduced gradient variance, better alignment with deployment, and strong empirical gains; the approach can generalize to other sequential decision problems involving costly feature acquisition.

Abstract: Active feature acquisition (AFA) considers prediction problems in which features are costly to obtain and the learner adaptively decides which feature values to acquire for each instance and when to stop and predict. AFA can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), which naturally admits a sequential decision-making perspective. In this paper, we present non-myopic pathwise policy gradients (NM-PPG), a new AFA method built around this formulation. We introduce a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process that enables pathwise gradients through the full acquisition trajectory, avoiding the high variance of standard score-function policy gradients while allowing end-to-end optimization of a non-myopic acquisition policy. To better align training with deployment, we further develop a straight-through rollout scheme that follows hard feature acquisitions in the forward pass while backpropagating through the corresponding soft relaxation in the backward pass. We stabilize optimization with entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that NM-PPG yields superior performance relative to state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

</details>


### [156] [OpenG2G: A Simulation Platform for AI Datacenter-Grid Runtime Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05519)
*Jae-Won Chung,Zhirui Liang,Yanyong Mao,Jiasi Chen,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Vladimir Dvorkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenG2G is a modular co-simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid coordination that enables evaluation of control strategies and their impact on grid flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI compute growth and datacenter buildouts strain electrical grids, causing interconnection delays; rapid datacenter power flexibility and coordinated control are needed to ease grid constraints.

Method: Develop OpenG2G with a datacenter backend using real production AI service measurements, a grid backend based on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface to connect them; supports classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers in a modular, extensible architecture.

Result: OpenG2G can answer a broad set of coordination questions and quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and grid-coordination outcomes; demonstrated with realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

Conclusion: The platform enables flexible, comparative evaluation of datacenter-grid coordination strategies and supports design of effective, field-relevant coordination mechanisms.

Abstract: AI's growing compute demand and new datacenter buildouts present major capacity and reliability challenges for the electricity grid, leading to multi-year interconnection delays for new datacenters and bottlenecking AI growth. To ease this strain, datacenters increasingly offer rapid power flexibility in response to grid signals, where the datacenter can increase or decrease its power consumption by adapting its workload in real time.
  In order to understand the impact of large datacenters on the grid and to facilitate the design of effective coordination strategies, we build OpenG2G, a simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid runtime coordination. We show that OpenG2G is capable of answering a wide range of coordination questions by allowing users to implement and compare various control paradigms (including classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers), and quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes. This versatility is enabled by OpenG2G's modular and extensible architecture: a datacenter backend driven by real measurements of production-grade AI services, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface that closes the loop between them. We describe the design of OpenG2G and demonstrate its usefulness through realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

</details>


### [157] [Bayesian Rain Field Reconstruction using Commercial Microwave Links and Diffusion Model Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05520)
*Badr Moufad,Albina Ilina,Hai Victor Habi,Salem Lahlou,Yazid Janati,Hagit Messer,Eric Moulines*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bayesian rainfield reconstruction from Commercial Microwave Links (CMLs) using diffusion-model priors, enabling training-free posterior sampling and improving performance over censored Gaussian-process baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CMLs provide dense spatial coverage but yield path-integrated measurements that complicate ground-level rainfall reconstruction. Treating CMLs as point sensors and neglecting line integration degrades performance, especially under heterogeneous rainfall. Diffusion models offer high-fidelity, realistic spatial priors for rainfall fields.

Method: Formulate rainfall estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem with a diffusion-model prior; enable training-free posterior sampling using Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods; compare against censored Gaussian-process priors; evaluate on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Result: Diffusion-model priors better preserve key rainfall statistics than censored Gaussian processes; the Bayesian framework allows training-free posterior sampling via various methods and yields consistent improvements over established CML-based reconstruction baselines on both synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: Diffusion-model priors enhance CML-based rainfall field reconstruction and enable versatile posterior sampling without training; the approach improves robustness to heterogeneous precipitation and demonstrates a viable path for Bayesian inverse problems in geophysical rainfall sensing.

Abstract: Commercial Microwave Links (CMLs) offer dense spatial coverage for rainfall sensing but produce path-integrated measurements that make accurate ground-level reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically oversimplify CMLs as point sensors and neglect line integration relating rainfall to signal attenuation, resulting in degraded performance under heterogeneous precipitation. In this work, we view rain field reconstruction as a Bayesian inverse problem with Diffusion Models (DMs) as high-fidelity spatial priors. We show that diffusion models better preserve key rainfall statistics compared to censored Gaussian processes. Framing rainfall estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem with a DM prior enables training-free posterior sampling using a broad family of methods, including Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over established CML-based reconstruction baselines.

</details>


### [158] [MOSAIC: Module Discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal Learning for Scientific Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05524)
*Shicheng Fan,Nour Elhendawy,Jianle Sun,Ke Fang,Kun Zhang,Yihang Wang,Lu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sparse temporal VAE (MOSAIC) enables identifiability of latent factors in causal representation learning while mapping each latent to a sparse, interpretable subset of observed variables, yielding module-level interpretability for scientific time series; with guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifiability in CRL does not guarantee interpretability; scientific domains require semantically meaningful latent structure. The goal is to transfer observation semantics to latent representations by aligning latent factors with observed variables.

Method: Introduce MOSAIC, a sparse temporal VAE that combines regime-conditioned temporal-variation identifiability with support recovery over observed variables. Latents are identified via regime-dependent temporal variation; each latent is decoded by a sparse additive decoder to recover an associated sparse set of observations, yielding module-level interpretability. Theoretical results include identifiability of ANOVA main-effect supports under general smooth mixing and finite-sample guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant.

Result: Empirical demonstrations show MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, the Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

Conclusion: MOSAIC provides a principled pathway to interpretable latent structure in scientific time series by integrating identifiable causal representation learning with semantically meaningful module discovery, supported by theoretical guarantees and broad empirical validation.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) seeks to recover latent variables with identifiability guarantees, typically up to permutation and component-wise reparameterization under appropriate assumptions. However, identifiability does not imply interpretability: latent semantics are typically assigned post hoc by alignment with known ground-truth factors. This limitation is particularly acute in scientific time series, where underlying mechanisms are unknown and discovering interpretable structure is a primary goal. In contrast, scientific observations (such as residue-pair distances, climate indices, or process sensors) are inherently semantic, as they correspond to named physical quantities. This raises a key question: can the interpretability of observations be transferred to the identifiable latent space? We propose MOSAIC (Module discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal learning), a sparse temporal VAE that integrates temporal CRL identifiability with support recovery over observed variables. MOSAIC identifies latent variables via regime-conditioned temporal variation, and recovers for each latent a sparse set of associated observations through an additive decoder, yielding module-level interpretability. We show that ANOVA main-effect supports are identifiable under general smooth mixing functions, and provide finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant. Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, the Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

</details>


### [159] [Energy Generative Modeling: A Lyapunov-based Energy Matching Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05530)
*Yixuan Wang,Wenqian Xue,Warren E. Dixon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies training and sampling in static scalar energy models via a nonlinear control view on Wasserstein space; derives finite-step stopping for Langevin, shows no Lyapunov certificate for deterministic gradient flow, and proves additive energies preserve Gibbs measures and Lyapunov certificates; connects to barrier methods and contraction metrics with synthetic-distribution validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a unified, autonomous framework for static scalar energy generative models that merges training and sampling and enables analytic control-theoretic guarantees and design tools through the density transport viewpoint.

Method: Cast density transport on Wasserstein space as a nonlinear control problem using KL divergence as a Lyapunov function. Derive (i) a finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling, (ii) nonexistence of a Lyapunov certificate for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy, and (iii) that additive composition of trained scalar energies preserves a Gibbs invariant measure and maintains the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Discuss implications for barrier functions and contraction metrics. Validate predictions with experiments on synthetic distributions.

Result: Two main analytic results: a finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and the absence of a Lyapunov certificate for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy landscape; additive energy composition preserves the Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the Lyapunov certificate; methodological bridge to nonlinear control tools with experimental validation on synthetic distributions.

Conclusion: The reformulation unites static-energy generative modeling with nonlinear control theory, enabling barrier-function design for constrained generation and contraction-based acceleration for sampling, with empirical validation supporting the theoretical claims.

Abstract: Generative models based on static scalar energy functions represent an emerging paradigm in which a single time independent potential drives sample generation through its gradient field, eliminating the need for time conditioning entirely. We unify the training and sampling phases of this paradigm, conventionally treated as separate procedures, within a single framework: density transport on the Wasserstein space, cast as a nonlinear control problem in which the Kullback Leibler (KL) divergence serves as a Lyapunov function. Training and sampling are then two instances of this same master dynamics, differing only in initial condition. Within this autonomous framework we develop two analytic results. First, since the Lyapunov certificate is asymptotic, we derive a finite step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and prove that no Lyapunov certificate exists for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy landscape. Second, the reformulation brings the toolkit of nonlinear control theory to bear on static scalar energy generative modeling, that is, we show that additive composition of trained scalar energies retains an explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Beyond these immediate results, this reformulation bridges static scalar energy generative models with the full toolkit of nonlinear control theory, opening the door to barrier functions for constrained generation and contraction metrics for accelerated sampling. Experiments on synthetic distributions validate the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [160] [Adversarial Graph Neural Network Benchmarks: Towards Practical and Fair Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05534)
*Tran Gia Bao Ngo,Zulfikar Alom,Federico Errica,Murat Kantarcioglu,Cuneyt Gurcan Akcora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large-scale benchmark showing that standardized evaluation protocols drastically affect observed adversarial GNN robustness; re-evaluates 7 attacks and 8 defenses across 6 datasets, revealing that target selection and training procedures heavily influence results and advocating for standardized, robust evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inconsistent experimental setups across adversarial GNN studies hinder comparability and may lead to conflicting conclusions; a fair benchmark is needed to assess true robustness.

Method: Re-evaluation in poisoning and evasion settings within a unified framework. The study re-tests seven widely used attacks and eight defenses across six graph datasets, totaling ~453k experiments. It also analyzes how factors like target node selection and the attacked model's training process influence attack effectiveness.

Result: There are substantial differences in attack performance under a fair and robust procedure. Previously overlooked factors can completely distort performance insights, underscoring the importance of standardized evaluations in adversarial graph ML.

Conclusion: This work advocates for standardized, fair, and robust evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research and provides a framework to enable reproducible, comparable studies across attacks, defenses, and datasets.

Abstract: Adversarial learning and the robustness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are topics of widespread interest in the machine learning community, as documented by the number of adversarial attacks and defenses designed for these purposes. While a rigorous evaluation of these adversarial methods is necessary to understand the robustness of GNNs in real-world applications, we posit that many works in the literature do not share the same experimental settings, leading to ambiguous and potentially contradictory scientific conclusions. In this benchmark, we demonstrate the importance of adopting fair, robust, and standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research. We perform a comprehensive re-evaluation of seven widely used attacks and eight recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion scenarios, across six popular graph datasets. Our study spans over 453,000 experiments conducted within a unified framework. We observe substantial differences in adversarial attack performance when evaluated under a fair and robust procedure. Our findings reveal that previously overlooked factors, such as target node selection and the training process of the attacked model, have a profound impact on attack effectiveness, to the extent of completely distorting performance insights. These results underscore the urgent need for standardized evaluations in adversarial graph machine learning.

</details>


### [161] [Towards Scalable One-Step Generative Modeling for Autoregressive Dynamical System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05540)
*Tianyue Yang,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MeLISA delivers fast, pixel-space autoregressive surrogates with blockwise updates and consistency losses, achieving long-horizon turbulence statistics without latent encoders or diffusion at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional turbulent dynamics require surrogates that are both accurate in the short term and faithful to long-horizon statistics. Neural operators drift over long rollouts; diffusion-based surrogates add inference cost and complexity. A latent-free, efficient, scalable solution is needed.

Method: A latent-free autoregressive model built on MeanFlow in pixel space. Uses a blockwise stochastic transition kernel to generate each forecast block with a single model evaluation, avoiding encoders and iterative denoising. Stabilizes long rollouts via Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective and Time Increment Consistency loss. Evaluated with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on 256×256 Kolmogorov flow and 192×192 turbulent channel-flow. 

Result: MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term forecasting and long-horizon statistics (energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, mixing-rate dynamics). Inference speeds are comparable to, or faster than, neural operators. Compact models use 3.7–5.7M parameters; DiT variants scale up to ~150M. Demonstrates strong parameter efficiency and scalable performance.

Conclusion: MeLISA provides efficient rollout and improved long-horizon statistical fidelity for high-dimensional turbulent dynamics, with a latent-free, pixel-space design and scalable backbones that deliver competitive or superior performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Fast surrogate modeling for high-dimensional physical dynamics requires more than low short-term error: useful models must roll out efficiently while preserving the statistical structure of long trajectories. Neural operators provide inexpensive autoregressive forecasts but can drift in turbulent regimes, whereas rolling diffusion and latent generative surrogates can represent stochastic transitions at the cost of multi-step denoising, noise-schedule design, or auxiliary compression models. We propose MeanFlow Long-term Invariant Spatiotemporal Consistency Autoregressive Models (MeLISA), a latent-free autoregressive generative surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow. MeLISA defines a blockwise stochastic transition kernel that generates each forecast block with a single model evaluation, avoiding latent encoders and iterative diffusion solvers at inference time. To stabilize long-horizon rollouts, MeLISA combines a Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective that learns conditional spatiotemporal generation from partially observed temporal windows with a Time Increment Consistency loss that constrains multi-lag finite increments and targets temporal-correlation structure. We evaluate MeLISA with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on two high-resolution benchmarks, extended 2D Kolmogorov flow at $256 \times 256$ and turbulent channel-flow slice at $192 \times 192$. MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term forecasting accuracy and long-horizon statistical metrics, including energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, and mixing-rate-related dynamics, while achieving inference speeds comparable to, and in some cases faster than, neural operators. Compact 3.7-5.7M-parameter variants already deliver strong parameter efficiency, and DiT variants provide a scalable path up to 150M parameters. Overall, MeLISA benefits both rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical accuracy.

</details>


### [162] [FedeKD: Energy-Based Gating for Robust Federated Knowledge Distillation under Heterogeneous Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05553)
*Quang-Huy Nguyen,Jiaqi Wang,Wei-shinn Ku*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedeKD is a reliability-aware FKD framework that estimates sample-wise trust via an energy-based gate to weight cross-client knowledge transfer, using per-client private models and a lightweight shared proxy aggregated on the server; no public data required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In federated learning with heterogeneous data and asymmetric models, negative transfer occurs. FKD methods often rely on public data or assume uniform reliability, limiting robustness in practice.

Method: Each client maintains a high-capacity private model and a lightweight proxy model. Proxies are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy that guides updates of private models. An energy-based gating mechanism converts private–proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights to weight backward distillation, enabling selective knowledge transfer without public data.

Result: Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets show that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while preserving strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: FedeKD provides robust reliability-aware FKD via sample-wise trust weighting, improving robustness to data heterogeneity without requiring public data and performing well across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) operates in heterogeneous environments, where variations in data distributions and asymmetric model design often result in negative transfer. While federated knowledge distillation (FKD) avoids direct model parameter sharing, existing methods typically rely on public datasets or assume that transferred knowledge is uniformly reliable, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper presents FedeKD, a reliability-aware FKD framework that makes sample-wise trust estimation an explicit component of knowledge transfer, without relying on additional public data. Each client maintains a high-capacity private model for local learning and a lightweight shared proxy model for cross-client knowledge exchange. During training, proxy models are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy, which is then used to guide updates of the private models. At the core of FedeKD is an energy-based gating mechanism that converts task-specific private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation. This mechanism enables sample-wise weighting of knowledge transfer, where the proxy model contributes more to reliable samples while down-weighting unreliable ones. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [163] [Accelerating LMO-Based Optimization via Implicit Gradient Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05577)
*Won-Jun Jang,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMO-IGT: implicit gradient transport enables single-gradient-per-iteration stochastic LMO with improved convergence; unified RSF framework; Muon-IGT shows best empirical performance; theoretical complexities: O(ε^-4) for plain, O(ε^-3) for variance-reduced, O(ε^-3.5) for LMO-IGT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome overhead of variance reduction and fragmented theory in LMO-based optimization; enable acceleration in both constrained and unconstrained settings with minimal gradient evaluations.

Method: Introduce implicit gradient transport (IGT) into stochastic LMO; propose LMO-IGT class; unified RSF (regularized support function) measure; transport points to evaluate stochastic gradients; analyze iteration complexities; instantiate with Muon-IGT.

Result: Theoretical: LMO-IGT achieves O(ε^-3.5) iteration complexity with a single stochastic gradient per iteration; variance-reduced LMO achieves O(ε^-3) but requires additional gradient evaluations; plain stochastic LMO is O(ε^-4). Empirical: LMO-IGT consistently outperforms stochastic LMO with negligible overhead; Muon-IGT provides strongest overall performance across evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: IGT provides a practical acceleration mechanism for LMO-based optimization; the RSF-based unified framework bridges gradient-norm and Frank–Wolfe-gap notions and supports both constrained and unconstrained settings; single-gradient-per-iteration LMO-IGT is competitive and favorable in practice.

Abstract: Recent optimizers such as Lion and Muon have demonstrated strong empirical performance by normalizing gradient momentum via linear minimization oracles (LMOs). While variance reduction has been explored to accelerate LMO-based methods, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead due to additional gradient evaluations. At the same time, the theoretical understanding of LMO-based methods remains fragmented across unconstrained and constrained formulations. Motivated by these limitations, we propose \emph{LMO-IGT}, a new class of stochastic LMO-based methods leveraging implicit gradient transport (IGT). We further introduce a unified framework for stochastic LMO-based optimization together with a new stationarity measure, the \emph{regularized support function} (RSF), which bridges gradient-norm and Frank--Wolfe-gap notions within a common framework. By evaluating stochastic gradients at transported points, LMO-IGT accelerates convergence while retaining the single-gradient-per-iteration structure of standard stochastic LMO. Our analysis establishes that stochastic LMO achieves an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-4})$, variance-reduced LMO achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$ at the cost of additional gradient evaluations, and LMO-IGT achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3.5})$ using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirically, LMO-IGT consistently improves over stochastic LMO counterparts with negligible overhead. Among its instantiations, Muon-IGT achieves the strongest overall performance across evaluated settings, demonstrating that IGT provides an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for modern LMO-based optimization.

</details>


### [164] [AeroJEPA: Learning Semantic Latent Representations for Scalable 3D Aerodynamic Field Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05586)
*Francisco Giral,Abhijeet Vishwasrao,Andrea Arroyo Ramo,Mahmoud Golestanian,Federica Tonti,Adrian Lozano-Duran,Steven L. Brunton,Sergio Hoyas,Hector Gomez,Soledad Le Clainche,Ricardo Vinuesa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AeroJEPA introduces a latent-prediction framework for aerodynamic fields that scales to high-resolution outputs and yields semantically meaningful latent representations, enabling interpolation, probing, and design optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current aerodynamic surrogate models struggle with very large 3D boundary-layer fields and produce latent spaces that are not readily usable for analysis or design.

Method: AeroJEPA uses a joint-embedding predictive architecture that predicts a target latent flow representation from a context latent representation of geometry and operating conditions, with an optional continuous implicit decoder to reconstruct the field. This decouples latent prediction from field resolution and encourages semantic organization of the latent space.

Result: The approach is competitive as a continuous surrogate, scales to high-resolution outputs, and learns latent representations of geometry and aerodynamics not directly supervised. It achieves validation on HiLiftAeroML and SuperWing, with latent space enabling controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and constrained design latent optimization.

Conclusion:  Predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

Abstract: Aerodynamic surrogate models are increasingly used to replace repeated high-fidelity CFD evaluations in many-query design settings, but current approaches still face two important limitations: they often scale poorly to the very large fields arising in realistic 3D aerodynamics, and they rarely produce latent representations that are directly useful for analysis and design. We introduce AeroJEPA, a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for aerodynamic field modeling that addresses both issues. Rather than predicting the full flow field directly from geometry, AeroJEPA predicts a target latent representation of the flow from a context latent representation of the geometry and operating conditions, and optionally reconstructs the field through a continuous implicit decoder. This formulation decouples latent prediction from field resolution while encouraging the latent space to organize semantically. We evaluate AeroJEPA on two complementary datasets: HiLiftAeroML, which stresses the method in a high-fidelity regime with extremely large boundary-layer fields, and SuperWing, which tests large-scale generalization and latent-space optimization over a broad family of transonic wings. Across these benchmarks, AeroJEPA is competitive as a continuous surrogate for aerodynamic fields, scales naturally to high-resolution outputs, and learns context and predicted latents that encode geometry and aerodynamic quantities not used directly as supervision. We further show that the resulting latent space supports controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and a constrained design latent-optimization experiment. These results suggest that predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

</details>


### [165] [When Can Voting Help, Hurt, or Change Course? Exact Structure of Binary Test-Time Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05592)
*Yi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Voting under de Finetti exchangeability is governed by a latent distribution of per-example correctness; voting curves can be nonmonotone and infinitely many trend changes, driven by latent mixtures. An odd-budget curve uniquely encodes a signed voting signature via signed Hausdorff moments; direct per-example information recovers the full signature, while grouped labels only reveal a finite prefix.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical fixed-competence theory yields monotone voting behavior around the majority threshold. The paper argues that this is incomplete: exchangeable repeated correctness can produce complex, nonmonotone voting patterns. Understanding the latent structure clarifies identifiability, estimation regimes, and shape phenomena of voting curves.

Method: Use de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness to model voting as a latent mixture over per-example correctness probabilities. Define a signed voting signature that records excess latent mass above the majority threshold at binomial-variance scales. Prove equivalence between the odd-budget voting curve and the signed signature: curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments and the full curve recovers the signature uniquely. Analyze nonmonotonicity, nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint rates, and characterize estimation regimes separating per-example probability information from grouped-label information.

Result: Voting curves can be nonmonotone and exhibit infinitely many trend changes, even with simple latent mixtures. The full latent law is not determined by the observed curve; the object recoverable from voting is the signed voting signature. There is an equivalence: the odd-budget curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments and the full curve uniquely determines the signature. Distinct phenomena such as nonidentifiability and end behavior are explained, and estimation regimes are clarified: direct per-example data targets the full signature, while fixed-depth grouped labels only reveal a finite prefix.

Conclusion: The majority-voting intervention has a richer information structure than fixed-competence theory suggests. By framing voting via a latent per-example correctness distribution and the signed voting signature, one can understand nonmonotone curves, identifiability limits, and the exact information captured by different data collection schemes. This guides what to estimate (the full signature) versus what is recoverable from grouped labels (a finite prefix).

Abstract: Majority voting is one of the few black-box interventions that can improve a fixed stochastic predictor: repeated access can be cheaper than changing a high-capability model. Classical fixed-competence theory makes this intervention look monotone -- more votes help above the majority threshold and hurt below it. We show that this picture is fundamentally incomplete. Under the de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness, voting is governed by a latent distribution of per-example correctness probabilities. Even simple latent mixtures can generate sharply different voting curves, including nonmonotone behavior and, in an explicit construction, infinitely many trend changes. The full latent law determines the curve, but the curve does not determine the law. The exact object recovered by voting is a signed voting signature: at each binomial variance scale, it records excess latent mass above rather than below the majority threshold. Our main theorem proves that the complete odd-budget curve and this signature are equivalent: the curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments, and the full curve recovers the signature uniquely. This viewpoint explains shape phenomena, branch-symmetric nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint rates. It also separates estimation regimes: direct per-example success-probability information targets the full signature, whereas fixed-depth grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.

</details>


### [166] [Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05609)
*Jianyu Xu,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a polynomial-time Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing algorithm for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations under agnostic, non-Lipschitz noise; achieves near-optimal regret ~O(T^{2/3}) and closes the gap to lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address non-Lipschitz, discontinuous demand curves in contextual pricing that invalidate smooth interpolation, aiming to close the regret gap from O(T^{3/4}) to the optimal ~O(T^{2/3}) and match known lower bounds.

Method: Integrates randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection within a UCB-style framework; named Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing; ensures polynomial-time computation.

Result: Achieves ~O(T^{2/3}) regret; matches Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) lower bounds up to logarithmic factors; improves over Xu and Wang (2022).

Conclusion: Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, closes the long-standing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing with agnostic non-Lipschitz noise; demonstrates effectiveness of combining estimation, conservative probing, and redirection in non-smooth demand settings.

Abstract: We study contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise, whose induced demand curve may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. Such discontinuities break the cross-context interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand pricing algorithms, while the best previous method achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret. We propose Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing, a polynomial-time algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection. Our algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors and improving over the previous upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022). Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution.

</details>


### [167] [LLMSpace: Carbon Footprint Modeling for Large Language Model Inference on LEO Satellites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05615)
*Lei Jiang,Adrian Ildefonso,Daniel Loveless,Fan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMSpace is the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites, jointly modeling operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators/memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics to reveal trade-offs between carbon footprint, latency, hardware design, and lifetime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs drive rapidly growing energy demand and a potential carbon crisis. Solar-powered AI-enabled LEO satellites could mitigate terrestrial electricity use, but their complete lifecycle carbon footprint (launch emissions, manufacturing, radiation-hardened hardware) is poorly understood.

Method: Introduce LLMSpace, a carbon modeling framework that jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload patterns (prefill-decode behavior, token generation). Uses realistic satellite and GPU configurations to simulate trade-offs.

Result: LLMSpace exposes key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design choices, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference.

Conclusion: LLMSpace enables quantitative assessment of space-based LLM inference design choices, providing guidance for reducing carbon footprint; source code is available at the project repository.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) impose rapidly growing energy demands, creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis driven by large-scale inference. Solar-powered, AI-enabled low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites have been proposed to mitigate terrestrial electricity consumption, but their lifecycle carbon footprint remains poorly understood due to launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware requirements. This paper presents \textit{LLMSpace}, the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics such as prefill-decode behavior and token generation. Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference. Source code: https://github.com/UnchartedRLab/LLMSpace.

</details>


### [168] [Region-adaptable retrieval of coastal biogeochemical parameters from near-surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance using physics-aware meta-learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05623)
*Yiqing Guo,Nagur R. C. Cherukuru,Eric A. Lehmann,S. L. Kesav Unnithan,Tim J. Malthus,Gemma Kerrisk,Xiubin Qi,Faisal Islam,Tisham Dhar,Mark J. Doubell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework that uses a forward-model–generated synthetic dataset to pretrain a region-agnostic BGC retrieval model, then fine-tunes it for specific Australian coastal regions with local samples, yielding improved cross-site generalization for near-surface Rrs-based BGC parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizing hyperspectral BGC retrieval across diverse coastal waters is difficult due to region-specific bio-optical properties and BGC ranges; leveraging physics-driven data generation and meta-learning can enable robust, data-efficient transfer to new regions.

Method: Stage 1: Use a bio-optical forward model to generate a large synthetic Rrs–BGC dataset from an in situ spectral library representing Australian coastal waters; pretrain a region-agnostic base model using meta-learning. Stage 2: Fine-tune the pretrained model for each region with local in situ samples. Validation on five geographically distinct Australian sites with hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements; compare against five benchmark models.

Result: 1) Clear regional distinctions in BGC parameters and their Rrs signatures across sites. 2) Synthetic data are physically plausible and resemble real samples in parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations. 3) The proposed method outperforms five benchmark models in BGC retrieval. 4) Time-series of measured vs. model-predicted BGC parameters show good agreement in magnitude and temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: A two-stage physics-aware meta-learning approach that combines physics-based data generation with meta-learning and region-specific fine-tuning enhances cross-site BGC retrieval from near-surface Rrs, supporting more accurate and transferable coastal water quality monitoring.

Abstract: Hyperspectral in situ sensing has shown promise in retrieving aquatic biogeochemical (BGC) parameters, such as total suspended solids, dissolved organic carbon, and total chlorophyll-a, for cost-effective monitoring of coastal water quality. However, generalising such retrieval algorithms across water bodies remains challenging, as the relationship between remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) and BGC parameters can vary considerably from one region to another due to regional distinctions in environmental conditions and biogeochemistry that lead to different BGC ranges and bio-optical properties. In this study, we propose a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal BGC parameters from near-surface Rrs observations. In the first stage, a bio-optical forward model is used to generate a large synthetic dataset based on an in situ bio-optical spectral library with broad representativeness of Australian coastal waters. This dataset is then used to pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning, allowing the model to learn fundamental physical relationships. In the second stage, the pretrained base model is fine-tuned for specific regions with local samples. We collected in situ hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements from five geographically distinct sites in Australian coastal waters. Our experimental results suggest: (1) the BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures exhibited clear regional distinctions among the experimental sites; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and closely aligned with real-world samples in both parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; and (4) time series of in situ measured and model-predicted BGC parameters showed good agreement in both magnitude and temporal dynamics.

</details>


### [169] [Scaling Pretrained Representations Enables Label-Free Out-of-Distribution Detection Without Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05638)
*Brett Barkley,Preston Culbertson,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Frozen pretrained representations alone enable effective label-free OOD detection across vision and language, with two detectors (global Mahalanobis and ReSCOPED diffusion-based typicality) showing comparable performance as representations scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether effective OOD detection requires class-conditional modeling or supervised fine-tuning, or whether the geometry of frozen pretrained features suffices for label-free OOD detection under distribution shift.

Method: Empirically evaluate 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language. Compare two label-free detectors: (1) a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations; (2) ReSCOPED, a lightweight diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on local features.

Result: Both detectors' performance improves with representation quality; as representations scale, absolute performance gaps between the two detectors vanish across modalities, indicating detector-agnosticity at scale.

Conclusion: Label-free OOD detection can rely on the geometry of frozen pretrained backbones; detector choice becomes less important as backbone scale increases, enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

Abstract: Models trained with deep learning often fail to signal when inputs fall outside their training data manifold, leading to unreliable predictions under distribution shift. Prior work suggests that effective out-of-distribution (OOD) detection often requires class-conditional modeling or specialized models obtained through supervised fine-tuning. We revisit this assumption in modern pretrained models and show that their frozen representations already encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection. Across 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language, we compare two complementary label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight, diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on the same features at a local level. Despite their different detection mechanisms, representation scaling reveals a consistent regime-dependent pattern: both local and global detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between the two detectors disappear across both language and vision tasks as representations scale. These results suggest that label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones, reducing the importance of detector choice as backbone scale increases and enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

</details>


### [170] [Information-Preserving Domain Transfer with Unlabeled Data in Misspecified Simulation-Based Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05652)
*Joon Jang,Eunho Jeong,Kyu Sung Choi,Hyeonjin Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A domain-transfer SBI method (SPIN) preserving parameter-relevant information to improve posterior inference under misspecification by mapping unlabeled real data into the simulator domain during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model misspecification undermines SBI; relying on marginal distribution alignment between simulated and real data is insufficient for accurate posterior inference.

Method: Train with labeled simulator data while translating observations between real and simulator domains in both directions; optimize to preserve parameter-relevant mutual information using original simulator labels; at test time map real observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference without real-world labels or paired data.

Result: SPIN yields improved real-world posterior inference on synthetic and physical benchmarks, with larger gains as misspecification increases.

Conclusion: SPIN demonstrates that parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer can mitigate misspecification in SBI, enabling accurate posterior inference without real-world parameter labels or paired data.

Abstract: Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides amortized Bayesian parameter inference from simulator-generated data without requiring explicit likelihood evaluation. Its reliability can degrade under model misspecification, where real-world observations are not well represented by the simulator used for training. Existing methods using unlabeled real-world data often align simulated and real-world data distributions, but marginal alignment alone does not directly preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference. We propose SPIN, an SBI framework with parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer using unlabeled, unpaired real-world observations. During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, the learned real-to-simulator transport maps real-world observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference, without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired real--simulator observations. Across controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with the improvement becoming clearer as misspecification increases.

</details>


### [171] [Structural Correspondence and Universal Approximation in Diagonal plus Low-Rank Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05659)
*Ying Chen,Aoxi Li,Jihun Kim,Javad Lavaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-rank layers alone can struggle with function approximation; introducing a diagonal component (DLoR: Diagonal plus Low-Rank) enables universal approximation with favorable depth over width scaling, without requiring dense priors or specific activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish theoretical universality guarantees for parameter-efficient low-rank neural networks and to overcome the expressivity bottleneck observed when using pure low-rank layers without pretrained priors.

Method: The authors first show that purely rank-1 layers can interpolate scalar data but fail at general function approximation. They introduce a Structural Correspondence framework and prove that a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure suffices to achieve universal approximation by reconstructing full-rank transformations either through additive width expansion or multiplicative depth. They analyze asymptotic Taylor remainders to show universal approximation for general activations, and compare depth versus width in parameter-to-expressivity scaling.

Result: DLoR networks achieve universal approximation, effectively reconstruct any full-rank transform; dense matrices and specific activations are not necessary for expressivity. Multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width.

Conclusion: Dense matrices and particular activation assumptions are not prerequisites for universal expressivity. A minimal structural augmentation (DLoR) preserves parameter efficiency while restoring the universal approximation property, with depth-based implementations offering favorable scaling.

Abstract: The massive computational costs of scaling modern deep learning architectures have driven the widespread use of parameter-efficient low-rank structures, such as LoRA and low-rank factorization. However, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power are less explored, often relying on restrictive priors like a pretrained base matrix, ReLU activations or non-verifiable singularity conditions. We first investigate the limits of neural networks constrained strictly to low-rank manifolds without pretrained dense priors. We demonstrate a theoretical paradox: while purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets, they collapse for function approximations. To overcome this bottleneck without surrendering parameter efficiency, we introduce a unified \textit{Structural Correspondence} framework. We prove that augmenting low-rank layers with only a minimal sparse diagonal component, say a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure, is sufficient to reach Universal Approximation. We show that any full-rank transformation can be exactly reconstructed using these DLoR components by trading off network width (additive decomposition) or depth (multiplicative decomposition). By tracking asymptotic Taylor remainders, we prove that DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Finally, we establish that multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Our results show that dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity.

</details>


### [172] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05660)
*Yufeng Yang,Fangning Zhuo,Ziyi Chen,Heng Huang,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO) to handle distributional shifts, with Pareto-type solutions and two MGDA algorithms. The double-loop MGDA uses a dual-variable inner loop achieving O(ε^{-12}) sample complexity to reach an ε-Pareto-stationary point; a single-loop double-clip MGDA with gradient clipping achieves O(ε^{-4}) by removing double sampling. Nonconvex, no boundedness assumptions. Experiments show competitiveness with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address distributional shifts in multi-objective learning by optimizing under worst-case distributions for each objective, aiming for robust Pareto-optimality.

Method: Formulate DR-MOO with Pareto-type solutions; apply Lagrangian dual reformulation. Develop two MGDA-based algorithms: (1) a double-loop MGDA with an inner loop estimating dual variables; (2) a single-loop double-clip MGDA using gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased estimates, eliminating double sampling.

Result: The double-loop variant achieves total sample complexity O(ε^{-12}) for ε-Pareto-stationarity; the single-loop double-clip variant improves to O(ε^{-4}); theory holds in nonconvex settings without requiring bounded objectives or gradients; experiments show competitive performance against state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

Conclusion: DR-MOO with Pareto-type solutions and MGDA algorithms can handle distributional shifts efficiently in nonconvex settings, with strong theoretical guarantees on sample complexity and practical performance demonstrated experimentally.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has received growing attention in applications that require learning under multiple criteria. However, the existing MOO formulations do not explicitly account for distributional shifts in the data. We introduce distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under their respective worst-case distributions. We propose Pareto-type solution concepts for DR-MOO and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms (MGDA) with provable guarantees. Leveraging a Lagrangian dual reformulation, we first design a double-loop MGDA that uses an inner loop to estimate dual variables and achieves a total sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-12})$ for reaching an $ε$-Pareto-stationary point. To further improve efficiency, we incorporate gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling. This yields a single-loop double-clip MGDA with substantially improved sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$. Our theory applies to the nonconvex setting and does not require bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our methods are competitive with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

</details>


### [173] [Temporal Functional Circuits: From Spline Plots to Faithful Explanations in KAN Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05685)
*Naveen Mysore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Functional Circuits convert latent KAN edge visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations for time-series forecasting, via edge-wise input lags, activation-based ranking, and edge interventions, while maintaining competitive predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide mechanistic, interpretable explanations for time-series forecasts produced by Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), overcoming the opacity of latent edge functions and enabling faithful, temporally-grounded diagnostics.

Method: Use a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base plus a sparsely activated KAN correction. Map each edge to input lags with output-aware attribution, rank edges by learned activation range, and validate faithfulness with edge-level interventions (zeroing and spline removal). Evaluate by perturbation (spline removal) and regime analysis (gate opening with complexity).

Result: Removing the learned B-spline component while keeping the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, indicating the spline carries predictive value. On synthetic regimes, the gate opens progressively with complexity. In regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture remains competitive with linear, attention, and MLP baselines, while offering edge-based explanations not available from MLP corrections.

Conclusion: Temporal Functional Circuits provide faithful, temporally grounded explanations for KANs in time-series forecasting, integrating interpretability with competitive accuracy; the spline component is predictive, and edge gating captures complexity that enhances predictive performance and explanation quality.

Abstract: Unlike MLPs, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) expose explicit learnable edge functions on every connection, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting. This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations. Built on a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and a sparsely activated KAN correction, the framework (i) maps each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, (ii) ranks edges by learned activation range, and (iii) validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions including zeroing and spline removal. Removing the learned B-spline component while retaining the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, providing evidence that the spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond the base activation. On four synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives, while providing interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot offer.

</details>


### [174] [Budgeted Attention Allocation: Cost-Conditioned Compute Control for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05697)
*Amrit Nidhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Budgeted Attention Allocation studies a monotone head-gating mechanism to trade attention cost for accuracy across budgets in transformers, showing feasible speedups on CPU with varying accuracy but not universal dominance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a reproducible, controllable checkpoint across budgeted operating points that converts attention cost constraints into measurable performance gains on CPU-based inference.

Method: Introduce budgeted attention gating with dense warm-start for stability. Evaluate on synthetic sequence tasks and real datasets (AG News with a word-level transformer; BERT-Mini on AG News and DBpedia14) using hard-gate adaptation, budgeted structural pruning, and post-hoc baselines, comparing against dense full attention. Report accuracy vs estimated cost, and CPU speedups.

Result: On a robust synthetic task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News, 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. BERT-Mini AG News: budgeted gates yield 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at 0.50; a zero-shot dense post-hoc baseline reaches 86.1%, improving to 87.9% after a recovery epoch. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at 0.50 vs 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain competitive. Overall, the work offers a reproducible feasibility study of a controllable budget checkpoint across budgets that trades attention cost for accuracy and can be converted into speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

Conclusion: Budgeted gating provides a feasible, reproducible mechanism to operate transformers under varying attention budgets, enabling cost-accuracy-speed tradeoffs and practical CPU speedups; however, it does not claim universal superiority across tasks and budgets.

Abstract: Transformers usually expose one inference cost per trained model, while deployed systems often need multiple cost-quality operating points. We study Budgeted Attention Allocation, a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget. Dense warm-starting is important for stability: on a robust synthetic sequence task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% accuracy at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News with a custom word-level transformer, hard-gate adaptation turns soft cost control into measured single-thread CPU speed, reaching 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. In pretrained BERT-Mini AG News, budgeted structural pruning reaches 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; a validation-ranked zero-shot dense post-hoc structural baseline reaches 86.1%, and one recovery epoch raises that per-budget specialist to 87.9%. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at exact budget 0.50 versus 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain strong. The contribution is therefore not universal dominance, but a reproducible feasibility study of one controllable checkpoint across budgets that can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

</details>


### [175] [On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05710)
*Wei Yao,Wang Zhaoyang,Gengze Xu,Chen Qian,Dongrui Liu,Ziqiao Wang,Yong Liu,Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pre-training is essential for Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG); with spectral initialization in a high-dimensional single-index/spiked Gaussian model, a geometric warm start enables W2SG within an effectively strong-convex region, yielding a generalization bound that shows initial gains followed by saturation due to the weak supervisor’s bias; empirical tests and large-scale LLM checkpoint analysis reveal W2SG arises via a phase transition tied to pre-training progression rather than being innate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fundamental, under-explored role of pre-training in enabling W2SG, bridging theory (impossibility with random initialization) and large-scale empirical evidence, and clarifying when and how pre-training aids generalization beyond a weak supervisor.

Method: Theoretical: formalize W2SG within a high-dimensional single-index model with spiked Gaussian data; model pre-training as a spectral initialization that places the model in an 'effective region' with perturbed strong-convexity. Prove W2SG is achievable from this warm start and derive a generalization bound that reflects optimization dynamics (initial gains, then a saturation error due to the weak bias). Empirical: controlled synthetic simulations to validate assumptions and theory; large-scale evaluation across hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models.

Result: W2SG is achievable with a proper pre-training-induced warm start, not from random initialization. The generalization bound captures the observed optimization dynamics: early performance gains followed by a saturation limit set by the weak supervisor bias. Empirically, assumptions hold in synthetic tests, and a phase transition in W2SG emerges as pre-training progresses, demonstrated by hundreds of LLM checkpoint analyses.

Conclusion: Pre-training is a necessary prerequisite for W2SG; the emergence of W2SG is tied to a phase transition during pre-training, implying that W2SG is not an innate capability but a consequence of the pre-training trajectory shaping the optimization landscape.

Abstract: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

</details>


### [176] [Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05718)
*Yui Hashimoto,Takayuki Nishio,Yuichi Kitagawa,Takahito Tanimura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CE-FI enables privacy-preserving cooperative inference among pretrained models without sharing parameters or raw inputs or requiring a common encoder; it uses a Consensus Embedding layer to align heterogeneous representations and a Cooperative Output layer to generate predictions, trained with unlabeled data. It improves performance under non-IID conditions on CIFAR-10/100 and shows cross-domain applicability, with representation alignment identified as the main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for collaborative inference across independently deployed models in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings, avoiding leakage of inputs or parameters and without a shared encoder.

Method: Introduce Consensus Embedding (CE) layer to map heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer to produce predictions from embeddings. Train both layers using only shared unlabeled data, enabling inference-time cooperation without sharing model parameters, raw inputs, or requiring a common encoder.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under diverse non-IID conditions shows CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and is competitive with methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional experiments on text and time-series tasks indicate broader applicability, though performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

Conclusion: CE-FI offers a practical framework for privacy-preserving cooperative inference among pretrained models, expanding cross-model collaboration without parameter/input sharing. The approach is effective under image-classification benchmarks and shows potential in other modalities; improving representation alignment could unlock further gains.

Abstract: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

</details>


### [177] [WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05728)
*Dhruv Suri,Helgi Hilmarsson,Shourya Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Correct baselines remove primal-only warm-start gains for AC-OPF; full primal-dual-barrier state yields significant IPOPT speedups; a topology-aware WARP model with dual-labeled data provides robust, large reductions and a reproducible benchmark suite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate the true effectiveness of warm-start methods for AC-OPF with interior-point solvers, address evaluation biases, and provide reproducible benchmarks.

Method: Critical analysis of IPM convergence with IPOPT; compare against the solver’s default midpoint bounds vs flat start; conduct oracle experiments supplying the full primal-dual-barrier state; develop WARP, a topology-conditioned GNN that predicts (x, λ, z, μ); assemble dual-labeled IPOPT datasets and corrected evaluation protocol.

Result: Primal-only warm-starts do not reduce iterations under the corrected baseline; supplying the full primal-dual-barrier state reduces iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction); WARP achieves ~76% iteration reduction and handles N-1 topology variations without retraining; a benchmark suite is released.

Conclusion: Acceleration in AC-OPF IPMs requires dual variables and barrier-state information; evaluating warm-starts must use correct baselines and robust benchmarks; the proposed WARP model and datasets enable rigorous assessment and reveal limits of primal-only approaches.

Abstract: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers.
