Result: Evaluations on real-world data show DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep-learning baselines, delivering accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even with few stations and in cross-regional scenarios.

Conclusion: DropsToGrid demonstrates a practical, scalable approach to high-resolution rainfall densification with quantified uncertainty, promising improvements for forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation across diverse regions.

Abstract: High-resolution rainfall observations are crucial for weather forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation. Traditional operational measurements are often biased and low-resolution, limiting their ability to capture local rainfall. Accurate high-resolution rainfall maps require integrating sparse surface observations, yet existing deep learning densification methods are hindered by rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion. We present DropsToGrid, a Neural Process-based method that generates dense rainfall fields by fusing temporal sequences from noisy, irregularly distributed private weather stations with spatial context from radar. Leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion, the model produces stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates and explicitly quantifies uncertainty. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines, generating accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even when only few stations are available and in cross-regional scenarios.

</details>


### [204] [Near-Policy: Accelerating On-Policy Distillation via Asynchronous Generation and Selective Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05940)
*Miao Rang,Zhenni Bi,Hang Zhou,Kai Han,Xuechun Wang,An Xiao,Xinghao Chen,Yunhe Wang,Hanting Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Near-Policy Distillation (NPD) decouples student generation from training to reduce distribution mismatch in autoregressive distillation. It uses sparse updates and a Δ-IFD filtering heuristic to stabilize learning, achieving major speedups and competitive performance with SFT and larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard distillation suffers distribution mismatch; on-policy methods address this but are computationally heavy. A more efficient, near-policy approach can combine the benefits of supervised fine-tuning with improved stability.

Method: Asynchronous decoupling of generation and training; supervised fine-tuning with sequence packing; sparse student updates; Δ-IFD filtering to filter out extreme out-of-distribution samples and stabilize gradients; aims to keep updates in a proximal learning zone.

Result: 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines; outperforms SFT by 8.09%; achieves 68.73% on openPangu-Embedded-1B, surpassing Qwen3-1.7B; code forthcoming.

Conclusion: NPD improves training efficiency and stability for autoregressive distillation, narrows the exploration space for downstream RL, and yields strong performance with a lighter model; promising direction with publicly released code.

Abstract: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models often suffers from distribution mismatch. While on-policy methods mitigate this by leveraging student-generated outputs, they rely on computationally expensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks. To improve efficiency, we propose Near-Policy Distillation (NPD), an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training. This reformulation enables Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with sequence packing. However, asynchronous updates inevitably introduce policy lag and sample noise, which can cause the behavior to drift from near-policy toward off-policy. To counteract this without sacrificing efficiency, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the $Δ$-IFD filtering mechanism, a heuristic sample selection mechanism that empirically stabilizes the optimization trajectory. By filtering extreme out-of-distribution samples, $Δ$-IFD prevents noise from dominating the gradients, ensuring updates remain within a safe proximal learning zone. Empirically, the NPD framework achieves a 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Crucially, by effectively narrowing the exploration space for subsequent RL, our method enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the substantially larger Qwen3-1.7B. Codes will be released soon.

</details>


### [205] [Knowing but Not Correcting: Routine Task Requests Suppress Factual Correction in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05957)
*Zixuan Chen,Hao Lin,Zizhe Chen,Yizhou Tian,Garry Yang,Depeng Wang,Ya Guo,Huijia Zhu,James Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit correction suppression: in task prompts they often comply with false premises; a benchmark across eight models shows high suppression rates; two training-free interventions (CDS and DPA) substantially improve factual strictness, with DPA also preserving reasoning; introduces 'factual strictness' as a reliability dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to quantify and understand why LLMs correct false premises in isolation but not in task-oriented prompts, and to develop data-free remedies for reliability.

Method: Construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to evaluate suppression across eight models; perform mechanistic analysis of where suppression arises (knowledge vs. output selection); propose two training-free interventions—Correction Direction Steering (CDS) and Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA); evaluate on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B.

Result: Suppression rates range from 19% to 90%; four models exceed 80% suppression. CDS raised correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B from 0% to 58.2%; DPA preserved or improved reasoning in both models; both methods substantially improved factual strictness.

Conclusion: Correction suppression is a prevalent risk and constitutes a new reliability dimension termed factual strictness. Suppression arises during output selection rather than knowledge encoding; CDS and DPA offer training-free remedies with potential broad applicability.

Abstract: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, yet when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they often comply rather than correct. We term this failure mode \emph{correction suppression} and construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate it across eight models. Suppression rates range from 19\% to 90\%, with four models exceeding 80\%, establishing correction suppression as a prevalent and severe phenomenon. Mechanistic analysis reveals that suppression is not a knowledge failure: the model registers the error internally but task context diverts early-layer attention from the false claim as output intent crystallizes toward compliance at middle layers. We characterize this as \emph{knowing but not correcting} -- suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding. Guided by this mechanism, we propose two training-free interventions. Correction Direction Steering (CDS) estimates a correction-compliance direction from matched pairs and injects it at middle layers before output intent crystallizes. Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) localizes payload tokens via attention divergence between early and late layers and amplifies their representation at the final layer, requiring no calibration data. Experiments on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B show both methods substantially improve factual strictness. CDS achieves the highest correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B (0\%$\to$58.2\%). DPA is the only method that preserves or improves reasoning capability on both models. These findings introduce \emph{factual strictness} -- the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures -- as a new dimension of model reliability.

</details>


### [206] [Uncertainty Estimation via Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05964)
*Eunseo Choi,Ho-Yeon Kim,Jaewon Lee,Taeyong jo,Myungjun lee,Heejin Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM) provides sampling-free, distribution-free uncertainty estimation by decomposing model outputs into a magnitude and a direction on the unit hypersphere; uncertainty is the violation of the geometric constraint, enabling deterministic, interpretable estimates for regression and classification that rival ensembles/evidential methods at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification is critical in high-stakes domains; existing approaches often rely on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, limiting practicality and reliability.

Method: Output decomposition into a scalar magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere; uncertainty interpreted as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint; resulting in a deterministic, sampling-free, distribution-free estimator applicable to both regression and classification.

Result: Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks show HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with significantly lower inference cost and stronger alignment between confidence and error.

Conclusion: Exploiting geometric structure in the output space yields a versatile, efficient framework for uncertainty estimation, positioning HCM as a competitive alternative to conventional sampling-based or distributional-method approaches.

Abstract: Quantifying uncertainty in neural network predictions is essential for high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and manufacturing. While existing approaches often depend on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, we propose Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM), a simple yet principled framework for sampling-free and distribution-free uncertainty estimation. HCM decomposes outputs into a magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere, enabling a novel interpretation of uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint. This yields deterministic and interpretable estimates applicable to both regression and classification. Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks demonstrate that HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment. Our results highlight the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and position HCM as a versatile alternative to conventional techniques.

</details>


### [207] [Beyond Uniform Credit Assignment: Selective Eligibility Traces for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05965)
*Chaoli Mou,Zhan Zhuang,Xinning Chen,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Selective eligibility traces (S-trace) improve credit assignment in critic-free RL for LLMs, outperforming GRPO/GSPO while boosting sample and token efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critic-free methods like GRPO use uniform credit assignment, which dilutes responsibility across steps and hurts learning efficiency in reasoning tasks. A fine-grained credit mechanism can better assign value to key reasoning steps.

Method: Introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces approach. Build S-trace as a sparse eligibility traces mechanism that selectively masks low-entropy tokens to reduce variance and enable fine-grained credit assignment. The paper also contextualizes GSPO as a special case of eligibility traces with uniform credit assignment.

Result: Empirical results show S-trace outperforming GRPO (0.49% gain on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16% on Qwen3-4B) and maintaining 2.98% improvement at Qwen3-8B on average pass@16, with improved sample and token efficiency.

Conclusion: S-trace provides a principled, scalable approach to credit assignment in critic-free RL for LLMs, surpassing prior methods and clarifying the relationship between GSPO and eligibility traces under uniform credit assignment.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key approach for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, widely used critic-free algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) necessitate a ``uniform credit assignment'' assumption that indiscriminately broadcast trajectory-level advantages, hindering learning efficiency by failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps. To address this limitation, we propose Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace). Grounded in the intuition of partial trust region preservation, we initially introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces method, upon which we build S-trace, implementing a sparse eligibility traces mechanism to further mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens. Theoretically, we contextualize the recent Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) method within the critic-free eligibility traces framework, identifying it as a special instance of the eligibility traces method operating under uniform credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that S-trace not only outperforms GRPO, showing gains of 0.49\% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16\% on Qwen3-4B, and maintaining a robust 2.98\% improvement when scaled further to Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, but notably achieves this with simultaneously higher sample and token efficiency.

</details>


### [208] [Sharper Guarantees for Misspecified Kernelized Bandit Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05967)
*Davide Maran,Csaba Szepesvári*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Misspecification penalties in kernelized bandits can be reduced from sqrt(d_eff) (offline) or sqrt(γ_n) n ε (online) to logarithmic/polylog growth for a broad class of kernels via spectral localization and domain splitting; offline simple-regret gains depend on a spectral Lebesgue constant; online regret bound becomes Õ(√(γ_n n) + n ε) under mild eigendecay, without the extra √(γ_n) factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate the adverse impact of misspecification in kernelized bandits and improve dependence on kernel complexity in both offline and online guarantees.

Method: Offline: derive high-probability simple-regret bounds with misspecification controlled by a spectral Lebesgue constant. Online: modify a domain-splitting algorithm and analyze under localized eigendecay to obtain cumulative regret bound Õ(√(γ_n n) + n ε), removing the extra √(γ_n) factor. Core idea is spectral localization and spatial localization via domain splitting.

Result: Offline: logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra; polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. Online: regret bound Õ(√(γ_n n) + n ε) under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, avoiding the prior extra √(γ_n) penalty.

Conclusion: Localization is the unifying principle: spectral localization controls the offline Lebesgue constant, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue online, preventing local misspecification errors from spreading; this enables reduced misspecification amplification for a broad kernel class.

Abstract: Existing guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandit optimization pay for misspecification through kernel complexity: in generic offline bounds, the misspecification level $\varepsilon$ is multiplied by $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$, where $d_\mathrm{eff}$ is the kernel effective dimension, while in online regret bounds, the corresponding penalty is $\sqrt{γ_n}\,n\varepsilon$, where $γ_n$ is the maximum information gain after $n$ rounds of interaction.
  In this work, we show that, for a large class of kernels, the misspecification amplification can be reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth. In the offline setting, we first prove high-probability simple-regret bounds whose misspecification term is governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant. This yields logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. In the online setting, we modify a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term. The common principle is localization: spectral localization controls the Lebesgue constant of the offline approximation operator, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue of this mechanism in the online setting, preventing local misspecification errors from being amplified globally.

</details>


### [209] [Training Transformers for KV Cache Compressibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05971)
*Yoav Gelberg,Yam Eitan,Michael Bronstein,Yarin Gal,Haggai Maron*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We propose KV-CAT, a training method that encourages compressible KV representations to improve post-hoc KV compression for long-context models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context language modeling is bottlenecked by the Key-Value (KV) cache, with memory and decode-time costs scaling with prefix length. Post-hoc KV compression is limited by the model's internal representations, which may not be readily compressible.

Method: KV-CAT performs continued pretraining with a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training, forcing the model to use fewer KV slots and learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression.

Result: KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

Conclusion: Compressibility is a property of learned representations, not context alone. Training to promote compressible representations yields better downstream compression; KV-CAT is effective across multiple tasks.

Abstract: Long-context language modeling is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with the prefix length. This bottleneck has motivated a range of context-compression methods, from token-level summarization to recent optimization-based KV compression methods. These post-hoc methods operate on the KV cache of a fixed pretrained model, so their effectiveness is fundamentally limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed. In this work, we formalize the notion of KV compressibility and show that it is a property of the learned representations, rather than of the context alone. We prove that almost any sequence-to-vector function admits both highly compressible and inherently non-compressible transformer implementations, highlighting the need to guide transformers toward compressible representations during training. Motivated by this, we propose KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure that incentivizes the emergence of compressible representations. We introduce a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training. This forces the model to use fewer KV slots and encourages it to learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression. Empirically, we show that KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

</details>


### [210] [Physical Fidelity Reconstruction via Improved Consistency-Distilled Flow Matching for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05975)
*Sicheng Ma,Tianyue Yang,Xiuzhe Wu,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A high-fidelity flow-matching model can be distilled into a compact one-step consistency model that performs fast, conditional reconstructions from low-fidelity observations with competitive accuracy and substantial speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce latency and computational cost of diffusion/flow-matching-based flow-field reconstruction in latency-sensitive scientific workflows by compressing a high-capacity teacher into a low-parameter, fast, deployment-friendly student.

Method: Distill an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. At inference, incorporate low-fidelity observations by initializing the trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, enabling the unconditional high-fidelity model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. Evaluate on three fluid benchmarks (Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, Kolmogorov Flow) with coarse-to-fine reconstruction up to 256×256.

Result: The distilled student retains similar spectrum-metric performance to the teacher while using ~50% fewer parameters and delivering a 12× inference speedup over the teacher. With the same training budget, the student also surpasses a one-step consistency model trained from scratch by 23.1% in SSIM.

Conclusion: Teacher distillation yields faster-to-train, cheaper-to-run, and easier-to-deploy compact reconstruction models for scientific flow reconstruction, offering a promising path to deploy high-capacity scientific generative models in real-time and resource-constrained settings.

Abstract: Reconstructing high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations is a central problem in scientific machine learning, yet recent diffusion and flow-matching models typically rely on iterative sampling, making them costly for latency-sensitive workflows such as ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. We study whether a high-fidelity flow-matching generative model can be compressed into a compact one-step model for fast scientific flow reconstruction. Our approach distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, allowing an unconditional high-fidelity flow model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. We evaluate this distillation strategy on three fluid benchmarks, Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow, using coarse-to-fine reconstruction as a controlled testbed at field sizes up to $256 \times 256$. Across these settings, the distilled student retains similar performance of the teacher's model on spectrum metrics, while using roughly half as many parameters and achieving a $12\times$ inference speedup over the flow-matching teacher. Under the same training budget, the distilled student also outperforms a one-step consistency model trained directly from scratch by $23.1\%$ in SSIM, showing that teacher distillation improves training efficiency rather than merely accelerating sampling. These results suggest a promising route for turning future high-capacity scientific generative models into compact reconstruction models that are faster to train, cheaper to run, and easier to deploy.

</details>


### [211] [Towards Steering without Sacrifice: Principled Training of Steering Vectors for Prompt-only Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05983)
*Yuntai Bao,Qinfeng Li,Xinyan Yu,Xuhong Zhang,Ge Su,Wenqi Zhang,Liu Yan,Haiqin Weng,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Joint training of steering factors and directions eliminates per-SV factor selection; introduces Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes only on prompt tokens to reduce generation disruption. PrOSV outperforms full-sequence SVs (FSSVs) on AxBench under joint training and offers a better utility-robustness tradeoff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fine-tuned steering vectors (SVs) face two drawbacks: (1) requiring careful, per-SV tuning of steering factors to balance steering strength and generation quality; (2) full-sequence SVs (FSSVs) intervene across the whole generation, which can degrade quality regardless of factor settings. This motivates more scalable training and lighter interventions.

Method: 1) Propose joint training of steering factors and directions to remove the need for post-hoc factor selection; leverage neural network scaling theory to show that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors aid stability and efficiency. 2) Introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), which intervenes only on a few prompt tokens, inspired by representation fine-tuning.

Result: Empirical results show PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when combined with the joint-training scheme. PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSVs.

Conclusion: Joint training of steering factors and directions, plus the PrOSV architecture, yields effective, lightweight SVs that require less manual tuning and cause less disruption to generation, while offering improved utility and robustness.

Abstract: Recently, steering vectors (SVs) have emerged as an effective and lightweight approach to steer behaviors of large language models (LLMs), among which fine-tuned SVs are more effective than optimization-free ones. However, current approaches to fine-tuned SVs suffer from two limitations. First, they require careful selection of steering factors on a per-SV basis to balance steering effectiveness and generation quality at inference time. Second, they operate as full-sequence SVs (FSSVs), which can sacrifice generation quality regardless of factor selection due to excessive intervention on the model generation process. To address the first limitation, we propose joint training of steering factors and directions, such that post-hoc factor selection is no longer required. Using neural network scaling theory, we find that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors are essential for stability and efficiency of joint training. To tackle the second limitation, we draw inspiration from representation fine-tuning and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), an SV that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens. Our empirical results show that PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when using our joint training scheme. We also find that PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

</details>


### [212] [DiBA: Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation for Neural Network Weight Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05994)
*Nobutaka Ono*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiBA is a compact matrix factorization for neural network weights that uses diagonal and binary matrices to approximate dense weights, reducing storage and FLOPs; it is optimized via DiBA-Greedy and deployed through DiBARD, showing empirical gains on pretrained models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To compress dense neural network weights (e.g., linear layers, 1x1 convs, attention, embeddings) with a storage- and compute-efficient factorization that supports fast matrix-vector products and preserves accuracy.

Method: Factorize A in R^{m x n} as A ≈ D1 B1 D2 B2 D3, where D1, D2, D3 are diagonal and B1, B2 are 0/1 binary matrices; intermediate dimension k controls storage/accuracy. Propose DiBA-Greedy (alternating optimization with closed-form least-squares for diagonals and exact one-bit improvement tests for binaries) and DiBARD (freeze binary matrices and retune only diagonals on downstream data).

Result: DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements with increasing theoretical storage ratio on 40 dense weight matrices from public pretrained models. In two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands AST from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing binary factors.

Conclusion: DiBA provides a scalable, storage- and compute-efficient weight compression framework with practical downstream gains; DiBA-Greedy offers effective optimization and DiBARD enables adaptation while preserving discrete binary factors, enabling deployment with reduced resource requirements.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose DiBA (Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation), a compact matrix factorization for neural network weight compression. Many components of modern networks, including linear layers, $1\times1$ convolutions, attention projections, and embedding layers, have dense matrix weights. DiBA approximates $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ by $\widehat A=D_1B_1D_2B_2D_3$, where $D_1,D_2,D_3$ are diagonal matrices and $B_1,B_2$ are $0/1$ binary matrices. The intermediate dimension $k$ controls the trade-off between theoretical storage and approximation accuracy. For matrix-vector products, DiBA decomposes dense multiplication into three element-wise scaling operations and two binary mixing operations, reducing the floating-point multiplication count from $mn$ to $m+k+n$. For optimization, we introduce DiBA-Greedy, an alternating solver that combines closed-form least-squares updates for the diagonal factors with exact one-bit improvement tests for the binary factors. We also introduce DiBARD (DiBA with Retuning only Diagonal factors), which replaces dense-matrix layers by DiBA factors, freezes the binary matrices, and retunes only the diagonal entries on downstream data. This preserves compact binary mixing without discrete search during adaptation. On 40 dense weight matrices extracted from public pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the theoretical storage ratio increases. After DiBA replacement in two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing the binary factors.

</details>


### [213] [A Fine-Grained Understanding of Uniform Convergence for Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06004)
*Aryeh Kontorovich,Kasper Green Larsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates fine-grained uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing dimension- and structure-dependent thresholds. In inhomogeneous halfspaces (d≥2), standard VC bounds are essentially tight with population error Θ(d log(n/d)/n) for consistent hypotheses, and agnostic deviations scale as sqrt(τ log(1/τ)) at true error τ. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in R^2 show drastically better behavior: in the realizable case, consistent hypotheses have error O(1/n); in the agnostic case, a bandwise, log-free per-band deviation bound is proved using a critical-wedge localization argument, with a ln ln n overhead when unioning bands, and a matching lower bound confirms optimality. The results provide a near-complete, fine-grained picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces across dimensional and structural regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve a refined understanding of uniform convergence for linear separators that goes beyond worst-case VC bounds, highlighting how dimension and the homogeneity of the halfspace affect convergence rates and the behavior in realizable vs. agnostic settings.

Method: (i) Derive tight population error bounds for inhomogeneous halfspaces in d≥2 under consistency constraints; (ii) establish agnostic deviation bounds for these halfspaces with error τ; (iii) analyze homogeneous halfspaces in R^2, proving O(1/n) realizable misclassification/error bound; (iv) develop a bandwise, log-free deviation bound within dyadic risk bands using a critical-wedge localization technique; (v) perform a union over bands resulting in an unavoidable ln ln n overhead; (vi) construct matching lower bounds to show optimality.

Result: A fine-grained characterization: inhomogeneous halfspaces exhibit near-VC-tight rates with population error Θ(d log(n/d)/n) and agnostic deviations Θ(√(τ log(1/τ))) at error τ; homogeneous halfspaces in R^2 exhibit substantially better behavior with O(1/n) realizable error and a per-band log-free deviation bound in the agnostic case, plus a matching lower bound showing the ln ln n overhead is necessary.

Conclusion: The study unveils sharp dimensional and structural thresholds in uniform convergence for halfspaces, yielding a near-complete, fine-grained picture: dimension d and homogeneity (inhomogeneous vs homogeneous) govern whether convergence bounds are VC-tight or drastically improved, with a precise bandwise analysis in R^2 for homogeneous, agnostic learning establishing both upper and lower bounds and confirming optimality.

Abstract: We study the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ge 2$, we show that standard first-order VC bounds are essentially tight: even consistent hypotheses can incur population error $Θ(d\ln(n/d)/n)$, and in the agnostic setting the deviation scales as $\sqrt{τ\ln(1/τ)}$ at true error $τ$. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ exhibit a markedly different behavior. In the realizable case, every hypothesis consistent with the sample has error $O(1/n)$. In the agnostic case, we prove a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band via a critical-wedge localization argument. Unioning over bands incurs only a $\ln\ln n$ overhead, and we establish a matching lower bound showing this overhead is unavoidable. Together, these results give a fine-grained and nearly complete picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.

</details>


### [214] [Quantizing With Randomized Hadamard Transforms: Efficient Heuristic Now Proven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06014)
*Ran Ben-Basat,William Kuszmaul,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) nearly replicate uniform random rotations (URRs) for several modern compression schemes, and three RHTs are needed for robust vector quantization (VQ); a runtime moment-based adaptivity improves performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: URRs guarantee well-distributed coordinates but are costly; RHTs offer fast, orthogonal transforms. The paper investigates worst-case behavior and whether RHTs can match URR performance in practice, including for common schemes like DRIVE, QUIC-FL, and VQ.

Method: The authors show that composing two RHTs on a d-dimensional vector yields marginal coordinates of the normalized rotated vector that are within O(d^{-1/2}) of Gaussian in both Kolmogorov and 1-Wasserstein distances. They apply these Gaussian closeness bounds to analyses of DRIVE and QUIC-FL, showing two RHTs asymptotically match URR performance. For Vector Quantization, they prove that three RHTs are required to achieve decaying coordinate covariance, ensuring bounded multi-dimensional codebooks behave similarly under URRs and RHTs. They also propose a linear-time O(d) check on input moments to adaptively select the number of RHTs at runtime.

Result: Two RHTs suffice to achieve near-URR performance for DRIVE and QUIC-FL in the worst-case analysis, with asymptotic guarantees. However, two RHTs may be insufficient for VQ due to persistent coordinate covariance, for which three RHTs restore the desired decorrelation. A practical adaptive scheme using an O(d) moment check can adjust the number of RHTs at runtime to improve performance.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that RHTs can closely emulate URR performance for several modern compression tasks with two transforms, while three transforms are necessary for robust VQ; runtime adaptivity via moment checks offers practical gains without sacrificing asymptotic guarantees.

Abstract: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are a common preprocessing step in modern quantization approaches used for gradient compression, inference acceleration, KV-cache compression, model weight quantization, and approximate nearest-neighbor search in vector databases. In practice, URRs are often replaced by randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs), which preserve orthogonality while admitting fast implementations. The remaining issue is the performance for worst-case inputs. With a URR, each coordinate is individually distributed as a shifted beta distribution, which converges to a Gaussian distribution in high dimensions. Generally, one RHT is not suitable in the worst case, as individual coordinates can be far from these distributions. We show that after composing two RHTs on any $d$-sized input vector, the marginal distribution of every fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within $O(d^{-1/2})$ of a standard Gaussian both in Kolmogorov distance and in $1$-Wasserstein distance. We then plug these bounds into the analyses of modern compression schemes, namely DRIVE and QUIC-FL, and show that two RHTs achieve performance that asymptotically matches URRs.
  However, we show that two RHTs may not be sufficient for Vector Quantization (VQ), which often requires weak correlation across fixed-size blocks of coordinates (as opposed to only marginal distribution convergence for single coordinates). We prove that a composition of three RHTs leads to decaying coordinate covariance. This ensures that any fixed, bounded, multi-dimensional VQ codebook optimized for URRs has the same expected error when using three RHTs, up to an additive term that vanishes with the dimension.
  Finally, because practical inputs are rarely adversarial, we propose a linear-time ${O}(d)$ check on the input's moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs used at runtime to improve performance.

</details>


### [215] [Matrix-Decoupled Concentration for Autoregressive Sequences: Dimension-Free Guarantees for Sparse Long-Context Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06017)
*Pei-Sen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) provides dimension-free, tight concentration bounds for autoregressive, dependent sequences by using a causal resolvent to modulate the sensitivity vector, avoiding scalar variance inflation and attaining optimal constants for Markov chains and causal-tree bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core bottlenecks in existing sequence-level concentration for autoregressive LLMs: (i) decoupling dependencies from target sensitivities causes scalar collapse and O(N) variance proxies for sparse terminal rewards, and (ii) non-causal or partially-causal methods (even when tight) fail the strictly causal filtration required for sequential generation.

Method: Introduce a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, the Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework, where the concentration bound is governed by the exact matrix-vector product of the causal dependency resolvent and the reward sensitivity vector. Leverage directed d-separation for causal trees and preserve coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards to maintain a dimension-free variance proxy; shows optimal constants for Markov chains and order-optimal bounds for causal trees.

Result: Yields exact, dimension-free O(1) variance proxy; recovers optimal constants for Markov chains; provides order-optimal bounds for causal trees; validates stability of long-context reasoning in autoregressive settings with strictly causal structure.

Conclusion: MDC resolves both previously identified bottlenecks by delivering a strictly causal, matrix-aware concentration framework for sequence evaluations in autoregressive LLMs, offering rigorous justification for stable long-context reasoning and practical, tight bounds that are dimension-free.

Abstract: Sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on highly dependent token generation. Establishing tight concentration bounds for these processes remains a challenge due to two fundamental bottlenecks in existing frameworks: (i) classical inequalities typically separate dependency structures from target sensitivities, leading to a scalar collapse that inflates the variance proxy to a suboptimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for sparse terminal rewards; (ii) conversely, while certain spatial methods achieve tighter bounds, they lack the strictly causal filtration required by sequential generation, rendering them inapplicable to the autoregressive setting. To resolve both bottlenecks, we establish a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed strictly by the exact matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector. This Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework natively recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and exploits directed $d$-separation to yield order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, by exactly preserving the coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework, MDC mathematically prevents scalar collapse, guaranteeing a dimension-free $\mathcal{O}(1)$ variance proxy and providing a rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning.

</details>


### [216] [Multi-agent decision making: A Blackwell's informativeness approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06028)
*Zheng Zhang,Cuong C. Nguyen,Kevin Wells,Gustavo Carneiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper uses Blackwell informativeness to analyze multi-LLM decision-making; shows voting/debate are not more informative than pooled private info; proposes a product-of-posteriors estimator to approximate pooled posterior; demonstrates empirical gains on six QA benchmarks, surpassing existing multi-LLM debate/voting methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalize the informativeness of aggregation in multi-agent LLM systems and provide principled guarantees, addressing the ad-hoc nature of voting/debate methods.

Method: Theoretical analysis of information structures under Blackwell ordering for voting and debate; identify Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as the information-theoretic upper bound. Propose a practical QA method that estimates each agent's posterior and uses a product-of-posteriors estimator to approximate the pooled posterior.

Result: Voting and debate induce information structures no more informative than the pooled private information. The product-of-posteriors estimator for pooled posteriors improves QA performance, beating state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate/voting baselines on six benchmarks.

Conclusion: Establishes an information-theoretic upper bound on multi-LLM decision rules and offers a practical estimator that approaches this bound, yielding superior QA performance over existing aggregation methods.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has motivated research on decision-making in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents collaborate to achieve shared objectives. Existing aggregation approaches, such as voting and debate, are largely ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees regarding the informativeness of the resulting decisions. In this paper, we provide a principled approach to analyse decisions made in the multi-LLM setting using Blackwell's informativeness framework. Within the Blackwell information-structure abstraction, we show that voting and debate induce information structures that are no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents. This result identifies Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an information-theoretic upper-bound decision rule under the Blackwell ordering. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, we introduce a practical method for LLM-based question-answering (QA) tasks that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

</details>


### [217] [Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06032)
*Hugo Cazaux,Eyjólfur Ingi Ásgeirsson,Hlynur Stefánsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Synthetic time-series augmentation yields architecture-dependent effects; channel-mixing models benefit, channel-independent models often degrade; notable gains in low-resource Weather data with TimesNet + Seasonal-Trend generator; overall augmentation hurts the majority of trials; provides actionable guidelines and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the role of synthetic data in time series forecasting and derive concrete, actionable usage guidelines through a large-scale empirical study.

Method: Large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs; evaluating synthetic augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. Also tests curriculum switching and resource settings; code available.

Result: Channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) tend to benefit from synthetic augmentation; channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In low-resource settings, gains can be large (e.g., TimesNet with 10% Weather data). On average, augmentation hurts in 67% of trials. The Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across benchmarks; hard curriculum switching is detrimental (+24% MSE).

Conclusion: Provide guidelines: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, apply gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent; code is available for replication.

Abstract: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

</details>


### [218] [Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06036)
*Licheng Pan,Haochen Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yunsheng Lu,Yongqi Tong,Yinuo Wang,Shijian Wang,Zhixuan Chu,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelectiveRM uses optimal transport to robustly train RLHF reward models under noisy preferences via Joint Consistency Discrepancy and Mass Relaxation (partial transport), achieving tighter risk bounds and better empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world RLHF data are corrupted by noisy, non-homogeneous linguistic preferences; existing denoisers fail to capture complexity and outliers; need robust, principled approach.

Method: Propose SelectiveRM framework with Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align model-prediction and preference distributions; Mass Relaxation via partial transport to drop samples that violate semantic consistency; theoretical analysis showing a tighter upper bound on clean risk.

Result: Empirical results show significant improvement over state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: SelectiveRM provides a principled, transport-based approach to robust RLHF reward modeling with theoretical guarantees and proven empirical gains; partial transport helps avoid fitting noisy outliers.

Abstract: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [219] [Requests of a Feather Must Flock Together: Batch Size vs. Prefix Homogeneity in LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06046)
*Saksham Rathi,Preeti,Mythili Vutukuru*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feather is a prefix-aware RL scheduler with a lightweight Chunked Hash Tree (CHT) for fast prefix detection, achieving 2–10x higher end-to-end throughput in autoregressive LLM decoding by optimizing batch size and prefix homogeneity; integrates into vLLM and SGLang and outperforms existing schedulers, especially on prefix-sharing workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding is memory-bound due to KV cache; existing schedulers batch requests to maximize GPU memory efficiency but ignore optimal batch granularity when prefixes are shared. Prefix reuse is limited by CPU-heavy radix-tree traversals, leaving potential throughput on the table.

Method: Introduce Feather, an RL-based scheduler that learns the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. Develop Chunked Hash Tree (CHT) to detect shared prefixes quickly without expensive tree traversals. Implement integration into vLLM and SGLang and evaluate on workloads with varying prefix sharing.

Result: Feather achieves 2–10x higher end-to-end throughput compared to state-of-the-art schedulers on prefix-shared workloads, and performs at least as well as the status quo when prefix sharing is insufficient. It reduces the total number of KV cache accesses, outperforming prefix-aware attention kernels with the same goal.

Conclusion: RL-driven, prefix-aware scheduling with fast prefix detection significantly improves throughput for autoregressive decoding without sacrificing performance on non-prefix-sharing workloads, by reducing KV cache accesses and exploiting locality.

Abstract: Auto-regressive token generation in large language models is memory-bound because it requires "attending to" key and value tensors (KV cache) of all previous tokens. Prior work aims to improve the efficiency of this decode process by batching multiple requests together, and maximizing batch size subject to GPU memory constraints. The key observation of our work is that with prefix-sharing workloads, smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches -- where all requests share a common prefix -- can achieve higher decode throughput than larger, heterogeneous batches, due to better spatial and temporal locality during KV cache accesses. However, prefix-aware schedulers in state-of-the-art inference engines maximize prefix reuse within a batch only to reduce KV cache memory footprint, but do not stop batch formation at smaller homogeneous batches that could have performed better. Further, we show that shared prefix detection in existing schedulers relies on radix-tree traversals, incurring substantial CPU overhead that is often comparable to GPU execution time. This paper presents Feather, a prefix-aware scheduler that uses reinforcement learning (RL) to learn the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. We also introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure that enables fast prefix detection and efficient request selection for the RL scheduler, avoiding expensive tree traversals. We integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang, and our evaluation shows that Feather achieves 2--10$\times$ higher end-to-end throughput as compared to existing schedulers, while doing no worse than the status quo when the workload does not have enough prefix sharing. Feather achieves these gains by reducing the total number of KV cache accesses, surpassing the performance of prefix-aware attention kernels that have the same goal.

</details>


### [220] [TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06047)
*Duong Nguyen,Mohammed Jawhar,Nicolas Chesneau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFM-Retouche introduces a lightweight, input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic to adapt Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) without full fine-tuning; achieves top performance with efficient per-task tuning on TabArena-Lite, exemplified by TabICLv2-Retouche.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TFMs have strong zero-shot performance but fixed inductive biases at inference; adapting them to data typically requires expensive full fine-tuning or architecture-specific PEFT methods, with mixed evidence on weight-space fine-tuning benefits. A lightweight, architecture-agnostic input-space adaptation could enable efficient, robust dataset-specific alignment.

Method: Propose TFM-Retouche: a small residual adapter in the input space that, when frozen TFMs are used, learns to adjust inputs to better align with the pretrained model biases. Trained end-to-end with the TFM frozen; includes a post-training identity guard that reverts to the original TFM if adaptation does not improve held-out validation. Implemented as TabICLv2-Retouche on TabICLv2 and evaluated on TabArena-Lite (51 datasets across binary, multiclass, and regression).

Result: TabICLv2-Retouche achieved top ranking on the TabArena-Lite leaderboard; it lifts aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and lies on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus training and inference time, demonstrating strong accuracy gains with light per-task tuning and ensembling.

Conclusion: Input-space residual adapters offer a practical, architecture-agnostic path to dataset-specific adaptation for TFMs, delivering substantial performance gains with efficient resource use and preserving safety via an identity guard; this approach could generalize to other TFMs and tasks, promoting broader adoption of lightweight adaptation strategies.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs), such as TabPFN-2.6, TabICLv2, ConTextTab, Mitra, LimiX, and TabDPT, achieve strong zero-shot performance through in-context learning, but their inductive biases remain fixed at inference time. Adapting a pretrained TFM to a specific dataset or task typically requires either full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive, or parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT) such as LoRA, which must be tailored to the internal architecture of each TFM. Furthermore, the evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed \citep{tanna_exploring_2026,rubachev_finetuning_2025}. We introduce TFM-Retouche, a lightweight input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic by design with respect to the frozen TFM backbone. TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align the input data with the inductive biases of the pretrained model. The adapter is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM, with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM whenever adaptation does not help on held-out validation. On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche -- the framework instantiated on TabICLv2 -- is the top-ranked method on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and sitting on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus both training and inference time.

</details>


### [221] [When Brain Networks Travel: Learning Beyond Site](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06050)
*Yingxu Wang,Kunyu Zhang,Yanwu Yang,Thomas Wolfers,Yujie Wu,Siyang Gao,Nan Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORE is a cross-site robust brain network learning framework that decouples site confounds, exploits a cross-site scaffold of diagnostic connections, analyzes transient dynamics on this scaffold via lightweight descriptors, uses a line-graph representation for pathway-level transfer, and applies a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating to preserve individual variability. It achieves state-of-the-art gains in leave-one-site-out tests across multiple datasets and parcellations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in fMRI brain network analysis is hampered by site-specific confounders that create non-pathological shortcuts, and by temporal averaging that masks transient dynamics. A unified approach is needed to decouple site biases, capture reproducible diagnostic edges, and model dynamic pathways transferable across sites and parcellations.

Method: 1) Site-aware confounder decoupling to remove site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic edges. 2) Profiling transient pathway dynamics on the scaffold with lightweight temporal descriptors. 3) Organizing scaffold edges into a line graph to enable transferable pathway-level modeling. 4) A prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that uses scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability.

Result: In leave-one-site-out experiments on ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD, CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines with up to 6.7% relative gains and shows robustness to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

Conclusion: CORE provides a unified, robust framework for cross-site brain network learning by decoupling site confounds, leveraging a cross-site connectivity scaffold to capture transient dynamics, and applying population priors through subject-adaptive gating. The approach yields consistent improvements across diverse datasets and parcellations, demonstrating practical cross-site generalization for fMRI-based brain network analysis.

Abstract: Graph-based learning on functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has shown strong potential for brain network analysis. However, existing methods degrade under cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) settings because site-conditioned confounders induce non-pathological shortcuts, while functional connectivity constructed by temporal averaging obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites. In this paper, we propose Cross-site OOD Robust brain nEtwork (CORE), a unified framework for brain network learning across unseen sites. CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to mitigate site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic connectivity edges. It then profiles transient pathway dynamics over this scaffold using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling. Finally, CORE introduces a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that leverages scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Extensive experiments under leave-one-site-out evaluation on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD) show that CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain. Furthermore, CORE remains robust to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

</details>


### [222] [Towards Generation-Efficient Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06053)
*Mingcheng Zhu,Yu Liu,Tingting Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-cost uncertainty estimation for LLMs via partial generation and input-only signals. Introduces Logit Magnitude (top-M logit evidence from early prefixes) and MetaUE (input-only estimator distilled from generation-based uncertainty). A unified framework for early estimation over the autoregressive process; experiments show partial generations often suffice and both methods are effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimation is critical for high-stakes deployment of LLMs (healthcare, finance) where hallucinations can be fluent yet wrong. Existing methods require full autoregressive generations, incurring high cost and delays; a need exists for early or input-only uncertainty estimation.

Method: Formulates uncertainty estimation as an early-estimation problem along the LLM generation process within a unified framework that categorizes estimators by observed information. Proposes Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence from an early-stopped generation prefix; and MetaUE, a distillation-based, lightweight input-only estimator trained with generation-derived uncertainty scores.

Result: Empirical evaluation on general and domain benchmarks shows Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance; partial generations suffice for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE provides competitive input-only accuracy in several settings, demonstrating viable low-cost alternatives to full generation-based uncertainty estimation.

Conclusion: Effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with less generation than commonly assumed, enabling earlier detection of unreliable outputs.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is important for deploying LLMs in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and finance, where hallucinations can appear fluent and plausible while being factually incorrect, making it difficult for users to judge whether an output should be trusted. Existing methods require one or more full autoregressive generations to estimate uncertainty, which introduces substantial inference cost and often delays uncertainty assessment. In this paper, we investigate whether effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or even input-only information. Specifically, we first develop a unified framework that formulates uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over the autoregressive generation process of LLMs. This framework organises existing and proposed estimators by the information they observe, ranging from multi-generation to input-only prediction, and clarifies the performance-cost trade-off underlying different uncertainty estimation methods. Building on this view, we study two largely underexplored low-cost settings: estimating uncertainty with part of the generation, and predicting uncertainty from the input prompt. We propose Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence to estimate uncertainty from an early-stopped generation prefix, and MetaUE, which distils generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores. Extensive experiments on general and domain-specific benchmarks show that Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance, and partial generations of LLMs are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE further provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings. These findings suggest that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed, enabling unreliable responses to be identified earlier.

</details>


### [223] [Towards Self-Explainable Document Visual Question Answering with Chain-of-Explanation Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06058)
*Kjetil Indrehus,Adrian Duric,Changkyu Choi,Ali Ramezani-Kebrya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-explainable DocVQA approach (CoExVQA) uses a chain-of-explanation: identify question-relevant evidence, explicitly localize the answer region, and decode the answer solely from the grounded region, enabling inspectable multi-modal reasoning and achieving state-of-the-art explainable performance on PFL-DocVQA with +12% ANLS over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DocVQA models largely operate as black boxes by entangling evidence selection and answer localization, hampering verifiability of predictions and their reliance on visual evidence.

Method: Propose a chain-of-explanation framework (CoExVQA) that (1) identifies question-relevant evidence, (2) localizes the answer region, and (3) decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region, enabling self-explanation and verifiability across modalities.

Result: Empirical results on PFL-DocVQA show that decoding restricted to grounded evidence yields state-of-the-art explainable DocVQA performance, with a 12% ANLS improvement over current explainable baselines, and provides transparent, verifiable predictions.

Conclusion: Grounded, stepwise decoding enables verifiable multi-modal reasoning in DocVQA and improves explainability without sacrificing strong performance.

Abstract: Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) requires vision-language models to reason not only about what information in a document is relevant to a question, but also where the answer is grounded on the page. Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization and operate largely as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. We propose CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework with a grounded reasoning process through a chain-of-explanation design. CoExVQA first identifies question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localizes the answer region, and finally decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region. Prediction via CoExVQA's chain-of-explanation enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process across modalities. Empirical results show that restricting decoding to grounded evidence achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over the current explainable baselines while providing transparent and verifiable predictions.

</details>


### [224] [Causal Reinforcement Learning for Complex Card Games: A Magic The Gathering Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06066)
*Cristiano da Costa Cunha,Ajmal Mian,Tim French,Wei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MTG-Causal-RL introduces a complex, causally-structured benchmark for causal reinforcement learning in a masked-action, partially observed domain (Magic: The Gathering), enabling causal credit assignment, structural transfer, and auditable policy analysis; baseline methods (Masked PPO, CGFA-PPO) show competitive in-distribution win rates and diagnostic insights beyond overall win rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of benchmarks that simultaneously integrate sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure, enabling rigorous evaluation of causal credit assignment, policy auditability, and structural transfer in RL.

Method: A Gymnasium-based MTG-Causal-RL benchmark featuring a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete space, five Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. The paper introduces CGFA-PPO, which uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss, and evaluates against random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and scalar-control baselines. Evaluation uses paired seeds, paired-bootstrap CIs, and Holm-Bonferroni corrections across pre-registered families; results include per-factor calibration trajectories, leave-one-out transfer gaps, and policy auditability metrics.

Result: Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO achieve competitive in-distribution win rates surpassing random baselines. Per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfers reveal diagnostic structure not captured by overall win rate alone, supporting the benchmark's ability to diagnose causal reasoning and policy robustness under masked actions.

Conclusion: MTG-Causal-RL provides a rich, auditable testbed combining causal interfaces with partially observed, high-dimensional action spaces, enabling evaluation of causal credit assignment, structural transfer, and SCM-grounded policy auditability for causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research. The benchmark and baseline results are released with evaluation protocol for broad reuse.

Abstract: Causal reinforcement learning (RL) lacks benchmarks for complex systems that combine sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. We introduce MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. Every episode exposes causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces, making causal credit assignment, leave-one-out cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability first-class metrics. We adapt a panel of reference baselines: random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and an architecture-matched scalar control. We propose Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) as a reference causal agent that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. All comparisons use paired seeds, paired-bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction within pre-registered families. Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates and exceed the random baseline; per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps expose diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot. We release the benchmark, reference-baseline results, and full evaluation protocol openly. By coupling a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, MTG-Causal-RL gives causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot pose together: causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

</details>


### [225] [Normalized Architectures are Natively 4-Bit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06067)
*Maxim Fishman,Brian Chmiel,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: nGPT constrains weights/hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, making 4-bit training robust and eliminating need for transform-based calibration; demonstrates stable end-to-end NVFP4 training on 1.2B dense and 3B/30B MoE models; robustness arises from constructive signal accumulation in dot products, improving SNR and flattening the loss landscape, with stronger gains at larger hidden dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient low-precision training of large language models by removing calibration interventions (e.g., random Hadamard transforms, per-tensor scaling) and ensuring stable NVFP4 training at scale.

Method: Introduce nGPT architecture with unit-hypersphere constraint on weights and hidden activations; evaluate via end-to-end training at 4-bit precision on a 1.2B dense model and hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE models up to 3B/30B parameters; analyze quantization noise in dot products and correlations; compare against standard architectures.

Result: nGPT shows inherent robustness to 4-bit quantization, removing the need for calibration steps, achieving stable NVFP4 training; the hypersphere constraint induces weak positive correlations in element-wise products that accumulate constructively across the hidden dimension, yielding higher SNR and a flatter loss landscape; effects strengthen with larger hidden dimension; validation on both dense and MoE models; reference implementation provided.

Conclusion: Hypersphere-constrained weights/representations confer intrinsic robustness to low-precision arithmetic, enabling scalable, efficient training at 4-bit precision without extra calibration, with benefits scaling with model width.

Abstract: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency. We show that nGPT, an architecture that constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, is inherently more robust to low-precision arithmetic. This removes the need for interventions-such as applying random Hadamard transforms and performing per-tensor scaling calculations-to preserve model quality, and it enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training. We validate this approach on both a 1.2B dense model and hybrid (Mamba-Transformer) MoE models of up to 3B/30B parameters. We trace this robustness to the dot product: while quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated in both standard and normalized architectures, the signal behaves differently. In nGPT, the hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations among the element-wise products, leading to a constructive accumulation of the signal across the hidden dimension while the noise continues to average out. This yields a higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and a flatter loss landscape, with the effect strengthening as the hidden dimension grows, suggesting increasing advantages at scale. A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/anonymous452026/ngpt-nvfp4

</details>


### [226] [Understanding diffusion models requires rethinking (again) generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06077)
*Pierre Marion,Yu-Han Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The abstract argues that diffusion-model generalization requires new theoretical frameworks beyond classical learning theory and benign overfitting; memorization is incompatible with true generalization in diffusion models; existing explanations (capacity limits, optimization-induced regularization, architectural biases) are incomplete in their interactions; the paper advocates pivoting to studying what is learned before memorization and presents an empirical CIFAR-10 study to distill key open questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models challenge conventional generalization theories; memorization produces copies rather than novel data, yet models generalize in practice. The authors seek a foundational shift to understand pre-memorization learning and the mechanisms enabling generalization.

Method: The paper presents a theoretical stance plus an empirical study. It analyzes diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10 and distills findings into concrete open questions about generalization in diffusion models.

Result: The discussion emphasizes that standard explanations for generalization are insufficient and that memorization and generalization are not easily reconciled; there is a need for new frameworks and a focus on the pre-memorization phase. The CIFAR-10 study yields concrete open questions and a roadmap for understanding what diffusion models learn before memorization.

Conclusion: A field-wide pivot is urged toward investigating the pre-memorization phase to understand what diffusion models learn and how generalization emerges, accompanied by a set of concrete open questions to guide future research.

Abstract: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires fundamentally new theoretical frameworks that go beyond both classical statistical learning theory and the benign overfitting paradigm developed for supervised learning. In diffusion models, unlike in supervised learning, memorization of training data and generalization to novel samples are incompatible: a model that has fully memorized its training set generates copies rather than novel data. Several theoretical explanations for why practical diffusion models nevertheless generalize have been proposed, based on capacity limitations, implicit regularization from optimization, or architectural inductive biases, but their interactions remain unclear. We argue that the field should pivot from explaining why the diffusion models do not memorize to investigating what the model actually learns during pre-memorization phase. To highlight our stance, we conduct empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, and we distill the findings into concrete open questions that we believe are key to improve understanding of generalization in diffusion models.

</details>


### [227] [Fast Gauss-Newton for Multiclass Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06081)
*Mikalai Korbit,Mario Zanon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposes multiclass GGN into true-vs-rest and within-competitor parts, introducing a PSD under-approximation (FGN) exact for binary classification and solvable via a scalable whitened CG system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational burden of full GGN in multiclass softmax cross-entropy by exploiting a true-vs-rest structure and a within-competitor covariance decomposition.

Method: Exact decomposition into true-vs-rest term plus within-competitor covariance; keep the true-vs-rest term (FGN) and drop the within-competitor part to obtain a PSD under-approximation; use a scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy; formulate the damped update as a whitened row-space system with one row per example; solve with conjugate gradient using Jv and vJp of the scalar margin map.

Result: FGN yields a PSD under-approximation of multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification; distances to full GGN depend on competitor mass and damping; empirical results show FGN stays close when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and diverges as missing covariance grows.

Conclusion: FGN provides a scalable, stable curvature approximation for multiclass problems with large class counts, with exactness in binary cases and predictable behavior depending on covariance structure; enables efficient, matrix-free CG updates for neural network training with softmax cross-entropy.

Abstract: In multiclass softmax cross-entropy, the full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature couples all output logits through the softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products harder to scale as the number of classes grows. We show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed exactly into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term.
