Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) retains the first term and drops the second, yielding a positive semidefinite under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. The derivation uses an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy: the loss and gradient are unchanged, and the approximation enters only at the curvature level. Exploiting the FGN curvature structure, the damped update can be written as an equivalent whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch example. We solve this system matrix-free by conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map. Targeted mechanism experiments and an evaluation on a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predictions from the decomposition: FGN stays closest to the full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

</details>


### [228] [Beyond Autoregressive RTG: Conditioning via Injection Outside Sequential Modeling in Decision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06104)
*Yongyi Wang,Hanyu Liu,Lingfeng Li,Bozhou Chen,Ang Li,Qirui Zheng,Xionghui Yang,Chucai Wang,Wenxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SlimDT trims the RTG token and injects RTG into states; reduces sequence length by ~1/3; improves inference efficiency; matches or exceeds DT and SOTA on D4RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DT uses a scalar RTG token as a stand-in for future rewards, which adds no information per token but adds computational cost and conflicts with transformer efficiency due to quadratic attention; aim to improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.

Method: Replace autoregressive sequence of (RTG, state, action) with (state, action) sequence, embedding RTG into state representations before the Transformer; keep the same training objective but without RTG token; train with offline RL data; evaluate on D4RL.

Result: SlimDT outperforms standard DT across various D4RL tasks and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art offline RL methods.

Conclusion: Separating a sparse conditioning signal (RTG) from a dense, information-rich sequence yields compute savings and performance gains; the approach improves inference efficiency and maintains or improves task performance.

Abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) formulates offline reinforcement learning as autoregressive sequence modeling, achieving promising results by predicting actions from a sequence of Return-to-Go (RTG), state, and action tokens. However, RTG is a scalar that summarizes future rewards, containing far less information than typical state or action vectors, yet it consumes the same computational budget per token. Worse, the self-attention cost of Transformers grows quadratically with sequence length, so including RTG as a separate token adds unnecessary overhead. We propose SlimDT, which removes RTG from the autoregressive sequence. Instead, we inject RTG information into the state representations before the sequential modeling step, allowing the Transformer to process only a compact (state, action) sequence. This reduces the sequence length by one-third, directly improving inference efficiency. On the D4RL benchmark, SlimDT surpasses standard DT across various tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods. Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence thus yields both computational gains and higher task performance.

</details>


### [229] [BoostLLM: Boosting-inspired LLM Fine-tuning for Few-shot Tabular Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06117)
*Yi-Siang Wang,Kuan-Yu Chen,Yu-Chen Den,Darby Tien-Hao Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BoostLLM reframes LLM fine-tuning as multi-round residual boosting with sequential PEFT adapters, incorporating a tabular path-based view as a structured teacher; shows improvements over standard fine-tuning, matches/surpasses XGBoost across shots, scales with stronger trees and longer boosting, suggesting boosting as a general training principle for structured-data LLM fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle in low-data regimes for structured/tabular data; need a training principle that leverages boosting and structured inductive bias to improve data efficiency.

Method: Train sequential PEFT adapters as weak learners in a residual boosting loop. Include a second input view of decision-tree paths alongside raw features; early-training path view guides learning, then model shifts to feature-driven representations. Evaluate across multiple LLM backbones and datasets; compare to XGBoost and GPT-4o-based baselines; explore scaling with stronger trees and extended boosting horizon with stabilization.

Result: BoostLLM yields consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning, matches or surpasses XGBoost across varying shot counts, and outperforms GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model; scaling (stronger trees, longer boosting) yields further gains; suggests boosting can be a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning on structured data.

Conclusion: Boosting is a viable framework for efficient LLM fine-tuning on tabular data, especially in low-data settings; jointly leveraging PEFT-based weak learners and a tree-path inductive bias can yield competitive performance and scalability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adapted to tabular prediction by serializing structured features into natural language, but their performance in low-data regimes remains limited compared to gradient-boosted decision trees (GBDTs). In this work, we revisit the boosting paradigm, traditionally associated with tree ensembles, and ask whether it can be applied as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning. We propose BoostLLM, a framework that transforms parameter-efficient fine-tuning into a multi-round residual optimization process by training sequential PEFT adapters as weak learners. To incorporate tabular inductive bias, BoostLLM integrates decision-tree paths as a second input view alongside raw features; analysis reveals that the path view acts as a structured teacher in early training steps before the model shifts toward feature-driven representations. Empirically, BoostLLM achieves consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning across multiple LLM backbones and datasets, matching or surpassing XGBoost across a wide range of shot counts and outperforming GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model. We further show that the framework scales: pairing with stronger tree models and extended boosting horizons yields additional gains under appropriate stabilization. These results suggest that boosting can serve as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning, particularly in low-data regimes for structured data.

</details>


### [230] [Listwise Policy Optimization: Group-based RLVR as Target-Projection on the LLM Response Simplex](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06139)
*Yun Qu,Qi Wang,Yixiu Mao,Heming Zou,Yuhang Jiang,Yingyue Li,Wutong Xu,Lizhou Cai,Weijie Liu,Clive Bai,Kai Yang,Yangkun Chen,Saiyong Yang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) for RL with verifiable rewards in LLMs, recasting group-based policy gradient as implicit projection onto a target distribution on the response simplex and introducing explicit target-projection via exact divergence minimization within a constrained proximal RL objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the implicit target in group-based policy gradient methods for RLHF-style training and provide a principled, stable optimization framework that guarantees improvements and allows flexible divergence choices.

Method: Restrict the RL objective to the response simplex and perform exact divergence-based projection (target-projection) during policy updates, decoupling the projection from the policy step to ensure monotonic improvement and stability; derive monotonic ascent properties and self-correcting gradients.

Result: Empirically, LPO yields consistent improvements over standard policy-gradient baselines under matched targets, while preserving stability and diversity of responses across diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones.

Conclusion: LPO offers a unified, target-projection framework for RL with verifiable rewards, enabling stable, monotonic improvement and flexible divergence choices, with broad applicability to LLM policy optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for large language models (LLMs) post-training to incentivize reasoning capacity. Among existing recipes, group-based policy gradient is prevalent, which samples a group of responses per prompt and updates the policy via group-relative advantage signals. This work reveals that these optimization strategies share a common geometric structure: each implicitly defines a target distribution on the response simplex and projects toward it via first-order approximation. Building on this insight, we propose Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) to explicitly conduct the target-projection, which demystifies the implicit target by restricting the proximal RL objective to the response simplex, and then projects the policy via exact divergence minimization. This framework provides (i) monotonic improvement on the listwise objective with bounded, zero-sum, and self-correcting projection gradients, and (ii) flexibility in divergence selection with distinct structural properties through the decoupled projection step. On diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones, LPO consistently improves training performance over typical policy gradient baselines under matched targets, while intrinsically preserving optimization stability and response diversity.

</details>


### [231] [SymDrift: One-Shot Generative Modeling under Symmetries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06140)
*Samir Darouich,Vinh Tong,Lluís Pastor-Pérez,Tanja Bien,Loay Mualem,Mathias Niepert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymDrift: a symmetry-aware one-shot drift model for 3D physical systems that achieves invariant generation without costly symmetrization, via (i) coordinate-space symmetrized drift with optimal alignment and (ii) a G-invariant embedding; delivers state-of-the-art one-shot performance, competitive with multi-step methods, and up to 40x speedups for high-throughput tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D physical systems (e.g., molecules) require generation distributions invariant to global symmetries (rotations). While equivariant diffusion/flow methods can handle invariances, they are costly due to multi-step sampling. Drifting models offer single-step generation but face symmetry-induced drift mismatches between the generator and the symmetrized target distribution, unless costly symmetrization is performed.

Method: Introduce SymDrift with two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment to enforce symmetry consistency, and (ii) a G-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. This yields a symmetry-aware drifting field that aligns with the target distribution without explicit symmetrization of the empirical data.

Result: Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on standard benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation, while remaining competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches. One-shot inference reduces computational overhead by up to 40× compared to existing baselines, enabling high-throughput applications such as virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration.

Conclusion: SymDrift provides a practical, efficient path to symmetry-invariant, single-shot generative modeling of 3D physical systems. By integrating a symmetry-aware drift and invariant embeddings, it matches or exceeds one-shot baselines and approaches the performance of costly multi-step methods, with substantial speedups suitable for large-scale tasks.

Abstract: Generative modeling of physical systems, such as molecules, requires learning distributions that are invariant under global symmetries, such as rotations in three-dimensional space. Equivariant diffusion and flow matching models can incorporate such invariances effectively, even when trained on a non-invariant empirical distribution, but they typically rely on costly multi-step sampling. Recently, drifting models have emerged as an efficient alternative, enabling single-step generation and achieving state-of-the-art performance in generative modeling tasks. However, we show that drifting models face a symmetry-specific challenge, since an equivariant generator does not generally produce the same drifting field as the one obtained from the symmetrized target distribution. Addressing this issue would require expensive symmetrization of the empirical distribution. To avoid this cost, we propose SymDrift, a framework that makes the drifting field itself symmetry-aware. We introduce two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment, and (ii) a $G$-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on standard benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation, while remaining competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches. By enabling one-shot inference, SymDrift reduces computational overhead by up to 40$\times$ compared to existing baselines, making it promising for high-throughput applications such as virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration.

</details>


### [232] [Matrix-Valued Optimism is Matrix-Valued Augmentation: Additive Hybrid Designs for Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06141)
*Jiayi Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends AL and optimistic primal-dual equivalence to matrix-valued corrections; proves an additivity principle for symmetric corrections; derives a closed-form hybrid rule to split corrections and set steps; experiments show improvements over endpoints under controlled ill-conditioning, with a limitation on cancellation requiring large corrections as conditioning worsens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal-dual updates beyond scalar parameters, enabling matrix-valued corrections to improve stability and performance in equality-constrained optimization.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving an additivity principle for symmetric matrix corrections; derive a step-size–limited design problem; obtain a closed-form hybrid rule that allocates a matrix correction between augmented and optimistic channels and selects primal/dual steps using local spectral weights; empirical evaluation on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled constraint-Jacobian conditioning.

Result: An ideal (unconstrained) primal trajectory depends only on the sum of correction matrices, not on how it is split; augmented correction affects primal curvature while optimistic correction affects dual memory scale; the hybrid rule outperforms pure endpoints and aligns with a grid-search oracle, remaining competitive with first-order baselines under moderate ill-conditioning.

Conclusion: Matrix-valued corrections offer design flexibility while preserving an additive principle; exact cancellation demands increasingly large corrections as the constraint Jacobian’s conditioning worsens, outlining a fundamental limitation but with practical gains in many settings.

Abstract: Augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal--dual methods stabilize equality-constrained optimization through seemingly different mechanisms: the former adds constraint-dependent primal curvature, while the latter adds dual memory. Recent work has shown that these mechanisms are equivalent for scalar parameters. We extend this equivalence to matrix-valued correction. We prove an additivity principle: for symmetric matrix parameters, the ideal primal trajectory depends only on the summed correction matrix, not on how it is split between augmented and optimistic channels. This exposes a design freedom: algebraically equivalent decompositions can have different finite-step feasibility because augmented correction affects primal curvature, whereas optimistic correction affects the scale of the dual memory correction. We formulate the resulting step-size-limited design problem and derive a closed-form hybrid rule that selects a matrix correction, splits it between the two channels, and chooses primal and dual steps using local spectral weights. Experiments on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled constraint-Jacobian conditioning show that the hybrid design improves over pure augmented and pure optimistic endpoints, closely tracks a grid-search hybrid oracle, and is competitive with first-order primal--dual baselines under mild-to-moderate ill-conditioning. The experiments also identify the expected limitation: exact cancellation requires increasingly large matrix corrections as the constraint Jacobian becomes ill-conditioned.

</details>


### [233] [Unifying Goal-Conditioned RL and Unsupervised Skill Learning via Control-Maximization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06145)
*Alireza Modirshanechi,Benjamin Eysenbach,Peter Dayan,Eric Schulz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies GCRL and MISL under the umbrella of control maximization; shows three canonical GCRL formulations are fundamentally inequivalent yet share a common interpretation via goal-sensitivity; MISL objectives bound by formulation-specific downstream goal-sensitivities, establishing a correspondence between MISL and downstream GCRL tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address theoretical gaps in why MISL-based pretraining aids goal-reaching and reconcile the diverse definitions in GCRL and MISL; provide a principled foundation linking skill diversity to downstream goal-sensitivity.

Method: Theoretical analysis that (1) formalizes three canonical GCRL formulations, (2) proves their inequivalence, (3) interprets good GCRL performance as high sensitivity of future trajectories to the commanded goal, (4) views MISL as measures of skill-sensitivity, (5) derives bounds showing MISL objectives are governed by downstream goal-sensitivity, and (6) establishes a mapping from each GCRL formulation to a corresponding MISL objective.

Result: Proves the three GCRL formulations are inequivalent (can yield different optimal policies in the same environment); shows a common interpretation in terms of goal-sensitive trajectory control; derives bounds demonstrating MISL objectives bound by the downstream goal-sensitivities; demonstrates that for every GCRL formulation there exists a matching MISL objective such that greater skill diversity increases downstream goal sensitivity.

Conclusion: Provides a theoretical foundation for RL pretraining in goal-conditioned settings; guides practitioners on selecting pretraining objectives based on the intended downstream task class and clarifies how MISL-based diversity translates into improved goal sensitivity for downstream goals.

Abstract: Unsupervised pretraining has driven empirical advances in goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), but its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. In particular, an influential class of methods, mutual information skill learning (MISL), discovers behaviorally diverse skills that can later be used for downstream goal-reaching. However, it remains a theoretical mystery why skills learned through MISL should support goal-reaching. A subtle challenge is that both GCRL and MISL are umbrella terms: different GCRL tasks use distinct criteria for measuring goal-reaching performance, while different MISL methods optimize distinct notions of behavioral diversity. We address this challenge and unify GCRL and MISL as instances of control maximization. We identify three canonical GCRL formulations and prove that they are fundamentally inequivalent: they can induce incompatible optimal policies even in the same environment. Nevertheless, they all share a common interpretation: a well-performing goal-conditioned policy is one whose future trajectory is highly sensitive to the commanded goal, with the precise notion of sensitivity determined by the GCRL formulation. Noting that MISL objectives can be understood as measures of skill-sensitivity akin to goal-sensitivity, we show that MISL objectives are bounded by formulation-specific downstream goal-sensitivities. These bounds establish a precise correspondence between MISL methods and downstream GCRL tasks: for every GCRL formulation, there exists a matching MISL objective for which more diverse skills afford greater downstream goal sensitivity. Our results thus lay a theoretical foundation for RL pretraining and have important practical implications, such as suggesting which pretraining objectives to use when a user cares about a specific class of downstream tasks.

</details>


### [234] [AdaGamma: State-Dependent Discounting for Temporal Adaptation in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06149)
*Yaomin Wang,Jianting Pan,Ran Tian,Xiaoyang Li,Yu Zhang,Hengle Qin,Tianshu YU*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaGamma introduces a state-dependent discount in deep actor-critic RL with a return-consistency regularizer, showing theoretical well-posedness and empirical gains across SAC/PPO and an online JD Logistics A/B test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL uses a fixed discount gamma, producing a fixed planning horizon and bootstrapping strength. State-dependent discounting is appealing to match value backup structure to each state, but naive deep actor–critic methods can suffer from TD-error collapse and instability.

Method: Learn a parametric state-dependent discount function alongside the value function, and optimize a return-consistency objective to regularize backups. The paper analyzes the Bellman operator under state-dependent discounting for well-posedness and demonstrates integration into SAC and PPO. Empirical evaluation includes continuous-control benchmarks and an online A/B test on the JD Logistics platform.

Result: AdaGamma yields consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks when attached to SAC/PPO, and achieves statistically significant gains in the JD Logistics online A/B test.

Conclusion: State-dependent discounting can be effective in deep RL when paired with a return-consistency regularizer that prevents degenerate target manipulation; AdaGamma provides a practical, theory-backed approach with broad applicability.

Abstract: The discount factor in reinforcement learning controls both the effective planning horizon and the strength of bootstrapping, yet most deep RL methods use a single fixed value across all states. While state-dependent discounting is conceptually appealing, naive deep actor--critic implementations can become unstable and degenerate toward TD-error collapse. We propose AdaGamma, a practical deep actor--critic method for state-dependent discounting that learns a state-dependent discount function together with a return-consistency objective to regularize the induced backup structure. On the theory side, we analyze the Bellman operator induced by state-dependent discounting and establish its basic well-posedness properties under suitable conditions. Empirically, AdaGamma integrates into both SAC and PPO, yielding consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks, and achieves statistically significant gains in an online A/B test on the JD Logistics platform. These results suggest that state-dependent discounting can be made effective in deep RL when coupled with a return-consistency objective that prevents degenerate target manipulation.

</details>


### [235] [Grokking or Glitching? How Low-Precision Drives Slingshot Loss Spikes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06152)
*Liu Hanqing,Jianjun Cao,Yuanze Li,Zijian Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Floating-point precision limits trigger Slingshot spikes via Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI), causing gradient drift and exponential growth of means, reframing training anomalies as finite-precision dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain Slingshot spikes beyond intrinsic optimization dynamics by identifying a numerical-precision mechanism that can drive abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence.

Method: Theoretical analysis of backpropagation under finite-precision arithmetic: show that when the correct-class logit gap exceeds the absorption threshold, the correct-class gradient rounds to zero while incorrect-class gradients remain nonzero, breaking gradient zero-sum. Derive a positive feedback loop that causes growth of global classifier mean and feature mean, formalizing Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI) and its impact on training dynamics and loss spikes.

Result: NFI creates a numerical feedback mechanism leading to exponential growth in parameter and feature norms, explaining loss spikes and gradient reappearance. Partial absorption may not produce visible spikes yet still disrupt gradient zero-sum and drive rapid growth; Slingshot is reframed as finite-precision dynamics rather than purely optimization behavior.

Conclusion: Slingshot is a consequence of floating-point finite-precision dynamics; NFI provides a testable explanation for abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence in late-stage training, suggesting numerical effects must be considered in interpreting training dynamics.

Abstract: Deep neural networks exhibit periodic loss spikes during unregularized long-term training, a phenomenon known as the "Slingshot Mechanism." Existing work usually attributes this to intrinsic optimization dynamics, but its triggering mechanism remains unclear. This paper proves that this phenomenon is a result of floating-point arithmetic precision limits. As training enters a high-confidence stage, the difference between the correct-class logit and the other logits may exceed the absorption-error threshold. Then during backpropagation, the gradient of the correct class is rounded exactly to zero, while the gradients of the incorrect classes remain nonzero. This breaks the zero-sum constraint of gradients across classes and introduces a systematic drift in the parameter update of the classifier layer. We prove that this drift forms a positive feedback loop with the feature, causing the global classifier mean and the global feature mean to grow exponentially. We call this mechanism Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI). This mechanism explains the rapid norm growth before a Slingshot spike, the subsequent reappearance of gradients, and the resulting loss spike. We further show that NFI is not equivalent to an observed loss spike: in more practical tasks, partial absorption may not produce visible spikes, but it can still break the zero-sum constraint and drive rapid growth of parameter norms. Our results reinterpret Slingshot as a numerical dynamic of finite-precision training, and provide a testable explanation for abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence in late-stage training.

</details>


### [236] [Entropy-Regularized Adjoint Matching for Offline RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06156)
*Abdelghani Ghanem,Mounir Ghogho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ME-AM integrates maximum entropy with adjoint matching in flow-based offline RL to overcome popularity bias and restricted exploration, using a Mirror Descent entropy objective and a Mixture Behavior Prior to broaden policy support while preserving vector-field continuity; shows competitive or superior results on sparse-reward continuous control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) stabilizes policy optimization but remains bound to a fixed behavior distribution, causing popularity bias and limited off-manifold exploration. Existing residual Gaussian policies reintroduce unimodal expressivity limits. A unified, flow-based approach could enable multimodal, high-reward actions without losing stability.

Method: Introduce Maximum Entropy Adjoint Matching (ME-AM) with two mechanisms: (1) Mirror Descent entropy maximization within the continuous flow framework to counteract popularity bias and enable better extraction of high-reward actions from offline data; (2) a Mixture Behavior Prior that expands the geometric support to include out-of-distribution high-reward regions, while preserving absolute continuity of the generative vector field.

Result: Empirically, ME-AM achieves competitive or superior performance relative to prior State-of-the-Art methods across a diverse suite of sparse-reward continuous control tasks.

Conclusion: ME-AM provides a unified, entropy-regularized, flow-based offline RL framework that broadens policy support and mitigates bias without sacrificing the stability or continuity of the generative model, yielding robust action selections in sparse-reward settings.

Abstract: Integrating expressive generative policies, such as flow-matching models, into offline reinforcement learning (RL) allows agents to capture complex, multi-modal behaviors. While Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) stabilizes policy optimization via the continuous adjoint method, it remains inherently bound to the fixed behavior distribution. This dependence induces a \textit{popularity bias} that can suppress high-reward actions in low-density regions, and creates a \textit{support binding} that restricts off-manifold exploration. Existing workarounds, such as appending \textit{residual} Gaussian policies, often re-introduce the expressivity bottlenecks associated with unimodal distributions. In this work, we propose \textit{Maximum Entropy Adjoint Matching} (ME-AM), a unified framework that addresses these limitations within the continuous flow formulation. ME-AM incorporates two mechanisms: (1) a Mirror Descent entropy maximization objective that mitigates the popularity bias to facilitate the extraction of optimal policies from offline datasets, and (2) a \textit{Mixture Behavior Prior} that mathematically broadens the geometric support to encompass out-of-distribution high-reward regions. By exploring this extended geometry, ME-AM identifies robust actions while preserving the absolute continuity of the generative vector field. Empirically, ME-AM demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to prior state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods across a diverse suite of sparse-reward continuous control environments.

</details>


### [237] [One Algorithm, Two Goals: Dual Scoring for Parameter and Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06166)
*Xinrui Chen,Liu Yang,Ou Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jointly selects parameters and data for fine-tuning via a bilevel optimization framework, introducing DualSFT which uses a shared gradient interaction matrix to produce one-shot dual-scoring rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce fine-tuning cost while maintaining or improving target-task performance by coordinating parameter masking and data subset selection, rather than using separate, potentially redundant scoring.

Method: Formulate parameter mask and data subset selection as two bilevel optimization problems with a common validation objective. Derive a local response-surrogate scoring rule; show parameter importance and data utility are column- and row-wise aggregations of a single gradient interaction matrix, yielding a row-column correspondence. Propose DualSFT, a one-shot algorithm that jointly scores and selects parameters and data from shared gradient statistics using first- and second-order approximations.

Result: Single-axis DualSFT variants improve target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within comparison groups; full DualSFT achieves a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets, on 3B-9B LLMs.

Conclusion: A shared gradient-based scoring framework enables efficient co-selection of parameters and data for fine-tuning; one-shot DualSFT provides improved joint performance under budget constraints compared to baselines, with potential for broader applicability to large models.

Abstract: In Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning, parameter and data selection are common strategies for reducing fine-tuning cost, yet they are typically driven by separate scoring mechanisms. When a parameter mask and data subset jointly determine restricted fine-tuning, this separation incurs redundant overhead and makes coordinated selection difficult. We cast parameter and data selection as two bilevel selection problems under a common validation objective and derive a shared local response-surrogate scoring rule. Under first- and second-order validation-improvement approximations, parameter importance and data utility emerge as column-wise and row-wise aggregations of a single gradient interaction matrix, yielding a closed-form row-column correspondence for co-extracting both signals. Building on this structure, we propose DualSFT (Dual-Selection Fine-Tuning), a one-shot dual-scoring algorithm that produces a parameter mask and data subset from shared gradient statistics. On 3B-9B LLMs, single-axis DualSFT variants strengthen target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within their comparison groups, while full DualSFT yields a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets.

</details>


### [238] [Mean Mode Screaming: Mean--Variance Split Residuals for 1000-Layer Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06169)
*Pengqi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Very deep diffusion transformers suffer a mean-dominated collapse (Mean Mode Screaming, MMS) that homogenizes token representations. The authors diagnose the mechanism via gradient decomposition and Softmax Jacobian geometry, and propose MV-Split residuals to stabilize training, enabling successful scaling to 1000 layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable stable training and scaling of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to hundreds/thousands of layers and understand the underlying collapse mechanism driving mean-dominated representations.

Method: Mechanistic auditing of training dynamics; exact decomposition of gradients into mean-coherent and centered components; analysis of the null-space effect of the Softmax Jacobian on attention-gradients; introduction of Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) residuals combining a centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement.

Result: MV-Split prevents divergent collapse in a 400-layer single-stream DiT, closely following the baseline trajectory without crashing and outperforming token-isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale; demonstrates stable training at 1000 layers on a scale-validation run.

Conclusion: Mean-dominated collapse in ultra-deep DiTs is tractable with MV-Split residuals, enabling stable training at extreme depths and providing a mechanistic account of MMS via gradient decomposition and Softmax Jacobian geometry.

Abstract: Scaling Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to hundreds of layers introduces a structural vulnerability: networks can enter a silent, mean-dominated collapse state that homogenizes token representations and suppresses centered variation. Through mechanistic auditing, we isolate the trigger event of this collapse as Mean Mode Screaming (MMS). MMS can occur even when training appears stable, with a mean-coherent backward shock on residual writers that opens deep residual branches and drives the network into a mean-dominated state. We show this behavior is driven by an exact decomposition of these gradients into mean-coherent and centered components, compounded by the structural suppression of attention-logit gradients through the null space of the Softmax Jacobian once values homogenize.
  To address this, we propose Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) Residuals, which combine a separately gained centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement. On a 400-layer single-stream DiT, MV-Split prevents the divergent collapse that crashes the un-stabilized baseline; it tracks close to the baseline's pre-crash trajectory while remaining substantially better than token-isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale across the full schedule. Finally, we present a 1000-layer DiT as a scale-validation run at boundary scales, establishing that the architecture remains stably trainable at extreme depth.

</details>


### [239] [In-Context Black-Box Optimization with Unreliable Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06187)
*Nicolas Samuel Blumer,Julien Martinelli,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Feedback-informed In-Context Black-Box Optimization (FICBO): a pretrained feedback-aware transformer with a structured feedback prior that leverages multiple auxiliary signals and estimates their reliability at test time to guide query selection, achieving robustness and improved performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable cross-task generalization across diverse feedback sources (experts, simulators, predictors) in black-box optimization, and to mitigate bias and distortion from weak signals.

Method: Pretrain a feedback-aware transformer conditioned on optimization history and cheap auxiliary feedback; model a structured feedback prior encoding access, relevance, and distortion of feedback sources; at test time, estimate source reliability by comparing observed objectives with auxiliary signals to inform query selection.

Result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world tasks show FICBO effectively exploits informative feedback while remaining robust to weak/misleading sources; outperforms baselines; provides interpretability of how the model perceives test-time sources.

Conclusion: FICBO offers a robust, generalizable approach to feedback-enabled black-box optimization, combining cross-task adaptation with reliable signal weighting to improve search efficiency and insight into decision-making.

Abstract: Black-box optimization in science and engineering often comes with side information: experts, simulators, pretrained predictors, or heuristics can suggest which candidates look promising. This information can accelerate search, but it can also be biased, input-dependent, or misleading. Feedback-aware BO methods typically handle one task at a time, limiting their ability to generalize over multiple sources of feedback. In-context optimizers address cross-task adaptation, but usually assume that optimization history is the only available signal at test time. We study feedback-informed in-context black-box optimization (FICBO), where a pretrained optimizer conditions on both the observed history and cheap auxiliary feedback for the current candidate set. We introduce a structured feedback prior that models how feedback sources vary in their access, relevance, and distortion relative to the true objective, and use it to pretrain a feedback-aware transformer. At test time, the model estimates source reliability in context by comparing observed objective values with auxiliary signals, improving query selection. On synthetic and real-world tasks, FICBO effectively exploits informative feedback while remaining robust to weak or misleading sources, improving over other baselines. Empirical investigations further illustrate how the model perceives test-time sources, offering insights into its interpretability and decision-making process.

</details>


### [240] [Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06190)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Modular reduction framework for budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts in the continuing setting; reduces to an unconstrained problem using surrogate rewards and online regression oracles; yields improved guarantees over stochastic-context methods and has a simple, efficient analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses decision-making with budget constraints under potentially adversarial contexts across an infinite or long horizon. Prior work largely assumes stochastic contexts; here, the goal is robustness and joint control of regret and budget violation in the continuing setting.

Method: Extends the SquareCB framework by introducing a modular reduction that uses online regression oracles to define surrogate rewards. This converts the constrained problem into a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate rewards, enabling the reuse of mature online learning tools.

Result: The proposed framework achieves improved guarantees for adversarial contexts while maintaining computational efficiency and a transparent analysis. It offers a practical reduction that handles budget constraints without resorting to overly pessimistic stochastic-context assumptions.

Conclusion: A reduction-based approach provides a robust, efficient method for budget-constrained contextual bandits in the presence of adversarial contexts in the continuing setting, with potential for empirical validation and extensions to broader settings.

Abstract: We study budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We focus on the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget for cumulative cost is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and the violation of the budget constraint. Building on the seminal $\mathsf{SquareCB}$ framework of Foster et al. [2018], we propose a simple and modular framework that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to prior works, which focus on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for more general adversarial contexts, together with an efficient algorithm with a compact and transparent analysis.

</details>


### [241] [Playing the network backward: A Game Theoretic Attribution Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06212)
*Jakob Paul Zimmermann,Jim Berend,Georg Loho,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified game-theoretic framework for backward attribution; gradients and alpha-beta-LRP arise from trajectory integrals; selected alpha-beta-LRP adaptation improves localization on ViT-B/16.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backward attribution methods (gradients, LRP, transformer-specific rules) lack a common framework for comparison and principled design; a unified, extensible formalism is needed.

Method: Cast backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, extending Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Interpret gradients and alpha-beta-LRP as integrals over game trajectories under equilibria; treat attribution maps as projections of trajectory distributions. Introduce game-theoretic constructs (policy regularization, risk aversion, extended action sets) to realize desired properties and derive novel backward-rule adaptations; implement a selected alpha-beta-LRP adaptation for ViT-B/16.

Result: The selected alpha-beta-LRP adaptation outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across multiple localisation metrics on ViT-B/16.

Conclusion: The framework unifies backward attribution methods under a common game-theoretic lens, enabling design of targeted explanation desiderata and yielding practical improvements on transformer models.

Abstract: Attribution methods explain which input features drive a model's prediction, making them central to model debugging and mechanistic interpretability. Yet backward attribution methods, including gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules, lack a shared framework in which to compare the underlying backward calculations. We introduce such a framework by recasting backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, so attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions rather than the primary object. Desired explanation properties, such as localisation focus, robustness to input noise, or stable attention routing, can be specified as game-theoretic concepts, including policy regularization, risk aversion, and extended action sets, and translate directly into novel adaptations of the well-known backward rules. On ViT-B/16, one such selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

</details>


### [242] [Bandit Learning in General Open Multi-agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06202)
*Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified open-system bandit framework with dynamic agent arrivals; introduces pre-training degree, stability, and global dynamic regret; proposes certified global-UCB with regret bounds showing linear entry uncertainty via pre-training degree and bounds tight via lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address non-stationarity and information accumulation in open systems where agents arrive/depart over time; relax restrictive assumptions common in prior work.

Method: Formulate a general open-system bandit with heterogeneous rewards and agent patterns; define pre-training degree, stability, and global dynamic regret; develop certified global-UCB learning algorithms; prove upper bounds and lower bounds.

Result: Regret bounds show entry uncertainty scales linearly with pre-training degree; in stable regimes, regret governed by time to identify a persistent optimal arm and agent patterns; lower bounds establish tightness in hard instances.

Conclusion: Provides a unified framework for open-system bandits with provable guarantees; highlights how entry dynamics and agent patterns shape regret; offers tightness results demonstrating the bounds are fundamental.

Abstract: Recent developments in digital platforms have highlighted the prevalence of open systems, where agents can arrive and depart over time. While bandit learning in open systems has recently received initial attention, existing work imposes structural assumptions that are frequently violated in practice. A learning paradigm for general open systems creates fresh challenges: newly arriving agents induce endogenous non-stationarity; agent patterns determine how quickly information accumulates; and new agents make regret scale further with the time horizon. To this end, we formulate a unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics, including heterogeneous rewards and general agent patterns. We introduce new concepts to capture the inherent complexities: the \emph{pre-training degree} of new agents quantifies how much information an agent carries upon entry, \emph{stability} measures the impact of new agents on the system, and \emph{global dynamic regret} compares the cumulative expected reward of all active agents with that of the varying optimal arms. We develop certified global-UCB learning methodologies with provable guarantees. Our regret bounds reveal that entry uncertainty enters linearly via the pre-training degree, while in stable regimes, regret is governed by the time needed to identify a persistent optimal arm, as well as by the agent patterns. We further show that these dependencies are tight via lower bounds in hard instances.

</details>


### [243] [When Labels Have Structure: Improving Image Classification with Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06274)
*April Chan,Davide D'Ascenzo,Sebastiano Cultrera di Montesano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE), a drop-in loss that uses a class hierarchy to propagate probability mass upward and to smooth the ground-truth signal along the root path, yielding improved classification accuracy across multiple datasets and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cross-entropy ignores semantic distances encoded in class hierarchies; leveraging hierarchy can align predictions with semantic similarity and potentially improve generalization, especially under limited labels or when classes are related.

Method: Two components: (1) prediction aggregation, where probability mass for a prediction is propagated upward through the class hierarchy so that parent nodes accumulate confidence from their descendants; (2) ancestral label smoothing, where the ground-truth signal is distributed along the path from the true class to the root, introducing structured noise that reflects hierarchical structure. The loss is a drop-in replacement for standard CE and is evaluated in two regimes: end-to-end training across six architectures on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, NABirds; and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features.

Result: End-to-end training: improvement in 15 of 18 architecture-dataset pairs, mean gain 4.66 percentage points over standard CE. Linear probing with frozen DINOv2-Large features: outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, with mean improvement 2.18 percentage points over the next best baseline.

Conclusion: HACE provides consistent, architecture-agnostic gains by incorporating hierarchical structure directly into the loss, functioning as a drop-in replacement for CE. It is effective across end-to-end training and linear probing settings and suggests broader applicability to tasks where class hierarchies are meaningful.

Abstract: Standard cross-entropy is the default classification loss across virtually all of machine learning, yet it treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring the semantic distances that a class hierarchy encodes. We propose Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE), a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy that incorporates a known class hierarchy directly into the loss. HACE combines two components: prediction aggregation, which propagates the model's probability mass upward through the class hierarchy to ensure that parent nodes accumulate the confidence of their children; and ancestral label smoothing, which distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. We evaluate HACE on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, and NABirds in two regimes: end-to-end training across six architectures spanning convolutional and attention-based designs, and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features. In end-to-end training, HACE improves accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 out of 18 architecture--dataset pairs, with a mean gain of 4.66\%. In linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features, HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, with a mean improvement of 2.18\% over the next best baseline.

</details>


### [244] [Memory Efficient Full-gradient Attacks (MEFA) Framework for Adversarial Defense Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06357)
*Yuan Du,Mitchel Hill,HanQin Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation with gradient checkpointing for iterative stochastic purification defenses enables exact white-box attacks that outperform prior approximate-gradient evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate benchmarking of iterative stochastic purification defenses is hindered by high memory demand for backprop through long purification trajectories and by uncontrolled stochasticity that varies robustness metrics across runs.

Method: Use gradient checkpointing to enable end-to-end gradients through long purification processes; develop a memory-efficient framework that combines checkpointed backprop with protocols that control stochastic variance; apply to diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with EBMs.

Result: Full-gradient adaptive attacks reveal vulnerabilities not seen with approximate gradients; stronger l_infty and l2 white-box attacks; improved ability to probe out-of-distribution robustness.

Conclusion: Exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses, and the proposed framework mitigates memory bottlenecks while preserving gradient accuracy.

Abstract: This work studies the robust evaluation of iterative stochastic purification defenses under white-box adversarial attacks. Our key technical insight is that gradient checkpointing makes exact end-to-end gradient computation through long purification trajectories practical by trading additional recomputation for substantially lower memory usage. This enables full-gradient adaptive attacks against diffusion- and Langevin-based purification defenses, where prior evaluations often resort to approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints. These approximations can weaken the attack signal and risk overestimating robustness. In parallel, stochasticity in iterative purification is frequently under-controlled, even though different purification trajectories can substantially change reported robustness metrics. Building on this insight, we introduce a memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation framework for stochastic purification defenses. The framework combines checkpointed backpropagation with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability, thereby reducing memory bottlenecks while preserving exact gradients. We evaluate diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs), demonstrating that full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities missed by approximate-gradient evaluations. Our framework yields stronger state-of-the-art $\ell_{\infty}$ and $\ell_{2}$ white-box attacks and further supports probing out-of-distribution robustness. Overall, our results show that exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses.

</details>


### [245] [Contrastive Identification and Generation in the Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06211)
*Xiaoyu Li,Andi Han,Jiaojiao Jiang,Junbin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contrastive identification and generation in the limit with relational supervision; exact characterizations, a contrastive closure dimension, tight sample complexity for generation, and a crossing-graph framework revealing identifiability boundaries and robustness under corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend classical identification in the limit to relational (contrastive) supervision signals, addressing the gap between positive-only data and pairwise/relational supervision; understand identifiability, generation, and robustness under noise.

Method: Formalize contrastive data as unordered pairs {x,y} with h(x) ≠ h(y) for an unknown target h; derive a tell-tale-like condition for contrastive identifiability; introduce the contrastive closure dimension; obtain exact/near-tight results for uniform contrastive generation; establish a strict hierarchy showing mutual incomparability with classical identification; prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption; unify results through the crossing graph that encodes ambiguity, generation obstructions, and corruption.

Result: Noiseless setting: complete characterization of contrastive identifiable classes; introduction of contrastive closure dimension; exact characterization and tight sample complexity for uniform contrastive generation; a hierarchy showing contrastive generation and (classical) identification are mutually incomparable. Adversarial setting: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive exemplars with even one corrupted observation. Unifying tool: the crossing graph.

Conclusion: The crossing graph unifies pairwise ambiguity, generation obstacles, and corruption defects in a single framework. The work clarifies identifiability boundaries under relational supervision, linking contrastive learning with classical identification and generation, and opens directions for algorithmic and complexity investigations in noisy relational settings.

Abstract: In the classical identification in the limit model of Gold [1967], a stream of positive examples is presented round by round, and the learner must eventually recover the target hypothesis. Recently, Kleinberg and Mullainathan [2024] introduced generation in the limit, where the learner instead must eventually output novel elements of the target's support. Both lines of work focus on positive-only or fully labeled data. Yet many natural supervision signals are inherently relational rather than singleton, which encode relationships between examples rather than labels of individual ones. We initiate the study of contrastive identification and generation in the limit, where the learner observes a contrastive presentation of data: a stream of unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$ satisfying $h(x)\ne h(y)$ for an unknown target binary hypothesis $h$, but which element is positive is hidden from the learner. We first present three results in the noiseless setting: an exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes (a one-line geometric refinement of Angluin [1980]'s tell-tale condition), a combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension (a contrasitive analogue of the closure dimension in Raman et al. [2025]) and exactly characterizing uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity, and a strict hierarchy in which contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. We then prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation. The unifying technical object is the common crossing graph, which encodes pairwise ambiguity, family-level generation obstructions, and corruption defects in a single coverage-and-incidence language.

</details>


### [246] [E = T*H/(O+B): A Dimensionless Control Parameter for Mixture-of-Experts Ecology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06415)
*Qingjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes E = T*H/(O+B) as a universal MoE health diagnostic; shows E >= 0.5 eliminates dead experts across 12 controlled experiments; cross-modal validation across vision and language; reduces reliance on handcrafted load-balancing losses; offers insights into dynamics (resuscitation, ortho toxicity, complexity, overfitting, tier collapse) and temperature invariance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify diverse MoE hyperparameters into a single scalar that predicts and stabilizes training by preventing dead expert collapse, applicable across vision and language tasks.

Method: Define E from routing temperature T, entropy weight H, oracle weight O, and balance weight B. Conduct 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling ~11k training epochs. Evaluate on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet-200, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. Analyze additional findings on ecological health, dead/exhausted experts, resuscitation, and sensitivity to dataset and task characteristics.

Result: E >= 0.5 guarantees zero dead experts; cross-modal validity demonstrated. Additional findings: dead experts can resuscitate; orthogonality toxicity is dataset-dependent; task complexity shifts the critical E; overfitting decoupled from ecological health; three-tier MoE often collapses to two-tier; ecological structure is temperature-invariant over a 50x range; E acts as a diagnostic akin to Reynolds number.

Conclusion: E serves as a unified diagnostic for MoE training, enabling principled control without handcrafted load-balancing losses and supporting cross-modal generality and rich ecological insights.

Abstract: We introduce E = T*H/(O+B), a dimensionless control parameter that predicts whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models will develop a healthy expert ecology or collapse into dead experts. E combines four hyperparameters -- routing temperature T, routing entropy weight H, oracle weight O, and balance weight B -- into a single quantity. Through 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling over 11,000 training epochs, we establish that E >= 0.5 alone is sufficient to guarantee zero dead experts, removing the necessity for handcrafted load-balancing auxiliary losses. We validate this cross-modally on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet-200, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. Six additional findings emerge: (1) dead experts can resuscitate -- triggered by balance loss driving router re-exploration; (2) ortho toxicity is dataset-dependent, not universal; (3) task complexity shifts the critical E threshold; (4) model overfitting is decoupled from expert ecological health; (5) three-tier MoE spontaneously collapses into a two-tier functional structure; (6) ecological structure is temperature-invariant across a 50x range. We propose that E serves as a unified diagnostic for MoE training, analogous to the Reynolds number in fluid dynamics.

</details>


### [247] [Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06440)
*Daniel Uyterlinde,Swasti Shreya Mishra,Pascal Mettes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models (HypCBM) embed concepts in hyperbolic space to leverage hierarchical structure via entailment cones, using activation margin as a sparse, hierarchy-aware signal; includes adaptive scaling for corrections. Performs comparably to Euclidean CBMs with 20x less data, with better hierarchical consistency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between flat Euclidean concept embeddings and the inherent hierarchical structure of concepts to improve interpretability, data efficiency, and robustness in concept bottleneck models.

Method: Post-hoc HypCBM framework that grounds the bottleneck in hyperbolic space; models concept activation as asymmetric geometric containment in entailment cones; uses the margin of inclusion within a concept's cone as the activation signal without extra supervision; introduces an adaptive scaling law to propagate user corrections through the concept tree.

Result: HypCBM achieves performance comparable to post-hoc Euclidean CBMs trained on 20× more data in sparse regimes, with stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry provides a principled, data-efficient, hierarchy-aware approach for interpretable CBMs; entailment cones yield natural activation signals and enable coherent, hierarchy-faithful interventions without additional supervision.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have become a popular approach to enable interpretability in neural networks by constraining classifier inputs to a set of human-understandable concepts. While effective, current models embed concepts in flat Euclidean space, treating them as independent, orthogonal dimensions. Concepts, however, are highly structured and organized in semantic hierarchies. To resolve this mismatch, we propose Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models (HypCBM), a post-hoc framework that grounds the bottleneck in this structure by reformulating concept activation as asymmetric geometric containment in hyperbolic space. Rather than treating entailment cones as a pre-training penalty, we show they encode a natural test-time activation signal: the margin of inclusion within a concept's entailment cone yields sparse, hierarchy-aware activations without any additional supervision or learned modules. We further introduce an adaptive scaling law for hierarchically faithful interventions, propagating user corrections coherently through the concept tree. Empirically, HypCBM rivals post-hoc Euclidean models trained on 20$\times$ more data in sparse regimes required for human interpretability, with stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions.

</details>


### [248] [AffineLens: Capturing the Continuous Piecewise Affine Functions of Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06218)
*Yi Wei,Xuan Qi,Furao shen,Jian Zhao,Vittorio Murino,Cigdem Beyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AffineLens provides a practical framework to compute and visualize the region partition of piecewise affine neural networks on bounded inputs, enabling direct comparison of architectures via region-count metrics and boundary visuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current interpretability/expressivity analyses rely on indirect proxies and lack practical tools to enumerate and visualize the induced region partition under realistic architectures and bounded input domains.

Method: Identify neuron-induced hyperplanes intersecting a bounded input polytope, enumerate the resulting affine sub-regions in a layer-wise fashion, and return non-empty maximal CPA regions with interior representatives. Provide visualizations of region partitioning and decision boundaries. The approach relies on the affine restriction property of CPA networks under fixed activation patterns and supports a broad class of components (batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, MLPs, and convolutions).

Result: Offers a unified pipeline to compute and visualize CPA region partitions, enabling quantitative region-count metrics and qualitative inspection. Empirically study architecture expressivity by comparing networks across region complexity metrics and analyzing how design choices shape the geometry of learned functions.

Conclusion: AffineLens delivers a practical, geometry-grounded tool for interpretability and expressivity analysis of PANNs, linking network design to the topology of induced affine regions; it highlights how architectural choices influence the partitioning of input space and the resulting decision boundaries.

Abstract: Piecewise affine neural networks (PANNs) provide a principled geometric perspective on neural network expressivity by characterizing the input--output map as a continuous piecewise affine (CPA) function whose complexity is governed by the number, arrangement, and shapes of its affine regions. However, existing interpretability and expressivity analyses often rely on indirect proxies (e.g., activation statistics or theoretical upper bounds) and rarely offer practical, accurate tools for enumerating and visualizing the induced region partition under realistic architectures and bounded input domains. In this work, we present AffineLens, a unified framework for computing the hyperplane arrangements and polyhedral structures underlying PANNs. Given a calibrated (bounded) input polytope, AffineLens identifies the subset of neuron-induced hyperplanes that intersect the domain, enumerates the resulting affine sub-regions in a layer-wise manner, and returns provably non-empty maximal CPA regions together with interior representatives. The framework further provides visualizations of region partitioning and decision boundaries, enabling qualitative inspection alongside quantitative region counts. By exploiting the affine restriction property of CPA networks under fixed activation patterns, AffineLens supports a broad class of modern components, including batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, multilayer perceptrons, and convolutional layers. Finally, we use AffineLens to perform a systematic empirical study of architectural expressivity, comparing networks through region complexity metrics and revealing how design choices influence the geometry of learned functions.

</details>


### [249] [Agentic AIs Are the Missing Paradigm for Out-of-Distribution Generalization in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06522)
*Xin Wang,Haibo Chen,Wenxuan Liu,Wenwu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OOD for foundation models is structurally distinct from standard OOD; model-centric methods face a parameter coverage ceiling; agentic systems with perception, strategy, action, closed-loop verification extend beyond that ceiling; authors advocate a complementary, agentic-first research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models operate in open-world settings with partially observed pretraining/post-training distributions; conventional OOD research is not sufficient; a new agentic paradigm is proposed as a missing piece.

Method: 1) Stage-aware formalization of OOD accommodating partially observed multi-stage training. 2) Prove a parameter coverage ceiling: there exist practically relevant inputs that no model-centric method can handle within tolerance ε due to parameter-based representation. 3) Characterize agentic OOD systems by four structural properties—perception, strategy selection, external action, and closed-loop verification—and show they extend the reachable set beyond the ceiling. 4) Respond to seven counterarguments, conceding two, and outline a research agenda.

Result: A theoretical framework identifying a parameter-coverage ceiling for model-centric OOD and showing that agentic OOD systems can surpass this ceiling; demonstrates complementarity between model-centric and agentic approaches and outlines initial steps toward a formal research program.

Conclusion: Agentic (agent-based) methods are a necessary complement to model-centric approaches for FM-OOD; progressing FM-OOD requires recognizing the agentic paradigm as a first-class research direction alongside traditional model-centric work.

Abstract: Foundation models (FMs) are increasingly deployed in open-world settings where distribution shift is the rule rather than the exception. The out-of-distribution (OOD) phenomena they face -- knowledge boundaries, capability ceilings, compositional shifts, and open-ended task variation -- differ in kind from the settings that have shaped prior OOD research, and are further complicated because the pretraining and post-training distributions of modern FMs are often only partially observed. Our position is that OOD for foundation models is a structurally distinct problem that cannot be solved within the prevailing model-centric paradigm, and that agentic systems constitute the missing paradigm required to address it. We defend this claim through four steps. First, we give a stage-aware formalization of OOD that accommodates partially observed multi-stage training distributions. Second, we prove a parameter coverage ceiling: there exist practically relevant inputs that no model-centric method (training-time or test-time) can handle within tolerance $\varepsilon$, for reasons intrinsic to parameter-based representation. Third, we characterize agentic OOD systems by four structural properties -- perception, strategy selection, external action, and closed-loop verification -- and show that they strictly extend the reachable set beyond the ceiling. Fourth, we respond to seven counterarguments, conceding two, and outline a research agenda. We do not claim that agentic methods subsume model-centric ones; we argue that the two are complementary, and that progress on FM-OOD requires explicit recognition of the agentic paradigm as a first-class research direction.

</details>


### [250] [Memory Inception: Latent-Space KV Cache Manipulation for Steering LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06225)
*Andy Zeyi Liu,Michael Zhang,Ilana Greenberg,Adam Alnasser,Lucas Baker,John Sous*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory Inception (MI): a training-free method that steers LLMs in latent attention space via text-derived KV banks inserted at selective layers; balances control and memory efficiency, enabling updateable and structured guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for steering that combines the strengths of prompting and activation methods while avoiding token cache overhead; enable persistent, structured, or expensive-to-store guidance.

Method: Insert latent KV banks at chosen layers derived from text guidance; steer in latent attention by selective KV allocation; no model retraining or full prompt materialization; apply at targeted layers to influence routing. 

Result: MI achieves best overall drift-control trade-off on personality-steering tasks; enables mid-conversation post-shift alignment (e.g., Qwen3); outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject×mode cells) while reducing content-matched KV storage by up to 118x.

Conclusion: MI is a powerful steering mechanism when guidance is persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript; provides efficient, updateable control without retraining.

Abstract: Steering large language models (LLMs) is usually done by either instruction prompting or activation steering. Prompting often gives strong control, but caches guidance tokens at every layer and can clutter long interactions; activation steering is compact but typically weaker and does not support large structured reminders. We introduce memory inception (MI), a training-free method that steers in latent attention space by inserting text-derived key-value (KV) banks only at selected layers. Rather than materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache, MI treats steering as selective KV allocation, injecting latent slots only where the model routes to them. On matched personality-steering tasks, MI gives the best overall control--drift trade-off, remaining competitive with prompting while consistently outperforming CAA. On updateable guidance, MI supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without rewriting the visible transcript, achieving the highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3. On structured reasoning, MI outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject$\times$mode cells), serving as proxies for structured reasoning in verifiable domains, while cutting content-matched KV storage by up to 118$\times$. These results position MI as a powerful steering method when guidance is persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript.

</details>


### [251] [SoftSAE: Dynamic Top-K Selection for Adaptive Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06610)
*Jakub Stępień,Marcin Mazur,Jacek Tabor,Przemysław Spurek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SoftSAE introduces an adaptive sparse autoencoder with a differentiable Soft Top-K to learn per-input sparsity, addressing fixed-K limitations in TopK SAEs and improving mechanistic interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed sparsity (K) across inputs ignores varying intrinsic dimensionality on data manifolds; adaptive sparsity can produce more faithful representations and interpretation.

Method: Incorporates a differentiable Soft Top-K operator into an SAE to produce input-dependent sparsity k; end-to-end training yields sparse monosemantic features with variable numbers of active units per input.

Result: Empirical results show meaningful feature discovery and appropriate per-example feature counts; code is provided.

Conclusion: SoftSAE offers a more flexible, interpretable sparse representation by aligning sparsity with input complexity, improving explanation length and fidelity.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have become an important tool in mechanistic interpretability, helping to analyze internal representations in both Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). By decomposing polysemantic activations into sparse sets of monosemantic features, SAEs aim to translate neural network computations into human-understandable concepts. However, common architectures such as TopK SAEs rely on a fixed sparsity level. They enforce the same number of active features (K) across all inputs, ignoring the varying complexity of real-world data. Natural data often lies on manifolds with varying local intrinsic dimensionality, meaning the number of relevant factors can change significantly across samples. This suggests that a fixed sparsity level is not optimal. Simple inputs may require only a few features, while more complex ones need more expressive representations. Using a constant K can therefore introduce noise in simple cases or miss important structure in more complex ones. To address this issue, we propose SoftSAE, a sparse autoencoder with a Dynamic Top-K selection mechanism. Our method uses a differentiable Soft Top-K operator to learn an input-dependent sparsity level k. This allows the model to adjust the number of active features based on the complexity of each input. As a result, the representation better matches the structure of the data, and the explanation length reflects the amount of information in the input. Experimental results confirm that SoftSAE not only finds meaningful features, but also selects the right number of features for each concept. The source code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SoftSAE-8F71/.

</details>


### [252] [Soft Deterministic Policy Gradient with Gaussian Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06228)
*Hyunjun Na,Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Soft-DPG/Soft DDPG via Gaussian-smoothed Bellman equation to avoid reliance on critic action-gradients, enabling well-defined gradients for non-smooth Q-functions; empirically competitive on dense rewards and superior on discretized reward variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPG relies on differentiable critic action gradients; non-smooth rewards yield ill-defined gradients and instability; need a gradient that remains defined for non-smooth Q-functions.

Method: Define a smoothed Bellman-based action-value function using Gaussian smoothing; derive soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG) that avoids explicit critic action-gradients; instantiate into deep RL algorithm Soft DDPG.

Result: Empirical evaluations on continuous control benchmarks and discretized-reward variants show Soft DDPG competitive in dense rewards and improves performance in discretized rewards where standard DDPG struggles.

Conclusion: Gaussian smoothing stabilizes policy updates and broadens applicability to non-smooth Q-functions; Soft DDPG is a viable alternative to DDPG, particularly in irregular reward landscapes.

Abstract: Deterministic policy gradient (DPG) is widely utilized for continuous control; however, it inherently relies on the differentiability of the critic with respect to the action during policy updates. This assumption is violated in practical control problems involving sparse or discrete rewards, leading to ill-defined policy gradients and unstable learning. To address these challenges, we propose a principled alternative based on a smoothed Bellman equation formulated via Gaussian smoothing. Specifically, we define a novel action-value function based on a smoothed Bellman equation and derive the soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG).
