Our formulation eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients and ensures that the gradient remains well-defined even for non-smooth Q-functions. We instantiate this framework into a deep reinforcement learning algorithm, which we call soft deep deterministic policy gradient (Soft DDPG). Empirical evaluations on standard continuous control benchmarks and their discretized-reward variants show that Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments, where standard DDPG is more sensitive to irregular critic landscapes.

</details>


### [253] [Band Together: Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination against Evasion-based Promotion Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06238)
*Guanmeng Xian,Ning Yang,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC) to improve robustness of multimodal recommender systems against evasion-based promotion attacks by aligning perturbations across visual and textual modalities and treating all items as potential targets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal recommender systems are vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks. Existing defenses are largely single-modal and focus on poisoning threats, while cross-modal gradient mismatch can reduce defense effectiveness and underestimate worst-case risks.

Method: Introduce Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). Treat all items as potential targets and use a gradient alignment mechanism to synchronize perturbations across visual and textual modalities, maximizing adversarial strength during training to improve robustness.

Result: Empirical results show significant robustness improvements against promotion attacks with an acceptable trade-off in recommendation performance; code is released for reproduction.

Conclusion: UAT-MC offers a principled cross-modal adversarial training framework to mitigate evasion-based promotion attacks in multimodal recommenders by correcting gradient mismatch and coordinating perturbations across modalities.

Abstract: Multimodal recommender systems exploit visual and textual signals to alleviate data sparsity, but this also makes them more vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks. Existing defenses are largely limited to single-modal settings and mainly focus on poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored. In this work, we first identify a cross-modal gradient mismatch under the multi-user promotion setting, where visual and textual perturbations are optimized in inconsistent directions due to the dominance of distinct user groups. This phenomenon dilutes the attack effectiveness and leads robust training to underestimate worst-case risks. To address this issue, we propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). UAT-MC tackles the challenge of unknown targeted items in evasion-based attacks (as opposed to poisoning-based attacks) by treating all items as potential targets, and introduces a gradient alignment mechanism to explicitly correct this mismatch. This design ensures synchronized perturbations across modalities, thereby maximizing adversarial strength for robust training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/gmXian/UAT-MC.

</details>


### [254] [When Graph Language Models Go Beyond Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06239)
*Masatsugu Yamada,Mahito Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibrated diagnostic shows graph LMs learn structural regularities beyond memorization, especially for high-frequency patterns; scale-dependent: at large scale memorization declines while rank correlation remains near ceiling; rare patterns remain poorly covered; results robust to graph serialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentangle memorization from true structural alignment in graph language models, which current fidelity metrics fail to separate.

Method: Combine frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification. Evaluate using subgraph-rank correlation and all-generation Spearman correlation across two graph serializations; include fixed-subsample and novel-only analyses to isolate genuine structural learning from verbatim recall.

Result: On five TU benchmarks, LLaMA-style graph LMs reach high subgraph-rank correlation, but alignment is often matched/exceeded by the memorization bootstrap. At small scale fidelity resembles verbatim recall. At 3.75M graphs, memorization drops while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Frequent subgraph mining restricted to novel-only subset tracks the all-generation correlation, indicating alignment beyond recall. High-frequency patterns are well reproduced across scales; rare patterns remain underrepresented, with only marginal improvement as capacity grows. Results are robust to serialization (canonical DFS code vs. action sequences).

Conclusion: Graph LMs can acquire non-trivial structural regularities beyond memorization, particularly for frequent patterns, and these findings are robust across scales and representations. Evaluations should account for scale effects and use diagnostic frameworks beyond fidelity metrics to distinguish memorization from structural alignment.

Abstract: It remains unclear whether graph language models learn structural regularities or merely memorize training graphs; this cannot be resolved by current aggregate fidelity metrics alone. We develop a calibrated diagnostic protocol that combines frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment. Using this framework, we show that graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, primarily in the high-frequency regime. This is supported by the following empirical evidence: On five TU benchmarks, LLaMA-style graph language models reach high subgraph-rank correlation, yet their alignment is matched or exceeded by the memorization bootstrap in most cases. At small scale, under our bootstrap diagnostic, fidelity is largely indistinguishable from verbatim recall. In contrast, at large scale with 3.75M graphs, verbatim memorization drops sharply while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Crucially, in a separate fixed-subsample analysis, frequent subgraph mining restricted to the novel-only subset closely tracks the corresponding all-generation Spearman correlation, providing evidence that the alignment is not driven solely by verbatim recall. Across all scales, high-frequency patterns are well reproduced, while rare patterns remain poorly covered, and this deficit narrows only marginally as capacity increases. We observe the same scale-dependent crossover under two distinct graph serializations (canonical DFS code and action sequences), providing evidence of robustness in our analysis.

</details>


### [255] [Cumulative-Goodness Free-Riding in Forward-Forward Networks: Real, Repairable, but Not Accuracy-Dominant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06240)
*Amirhossein Yousefiramandi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cumulative free-riding in Forward-Forward training is real but repairable; three local remedies dramatically improve layer separation in deeper layers with minimal accuracy impact; architecture/augmentation choices dominate final accuracy and thus free-riding is not the main limiter in the studied settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why Forward-Forward training with cumulative goodness can cause later layers to rely on earlier layers' partial task separation (layer free-riding), formalize the effect, and evaluate remedies that avoid backpropagated gradients.

Method: Formal analysis under softplus FF showing exponential decay of the class-discrimination gradient with the accumulated margin; propose three local remedies (per-block, hardness-gated, depth-scaled); empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny ImageNet, using layer-separation diagnostics and final accuracy metrics; calibration tests to assess the impact of architecture and augmentation.

Result: Remedies yield 4×–45× improvements in deeper-layer separation statistics; final accuracy changes stay within ~1 percentage point for non-degenerate training; Tiny ImageNet confirms the same qualitative gap between layer-health metrics and final accuracy; architecture and augmentation have larger effects on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications.

Conclusion: Cumulative free-riding is a real and repairable optimization pathology in FF training, but is not the dominant factor limiting accuracy for the studied architectures and datasets; implementing the local remedies can substantially improve layer health without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Forward-Forward (FF) training allows each layer to learn from a local goodness criterion. In cumulative-goodness variants, however, later layers can inherit a task that earlier layers have already partially separated. We formalize this phenomenon as layer free-riding: under the softplus FF criterion, the class-discrimination gradient reaching block $d$ decays exponentially with the positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks. We then study three local remedies -- per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled -- that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics, with $4\times$--$45\times$ gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point for non-degenerate training procedures. Tiny ImageNet provides a tougher cross-dataset check for our selected block-wise configuration and reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments further show that architecture and augmentation choices have a larger effect on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications studied here. Cumulative free-riding is therefore a real and repairable optimization pathology. Nonetheless, for the FF training rules, architectures, and datasets we study, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

</details>


### [256] [A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18555)
*Ran Ben-Basat,Yaniv Ben-Itzhak,Gal Mendelson,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TurboQuant is a special-case, generally suboptimal variant of EDEN; unbiased EDEN with full b-bit quantization consistently outperforms TurboQuant in multiple settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how TurboQuant relates to the DRIVE/EDEN family, identify suboptimal choices, and quantify performance differences.

Method: Analytical comparison of TurboQuant variants (mse and prod) against EDEN with optimized scale S, plus conceptual connections (random rotations, shifted Beta, Lloyd-Max) and empirical replication of TurboQuant experiments.

Result: EDEN with optimized S generally outperforms TurboQuant_mse; unbiased EDEN outperforms TurboQuant_prod; TurboQuant’s two-stage approach is suboptimal due to suboptimal S, worse residual quantization, and chaining inefficiency; EDEN also matches the theoretical connections used in TurboQuant.

Conclusion: EDEN subsumes TurboQuant; biased and unbiased EDEN with proper S yield better quantization accuracy across regimes; TurboQuant remains a special case with limited performance.

Abstract: This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

</details>


### [257] [The Role of Node Features in Graph Pooling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06250)
*Jan von Pichowski,Alžbeta Hrabošová,Ingo Scholtes,Christopher Blöcker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pooling yields gains only when node features align with graph topology; formalizes alignment requirements and a feature-quality metric; empirical evidence shows benefits when conditions hold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why graph pooling often underperforms WL-1 expressive GNNs and identify when pooling is actually beneficial by examining the interplay between node features and topology.

Method: Theoretical analysis of feature-topology interaction; formalize necessary feature alignment requirements; introduce a quantitative feature-quality metric; conduct empirical evaluation across datasets to test the conditions.

Result: Identified that pooling effectiveness hinges on node-feature and topology alignment; proposed a feature-quality metric; empirical results demonstrate pooling can improve performance when the alignment requirements are satisfied.

Conclusion: Pooling can be beneficial in graph classification if node features satisfy the alignment requirements with the graph topology; a feature-quality metric helps diagnose when pooling is likely to help.

Abstract: Graph pooling is commonly applied in graph classification, yet its empirical gains over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs are often marginal or inconsistent. We study this gap by analysing the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. Our analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology -- a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. We formalise fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. Our empirical evaluation shows that, when these requirements are satisfied, pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets.

</details>


### [258] [The Weight Gram Matrix Captures Sequential Feature Linearization in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06258)
*Taehun Cha,Daniel Beaglehole,Adityanarayanan Radhakrishnan,Donghun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A feature-centric theory linking weight updates to feature dynamics via a Feature Learning Equation, introducing Virtual Covariance and Target Linearity to explain representation evolution and phenomena like Neural Collapse and linear interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how deep networks learn representations by tying weight updates to the implied evolution of features, unifying several empirical phenomena under a single framework.

Method: Derive the Feature Learning Equation; identify the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics; define Virtual Covariance as the covariance of implicitly evolving features under gradient descent; introduce Target Linearity as a measure of linear alignment between features and targets; analyze training and layer-wise dynamics to show sequential transformation toward target-linear structure.

Result: A linearization perspective that accounts for Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models; demonstrates that gradient descent implicitly drives features toward target-linear structure via the weight Gram matrix.

Conclusion: The proposed feature-centric framework provides a cohesive interpretation of DNN representation learning, explaining observed phenomena and offering a basis for future theoretical and empirical investigation.

Abstract: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. In this work, we propose a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution. We introduce a simple identity, the Feature Learning Equation, which identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics. This enables us to interpret gradient descent as implicitly inducing a hypothetical evolution of features, whose covariance structure - termed the Virtual Covariance - characterizes how representations evolve during training. Building on this perspective, we introduce Target Linearity, a measure quantifying the linear alignment between features and targets. By analyzing the training and layer-wise dynamics, we show that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of several empirical phenomena, including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

</details>


### [259] [Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06259)
*Marten van Dijk,Murat Bilgehan Ertan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tight, transparent analysis of the trade-off function for DP-SGD with random-shuffle subsampling within the f-DP framework, providing closed-form bounds in the regime σ ≥ sqrt(3/ln M) and Berry-Esseen-based tightness; demonstrates practical parameter regimes and a new LLN-based asymptotic method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain transparent, closed-form, and practically useful bounds for the trade-off function under DP-SGD with random-shuffle subsampling in the f-DP framework, addressing the opacity of Poisson-subsampling analyses and enabling actionable privacy-utility guidance.

Method: Analytical derivation using Berry-Esseen bounds for the trade-off function under random-shuffle subsampling within f-DP; comparison to Poisson subsampling; explicit parameter settings for E=1; composition across epochs; introduction of a generalized law of large numbers-based proof technique to obtain asymptotic diagonal limits.

Result: Tight bounds up to constant factors; concrete parameter settings: δ=1/100, σ=1 yield M ≈ 1.14×10^6 rounds and N ≈ 1.14×10^7 samples for meaningful DP in a single epoch; composition across epochs yields δ = O(√E) with E = O(√M); asymptotic result: f^{⊗E}(a) → 1−a uniformly in a ∈ [0,1] with δ = O(√E) when E = c_M^2 M and c_M → 0; this is contrasted with Poisson subsampling asymptotics; open question on explicit convergence rates.

Conclusion: Random shuffling yields transparent, interpretable closed-form bounds for the DP-SGD trade-off function in the f-DP framework, enabling practical privacy-utility trade-offs; the approach admits composition across epochs with constraints (E = O(√M)); an LLN-based generalized technique provides an asymptotic diagonal-limit; further work needed on explicit convergence rates versus Poisson subsampling.

Abstract: We derive a tight analysis of the trade-off function for Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) with subsampling based on random shuffling within the $f$-DP framework. Our analysis covers the regime $σ\geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$, where $σ$ is the noise multiplier and $M$ is the number of rounds within a single epoch. Unlike $f$-DP analyses for Poisson subsampling, which yield non-closed implicit formulas that can be machine computed but are non-transparent, random shuffling admits a tight analysis yielding transparent and interpretable closed-form bounds. Our concrete bounds, derived via the Berry-Esseen theorem, are tight up to constant factors within the proof framework. We demonstrate worked parameter settings for a single epoch ($E=1$) with a corresponding trade-off function $\geq 1-a-δ$, that is, only $δ$ below the ideal random guessing diagonal $1-a$: For $δ= 1/100$ and $σ= 1$, roughly $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ training samples suffice to achieve meaningful differential privacy. This is in contrast to recent negative results for the regime $σ\leq 1/\sqrt{2 \ln M}$. Our concrete bounds can be composed over multiple epochs leading to $δ$ having a linear in $E$ dependency, which restricts $E=O(\sqrt{M})$. To go beyond Berry--Esseen, we introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers that yields an asymptotic random guessing diagonal-limit result: if $E=c_M^2M$ with $c_M\to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function satisfies $f^{\otimes E}(a)\to 1-a$ uniformly in $a\in[0,1]$ with $δ$ having only an $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency. We compare this asymptotic regime with the corresponding Poisson subsampling asymptotic, and highlight the characterization of explicit convergence rates as an open question.

</details>


### [260] [Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06260)
*Wentao Yu,Bo Han,Jie Yang,Chen Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedGMC reframes heterogeneous graph federated learning as dual manifold calibration, enabling global commonality while preserving client-specific personalization by constructing semantic and structural manifolds via equidistant anchors and global templates, and dynamically refining via local manifolds; outperforms baselines on 11 graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneity in semantic and structural aspects across clients undermines global alignment in GFL. Rigid parameter/prototype alignment with a single global representation space compresses local distributions, reducing personalization and performance.

Method: FedGMC introduces a dual-manifold calibration framework. The server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold using equidistant semantic anchors to guide calibration of local semantic manifolds, and a global structural manifold via global structural templates to guide local structural manifolds. The server dynamically refines both global manifolds by aggregating local manifolds.

Result: Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs show that FedGMC better balances global commonality and local personalization, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: A unified manifold perspective for addressing semantic and structural heterogeneity in GFL. The dual calibration framework preserves diverse local distributions while leveraging global commonalities, with strong empirical gains and potential applicability to broader heterogeneous GFL settings.

Abstract: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables collaborative representation learning across distributed subgraphs while preserving privacy. However, heterogeneity remains a critical challenge, as subgraphs across clients typically differ significantly in both semantics and structures. Existing methods address heterogeneity by enforcing the rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes between clients and the server. However, these alignments implicitly rely on a restrictive global linearity assumption that summarizes local data distributions using a single and globally consistent representation space. This severely compresses the personalized representation space of clients and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC), a novel paradigm that tackles semantic heterogeneity and structural heterogeneity from a unified manifold perspective. Instead of enforcing rigid alignment, FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism that preserves global commonalities while maximizing the personalized representation space of local clients. Specifically, for semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors, so as to guide the calibration of local semantic manifolds. For structural heterogeneity, the server constructs a global structural manifold by building global structural templates, so as to guide the calibration of local structural manifolds. Finally, the server dynamically refines both global semantic manifolds and structural manifolds by aggregating local manifolds. Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, thereby significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [261] [Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06261)
*Eugenio Lomurno,Filippo Balzarini,Francesco Benelle,Francesca Pia Panaccione,Matteo Matteucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces TARDIS, an inference-time refinement framework for tabular data diffusion using a frozen backbone guided by Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR); achieves substantial utility gains over real-data-trained baselines and the TabDiff backbone on 15 datasets, within 1–80 minutes on a consumer GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited exploration of inference-time refinement for tabular diffusion models and the persistent gap between synthetic and real-data utility, by refining outputs without retraining the backbone.

Method: Weber: Use a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator to configure per-dataset score-level guidance; perform an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and optional soft-label distillation; optimize a unified Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) that minimizes symmetric Chamfer distance between synthetic and real samples both continuously (score-level gradients) and discretely (batch-ranking). Evaluate on 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks, comparing to the TabDiff backbone.

Result: Median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (CI +/− 3.3 to 16.4; p=0.016), 11 of 15 wins; mean +12.9% improvement over the TabDiff backbone (p<1e-4); matches backbone in manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy; achieves inference-time refinement within 1–80 minutes on a consumer-grade GPU.

Conclusion: Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone can reach or surpass real-data utility without retraining. BCR emerges as the dominant refinement pattern, with per-dataset search recovering BCR-aligned configurations; the approach is practical on consumer hardware and preserves fidelity, diversity, and privacy.

Abstract: Diffusion-based generators set the current state of the art for synthetic tabular data. These methods approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and closing this synthetic-real gap has so far been pursued exclusively at training time, via architectural advances, scaling, and retraining of monolithic generators. The inference-time alternative, i.e., refining the outputs of a pre-trained backbone with parameters left untouched, has remained largely unexplored for tabular synthesis. We introduce TARDIS (Tabular generation through Refinement, Distillation, and Inference-time Sampling), an inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained backbone, configured per dataset by a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, with each trial's objective set by an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and an optional soft-label distillation step. The search space encodes a single mathematical pattern we name Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples is minimized both continuously, via a score-level gradient, and discretely, via batch-ranking post-generation. The per-dataset search recovers BCR-aligned configurations on most datasets, evidence for BCR as the dominant refinement pattern. Across 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over the TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), matching the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone reaches and exceeds real-data utility in 1 to 80 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU.

</details>


### [262] [Can Attribution Predict Risk? From Multi-View Attribution to Planning Risk Signals in End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06264)
*Le Yang,Ruoyu Chen,Haijun Liu,Jiawei Liang,ShangQuan Sun,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning in autonomous driving using coarse-to-fine region attribution across six-view inputs; three predictive risk signals; shows correlation with risk metrics and good AUROC for collision detection; generalizes to held-out scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end planning models fuse multi-view inputs to generate multi-step trajectories, offering system integration but making decisions opaque and risks hard to localize. Existing explanations are decoupled from planning or rely on non-visual cues; attribution can directly reveal visual evidence but must capture both cross-view and spatial influence for continuous outputs.

Method: Propose a hierarchical (coarse-to-fine) region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them, optimized with an L2-consistency objective with the original trajectory. Extract three attribution statistics: attribution entropy (concentration of reliance in the joint visual space), within-camera spatial variance (spread within each view), and cross-camera Gini coefficient (unevenness across cameras) as predictive signals for planning risk.

Result: Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, GenAD show that the three attribution statistics correlate with planning risk (Spearman 0.30 ± 0.07 with trajectory error) and achieve AUROC 0.77 ± 0.04 for collision detection. The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under an alternative attribution baseline.

Conclusion: Hierarchical attribution maps can support risk identification in end-to-end planning by revealing which views and regions most influence trajectory generation, with predictive signals that generalized across scenes and baselines.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models generate future trajectories from multi-view inputs, improving system integration but introducing opaque decisions and hard-to-localize risks. Existing methods either rely on auxiliary monitoring models or generate textual explanations, but are decoupled from the planning process and fail to reveal the visual evidence underlying trajectory generation. While attribution offers a direct alternative, planning differs from image classification by taking six-view camera images as input and predicting continuous multi-step trajectories, requiring attribution to capture both critical views and regions and their influence on outputs. Moreover, whether attribution maps can support risk identification remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning. Specifically, using L2 consistency with the original trajectory as the objective, we design a coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them. We further extract three attribution statistics as predictive signals for planning risk, including attribution entropy to measure how concentrated the planner's reliance is over the joint visual space, within-camera spatial variance to characterize how spread out the attribution is within each view, and cross-camera Gini coefficient to quantify how unevenly attribution is distributed across the six cameras. Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD show that these statistics correlate with planning risk, achieving Spearman correlations of $0.30 \pm 0.07$ with trajectory error and AUROC of $0.77 \pm 0.04$ for collision detection. The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under an alternative attribution baseline.

</details>


### [263] [A Flow Matching Algorithm for Many-Shot Adaptation to Unseen Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06272)
*Tyler Ingebrand,Ruihan Zhao,Kushagra Gupta,David Fridovich-Keil,Sandeep P. Chinchali,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FP-FM learns a basis of velocity fields over training distributions and uses a least-squares projection to adapt generation to new target distributions at inference without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable direct, data-driven conditioning of generative models on samples from arbitrary target distributions, addressing the challenge of adapting to new distributions efficiently at inference.

Method: Train basis functions that span velocity fields for a set of training distributions; at inference, compute a least-squares projection of the target distribution onto this basis to obtain coefficients that parameterize the generation process; introduce variants by enriching the coefficient computation (e.g., time-dependent coefficients) to trade expressivity for compute.

Result: Significant improvements in precision and recall over baselines on synthetic and image-based datasets, with especially large gains for unseen distributions.

Conclusion: FP-FM enables efficient, zero-shot adaptation to diverse target distributions via a learned basis of velocity fields and simple projection, with controllable expressivity–compute trade-offs through coefficient design.

Abstract: While generative modeling has achieved remarkable success on tasks like natural language-conditioned image generation, enabling model adaptation from example data points remains a relatively underexplored and challenging problem. To this end, we propose Function Projection for Flow Matching (FP-FM), an algorithm that directly conditions generation on samples from the target distribution. FP-FM learns basis functions to span the velocity fields corresponding to a set of training distributions, and adapts to new distributions by computing a simple least-squares projection onto this basis. This enables efficient generation of samples from diverse target distributions without additional training at inference time. We further introduce multiple variants of FP-FM that provide a trade-off in expressivity and compute by enriching the coefficient calculation, e.g., by making the coefficients dependent on time. FP-FM achieves greatly improved precision and recall relative to baselines across synthetic and image-based datasets, with especially strong gains on unseen distributions.

</details>


### [264] [PACE: Prune-And-Compress Ensemble Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06278)
*Fabian Akkerman,Julien Ferry,Théo Guyard,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACE is a two-phase ensemble method that alternates generating diverse new learners with pruning the augmented ensemble, achieving compact, faithful, high-performing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large ensembles yield state-of-the-art accuracy but are expensive to deploy and hard to interpret or verify; a method that prunes redundant learners while promoting efficient compression and preserving faithfulness to the original ensemble is desirable.

Method: PACE interleaves pruning and compression in two phases. Phase 1 actively generates new, diverse learners via a theoretically grounded procedure to enrich the ensemble. Phase 2 prunes the enriched ensemble when no further relevant learners can be found. Throughout both phases, PACE allows fine-grained control over faithfulness to the original ensemble.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that PACE outperforms prior pruning and compression methods and provides principled control over faithfulness guarantees.

Conclusion: PACE offers a principled, effective framework that combines learner generation with pruning, yielding compact, faithful ensembles with strong performance.

Abstract: Ensemble models achieve state-of-the-art performance on prediction tasks, but usually require aggregating a large number of weak learners. This can hinder deployment, interpretability, and downstream tasks such as robustness verification. Remedies to this issue fall into two main camps: pruning, which discards redundant learners, and compression, which generates new ones from scratch. We introduce PACE, a framework that interleaves these paradigms in a two-phase strategy. First, new learners are actively generated via a theoretically grounded procedure to enhance the diversity of the initial ensemble. When no more relevant learners can be found, a second phase of pruning is performed on this enriched ensemble. During both operations, PACE allows fine control on the faithfulness to the original ensemble. Experiments show that our method outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled control of faithfulness guarantees.

</details>


### [265] [INEUS: Iterative Neural Solver for High-Dimensional PIDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06281)
*Jean-Loup Dupret,Davide Gallon,Patrick Cheridito*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INEUS is a meshfree iterative neural solver for PIDEs that uses single-jump sampling to handle nonlocal terms, reformulates PIDE solving as recursive regression; it yields global space-time solutions with contraction-based convergence for linear PIDEs and scalable results in high dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the computational burden of nonlocal jump integrals in PIDEs and the difficulty of obtaining global space-time solutions with PINNs, especially in high dimensions; aims for efficient, meshfree, global solvers with provable convergence.

Method: Meshfree neural solver INEUS; replaces explicit nonlocal integral evaluation with single-jump sampling; Reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems; learns a global solution over space-time; provides contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs; avoids differentiating full PIDE residuals.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear PIDEs; supports the claim with a convergence proof for the linear case; shows improved efficiency in handling nonlocal terms compared to full residual differentiation.

Conclusion: INEUS offers an efficient, scalable meshfree framework for PIDEs with a provable convergence guarantee in the linear case, suitable for high-dimensional problems and potentially advantageous over PINNs for nonlocal operators.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce INEUS, a meshfree iterative neural solver for partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs). The method replaces the explicit evaluation of nonlocal jump integrals with single-jump sampling and reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems. Like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), INEUS learns global solutions over the entire space-time domain, yet it offers a more efficient treatment of nonlocal terms and avoids the computationally expensive differentiation of full PIDE residuals. These features make INEUS particularly well suited for high-dimensional PDEs and PIDEs. Supported by a contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs, our numerical experiments show that INEUS delivers accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear examples.

</details>


### [266] [Attributions All the Way Down? The Metagame of Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06295)
*Hubert Baniecki,Przemyslaw Biecek,Fabian Fumagalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A metagame framework for second-order attribution interactions using Shapley values to quantify how one feature's attribution influences another; provides hierarchical decomposition and directional interaction indices; validated across LMs, vision-language encoders, and diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify second-order effects in model explanations and capture how attributions for one feature affect attributions for others; address limitations of first-order attributions.

Method: Define meta-attribution φ_{j→i}(f) as the Shapley value of the attribution method treated as a cooperative game; establish hierarchical decomposition of attributions into meta-attributions; prove directional extensions of existing interaction indices.

Result: Empirical demonstrations across three domains: (i) token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (ii) cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, (iii) interpreting text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

Conclusion: The metagame provides a principled framework for second-order attribution interactions, enabling richer interpretability across modalities and extending attribution theory with hierarchical and directional analyses.

Abstract: We introduce the metagame, a conceptual framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects of model explanations. For any first-order attribution $φ(f)$ explaining a model $f$, we measure the directional influence of feature $j$ on the attribution of feature $i$, denoted as meta-attribution $\varphi_{j \to i}(f)$, by treating the attribution method itself as a cooperative game and computing its Shapley value. Theoretically, we prove that attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions, and establish these as directional extensions of existing interaction indices. Empirically, we demonstrate that the metagame delivers insights across diverse interpretability applications: (i) quantifying token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (ii) explaining cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, and (iii) interpreting text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

</details>


### [267] [Region Seeding via Pre-Activation Regularization: A Geometric View from Piecewise Affine Nerual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06300)
*Yi Wei,Xuan Qi,Furao Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theory-guided region-seeding: a sufficient condition to increase the realized affine-region count of deep nets with piecewise affine activations by aligning neuron-switching surfaces with data; paired with a plug-and-play regularizer to seed partitions early, boosting expressivity and early accuracy with comparable final performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training underutilizes potential polyhedral regions in networks with continuous piecewise affine activations; existing region-count theory is architecture-centric and lacks guidance on optimization impact on partitions near data.

Method: Develop a sufficient condition: bring neuron-switching surfaces near data points so their intersections with local neighborhoods increase the local affine-region count. Propose a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that promotes early partitioning; validate by exact enumeration of realized regions and training on toy datasets and ImageNet-1k for classical models.

Result: Regularizer increases realized affine-region count (as measured by exact enumeration); improves early-stage accuracy and overall performance on toy datasets; achieves comparable or slightly improved final accuracy on ImageNet-1k.

Conclusion: A practical training-time tool to seed data-relevant partitions, linking architectural capacity with optimization dynamics; enables earlier and more refined polyhedral partitions without sacrificing final performance; computational considerations (e.g., region enumeration) and applicability to various piecewise affine activations are important future considerations.

Abstract: Deep networks with continuous piecewise affine activations induce polyhedral partitions of the input space, making the number of realized affine regions a natural measure of expressive capacity and a key determinant of how well the model can approximate nonlinear target functions. In practice, standard training realizes far fewer region refinements in data-visited neighborhoods than the architecture could in principle support, while existing region-count theory is primarily architectural and offers little guidance on how optimization shapes the realized partition near the data. Our theory provides a sufficient condition under which bringing neuron switching surfaces sufficiently close to data points ensures their intersection with local neighborhoods, which in turn implies a strict increase in the local affine-region count, yielding a principled training-time handle for seeding data-relevant partitions early in optimization. Guided by these results, we propose a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning while allowing task-driven refinement to dominate later in training. Experiments show that the regularizer increases the number of realized affine regions via exact enumeration and improves overall performance on toy datasets, while also improving early-stage accuracy and achieving comparable (or slightly improved) final accuracy on ImageNet-1k for classical models.

</details>


### [268] [Molecules Meet Language: Confound-Aware Representation Learning and Chemical Property Steering in Transformer-VAE Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06303)
*Zakaria Elabid,Jan Andrzejewski,Bartosz Brzoza,Attila Cangi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent representations from a Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES contain sequence-level confounds; using residualization and linear/nonlinear probes reveals monotonic, property-specific steering directions that can reflect chemical organization when validated on decoded molecules and with confound control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether unsupervised molecular latent spaces encode chemically meaningful structure or merely sequence-level shortcuts, by disentangling confounds in SELFIES-derived representations.

Method: Freeze a pretrained Transformer-VAE on SELFIES, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, treat probe weights as candidate global steering directions, apply residualization and confound-direction alignment analysis, and perform decoded-molecule traversal to validate steering.

Result: Under confound-aware evaluation, observed robust monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes indicate some properties admit stable global directions, others rely on local gradients.

Conclusion: Chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but robust validation requires decoded-molecule checks and explicit control for representation-level confounds.

Abstract: Molecular generative models often assume meaningful latent geometry, but apparent property predictability can reflect sequence-level shortcuts rather than chemical organization. We study this issue in an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES. After training, we freeze the model, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, and use the probe weights as candidate global steering directions. To separate chemical signal from SELFIES artifacts, we introduce a confound-aware evaluation based on residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal. This is necessary because SELFIES length, branch tokens, ring tokens, and token entropy are strongly encoded in the latent space. Under this confound-aware evaluation, we find robust monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes further show that some properties admit stable global directions, while others are better described by local latent gradients. Overall, our results show that chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds.

</details>


### [269] [Perceive, Route and Modulate: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06310)
*Siru Zhong,Zhao Meng,Haohuan Fu,Haoyang Li,Qingsong Wen,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR) introduces a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mechanism that dynamically modulates token representations in time series models using a learnable basis of adaptive response patterns, via a Perceive-Route-Modulate pipeline and residual Hadamard gating.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Local temporal dynamics in real-world time series shift over time, causing globally shared transformations to become suboptimal; existing forecasting models rely on fixed weight matrices and thus cannot adapt to changing local patterns. DPR targets this bottleneck by enabling time-aware, token-level recalibration.

Method: DPR uses a Perceive-Route-Modulate pipeline to compute a soft routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, producing a modulation vector that modulates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product. It is designed as a backbone-agnostic adapter and also presented as a minimalist standalone model called DPRNet.

Result: As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead and validates the approach across 12 benchmarks. DPRNet achieves competitive performance, supporting the claim that dynamic recalibration addresses a general bottleneck beyond macroscopic parameter scaling.

Conclusion: Dynamic recalibration at the token level addresses a core bottleneck in real-world time-series forecasting by adapting to shifting local dynamics; DPR provides a general, efficient mechanism that benefits a range of architectures and also stands as a competitive standalone model (DPRNet).

Abstract: Local temporal patterns in real-world time series continuously shift, rendering globally shared transformations suboptimal. Current deep forecasting models, despite their scale and complexity, rely on fixed weight matrices applied uniformly to all temporal tokens. This creates a static pattern response: models settle into a compromised average, unable to adapt to changing local dynamics. We introduce Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR), a backbone-agnostic mechanism that resolves this via token-level recalibration. Through a lightweight "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline, DPR computes a soft-routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, generating a time-aware modulation vector that recalibrates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product. As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead, confirming it addresses a general bottleneck. As a minimalist standalone model, DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, validating dynamic recalibration against macroscopic parameter scaling.

</details>


### [270] [When Does $\ell_2$-Boosting Overfit Benignly? High-Dimensional Risk Asymptotics and the $\ell_1$ Implicit Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06314)
*Ye Su,Jian Li,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes benign overfitting under the l1 implicit bias of greedy ensembles (l2-Boosting) in high dimensions. Uses CGMT and truncated Gaussian moments to resolve the non-smooth l1 interpolant; shows excess variance decays as Θ(σ^2/log(p/n)) under isotropic noise, and slow log-rate for spiked designs. Presents a non-smooth boosting flow with an early-stopping rule that recovers Lasso-like guarantees and minimax-optimal empirical rates for l1-bounded signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how l1-implicit bias in greedy (boosting) ensembles impacts benign overfitting and risk scaling, where non-linear coordinate thresholds complicate traditional spectral analyses.

Method: Couple Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with asymptotics of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments to analyze the non-smooth l1 interpolant. Study continuous-time l2-Boosting on p features, n samples; analyze spiked-isotropic designs; derive non-smooth subdifferential dynamics and propose an early-stopping rule.

Result: Benign overfitting does not occur at the linear rate under the l1 bias: noise is localized into sparse active sets, yielding excess variance Θ(σ^2/log(p/n)). In spiked designs, risk tends to zero only at Θ(σ^2/log(r2/n)). A tuning-free early stopping rule, under a bounded l1-path condition, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and achieves the minimax-optimal empirical rate for l1-bounded signals.

Conclusion: The l1-geometry with greedy boosting exhibits slower (logarithmic) risk decay relative to l2; non-smooth dynamics enable a practical early-stopping strategy that matches Lasso-type performance for l1-bounded signals, though precise signal-noise decompositions remain open.

Abstract: Benign overfitting is well-characterized in $\ell_2$ geometries, but its behavior under the $\ell_1$ implicit bias of greedy ensembles remains challenging. The analytical barrier stems from the non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds, which invalidates standard spectral resolvent tools. To isolate this algorithmic bias, we characterize the high-dimensional risk of continuous-time $\ell_2$-Boosting over $p$ features and $n$ samples. By coupling the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with delicate asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments, we analytically resolve the non-smooth $\ell_1$ interpolant. Under an isotropic pure-noise model, we prove that benign overfitting fails at the linear rate: greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets, and the excess variance decays at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(p/n))$ for noise variance $σ^2$. We remark that while this localization mechanism should persist in the presence of signals, the exact signal-noise decomposition remains an open problem. For spiked-isotropic designs with $k^*$ head eigenvalues and $r_2 = p - k^*$ tail dimensions, the risk converges to zero when $r_{2} \gg n$, but only at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(r_2/n))$, which is slower than the linear decay observed in $\ell_2$ geometries. To avoid this slow convergence, we analyze the non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of the boosting flow. This yields a tuning-free early stopping rule that, under a bounded $\ell_1$-path condition, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and attains the minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for $\ell_1$-bounded signals.

</details>


### [271] [Pro-KLShampoo: Projected KL-Shampoo with Whitening Recovered by Orthogonalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06316)
*Ruotong Sun,Ermin Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pro-KLShampoo uses spike-and-flat eigenstructure to constrain one Kronecker factor to an r-dim subspace with full spectrum and a shared eigenvalue elsewhere, paired with orthogonalization; yields faster, memory-efficient preconditioning and better validation loss than KL-Shampoo across GPT-2/LLaMA scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM pretraining requires efficient gradient preconditioning; KL-Shampoo and Muon represent two frontiers but are usually developed separately. Observing spike-and-flat eigenstructure enables a hybrid approach that can recover full preconditioner and improve efficiency.

Method: Identify spike-and-flat eigenstructure in KL-Shampoo preconditioners under rank-ρ gradient model; restrict one Kronecker factor to a parametric family: r-dim subspace with full spectral structure; remaining n-r directions share a single eigenvalue; apply orthogonalization on those directions; derive identity showing orthogonalization reproduces full KL-Shampoo algebra; implement Pro-KLShampoo; evaluate on GPT-2 124M/350M and LLaMA 134M/450M.

Result: Pro-KLShampoo outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach the target loss across four pre-training scales.

Conclusion: Exploiting spike-and-flat eigenstructure with partial spectral tracking and orthogonalization yields practical performance and memory advantages; this integrates two optimization directions and suggests broader applicability.

Abstract: Optimizers that exploit the matrix structure of gradients are central to modern LLM pre-training, with two distinct frontiers: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning -- most recently KL-Shampoo, which estimates the preconditioner via KL divergence minimization -- and orthogonalization of the gradient momentum, exemplified by Muon and analyzed as steepest descent under the spectral norm. The two routes are typically developed in isolation. We make a structural observation about KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners: their eigenvalue spectra exhibit a \emph{spike-and-flat} shape -- a few dominant eigenvalues followed by an approximately uniform tail -- across layers and training stages, holding exactly under a rank-$ρ$ signal-plus-noise gradient model. We exploit this structure by restricting one of KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors to a parametric family aligned with the spike-and-flat shape: full spectral structure on a tracked $r$-dimensional subspace, single shared eigenvalue across the remaining $n-r$ directions. On these directions, we apply orthogonalization. An identity shows that this orthogonalization recovers the algebraic form of full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner. On four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M / 350M, LLaMA 134M / 450M), Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank we test in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

</details>


### [272] [LINC: Decoupling Local Consequence Scoring from Hidden Matching in Constructive Neural Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06332)
*Shaofeng Qin,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LINC is a decoder-side local inference approach for constructive neural routing solvers that explicitly models one-step deterministic consequences, improving gaps on CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP compared to PolyNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the hidden deterministic one-step transitions (travel, waiting, slack, capacity changes) hidden inside decoder context, which can hinder learning global routing decisions; by making these consequences explicit, the model can reason more accurately.

Method: LINC computes explicit local consequences: centered relative consequences are compared with a shared linear local scorer; feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context; preserves standard global matching and avoids rediscovering transition arithmetic; applied to CVRPTW with extensions to CVRP and TSP.

Result: On CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83%/38.15% to 7.26%/14.71%; also improves external-benchmark gaps for TSP and CVRP.

Conclusion: LINC demonstrates that incorporating explicit local inference of one-step routing consequences preserves global matching while reducing solution gaps across key routing problems, with demonstrated gains on CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP.

Abstract: Constructive neural routing solvers usually score the next action by matching a decoder context to candidate embeddings, hiding deterministic one-step consequences such as travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes. We propose LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison), a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that computes these consequences explicitly. LINC uses them according to their decision role: centered relative consequences are compared by a shared linear local scorer, while feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context. This preserves standard global matching and relieves the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic. The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW) serves as the main constrained-routing stress test; the same interface extends to the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) and Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). In particular, for CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83\%/38.15\% to 7.26\%/14.71\%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

</details>


### [273] [Eliciting associations between clinical variables from LLMs via comparison questions across populations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06335)
*Fabian Kabus,Kian Kordtomeikel,Thomas Brox,Heinz Wiendl,Daiana Stolz,Harald Binder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a method to extract correlation and potential causal structure from LLMs using structured triplet comparison prompts, enabling invariant causal prediction (ICP) to identify candidate causal parents in COPD and MS without accessing model internals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To recover meaningful association and potential causal links from LLMs trained on heterogeneous biomedical literature, without probing internal activations, thereby informing medical decision-making across diverse patient populations.

Method: Use structured patient comparison triplet questions and a statistical model of LLM representations (without internal access) combined with prompt-level environment shifts. Apply invariant causal prediction (ICP) to infer conservative candidate parent links. Validate in COPD and MS domains.

Result:  elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable across environments, yet show statistically significant variation enabling ICP to extract a small set of invariant candidate parent links.

Conclusion: Indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure in LLMs and offer a cautious path from implicit correlations to causal statements aligned with LLM answering patterns.

Abstract: The training data of large language models (LLMs) comprises a wide range of biomedical literature, reflecting data from many different patient populations. We investigate how it might be possible to recover information on correlation and causal links between patient characteristics, as a key building block for medical decision making. To avoid the pitfalls of direct elicitation, we propose an approach based on structured comparison questions, specifically patient comparison triplet questions. This is combined with a statistical model for the LLM representation that provides estimates of correlations without access to activations or model internals. Intuitively, we consider how similarity decisions of LLMs based on a first variable are affected by providing information on a second variable for one of the patients being assessed. We then induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain correlation estimates for different subpopulations, which enables an invariant causal prediction (ICP) approach to obtain conservative candidate parent links. We demonstrate the method in two clinical domains, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and multiple sclerosis (MS). Across prompted environments, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in a statistically significant way that supports downstream invariance testing, such that ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links. These results show that indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offer a cautious route from implicit correlations to causal statements that are congruent with LLM answering patterns.

</details>


### [274] [Is Escalation Worth It? A Decision-Theoretic Characterization of LLM Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06350)
*Dylan Bouchard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decision-theoretic framework for model cascades using constrained optimization and duality; shows piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier for two-model cascades, and that the k-model frontier is the envelope of pairwise cascades with switching points; a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stages; empirical results: deterministic chains underperform the pairwise envelope; lightweight pre-generation routing often beats cascade policies; performance limited by upfront structural cost rather than number of stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify the geometry of the cost-quality frontier in model cascades and to provide principled design guidance for deferral thresholds and cascade structure under budget and quality constraints.

Method: Develops a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. Proves piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier for two-model cascades on decreasing-benefit confidence regions; links budget- and quality-constrained formulations via reciprocal shadow prices. Extends to k-model cascades by characterizing the frontier as the envelope of binomial(k,2) pairwise cascades with switching points. Derives first-order conditions for k-model cascades where a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. Empirically validates on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) using eight models from five providers, comparing deterministic threshold cascades, fixed chains, subsequences, and a lightweight router.

Result: The frontier is piecewise convex/concave on the confidence support; the overall k-model frontier equals the envelope of all pairwise cascades with switching points. A single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stages. Deterministic fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope; optimized subsequences offer negligible held-out gains. A lightweight pre-generation router surpasses the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly by avoiding cheap-model costs when sending queries to larger models. Overall, cascade performance is limited by structural cost rather than the availability of intermediate stages.

Conclusion: The primary bottleneck in cascade effectiveness is upfront structural cost: the cheap model incurs cost before escalation decisions. The pairwise envelope with a single shadow price provides principled guidance for cascade design, and routers can yield practical gains. Future work could explore stochastic or adaptive policies, budget uncertainty, and alternative cost structures.

Abstract: Model cascades, in which a cheap LLM defers to an expensive one on low-confidence queries, are widely used to navigate the cost-quality tradeoff at deployment. Existing approaches largely treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter, with limited guidance on the geometry of the resulting cost-quality frontier over a model pool. We develop a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For a two-model cascade, we establish piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support, with reciprocal shadow prices linking the budget- and quality-constrained formulations. Given a pool of $k$ models, we characterize the frontier achievable by deterministic two-model threshold cascades as the pointwise envelope over $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise cascades, with switching points where the optimal pair changes. For $k$-model cascades, we derive first-order conditions in which a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. We validate the framework on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) across eight models from five providers. Within the deterministic threshold-cascade class, full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequence cascades do not deliver practically meaningful held-out gains over it. A lightweight pre-generation router exceeds the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly because it avoids the cheap model's generation cost on queries sent directly to a larger model rather than because of a stronger routing signal. These results suggest that cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost, since cascades pay the cheap model before any escalation decision, rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

</details>


### [275] [Topological Signatures of Grokking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06352)
*Yifan Tang,Qiquan Wang,Inés García-Redondo,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological analysis reveals grokking as a topological transition: a sharp rise in H1 persistence (max and total) in embedding-derived point clouds across models trained on modular arithmetic with primes; a dominant long-lived cycle emerges, with structured secondary features, suggesting a unified geometric/topological view of representation learning and generalization beyond memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand grokking (delayed generalization) by characterizing the evolving latent structure of neural representations using persistent homology, to capture multi-scale global and local topology.

Method: Construct point clouds from embedding matrices across models and training runs (varying primes in modular arithmetic). Compute persistent homology (H1) to obtain persistence diagrams; track max and total H1 persistence, identify dominant long-lived features and secondary structures; compare with Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension; perform ablations across data regimes and control settings.

Result: Identify a clear topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of H1. Persistence diagrams show a dominant long-lived feature and increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the task's cyclic structure. Persistent homology provides a unified geometric/topological characterization of representation learning, capturing local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations link topological transitions to generalization rather than memorization.

Conclusion: Persistent homology offers a principled, interpretable framework to analyze how neural networks internalize latent structure during training and relates to grokking via topological transitions in learned representations.

Abstract: We study the grokking phenomenon through the lens of topology. Using persistent homology on point clouds derived from the embedding matrices of a range of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, we identify a clear and consistent topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of first homology ($H_1$). Persistence diagrams reveal the emergence of a dominant long-lived topological feature together with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the underlying cyclic structure of the task. Compared to existing spectral and geometric diagnostics -- specifically, Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension -- persistent homology provides a unified geometric and topological characterization of representation learning, capturing both local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations across data regimes and control settings show that these topological transitions are tied to generalization rather than memorization. Our results suggest that persistent homology offers a principled and interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure during training.

</details>


### [276] [Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Modelling with Missing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06355)
*Ignacio Peis,Pablo M. Olmos,Jes Frellsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MO-ARM is a missingness-aware, order-agnostic autoregressive model that can be trained on incomplete data under general missingness, enabling robust imputation and active information acquisition, and it outperforms standard imputation baselines on real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incomplete data is pervasive in real-world datasets. Order-agnostic autoregressive models are powerful but underexplored for missing data. Reinterpreting training as imputation under MCAR and extending to general missingness can enable principled learning and active data acquisition.

Method: (1) Show that standard training on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under MCAR, yielding robust out-of-sample imputation when missingness is high. (2) Propose MO-ARM: a principled framework for training autoregressive models directly on incomplete data under general missingness mechanisms. (3) Leverage amortized conditional density estimation to enable active information acquisition by sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream tasks. (4) Evaluate on real-world benchmarks and compare to established imputation baselines.

Result: Across real-world benchmarks, MO-ARM consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

Conclusion: MO-ARM provides a missingness-aware framework that enables robust imputation and informative data acquisition with strong empirical gains over traditional imputation methods.

Abstract: Order-Agnostic autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in deep generative modeling, yet their use in settings with incomplete data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we reinterpret them through the lens of missing data. First, we show that their standard training procedure on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under a missing completely at random mechanism, resulting in robust out-of-sample imputation performance in settings with high missingness. Second, we introduce the first principled framework for training them directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms. Third, we leverage their amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition, i.e., sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream prediction or inference. Across a suite of real-world benchmarks, our Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

</details>


### [277] [Preliminary Insights in Chronos Frequency Data Understanding and Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06361)
*Alessandro Pagani,Marco Cominelli,Liying Han,Gaofeng Dong,Sergio Benini,Francesco Gringoli,Mattia Savardi,Mani B. Srivastava,Trevor Bihl,Erik P. Blasch,Daniel O. Brigham,Kara Combs,Lance M. Kaplan,Federico Cerutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chronos' frequency-domain representations are probed to assess how well a temporal foundation model encodes sinusoidal content. Using online minimum description length probes on the decoder, the work reveals how frequency information is captured across the spectrum, highlighting regimes where representation quality degrades and providing guidance for signal processing and information fusion applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for time-series are touted for learning generic temporal representations, but their ability to encode fundamental signal properties, such as frequency content, is not well characterized. This study seeks to quantify and interpret Chronos' internal frequential representations under controlled sinusoidal inputs.

Method: Prepare discrete sinusoid signals at fixed frequencies and apply lightweight online minimum description length (MDL) probes to Chronos' decoder to test for the presence and separability of frequency information in internal representations.

Result: The probes yield insights into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum, identifying regimes where representation quality may degrade or require special handling.

Conclusion: The findings offer practical guidance for Chronos users in signal processing and information fusion tasks and contribute to broader efforts to improve interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

Abstract: This paper presents a preliminary analysis of the ability of Chronos foundation model to process and internally represent frequency domain information. Foundation models that process time-series data offer practitioners a unified architecture capable of learning generic temporal representations across diverse tasks and domains, reducing the need for task-specific feature engineering and enabling transfer across signal modalities. Despite their growing adoption, the extent to which such models encode fundamental signal properties remains insufficiently characterised. We address this gap by analysing Chronos under controlled conditions, starting from the simplest class of signals: discrete sinusoids generated at fixed frequencies. Using lightweight online minimum description length probes applied to the decoder architecture, we test for the presence and separability of frequency information in the model's internal representations. The results provide insight into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum and highlight regimes in which representation quality may degrade or require particular care. These findings offer practical guidance for users of Chronos in signal processing and information fusion contexts, and contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

</details>


### [278] [Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06364)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general flow matching framework (AuxPath-FM) introduces an auxiliary variable η drawn from arbitrary distributions into probability paths X_t = a(t) X_1 + b(t) X_0 + c(t) η, preserving the continuity equation and marginal objective; enables diverse priors (Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, Rademacher) and tasks like label-guided generation; offers theoretical generality and practical flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
