Motivation: To generalize conditional flow matching by relaxing the auxiliary noise to arbitrary distributions, enabling richer geometry and control via priors, while maintaining the theoretical guarantees and training objective.

Method: Propose AuxPath-FM, define probability path with auxiliary η from arbitrary distribution; show continuity equation preserved; show training objective aligns with marginal formulation; discuss specific distributions and design of a(t), b(t), c(t) functions; discuss potential for label-guided generation via encoding semantic info into η; theoretical analyses.

Result: The main results are theoretical: preservation of continuity equation and consistency with marginal objective; demonstration that various priors lead to different geometry; enabling flexibility; potential for improved generative performance with appropriate priors; possibly not empirical results in abstract.

Conclusion: AuxPath-FM provides a principled general foundation for probability path design in flow-based generative models, enabling flexible priors and applications such as label-guided generation, while retaining theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: We introduce a new generative modeling framework, \textbf{Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM)}, which generalizes conditional flow matching by incorporating an auxiliary variable drawn from an arbitrary distribution into the probability path. Unlike prior methods that restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, AuxPath-FM allows the variable $η$ to follow any distribution, producing trajectories of the form $X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η$. We theoretically demonstrate that this construction preserves the continuity equation and maintains a training objective consistent with the marginal formulation. This flexibility enables the design of diverse probability paths using various priors, including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher distributions, each offering unique geometric properties for generative flows. Furthermore, our framework allows for specialized tasks such as label-guided generation by encoding structured semantic information into the auxiliary distribution. Overall, AuxPath-FM provides a principled and general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

</details>


### [279] [A Unified Pair-GRPO Family: From Implicit to Explicit Preference Constraints for Stable and General RL Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06375)
*Hao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Pair-GRPO: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO, a unified preference-based RL optimization framework for LLM alignment, featuring a gradient equivalence to GRPO, theoretical guarantees, and strong empirical performance on RLHF benchmarks and MuJoCo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability of policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) for LLM alignment.

Method: Soft-Pair-GRPO minimally modifies GRPO by using binary pairwise preferences in place of scalar rewards while preserving the clipped surrogate and KL regularization; proves a first-order gradient equivalence with standard GRPO. Hard-Pair-GRPO adds explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting to further reduce gradient noise and policy drift. The authors provide theoretical guarantees for both variants (monotone policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, dynamic step-size convergence) and comprehensive experimental validation.

Result: Theoretical guarantees and extensive experiments show that Pair-GRPO variants outperform state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human-preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to broader RL settings, demonstrated on HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback, and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablations confirm the importance of core components.

Conclusion: Pair-GRPO offers a unified, stable, and theoretically grounded framework for preference-based RL in LLM alignment, with practical benefits across standard alignment benchmarks and general RL tasks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) alignment via reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in mainstream pairwise preference learning paradigms. To systematically address these limitations, we establish a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization centered on the Pair-GRPO family, comprising two tightly coupled variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO is a minimal modification of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that replaces group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure. We prove a critical gradient equivalence theorem: under first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of standard GRPO's gradient, explaining its empirical stability despite discarding continuous reward magnitudes. Building on this foundation, we propose Hard-Pair-GRPO, an advanced variant introducing explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and global policy drift. We provide comprehensive theoretical guarantees for both variants--including monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Extensive experiments on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF,UltraFeedback) and the MuJoCo continuous control task HalfCheetah-v4 demonstrate that our Pair-GRPO family consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general reinforcement learning. Ablation studies validate the critical contributions of each core component.

</details>


### [280] [MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06384)
*Alessandro Ronca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) propose a recurrence based on MinMax operations that addresses gradient issues, offers finite-memory expressivity, parallelizable evaluation, bounded states, and stable gradients, with strong empirical results on synthetic benchmarks and competitive next-token performance at 127M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To build a recurrent architecture that combines strong theoretical guarantees (finite-memory expressivity and gradient stability) with practical advantages (parallelizable computation and bounded activations), addressing common RNN issues like vanishing/exploding gradients while maintaining power for sequence modeling.

Method: Construct cascaded MinMax recurrent layers (RNCs). Theoretical analysis shows expressivity to cover all regular languages, parallel evaluation with log-length runtime given sufficient processors, and uniform bounds on state/activations and gradients; gradient w.r.t. past state is non-vanishing (often constant). Empirically validate on synthetic tasks and train a 127M-parameter MinMax RNC for next-token prediction.

Result: The framework is expressive enough to encompass all regular languages; evaluation can be performed in parallel with logarithmic time complexity in input length; states and activations are uniformly bounded; gradients exist almost everywhere and are bounded; the state gradient to past states does not vanish and can remain constant; empirically, MinMax RNCs solve several synthetic tasks better than considered SOTA RNNs and a 127M-parameter model achieves competitive performance on next-token prediction.

Conclusion: MinMax RNCs offer promising theoretical and empirical advantages for finite-memory sequence modeling, combining expressivity, stability, and efficiency. They warrant further benchmarking against standard architectures on diverse tasks to assess real-world impact and scalability.

Abstract: We show that the MinMax algebra provides a form of recurrence that is expressively powerful, efficiently implementable, and most importantly it is not affected by vanishing or exploding gradient. We call MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) the models obtained by cascading several layers of neurons that employ such recurrence. We show that MinMax RNCs enjoy many favourable theoretical properties. First, their formal expressivity includes all regular languages, arguably the maximal expressivity for a finite-memory system. Second, they can be evaluated in parallel with a runtime that is logarithmic in the input length given enough processors; and they can also be evaluated sequentially. Third, their state and activations are bounded uniformly for all input lengths. Fourth, at almost all points, their loss gradient exists and it is bounded. Fifth, they do not exhibit a vanishing state gradient: the gradient of a state w.r.t. a past state can have constant value one regardless of the time distance between the two states. Finally, we find empirical evidence that the favourable theoretical properties of MinMax RNCs are matched by their practical capabilities: they are able to perfectly solve a number of synthetic tasks, showing superior performance compared to the considered state-of-the-art recurrent neural networks; also, we train a MinMax RNC of 127M parameters on next-token prediction, and the obtained model shows competitive performance for its size, providing evidence of the potential of MinMax RNCs on real-world tasks.

</details>


### [281] [Data-Driven Covariate Selection for Nonparametric and Cycle-Agnostic Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06385)
*Ana Leticia Garcez Vicente,Gijs van Seeventer,Saber Salehkaleybar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A local, data-driven conditional-independence-based covariate selection method extends from acyclic to cyclic causal models via sigma-acyclification, enabling cycle-agnostic causal effect estimation with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify valid adjustment sets in observational data with latent confounding and feedback loops; standard methods rely on acyclicity or global structure learning, limiting applicability and efficiency.

Method: A covariate selection procedure that uses conditional independence information. The key theoretical contribution is showing that invariance of conditional independence under σ-acyclification yields soundness and completeness of the method in both cyclic and acyclic causal models.

Result: The paper proves that the independence-based selection guarantees extend to cyclic models; empirical validation on extensive synthetic datasets demonstrates reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

Conclusion: Provides a unified, cycle-agnostic framework for covariate selection and causal effect estimation that applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification, supported by synthetic-data experiments.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data requires identifying valid adjustment sets. This task is especially challenging in realistic settings where latent confounding and feedback loops are present. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, limiting applicability and computational efficiency. In this work, we study a local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information. While this method is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models, its validity in the presence of cycles has remained unclear. Our main contribution is to show that these guarantees extend to cyclic causal models. In particular, our result relies on the invariance of conditional independence assertions under $σ$-acyclification. These findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation, showing that the method applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, we validate this on extensive synthetic data, showing reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

</details>


### [282] [Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation: Bridging Exploitation and Imitation at the Token Level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06387)
*Nan Jia,Haojin Yang,Xing Ma,Jiesong Lian,Shuailiang Zhang,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Zequn Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOPD improves on-policy distillation by replacing negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions, yielding stronger performance, higher entropy, and better retained skills on math-reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD suffers from high-variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks due to insufficient corrective signals; a stable, efficient learning signal is needed for on-policy distillation.

Method: Introduce Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD) that preserves positive reinforcement while substituting ineffective negative updates with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions. Evaluate on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, comparing against standard OPD under strong and weak initialization.

Result: AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 (strong init) and 8.34 (weak init). It also maintains higher policy entropy during training and preserves capability better during sequential tool-use adaptation.

Conclusion: Localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions effectively addresses OPD's variance, vanishing gradients, and exploration bottlenecks, yielding more robust learning and better transfer to sequential tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own trajectories with token-level teacher feedback and often outperforms off-policy distillation and standard reinforcement learning. However, we find that its standard advantage weighted policy gradient suffers from three structural weaknesses, including high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.We therefore propose Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization, respectively. AOPD also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

</details>


### [283] [Consistent Geometric Deep Learning via Hilbert Bundles and Cellular Sheaves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06395)
*Kartik Tandon,Julian Gould,Tanishq Bhatia,Francesca Dominici,Alejandro Ribeiro,Claudio Battiloro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HilbNets enable learning on infinite-dimensional signals on manifolds via the connection Laplacian, with a two-stage sampling scheme that ensures convergence and transferability across samplings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a unified learning theory for infinite-dimensional signals on irregular domains; extend geometric learning to Hilbert bundles by using the connection Laplacian and proving convergence of discrete approximations.

Method: Define convolution via the connection Laplacian of a Hilbert bundle; sample to obtain a Hilbert Cellular Sheaf and an edge-wise coupling; prove the sheaf-Laplacian converges in probability to the connection Laplacian; discretize signals to form implementable HilbNets and prove convergence to the continuous model and transferability across sampling schemes.

Result: Proves probabilistic convergence of the sheaf-Laplacian to the true connection Laplacian; discretized HilbNets converge to the continuous architectures and are transferable across samplings; validated on synthetic and real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Extends geometric learning by lifting Laplacian-based methods to settings where the signal at each point lies in a Hilbert space, broadening applicability to infinite-dimensional, irregular-domain data.

Abstract: Modern deep learning architectures increasingly contend with sophisticated signals that are natively infinite-dimensional, such as time series, probability distributions, or operators, and are defined over irregular domains. Yet, a unified learning theory for these settings has been lacking. To start addressing this gap, we introduce a novel convolutional learning framework for possibly infinite-dimensional signals supported on a manifold. Namely, we use the connection Laplacian associated with a Hilbert bundle as a convolutional operator, and we derive filters and neural networks, dubbed as \textit{HilbNets}. We make HilbNets and, more generally, the convolution operation, implementable via a two-stage sampling procedure. First, we show that sampling the manifold induces a Hilbert Cellular Sheaf, a generalized graph structure with Hilbert feature spaces and edge-wise coupling rules, and we prove that its sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to the underlying connection Laplacian as the sampling density increases. Notably, this result is a generalization to the infinite-dimensional bundle setting of the Belkin \& Niyogi \cite{BELKIN20081289} convergence result for the graph Laplacian to the manifold Laplacian, a theoretical cornerstone of geometric learning methods. Second, we discretize the signals and prove that the discretized (implementable) HilbNets converge to the underlying continuous architectures and are transferable across different samplings of the same bundle, providing consistency for learning. Finally, we validate our framework on synthetic and real-world tasks. Overall, our results broaden the scope of geometric learning as a whole by lifting classical Laplacian-based frameworks to settings where the signal at each point lives in its own Hilbert space.

</details>


### [284] [SparseForge: Efficient Semi-Structured LLM Sparsification via Annealing of Hessian-Guided Soft-Mask](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06402)
*Liu Hanzuo,Chaofan Lin,Weixuan Sun,Yulong Wang,Key,Rayying,Mingyu Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training framework SparseForge optimizes sparsity masks directly for semi-structured pruning, using Hessian-aware importance estimation and progressive soft-mask annealing to achieve strong accuracy with far fewer retraining tokens; demonstrated on LLaMA-2-7B at 2:4 sparsity (5B tokens) achieving 57.27% zero-shot accuracy, beating the dense baseline and nearing a 40B-token sparse-retraining method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost of sparse model recovery by directly optimizing the sparsity mask instead of scaling retraining tokens; address strong structural coupling in semi-structured pruning and enable hardware-friendly structured sparsity.

Method: Post-training framework SparseForge using Hessian-aware importance estimation to rank candidate mask changes and progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery without large-scale sparse retraining.

Result: On LLaMA-2-7B at 2:4 sparsity, achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with 5B retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% and approaching the state-of-the-art method’s 57.52% with 40B tokens.

Conclusion: SparseForge improves the accuracy–efficiency trade-off for post-training semi-structured sparsity, with consistent improvements across model families.

Abstract: Semi-structured sparsity provides a practical path to accelerate large language models (LLMs) with native hardware support, but post-training semi-structured pruning often suffers from substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling. Existing methods rely on large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, resulting in high computational cost.
  We propose SparseForge, a post-training framework that improves recovery efficiency by directly optimizing the sparsity mask rather than scaling up retraining tokens. SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery. On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only $\textbf{5B}$ retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% accuracy and approaching the 57.52% result of a state-of-the-art method using $\textbf{40B}$ tokens. Such improvements on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off from SparseForge are shown to be consistent across model families.

</details>


### [285] [FRInGe: Distribution-Space Integrated Gradients with Fisher--Rao Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06404)
*Gabriele Martino,Sebastian Tschiatschek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRInGe: Fisher-Rao Integrated Gradients uses predictive-distribution space, maximum-entropy reference, and Fisher geodesics to produce calibration-friendly attributions; shows improved MAS scores on ImageNet while staying competitive on AUC and infidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IG can be brittle due to baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. A more faithful attribution method is needed that respects the geometry of predictive distributions.

Method: Define reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space using a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. Replace input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference. Realize the input-space trajectory via the pullback Fisher metric and stabilize with KL and Euclidean trust regions. Obtain attributions by integrating input gradients along this trajectory.

Result: Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics (MAS) and remains competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

Conclusion: FRInGe provides more calibration-aware attributions without sacrificing performance on other attribution metrics, leveraging Fisher geometry to define a distribution-space path for gradient integration.

Abstract: Gradient-based attribution methods are model-faithful and scalable, but Integrated Gradients (IG) can be brittle because explanations depend on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. We propose Fisher--Rao Integrated Gradients (FRInGe), which defines both the reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space. FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The corresponding input-space trajectory is realized through the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions; attributions are obtained by integrating input gradients along this trajectory. Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe most clearly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics, especially MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

</details>


### [286] [Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06433)
*Selin Ceydeli,Rui Wang,Kubilay Atasu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange scheme with federated parameter aggregation enables distributed GNNs to match centralized node representations for subgraph pattern detection under an extended-subgraph assumption; embedding freshness per step is crucial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph data are often partitioned across multiple parties, making centralized GNN training infeasible. This leads to a representation-equivalence gap where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. A privacy-preserving, distributed solution is needed.

Method: Clients exchange intermediate node embeddings at each forward-layer step, synchronizing representations while keeping raw features and labels private. The approach relies on shared model parameters and an extended-subgraph assumption. Federated parameter aggregation is used in tandem. Evaluations are conducted on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns.

Result: Embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary; together they recover most of the representation gap, outperforming either alone. Fresh per-step embeddings are essential to close the gap, whereas stale per-epoch embeddings are ineffective.

Conclusion: A practical framework for privacy-preserving distributed subgraph pattern detection that matches centralized GNN representations under suitable assumptions; effectiveness hinges on per-step embedding freshness and the extended-subgraph condition. The findings highlight the complementary nature of embedding exchange and federated aggregation in bridging the observability gap.

Abstract: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.

</details>


### [287] [FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06446)
*Junye Du,Zhenghao Li,Yushi Feng,Long Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFrozen is a two-stage federated optimization method for Transformers under client heterogeneity. It first warm-ups the full model, then freezes the query/key block (the attention kernel) and continues optimizing the value block. Interpreted under linear-attention, the warm-up is an inexact descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage optimizes the value block with a fixed kernel. A trade-off determines the optimal warm-up length. Empirical results show reduced bias-drift and improved stability and performance in heterogeneous FL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address client drift and instability in heterogeneous federated learning. Leverage the observed robustness of Transformer architectures by dissecting attention into kernel (Q/K) and transformation (V) components and analyzing their distinct optimization roles.

Method: Two-stage optimization: (1) full-model warm-up training; (2) freeze query/key block and continue optimizing value block. Use a linear-attention formulation. The warm-up is interpreted as an inexact descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective; the frozen stage becomes restricted optimization of the value block under a fixed kernel.

Result: The analysis reveals a bias-drift trade-off tied to warm-up length; simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior; experiments on real data show FedFrozen improves stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

Conclusion: Decomposing attention into Q/K and V provides a principled, effective control for FL optimization under heterogeneity. The warm-up length balances bias and drift; FedFrozen offers a practical approach to enhance Transformer performance in heterogeneous FL, validated by theory and experiments.

Abstract: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients remains a significant challenge for deep learning, primarily due to client drift arising from inconsistent local updates. Existing federated optimization methods typically address this issue through objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms. Recent studies, however, suggest that Transformer-based architectures may be inherently more robust than conventional models under heterogeneous federated training. Motivated by this observation, we investigate how different parameter components within the attention mechanism influence federated optimization. Specifically, we decompose the attention module into a query/key block, which determines the attention kernel, and a value block, which performs semantic transformation under the induced kernel. Based on this perspective, we propose FedFrozen, a two-stage federated optimization framework that first performs full-model warm-up training and then freezes the query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block. Under a linear-attention formulation, we show that the warm-up stage can be interpreted as an inexact descent procedure on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to a restricted value-block optimization problem under a fixed attention kernel. Our analysis further reveals an explicit trade-off that governs the choice of warm-up length. Simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments demonstrate that FedFrozen improves both the stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

</details>


### [288] [Scene-Adaptive Continual Learning for CSI-based Human Activity Recognition with Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06447)
*Wenhan Zheng,Yuyi Mao,Ivan Wang-Hei Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable continual learning approach for CSI-based HAR using Scene-Adaptive Mixtures of Experts with clustered specialists (SAMoE-C), achieving competitive accuracy with lower inference cost and small replay-buffer training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CSI-based HAR performance degrades under domain shifts across environments. Existing continual learning methods struggle with scalability, require large replay buffers, or incur increasing inference costs. A scalable, efficient cross-domain solution is needed.

Method: SAMoE-C formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system with scene-specific adaptation. It employs an attention-based semantic router that activates only a subset of experts per input, and clustered specialists to capture scene-specific patterns. A novel training protocol uses a tiny replay buffer to stabilize router-domain discrimination, enabling scalable continual learning with low overhead.

Result: On a four-scene CSI HAR dataset, SAMoE-C achieves competitive accuracy (near state-of-the-art) while significantly reducing inference cost. The approach demonstrates low training overhead and scalable, efficient cross-domain deployment.

Conclusion: SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings, by combining modular experts, selective router activation, and a lightweight training protocol.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI)-based human activity recognition (HAR) is vulnerable to performance degradation under domain shifts across varying physical environments. Continual learning (CL) offers a principled way to learn new domains sequentially while preserving past knowledge, but existing CL solutions for CSI-based HAR scale poorly with accumulating domains, rely on a large replay buffer, or incur linearly growing inference cost. In this letter, we propose Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system that enables scene-specific adaptation, via an attention-based semantic router that activates only selected experts for each input. Moreover, we develop a novel training protocol, which requires only a tiny replay buffer for stabilizing domain discrimination of the router. Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset demonstrate that SAMoE-C approaches the state-of-the-art accuracy, while maintaining a significantly lower inference cost. By jointly combining modular experts, selective activation with router and a lightweight training protocol, SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

</details>


### [289] [ORTHOBO: Orthogonal Bayesian Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06454)
*Maresa Schröder,Pascal Janetzky,Michael Klar,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrthoBO introduces a variance-reducing orthogonal acquisition estimator for Bayesian optimization, using a weight-optimized score-function control variate to suppress Monte Carlo noise, stabilizing rankings and improving hyperparameter optimization performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monte Carlo estimation noise in acquisition functions can flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal decisions in Bayesian optimization, especially with expensive evaluations.

Method: Develop an orthogonal acquisition estimator by subtracting an optimally weighted score-function control variate, creating an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions; combine with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transform; provide theoretical guarantees of variance reduction and target preservation; present the OrthoBO framework.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows variance reduction and preservation of the acquisition target; experiments demonstrate reduced acquisition variance, more stable candidate rankings, and strong performance; practical validation in neural network hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: Orthogonal variance reduction for acquisition estimation (OrthoBO) yields more stable decisions and improved performance in BO, particularly for expensive hyperparameter optimization tasks, and benefits from ensemble surrogates and log-transformed acquisition.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is widely used for hyperparameter optimization when model evaluations are expensive; however, noisy acquisition estimates can lead to unstable decisions. We identify acquisition estimation noise as a failure mode that was previously overlooked: even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified, finite-sample Monte Carlo error can perturb acquisition values. This can, in turn, flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal BO decisions. As a remedy, we aim at variance reduction and propose an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, which yields an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions and which thus reduces Monte Carlo variance. We further introduce OrthoBO: a Bayesian optimization framework that combines our orthogonal acquisition estimator with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. We show theoretically that our estimator preserves the target, leads to variance reduction, and improves pairwise ranking stability. We further verify the theoretical properties of OrthoBO through numerical experiments where our framework reduces acquisition estimation variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and achieves strong performance. We also demonstrate the downstream utility of OrthoBO in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

</details>


### [290] [Invariant Features in Language Models: Geometric Characterization and Model Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06458)
*Agnibh Dasgupta,Abdullah Tanvir,Xin Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a local geometric view of semantic invariance in language models: semantically equivalent inputs form structured regions in latent space where paraphrase variation lies in nuisance directions and semantics remain in invariant subspaces. Three contributions are: (1) geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method to separate semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Empirically, invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions; semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace; causal role of invariant components in outputs; and model-specific geometric patterns enable accurate attribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong paraphrase robustness in language models, the structure and origin of invariance are unclear. The work seeks a principled, geometric understanding of how semantic information is encoded and preserved in latent representations.

Method: Introduce a local geometric framework where paraphrase-equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space. Define nuisance directions along which paraphrase variations occur and invariant subspaces that preserve semantics. Develop a contrastive subspace discovery method to separate semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation. Apply invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution and validate across models and layers.

Result: Across models and layers, invariant structures appear in particular depth regions. Semantic displacement largely lies outside the nuisance subspace. Representation-level interventions reveal a causal role for invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution.

Conclusion: Semantic invariance can be understood as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning and supporting tasks like zero-shot attribution.

Abstract: Language models exhibit strong robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting that semantic information may be encoded through stable internal representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain unclear. We propose a local geometric framework in which semantically equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. Building on this view, we make three contributions: (1) a geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method that separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) an application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Across models and layers, empirical results support these contributions. Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions, semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace, and representation-level interventions indicate a causal role of invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution. These findings suggest that semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

</details>


### [291] [MINER: Mining Multimodal Internal Representation for Efficient Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06460)
*Weien Li,Rui Song,Zeyu Li,Haochen Liu,Gonghao Zhang,Difan Jiao,Zhenwei Tang,Bowei He,Haolun Wu,Xue Liu,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MINER is a plug-in that probes and fuses internal transformer layer signals to form a single compact dense vector for visual document retrieval, improving single-vector dense retrievers while preserving efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-relevant signals reside in internal representations of single-vector retrievers; layerwise analysis shows potential to enhance quality without changing the backbone or increasing storage/latency.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing attaches lightweight probes at each transformer layer to surface which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information; (2) Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion applies neuron-level masking on selected layers and fuses surviving signals into the final dense vector, without modifying the backbone.

Result: Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over the backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, MINER narrows the nDCG@5 gap to about 0.2 in some settings while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

Conclusion: MINER effectively leverages internal representations via a lightweight, two-stage plug-in to boost dense single-vector retrieval performance with minimal overhead and no backbone modification.

Abstract: Visual document retrieval has become essential for accessing information in visually rich documents. Existing approaches fall into two camps. Late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality through fine-grained token-level matching but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high serving costs. By contrast, dense single-vector retrievers retain storage and latency advantages but consistently lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. In this work, we first conduct a layerwise diagnostic on single-vector retrievers, revealing that retrieval-relevant signal resides in internal representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose MINER (Mining Multimodal Internal RepreseNtation for Efficient Retrieval), a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency. The first Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing stage attaches a lightweight probe at each layer, surfacing which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information. The subsequent Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion stage applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to the selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into the final dense vector. Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over its corresponding backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, in some settings MINER substantially narrows the nDCG@$5$ gap to $0.2$ while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

</details>


### [292] [Invariant-Based Diagnostics for Graph Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06462)
*Richard von Moos,Mathieu Alain,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using permutation-invariant graph invariants as a diagnostic benchmark, the paper shows invariants can be more expressive than standard GNNs, reveal structural heterogeneity, predict multi-task performance, and enable simple models that rival transformers or message-passing baselines across 26 datasets; thus invariants help assess when structure matters and should become standard for evaluating structure usage in graph models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark practices conflate node features with graph structure, obscuring whether models learn from connectivity. There is a need for a task-agnostic, permutation-invariant structural descriptor to diagnose when structure is essential.

Method: Propose graph invariants as permutation-invariant, task-agnostic descriptors. Empirically evaluate their expressivity and predictive power on 26 graph datasets. Compare invariant-based models to standard GNNs and transformer/message-passing baselines. Analyze structural heterogeneity and multi-task performance.

Result: Invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmarks, and predict multi-task performance. Simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes surpass, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.

Conclusion: Invariant baselines should become a standard tool for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model captures it, serving as a stepping stone toward graph foundation models.

Abstract: Progress on graph foundation models is hindered by benchmark practices that conflate the contributions of node features and graph structure, making it hard to tell whether a model actually learns from connectivity, or whether it even needs to. We propose addressing this using graph invariants, i.e., permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors that serve as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks. We show that (i) invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, (ii) invariants characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets, (iii) invariants predict multi-task performance, and (iv) simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets. Our results suggest that expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance, and that on tasks where structure matters, a non-trainable structural proxy often matches trained message-passing models. We thus posit that invariant baselines should become a standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model picks up on it, serving as a stepping stone towards graph foundation models.

</details>


### [293] [Diversity Curves for Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06466)
*Katharina Limbeck,Nadja Häusermann,Martin Carrasco,Guy Wolf,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces diversity curves as size-aware, interpretable graph-level embeddings by tracking the spread across edge-contraction coarsening levels; shows edge contraction improves expressivity and superiority over baselines for clustering, visualization, and cross-dataset graph comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the challenge of comparing graphs with varying cardinalities in unsupervised settings, seeking interpretable, scalable, and reliable representations that capture geometry across multiple scales.

Method: Build a coarsening hierarchy via edge contraction; define isometry-invariant 'spread' to encode metric diversity of a graph; track this spread across coarsening levels to form diversity curves; argue that coarsening improves expressivity and yields powerful graph-level representations.

Result: Diversity curves outperform baseline methods in practice, enabling clustering and visualization across varying sizes, discerning geometry of single-cell graphs, comparing molecular graph datasets, and characterising geometric shapes.

Conclusion: Diversity curves provide interpretable, scalable, and size-aware graph representations, allowing direct comparison across coarsening hierarchies; edge contraction coarsening enhances expressivity and utility across diverse graph-analysis tasks.

Abstract: Graph-level representations are crucial tools for characterising structural differences between graphs. However, comparing graphs with different cardinalities, even when sampled from the same underlying distribution, remains challenging. Unsupervised tasks in particular require interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations. Our work addresses these issues by tracking the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels. The resulting graph embeddings, which we denote diversity curves, are interpretable by construction, efficient, and directly comparable across coarsening hierarchies. Specifically, we track the spread of graphs, a novel isometry invariant that is inherently well-suited for encoding the metric diversity and geometry of graphs. We utilise edge contraction coarsening and prove that this improves expressivity, thus leading to more powerful graph-level representations than structural descriptors alone. Demonstrating their utility over a range of baseline methods in practice, we use diversity curves to (i) cluster and visualise simulated graphs across varying sizes, (ii) distinguish the geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) compare the structure of molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterise geometric shapes.

</details>


### [294] [No Triangulation Without Representation: Generalization in Topological Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06467)
*Johannes S. Schmidt,Martin Carrasco,Ernst Röell,Guy Wolf,Nello Blaser,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends the MANTRA benchmark to a broader class of manifolds, shows GNNs and higher-order message passing can saturate with proper representation, introduces a representation-diverse triangulation refinement protocol, and reveals models do not generalize topology beyond combinatorial structure, highlighting the need for topology-aware inductive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no consensus on evaluating topological deep learning models; topological refinements can misrepresent graph structure; a robust evaluation framework is needed to distinguish learning of combinatorial vs. topological properties.

Method: Extend MANTRA to manifolds with diverse homeomorphism types; evaluate GNNs and HOMP under varied representations and feature assignments; propose an evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement; analyze generalization beyond combinatorial data.

Result: Benchmark saturation by both GNNs and HOMP is contingent on representation/feature choices; the proposed protocol reveals limited generalization beyond combinatorial structure; provides scaffolding for rigorous topology-aware evaluation.

Conclusion: There is a gap in models that understand topology independent of scale; the work lays groundwork for topology-aware inductive biases and standardized evaluation in topological deep learning.

Abstract: Despite an ever-increasing interest in topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, there is no consensus on how to evaluate such models. This is exacerbated by the fact that topological objects permit operations, such as structural refinements, that are not appropriate for graph data. In this work, we extend MANTRA, a benchmark dataset containing manifold triangulations, to a larger class of manifolds with more diverse homeomorphism types. We show that, unlike prior claims, both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods can saturate the benchmark. However, we find that this is contingent on the right representation and feature assignment, emphasizing their importance in baseline models. We thus provide a novel evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement. Surprisingly, we find no indication that existing models are capable of generalizing beyond the combinatorial structure of the data. This points towards a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. Our work thus provides the necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable the development of topology-aware inductive biases.

</details>


### [295] [Hitting Time Isomorphism for Multi-Stage Planning with Foundation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06470)
*Magnus Victor Boock,Abdullah Akgül,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An operator-theoretic, latent geometry for offline RL that uses isomorphic embedding learning (IEL) to represent hitting times in a Hilbert-space displacement framework, enabling directed temporal geometry, robust long-horizon planning, and improved offline maze performance with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of symmetric distance metrics and triangle inequality violations in representation learning for RL. Seek a directed, temporal geometry that can capture decision-time progress from hitting-time observations, enabling robust offline policy learning and planning for long-horizon tasks.

Method: Propose a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework where latent displacements in a Hilbert space realize expected hitting times as linear functionals. Prove existence under latent linear closure and unique identifiability up to bounded linear isomorphism. In finite dimensions, bound global hitting-time error by one-step transition error times the environment's transient spectral radius. Develop finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derive Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL), a goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that integrates a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to align geometry with decision-time progress. Enable graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation.

Result: The framework yields a robust, asymmetric, compositional geometry for planning. Finite-dim guarantees link hitting-time error to transition error. IEL demonstrates improved performance on offline maze locomotion benchmarks and provides code availability for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Introduces a principled, isomorphic embedding approach to foundation policy learning with theoretical guarantees and practical planning advantages. The approach supports long-horizon offline RL and is validated empirically on maze tasks, with resources released for replication.

Abstract: We present a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline reinforcement learning that recovers the directed temporal geometry of a controlled Markov process from hitting time observations. While prior art often produces symmetric distances or fails to satisfy the triangle inequality, our framework learns a Hilbert-space displacement geometry where expected hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. We prove that this representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to a bounded linear isomorphism. For finite-dimensional implementations, we show that global hitting-time error is bounded by one-step transition error amplified by the environment's transient spectral radius. Furthermore, we provide finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derived from this theory, we curate Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) as a new goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that anchors a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to ensure that the learned geometry reflects actual decision-time progress. This asymmetric and compositional structure enables robust graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation. Our experiments demonstrate that IEL improves the state of the art of learning foundation policy policies from offline maze locomotion data. Our code can be found on https://github.com/MagnusBoock/IEL

</details>


### [296] [Efficient Serving for Dynamic Agent Workflows with Prediction-based KV-Cache Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06472)
*Haoyu Zheng,Fangcheng Fu,Jia Wu,Binhang Yuan,Yongqiang Zhang,Hao Wang,Yuanyuan Zhu,Xiao Yan,Jiawei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PBKV is a prediction-based KV-Cache management system for dynamic LLM-based workflows that predicts future agent invocations to retain high-reuse cache blocks in GPU memory, improving throughput on dynamic and static workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches fail to exploit cross-agent reuse in dynamic workflows; cache management is either per-agent or per-workflow with static sequences, but practical workflows are dynamic and depend on task context.

Method: PBKV predicts upcoming agent invocations by fusing historical workflow guidance with the target workflow context; uses these predictions to estimate cache reuse potential and keeps high-potential entries in GPU memory; employs conservative strategies in eviction and prefetching to be robust to prediction errors.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmarks shows up to 1.85x speedup over LRU for dynamic workflows and up to 1.26x speedup over KVFlow for static workflows.

Conclusion: PBKV effectively leverages predicted cache reuse to accelerate LLM-based workflows, particularly dynamic ones, by prioritizing and prefetching high-potential cache entries while guarding against prediction errors.

Abstract: LLM-based workflows compose specialized agents to execute complex tasks, and these agents usually share substantial context, allowing KV-Cache reuse to save computation. Existing approaches either manage KV-Cache at agent level and fail to exploit the reuse opportunities within workflows, or manage cache at the workflow level but assume that each workflow calls a static sequence of agents. However, practical workflows are typically dynamic, where the sequence of invoked agents and thus induced cache reuse opportunities depend on the context of each task. To serve such dynamic workflows efficiently, we build a system dubbed PBKV (\textbf{P}rediction-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{KV}-Cache Management). For each workflow, PBKV predicts the agent invocations in several future steps by fusing the guidance from historical workflows and context of the target workflow. Based on the predictions, PBKV estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps the high-potential entries in GPU memory. To be robust to prediction errors, PBKV utilizes the predictions conservatively during both cache eviction and prefetching. Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show that PBKV achieves up to $1.85\times$ speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to $1.26\times$ speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on the static workflow.

</details>


### [297] [Q-MMR: Off-Policy Evaluation via Recursive Reweighting and Moment Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06474)
*Xiang Li,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-MMR introduces a data-dependent reweighting approach for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. It learns per-data-point weights via a top-down moment-matching objective against a value-function discriminator, aiming to approximate the target policy's expected return. It provides a finite-sample, dimension-free guarantee under Q^π realizability, and connects to importance sampling and linear FQE while offering new insights on coverage in offline RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve off-policy evaluation (OPE) in finite-horizon MDPs by reducing reliance on function-class complexity, enabling data-driven weighting, and clarifying the role of coverage in offline RL.

Method: Learn a weight vector with one weight per data point by solving a moment-matching objective against a discriminator class approximating the Q^π function. The weights reweight observed rewards to match the target policy value, yielding a top-down inductive procedure that connects to importance sampling and linear FQE. The framework includes theoretical analyses, yielding a dimension-free finite-sample guarantee under realizability of Q^π and explores coverage.

Result: A principled OPE framework with data-dependent reweighting (Q-MMR) and a finite-sample, dimension-free guarantee under Q^π realizability. Demonstrates connections to IS and linear FQE and provides new insights into coverage for offline RL.

Conclusion: Q-MMR provides a theoretically grounded, sample-efficient OPE approach for finite-horizon MDPs, linking to existing methods and offering deeper understanding of coverage in offline RL. It suggests a versatile framework that can facilitate evaluation and analysis in offline settings.

Abstract: We present a novel theoretical framework, Q-MMR, for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. Q-MMR learns a set of scalar weights, one for each data point, such that the reweighted rewards approximate the expected return under the target policy. The weights are learned inductively in a top-down manner via a moment matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Notably, and perhaps surprisingly, a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee for general function approximation can be established under only the realizability of $Q^π$, with a dimension-free bound -- that is, the error does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class. We also establish connections to several existing methods, such as importance sampling and linear FQE. Further theoretical analyses shed new light on the nature of coverage, a concept of fundamental importance to offline RL.

</details>


### [298] [Operator-Guided Invariance Learning for Continuous Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06500)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Fei Xu Yu,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VPSD-RL discovers value-preserving Lie-group structures to stabilize continuous RL via diffusion models, learning infinitesimal generators, and using transition augmentation and regularization; yields data efficiency and robustness with exact/approximate guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-intensive, brittle RL in continuous time/space; existing symmetry-based methods are limited; need general, nonlinear, value-preserving structures that can be discovered automatically.

Method: Model RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined by Lie-group actions and pullback/pushforward operators. Exact preservation when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward. If Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman mismatch is small, approximate structures exist with guarantees. Discover exact/approximate structures by searching for associated Lie-group operators. Fit differentiable drift/diffusion/reward models; learn infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiate with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; apply to RL via transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: exact/approximate value-preserving structures exist; bounded generator/reward mismatch yields quantitative stability of the optimal value along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon. Empirically reported improvements in data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

Conclusion: VPSD-RL provides a principled framework to discover and leverage value-preserving structures via Lie-group operators in continuous RL, unifying diffusion-based modeling with structure discovery to enhance stability, data efficiency, and robustness.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) with continuous time and state/action spaces is often data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability and shift, motivating methods that exploit value-preserving structures to stabilize and improve learning. Most existing approaches focus on special cases, such as prescribed symmetries and exact equivariance, without addressing how to discover more general structures that require nonlinear operators to transform and map between continuous state/action systems with isomorphic value functions. We propose \textbf{VPSD-RL} (Value-Preserving Structure Discovery for Reinforcement Learning). It models continuous RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined through Lie-group actions and associated pullback operators. We show that a value-preserving structure exists exactly when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward functional. Further, approximate value-preserving structures with rigorous guarantees can be found when the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman mismatch is small. This framework discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures by searching for the associated Lie group operators. VPSD-RL fits differentiable drift, diffusion, and reward models; learns infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; and integrates them into continuous RL through transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization. We show that bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon, and observe improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

</details>


### [299] [Cubit: Token Mixer with Kernel Ridge Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06501)
*Chuanyang Zheng,Jiankai Sun,Yihang Gao,Yuehao Wang,Liangchen Tan,Mac Schwager,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka,Xiaodong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cubit introduces a kernel ridge regression (KRR) based token-mixing mechanism as a replacement for attention, along with a Limited-Range Rescale for stability, showing improved long-sequence modeling over Transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, kernel-based foundation for token mixing by reframing attention as Nadaraya-Watson regression and extending to KRR to enhance long-sequence modeling.

Method: Replace attention with the closed-form KRR solution that uses kernel similarities and the inverse kernel matrix for normalization; add Limited-Range Rescale (LRR) to keep value activations within a controlled range.

Result: Empirical results suggest Cubit achieves stronger long-sequence modeling capability, with performance gains that grow with sequence length.

Conclusion: Cubit offers a mathematically stronger, kernel-based alternative to Transformers with potential advantages for long sequences, warranting further exploration.

Abstract: Since its introduction in 2017, the Transformer has become one of the most widely adopted architectures in modern deep learning. Despite extensive efforts to improve positional encoding, attention mechanisms, and feed-forward networks, the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers remains attention. In this work, we show that the attention module in Transformers can be interpreted as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression, where it computes similarities between tokens and aggregates the corresponding values accordingly. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Cubit, a potential next-generation architecture that leverages Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), while the vanilla Transformer relies on Nadaraya-Watson regression. Specifically, Cubit modifies the classical attention computation by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve the training stability, we further propose the Limited-Range Rescale (LRR), which rescales the value layer within a controlled range. We argue that Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer, whose attention mechanism corresponds to Nadaraya-Watson regression. We validate this claim through comprehensive experiments. The experimental results suggest that Cubit may exhibit stronger long-sequence modeling capability. In particular, its performance gain over the Transformer appears to increase as the training sequence length grows.

</details>


### [300] [PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06505)
*Murat Bilgehan Ertan,Xiaochen Zhu,Phuong Ha Nguyen,Marten van Dijk,Srinivas Devadas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACZero is a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that achieves zero mutual information leakage I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0, delivering usable utility under strong privacy. It introduces two variants (PACZero-MI and PACZero-ZPL) and demonstrates competitive performance on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT models, notably achieving SST-2 accuracy of 88.99±0.91 at I=0, close to a non-private baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable private fine-tuning of large language models under strong privacy guarantees, addressing membership inference attacks (MIA) while maintaining usable utility. The work integrates PAC privacy with zeroth-order optimization to achieve zero information leakage in crucial steps.

Method: Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients with frequent unanimity steps where update directions converge; at these steps the released sign incurs zero conditional mutual information. Two variants: PACZero-MI (budgeted mutual information via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL (I=0 via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). Evaluation uses SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter fine-tuning.

Result: On SST-2 with OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at I=0, PACZero-ZPL achieves 88.99±0.91, within 2.1 pp of non-private MeZO baseline (91.1). No prior method achieves usable utility in the high-privacy regime ε<1; PACZero-ZPL yields competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B.

Conclusion: PACZero demonstrates that PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms can deliver usable utility under strong privacy (I=0), with a tunable trade-off between privacy and utility via PACZero-MI and PACZero-ZPL. It bridges the gap where DP-based ZO methods struggle under high privacy, showing practical applicability for private LLM fine-tuning.

Abstract: We introduce PACZero, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that delivers usable utility at $I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0$. This privacy regime bounds the membership-inference attack (MIA) posterior success rate at the prior, an MIA-resistance level the DP framework matches only at $\varepsilon=0$ and infinite noise. All DP-ZO comparisons below are matched at the MIA posterior level. The key insight is that PAC Privacy charges mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients creates frequent unanimity, steps at which every candidate subset agrees on the update direction; at these steps the released sign costs zero conditional mutual information. We propose two variants that span the privacy-utility trade-off: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL ($I=0$ via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). We evaluate on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter tracks. On SST-2 OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at $I=0$, PACZero-ZPL reaches ${88.99\pm0.91}$, within $2.1$pp of the non-private MeZO baseline ($91.1$ FT). No prior method produces usable utility in the high-privacy regime $\varepsilon<1$, and PACZero-ZPL obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at $I=0$.

</details>


### [301] [Is One Layer Enough? Understanding Inference Dynamics in Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06510)
*Amir Rezaei Balef,Mykhailo Koshil,Katharina Eggensperger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) are effective in small-to-medium tabular tasks, but their inference mechanics are understudied. This work conducts a large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models, identifying depthwise redundancy and iterative refinement in inference. They propose a looped single-layer model with ~20% of parameters achieving comparable performance to the original models, suggesting potential parameter-efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how predictions emerge across depth in TFMs for tabular data, and to uncover whether complex multi-layer architectures are necessary for good performance, especially during inference. The goal is to reveal mechanistic insights into layerwise dynamics and dynamics-specific stages in tabular ICL models.

Method: Empirical, large-scale analysis across six state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. The study inspects layerwise contributions, inference stages, and latent-space dynamics, comparing across depth and models. Based on findings, they design a proof-of-concept looped single-layer architecture that mimics multi-layer computations with reduced parameters.

Result: Found substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, indicating iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. The looped single-layer model with 20% of the original parameters achieves comparable performance to the full models on benchmark tasks.

Conclusion: Layerwise dynamics in tabular TFMs exhibit notable redundancy, enabling parameter-efficient architectures that retain performance. This motivates future work on mechanistic analyses of inference and the development of compact, efficient tabular foundation models.

Abstract: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate small to medium tabular predictive benchmark tasks, yet their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. We explore how predictions emerge across depth, identify distinct stages of inference and reveal latent-space dynamics that differ from those of language models. Our findings indicate substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, suggesting iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. Guided by these insights, we design a proof-of-concept, looped single-layer model that uses only 20% of the original model's parameters while achieving comparable performance. The code is available at https://github.com/amirbalef/is_one_layer_enough.

</details>


### [302] [Efficient Techniques for Data Reconstruction, with Finite-Width Recovery Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06519)
*Edward Tansley,Roy Makhlouf,Estelle Massart,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified optimization framework for data reconstruction from neural network training data, with finite-width guarantees in the random-feature regime, subspace-aware improvements reducing width requirements, and an efficient practical algorithm that leverages first-layer weight changes to identify a low-dimensional subspace for reconstruction; validated on synthetic data and CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data reconstruction attacks threaten privacy by recovering training data from trained models. The work aims to unify existing approaches into a single optimization formulation, establish finite-width reconstruction guarantees in a random-feature setting, and relax width requirements by exploiting low-dimensional data subspaces while providing a practical algorithm and empirical validation.

Method: Propose a unified optimization problem based on initial and trained parameters. Analyze in the random-feature model to derive PAC-style, finite-width reconstruction guarantees. Show relaxed width requirements when data lie in a low-dimensional subspace. Propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that infers a low-dimensional subspace from changes in the first-layer weights and performs reconstruction using last-layer weights, reducing search space and required width. Validate with experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10, comparing to full-space methods.

Result: In random-feature settings, the formulation yields high-probability data reconstruction when network width is sufficiently large (finite-width, PAC-style bounds). When data lie in a subspace, width requirements are relaxed with bounds depending on subspace dimension. The proposed subspace-aware algorithm outperforms standard full-space reconstruction methods. Experiments on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 corroborate improved reconstruction quality.

Conclusion: A unified optimization perspective facilitates data reconstruction analysis for neural networks, revealing finite-width guarantees in simplified models and practical subspace-aware methods that reduce width and search space. The work underscores privacy risks and provides an actionable algorithm with empirical support.

Abstract: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks aim to recover the data on which the network has been trained and pose a significant threat to privacy, especially if the training dataset contains sensitive information. Here, we propose a unified optimization formulation of the data reconstruction problem based on initial and trained parameter values, incorporating state-of-the-art proposals. We show that in the random feature model, this formulation provably leads to training data reconstruction with high probability, provided the network width is sufficiently large; this unprecedented finite-width result uses PAC-style bounds. Furthermore, when the data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, we show that the network width requirement for successful reconstruction can be relaxed, with bounds depending on the subspace dimension rather than the ambient dimension. For general neural network models and unknown data orientations, we propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that approximates the low-dimensional data subspace through the change in the first-layer weights during training and uses only the last-layer weights for reconstruction, thus reducing the search space dimension and the required network width for high-quality reconstructions. Our numerical experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10 confirm that our subspace-aware reconstruction approach outperforms standard full-space techniques.

</details>


### [303] [On the Implicit Reward Overfitting and the Low-rank Dynamics in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06523)
*Hao Ye,Jisheng Dang,Junfeng Fang,Bimei Wang,Yizhou Zhang,Ning Lv,Wencan Zhang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR's gains concentrate in rank-1 components; periodic substitution reveals potential implicit reward overfitting, where test performance emerges despite low training rewards. The study identifies three properties: (1) rank-1 features preserve math reasoning but not other knowledge, (2) optimization of a specific singular spectrum with heavy-tailed layer spectra, and (3) increasing alignment of left singular vectors of rank-1 components, linked to sampling efficiency. These findings illuminate how RLVR shapes parameters and suggest directions to enhance RL and continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
