Analyze scalability and limitations of structured reasoning state and operators.

Result: ReFlect yields task success rates from 41% (gpt-4o-mini) to 56% (Claude Sonnet 4.5) across six models. Per-model gains over Direct CoT range from +7 percentage points to +29 points. SWE-bench patch-structural quality improves from 0% (Direct CoT) to 82–87% depending on model. The harness gain is inversely related to baseline success rate (slope -1.69, r = -0.76). Larger-scale models show limited benefit (15.0–18.7% pair-mean) when structured state and operators are added due to state-population limitations. ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

Conclusion: A deterministic, inference-time error-detection and recovery wrapper (ReFlect) can substantially improve LLM reasoning reliability, especially for weaker baselines, and is broadly applicable across model families without retraining.

Abstract: Current reasoning paradigms for LLMs include chain-of-thought, ReAct, and post-hoc self-critique. These paradigms rely on two assumptions that fail on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. As a result, errors accumulate silently across reasoning steps, leaving an open question: can a reasoning system effectively detect and recover from its own failures? We present ReFlect, a \emph{harness} system for LLM reasoning that creates standalone error detection and recovery logic as a deterministic wrapper around the model. Controlled experiments across 6 reasoning domains show that prompt-level self-critique produces formulaic templates that flag no issues in 90 of 100 audited reflection blocks, and the investigated LLMs wrongly accept a wrong answer in at least 76\% of cases. Our ReFlect harness achieves task success rates ranging from 41\% on gpt-4o-mini to 56\% on Claude Sonnet 4.5 across six models spanning small and frontier scale, with per-model gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7 pp on Qwen2.5-72B to +29 pp on Claude Sonnet 4.5, and additionally raises SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0\% (Direct CoT) to between 82\% (Qwen2.5-72B) and 87\% (GPT-4o). Notably, the harness gain is inversely proportional to the model's Direct CoT task success rate (the fitted slope is -1.69 with r=-0.76): each pp lost in baseline success rate is mechanically recovered by 1.69 pp of harness gain. We spot that adding structured reasoning state and operators yields only 15.0--18.7\% pair-mean on Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B because models at this scale cannot reliably populate the state its operators require. ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

</details>


### [378] [HyperLens: Quantifying Cognitive Effort in LLMs with Fine-grained Confidence Trajectory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05741)
*Chengda Lu,Xiaoyu Fan,Wei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HyperLens provides a high‑resolution probe to trace layer‑wise confidence trajectories in transformers, revealing that deeper layers magnify small confidence changes. This yields a cognitive‑effort metric: complex tasks incur higher effort. SFT can reduce cognitive effort and harm in‑domain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand inference dynamics in LLMs with limited-resolution analysis tools and to uncover a magnification mechanism in transformers that yields fine-grained confidence trajectories and a quantitative measure of cognitive effort.

Method: Introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe that traces confidence trajectories across transformer layers during inference. Evaluate across LLMs and datasets, extract a cognitive‑effort metric from the trajectories, and provide a mechanistic diagnosis for observed SFT side effects.

Result: Deeper layers magnify small changes in layer-wise confidence, producing fine-grained confidence trajectories. A consistent divergence in trajectories separates complex from simple tasks. The derived cognitive‑effort metric correlates with task complexity. Mechanistic diagnosis shows standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) can reduce cognitive effort and degrade in-domain performance.

Conclusion: Complex tasks require higher cognitive effort, a fundamental principle supported by HyperLens analyses. The proposed framework offers a quantitative, mechanistic lens on inference dynamics and explains the adverse side effects of SFT on certain tasks.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks, their inference dynamics remain poorly understood because of the limited resolution of existing analysis tools. In this work, we identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures: deeper layers inherently magnify the small changes of layer-wise confidence, providing a fine-grained confidence trajectory. Building on this insight, we introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe designed to trace confidence trajectories and quantify the cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and datasets, HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. We abstract this pattern into a quantitative cognitive effort metric. Our analysis reveals a fundamental principle: complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Finally, we provide a mechanistic diagnosis of a common side effect of standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): it can reduce cognitive effort and consequently degrade performance on in-domain tasks.

</details>


### [379] [Best Arm Identification in Generalized Linear Bandits via Hybrid Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05745)
*Qirun Zeng,Xuchuang Wang,Jiayi Shen,Xutong Liu,Fang Kong,Jinhang Zuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Likelihood-ratio confidence sequences unify heterogeneous generalized linear observations (absolute rewards and dueling feedback) for fixed-confidence best-arm identification in GLMs. A hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm adaptively allocates queries over arms and pairs, achieving δ-correctness with high-probability stopping-time bounds; extended to cost-aware feedback, with empirical gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fixed-confidence best-arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model that includes both absolute reward feedback and relative (dueling) feedback, and unify heterogeneous observations under a single statistical framework.

Method: Develop a likelihood-ratio–based confidence sequence that yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that tracks a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space (arms and duels) to adaptively allocate queries. Extend to a cost-aware setting with heterogeneous acquisition costs. Establish δ-correctness and high-probability stopping-time bounds.

Result: The proposed method achieves δ-correctness with high-probability bounds on stopping time. It improves sample efficiency over baselines in experiments and demonstrates effective adaptation to cost heterogeneity across feedback modalities.

Conclusion: A unified GLM-based framework for fixed-confidence best-arm identification with hybrid feedback is viable, with provable guarantees and practical sample efficiency gains; the cost-aware extension broadens applicability across feedback modalities.

Abstract: We study fixed-confidence best arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model: at each round, the learner may query either (i) absolute reward feedback from a single arm or (ii) relative (dueling) feedback from an arm pair, both governed by generalized linear models. We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

</details>


### [380] [Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05748)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,David Muench,Dimitri Bulatov,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A taxonomy and evaluation framework for XAI in safety-critical ATR shows post-hoc explanations have systematic failures (spurious, unstable, overtrusting); calls for robust, causally grounded, physically informed explainability to support assurance and validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ATR in safety-critical domains requires explanations that are not only interpretable but robust and verifiable to meet system-level assurance standards; current post-hoc XAI methods may undermine safety if explanations are misleading or brittle.

Method: Formalize XAI as an assurance-oriented assessment; identify paradigms (saliency-, attention-, and surrogate-based; detection-aware extensions); develop a taxonomy and evaluate methods across four dimensions: interpretability, robustness, manipulation vulnerability, and validation/verification suitability; analyze failure modes and limitations; discuss implications and directions.

Result: Highlight systematic limitations of current post-hoc XAI in ATR, including spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust from convincing visuals; conclude that these methods may be inadequate for safety-critical deployment and propose directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explainability methods.

Conclusion: Move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance in ATR; emphasize need for robustness, causality, and physical constraints to enable validation and verification.

Abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

</details>


### [381] [Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05770)
*Laura van Weesep,Sunay Chankeshwara,Leonardo De Maria,Florian David,Ola Engkvist,Gökçe Geylan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An uncertainty-aware RL-guided generative framework for designing permeable cyclic peptides using conformal prediction to calibrate predictions and constrain exploration within the predictor's domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive models used in RL-driven molecular design may be unreliable outside their domain; cyclic peptides are promising but underexplored; need to ensure permeability designs are both feasible and reliable; address the challenge of predicting membrane permeability for novel cyclic peptides.

Method: Integrates an RL-based generative model with a permeability predictor as the scoring function; incorporates conformal prediction to quantify predictive uncertainty at user-defined confidence; CP informs rewards to bias toward within-domain designs and reject high-uncertainty samples.

Result: Empirical demonstration that CP-informed rewards improve reliability and efficiency; reduces exploration outside applicability domain; first demonstration of combining CP with RL for de novo peptide design.

Conclusion: Conformal prediction enriches RL-guided design by linking uncertainty quantification to objective optimization, enabling more reliable discovery of permeable cyclic peptides and potentially extendable to other domains.

Abstract: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

</details>


### [382] [CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05773)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Sujan Kumar Saha,Farimah Farahmandi,Mark Tehranipoor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dataset-scale study proposes Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) and CircuitFormer to improve LLM-based analog circuit design, achieving strong syntactic/functional performance with a fixed-size vocabulary and compact model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcoming two bottlenecks in applying LLMs to analog design: lack of large paired NL-descriptions/netlists; inefficient general-purpose tokenizers that fail to capture circuit topology.

Method: 1) Build a large dataset of 31,341 netlist–NL description pairs across major analog circuit classes. 2) Introduce Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) that encodes netlist connectivity via frequent subcircuits, yielding O(1) vocabulary growth. 3) Train CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. 

Result: CKT reduces sequence length by 57% and achieves 2.3x compression with a fixed vocab of 512. CircuitFormer attains 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14% with 240x fewer parameters.

Conclusion: CKT enables topology-aware, scalable tokenization for circuit-language models and, together with the large annotated dataset, enables effective analog circuit design with a compact transformer, lowering model size without sacrificing performance. The dataset is publicly available for replication.

Abstract: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

</details>


### [383] [HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05776)
*Yu Feng,Zhen Tian,Haoran Luo,Xie Yu,Diancheng Cheng,Haoyue Zheng,Shuai Lyu,Ping Zong,Lianyuan Li,Xin Ge,Yifan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP) for domain-incremental learning uses an energy regularization loss to sharpen domain representation separability and a hybrid energy-distance cue fusion to improve domain selection and generalization, achieving improved unseen-domain performance (2.57% on CORe50).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shifts cause performance degradation and catastrophic forgetting in continual/domain-incremental learning. A mechanism that enhances domain representation separability and robust domain adaptation without full retraining is desired.

Method: Introduce an energy regularization loss to promote separable domain representations. Propose a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues for improved domain selection and generalization, inspired by Helmholtz free energy.

Result: Empirical results on multiple benchmarks (including CORe50) show superior performance on unseen domains, with a reported 2.57% accuracy gain, and reduced forgetting, indicating improved open-world adaptability. Code is provided.

Conclusion: HEDP demonstrates that combining energy-based regularization with energy-distance cue fusion is effective for domain-incremental learning, enhancing unseen-domain generalization and resilience to forgetting; the approach is ready for broader evaluation on additional benchmarks and ablations.

Abstract: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

</details>


### [384] [Von Neumann Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05780)
*Shekhar S. Chandra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Von Neumann Neurons (VNNs) within a cellular, diffusion-based computing framework, yielding self-architected networks whose structure is dictated by input/output layout; claims improved efficiency and tasks adaptability over standard DL, and positions Cellular Machines as a universal computational paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficiency, interpretability, and architectural optimization by deriving network structure from a cellular diffusion model inspired by von Neumann's early computational concepts; unify neural operators with cellular topology to enable adaptive, hardware-like networks.

Method: Develop VNNs as specialized, learnable neurons embedded in a cellular topology with diffusion-like interactions. Frame VNNs as neural operators learned via Green’s functions on the cellular grid, using convolutions on the diffusion topology. Prove that VNNs belong to a broader, universal class called Cellular Machines; provide initial experiments showing improved performance and parameter efficiency on basic tasks.

Result: VNN-based multilayer perceptrons outperform equivalent deep learning variants on basic tasks, with greater parameter efficiency and the ability to learn new task types; architecture self-designs based on input/output layout; suggests a hardware-inspired extension of Von Neumann architectures to cells.

Conclusion: VNNs offer a novel architectural principle linking diffusion-based cellular computation with neural operator learning. Cellular Machines are positioned as a universal computational framework, with potential hardware and methodological implications for building more efficient, adaptable networks.

Abstract: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

</details>


### [385] [Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05811)
*Yiming Lei,Yiqi Wang,Yujia Zhang,Bo Guan,Depei Zhu,Chunhui Wang,Zhuonan Hao,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graph-enhanced 'Sheet as Token' treats each worksheet as a dense semantic token for cross-sheet retrieval, improving scalability and accuracy over chunk-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spreadsheet understanding for language-model–based data agents is hindered when splitting sheets into rows/blocks, which fragments global sheet-level semantics across heterogeneous schemas; a unified sheet-level representation is desirable.

Method: Encode each worksheet into a compact dense token using schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features. A Graph Retriever builds a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, which a multi-stage graph transformer then fuses to retrieve supporting sheet sets.

Result: On a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus, sheet-level tokenization yields stable representations; graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited graph-side computation.

Conclusion: Sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

Abstract: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

</details>


### [386] [Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05812)
*Armaan A. Abraham,Lucy Xiaoyang Shi,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LQL introduces hinge loss-based stabilization to curb error compounding in long-horizon Q-learning by exploiting an optimality-tightening bound, achieving improved performance on online and offline-to-online benchmarks without extra networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bootstrapping in TD learning causes errors to propagate and accumulate over long horizons; there is a principled backstop: early optimal action sequences should not be worse than continuing observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior.

Method: Introduce a hinge loss penalty for violations of the bound, computed from network outputs already used for TD error; requires no auxiliary networks or extra forward passes; compatible with multiple SOTA methods.

Result: Across online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime when combined with various SOTA methods.

Conclusion: LQL provides a practical, efficient stabilization mechanism for Q-learning in long-horizon, off-policy settings, improving robustness and performance without additional computational burden.

Abstract: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

</details>


### [387] [AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05826)
*Yang Xu,Kun Yao,Yiming Deng,Zheng Fang,Kai Ming Ting,Ming Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AGPO counteracts boundary shrinkage in RLVR by suppressing incorrect reasoning with a negative-dominant strategy and focusing positive updates via a group-advantage mechanism based on intra-group variance, enabling sparse but informative updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR improves reasoning but often narrows the reasoning capability relative to base models (boundary shrinkage) and reduces coverage at scale; need to preserve exploration while suppressing poor paths.

Method: Introduce asymmetric policy optimization: (1) a negative-dominant reinforcement signal to dampen incorrect reasoning paths, (2) a group-adaptive positive update that scales with intra-group variance to highlight rare correct paths while downweighting trivial ones.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on five mathematical benchmarks and improves pass@k at scale; industrial deployment shows better data annotation quality and downstream student-model gains.

Conclusion: AGPO effectively mitigates boundary shrinkage in RLVR by maintaining exploration and promoting informative positive updates, yielding strong mathematical benchmarks and practical benefits in industry.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

</details>


### [388] [On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05833)
*Guanyu Zhu,Jining Luan,Hanwen Du,Xinyu Fang,Sibo Xu,Ersheng Ni,Hongji Li,Jincheng Fang,Ronghao Chen,Huacan Wang,Xuanqi Lan,Yongxin Ni,Yiqi Sun,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemBid injects semantic tokens derived from LLMs into offline bidding trajectories to improve controllability and generalization in auto-bidding, outperforming baselines across budgets; robust to constraints with consistent gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compact numerical state representations in auto-bidding struggle to capture high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-like guidance. LLMs offer semantic encoding, but integration must be careful to preserve numerical precision.

Method: Represent three semantic inputs (Task, History, Strategy) as tokens injected alongside numerical trajectory tokens. Use self-attention to fuse semantic and numeric information. Evaluate in offline RL and generative sequence modeling settings across diverse budget regimes.

Result: SemBid outperforms competitive offline RL and generative baselines, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness across objectives.

Conclusion: Semantic-numeric integration via token-level LLM semantics improves controllability and generalization in auto-bidding. Careful design of semantic integration is essential; naive concatenation is ineffective.

Abstract: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

</details>


### [389] [Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05842)
*Zaki Kurdya,Mohammed Zuqlam,Salem Amassi,Shady Telbany,Motaz Saad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Taklif.AI uses LLMs to generate personalized assignments that account for students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts, addressing engagement and plagiarism. Built on AWS serverless with Next.js; Llama 3.3 70B via LiteLLM; LangChain for orchestration; describes architecture, prompts, and guardrails; preliminary testing shows favorable reception with room for rigorous outcome evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance student engagement and reduce unethical practices by moving beyond performance-based personalization to include non-academic interests and cultural contexts.

Method: Structured prompt engineering pipeline with input/output guardrails; serverless AWS architecture; Next.js frontend; Llama 3.3 70B via LiteLLM; LangChain orchestration; description of system architecture, prompt design, and guardrails.

Result: Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students, 3 educators); 84% found personalization beneficial; discusses current capabilities and limitations.

Conclusion: Taklif.AI shows promise for personalized, context-aware assignment generation, but requires rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes and ongoing refinement of guardrails and evaluation methods.

Abstract: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

</details>


### [390] [AirQualityBench: A Realistic Evaluation Benchmark for Global Air Quality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05854)
*Xing Xu,Xu Wang,Yudong Zhang,Huilin Zhao,Zhengyang Zhou,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Global, multi-pollutant air-quality forecasting benchmark (AirQualityBench) that preserves missingness and real-world data masks to test models under realistic conditions; sanitized-data performance does not generalize to fragmented, global monitoring streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real monitoring networks suffer uneven coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and cost constraints; existing benchmarks with imputed/cleaned data fail to reflect these conditions.

Method: Assemble hourly data from 3,720 stations (2021–2025) for six pollutants; preserve provider-native observation masks; treat missingness as part of forecasting; report errors on valid future observations after converting back to physical concentrations; provide code and baselines.

Result: Strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented streams; demonstrates the need for mask-aware, scalable, and physically interpretable forecasting; benchmark is a realistic testbed.

Conclusion: AirQualityBench offers a realistic framework for evaluating forecasting under realistic data conditions; enables development of models robust to missingness and heterogeneity; resources available on GitHub.

Abstract: Air-quality forecasting models are commonly evaluated on regional, preprocessed, and normalized datasets, where missing observations are removed or artificially completed. Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [391] [SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05861)
*Yong Xiao,Haoran Zhou,Yujie Zhou,Marwan Krunz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SANEmerg enables emergent communication among resource-bounded agents in an AgentNet ecosystem, using bandwidth-adaptable signaling and MDL-based complexity regularization to improve task accuracy while reducing bandwidth and computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional networking decouples computation from communication, causing inefficiencies in large-scale, agentic AI systems. Emergent communication can coordinate heterogeneous agents under tight bandwidth constraints and dynamically inferred semantic intents.

Method: Proposes SANEmerg framework with (1) bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter prioritizing high-contribution message dimensions, (2) MDL-based complexity-regularizer to encourage computationally bounded signaling, (3) semantic intent detection and subtask mapping to agents, and (4) evaluation on an AgentNet prototype under bandwidth constraints.

Result: Experiments show significant performance gains over state-of-the-art, with higher task accuracy and notable reductions in bandwidth usage and computational overhead.

Conclusion: Emergent communication with bandwidth adaptation and MDL-based regularization is effective for semantic-aware AgentNet systems, enabling robust task execution under resource constraints; future work may address scalability, dynamic agent heterogeneity, and real-world deployment challenges.

Abstract: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

</details>


### [392] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDecomposer is a prior-free, set-prediction framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns, capable of recovering phase components, proportions, and structures without prior phase numbers or templates, improving reconstruction and identification on simulated and experimental datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase PXRD analysis is challenging due to mixtures; existing methods often assume single-phase inputs or require prior phase lists/templates. There is a need for a method that handles unknown numbers of phases without templates.

Method: Formulates multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem. The model outputs an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. It uses a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism and diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction to separate sources while preserving crystallographic fidelity. The framework is prior-free, not relying on candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase count.

Result: Extensive experiments on simulated and experimental datasets show substantial improvements in reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems. The method generalizes well to unseen mixtures and reduces dependence on prior-guided phase matching. The code is openly available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: XDecomposer provides a practical, data-driven route for source-resolved multiphase PXRD analysis, enabling robust multiphase structure inference without priors and demonstrating strong generalization to unseen mixtures.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


### [393] [Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05878)
*Basel Magableh,OmniRisk Research*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A composite risk primitive for the Internet of Value combining five engines (prediction, verification, sentiment fusion, agentic constraints, and Monte-Carlo scenario actions) anchored by two empirical artefacts; argues deployability and falsifiability of validator-loss decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle heterogeneous, partially trusted IoV with multi-faceted risk (route, liquidity, sentiment, policy) and provide an economically verifiable framework for forecasting and pre-committed actions.

Method: Architectural design outlining five integrated engines and a verification subnet; empirical anchoring via a 27-hour Solana liquidity stress test and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc with class-imbalance considerations; formalism for validator-loss decomposition.

Result: Proposes a concrete, testable architecture for risk management in IoV with (i) a modular engine suite, (ii) a verifiable prediction outputs scoring mechanism, and (iii) a formalizable loss decomposition; and reports preliminary empirical artefacts supporting deployability.

Conclusion: The architecture is feasible and falsifiable; it warrants further implementation and cross-network evaluation to validate the risk primitive in real-world IoV deployments.

Abstract: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

</details>


### [394] [Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05909)
*Yuhang Wang,Zhenxing Niu,Haoxuan Ji,Guangyu He,Linlin Zhang,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An MLLM unlearning framework that forgets targeted visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual and all textual knowledge, by freezing the LLM backbone and fine-tuning the visual module with Contrastive Visual Forgetting and null-space constraints, extended to continual unlearning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the multimodal unlearning challenge where visual and textual knowledge are tightly interwoven, requiring forgetting of specific visual concepts without degrading retention of other knowledge.

Method: Freeze the LLM backbone; fine-tune the visual module. Use Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to separate target from retained visual knowledge in feature space. Identify the null space of retained knowledge and constrain unlearning to operate within this space to reduce degradation. Extend the framework to continual unlearning with sequential forgetting requests.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate a strong balance between effective forgetting of targeted visual knowledge and robust retention of non-target visual and all textual knowledge.

Conclusion: The CVF with null-space constraints provides an effective approach for targeted and continual unlearning in MLLMs, achieving safe forgetting while preserving core knowledge.

Abstract: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

</details>


### [395] [PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05911)
*Millend Roy,Agostino Capponi,Vineet Goyal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Online learning-based personalized summarization for product reviews that updates user preference models through feedback on generated summaries, improving alignment with individual interests while preserving summary quality; validated via simulations on Amazon Reviews'23 dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address information overload and heterogeneity of user preferences; current generic summaries fail to adapt to different and evolving interests; need an online, feedback-driven approach to tailor summaries.

Method: Iterative online learning framework that maintains latent user preference vector over product attributes; generate summaries conditioned on current preferences; collect implicit/explicit feedback from summaries to update preferences; evaluated in controlled simulations on Amazon Reviews'23 dataset.

Result: Online preference learning yields better alignment with target user interests without sacrificing summary quality, demonstrated in controlled simulations.

Conclusion: Personalized adaptive summarization is feasible and effective; future work could extend to real-user experiments and richer feedback signals; extension to other domains with latent preference dynamics.

Abstract: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

</details>


### [396] [Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05913)
*Weihua Wang,Haoji Li,Feilong Bao,Lei Yang,Guanglai Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Wisteria unifies local motif capture and global sequence dependencies in a DNA language model using gated dilated convs, gated MLPs, and Fourier-based attention, achieving strong downstream performance and length generalization across multi-scale genomic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DNA language models emphasize long-range interactions but often ignore the coupling between local regulatory motifs and global dependencies. A unified, multi-scale framework could better capture genomic regulatory grammar and semantic structure.

Method: Builds on a Mamba-inspired architecture, adding gated dilated convolutions to model local motifs and regulatory patterns, gated multilayer perceptrons to refine global dependencies, and a Fourier-based attention mechanism to enable frequency-domain modeling, periodic extension, and length generalization; evaluated across four experimental settings with both short and long-range dependencies.

Result: Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines across four experimental settings, indicating effective unification of local and global dependency modeling for multi-scale genomic sequence analysis.

Conclusion: A unified multi-scale DNA language model that concurrently captures local motifs and long-range dependencies via gated convolutions, gated MLPs, and Fourier-based attention; shows improved performance and length generalization over existing baselines.

Abstract: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

</details>


### [397] [Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05921)
*Alex Bäuerle,Adam Connors,Alexander Novikov,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Ngân Vũ,Fernanda Viegas,Martin Wattenberg,Lucas Dixon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Identifies 'intentmaking' as an iterative workflow that pairs goal-definition with sensemaking in AI-assisted scientific discovery, based on a formative study of mathematicians using AlphaEvolve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how expert researchers interact with AI agents beyond fixed Q/A paradigms, informing design of collaborative, goal-driven AI tools for science.

Method: Formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve to tackle advanced problems; qualitative analysis to extract interaction patterns and workflow.

Result: Introduces intentmaking as a recurring cycle of defining/updating experimental goals (intent) and interpreting results (sensemaking); frames AlphaEvolve interactions as collaborative instrument use rather than opaque automation.

Conclusion: Design implications for AI tools in science: support iterative goal refinement and sensemaking, treat AI agents as collaborative instruments, and move beyond question-answer models to enable natural, goal-directed exploration.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

</details>


### [398] [Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05929)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue,Pierre Monnin,Miguel Couceiro,Fabien Gandon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-level categorization of language resource levels in LOD knowledge graphs to formalize low-, medium-, and high-resource languages for cross-lingual transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the Open Access Data divide across high- and low-resource languages in Multilingual LOD KGs and to provide a quantitative, formal definition of language resource levels to guide cross-lingual transfer decisions.

Method: Analyze language distributions across three major LOD KGs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) and develop a preliminary multi-level categorization to formalize language resource levels.

Result: Introduction of a preliminary categorization framework and a formal definition of language resource levels (low/medium/high) to identify cross-lingual transfer candidates.

Conclusion: The framework offers a basis for selecting cross-lingual transfer targets and advancing inclusive coverage of OAD in LOD KGs, pending empirical validation.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

</details>


### [399] [In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05931)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analyzes language distribution in LOD KGs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) and develops cross-lingual transfer and analogical reasoning strategies to improve multilingual KG completion and language coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open Access Data is uneven across languages, causing exclusion of many communities from the global digital transformation. Understanding language coverage in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs and enabling multilingual KG completion is essential for inclusive participation.

Method: 1) Define language-distribution variables: number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. 2) Analyze these variables across three multilingual LOD KGs—DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata. 3) Study cross-lingual transfer candidate selection strategies based on linguistic proximity and availability of curated alignments. 4) Explore analogical reasoning leveraging language dissimilarities to identify cross-language correspondences to improve KG completion and language coverage in LOD.

Result: This is a proposal; concrete results are not reported. Expected outcomes include language-coverage profiles for the three LOD KGs, a framework for cross-lingual transfer candidate selection, and evidence of potential gains from proximity-based transfer and analogical reasoning in multilingual KG completion.

Conclusion: The work aims to reduce the Open Access Data language gap by informing multilingual KG completion strategies and improving language coverage in LOD, enabling broader participation in the global knowledge graph ecosystem.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

</details>


### [400] [ICU-Bench:Benchmarking Continual Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05938)
*Yuhang Wang,Wenjie Mei,Junkai Zhang,Guangyu He,Zhenxing Niu,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark for privacy-critical documents, with 1,000 profiles, 9,500 images, 16,000 QA pairs, and 100 forget tasks, plus new metrics to assess forgetting, utility, and stability; experiments show current methods struggle in continual settings and highlight the need for dedicated multimodal continual unlearning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy concerns in training Multimodal LLMs on large-scale data and the lack of benchmarks to evaluate continual privacy deletion in realistic, multimodal deployments.

Method: Construct ICU-Bench from privacy-sensitive document data (medical reports, labor contracts) and associated modalities (images, QA pairs); define continual unlearning metrics; evaluate representative unlearning methods over long task sequences.

Result: Existing unlearning methods perform poorly in continual multimodal settings, grappling with a trade-off between forgetting efficacy, utility retention, and scalability across long sequences.

Conclusion: Multimodal continual unlearning methods tailored to privacy deletion are necessary to achieve effective, scalable privacy protection in MLLMs.

Abstract: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across many domains, their training on large-scale multimodal datasets raises serious privacy concerns, making effective machine unlearning increasingly necessary. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on static or short-sequence settings, offering limited support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments. To bridge this gap, we introduce ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical document data. ICU-Bench contains 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two document domains, medical reports and labor contracts, with 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Additionally, new continual unlearning metrics are introduced, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability throughout the continual unlearning process. Through extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench, we show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings and exhibit clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences. These findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

</details>


### [401] [MAS-Algorithm: A Workflow for Solving Algorithmic Programming Problems with a Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05949)
*Yuliang Xu,Xiang Xu,Yao Wan,Hu Wei,Tong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS-Algorithm introduces a modular, multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving, achieving notable performance gains over model-centric or prompting-based baselines, with robust ablations and tooling integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of single-model, costly, and poorly interpretable approaches by providing a unified, extensible, tool-enabled framework for structured algorithmic reasoning.

Method: Decomposes the solving process into modular stages implemented as interacting agents within a multi-agent workflow. Emphasizes structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination, inspired by competitive programming practices. Evaluated on a self-constructed benchmark across Qwen models and on LiveCodeBench-Pro, with ablation studies.

Result: Average acceptance-rate gain of 6.48% on the self-constructed benchmark across Qwen series models; parametric tuning yields only 0.89% improvement. Additional 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro. Consistent improvements across accuracy and efficiency metrics. Individual agents can contribute up to 27.7% in ablations. Error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors analyzed.

Conclusion: MAS-Algorithm shows strong potential for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning, offering generalizability across problem types, enhanced interpretability through structured workflows, and a scalable framework for integrating tools and coordinating multiple agents.

Abstract: Algorithmic problem solving serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating structured reasoning in AI coding systems, as it directly reflects a model's ability to perform structured reasoning in complex scenarios.Existing approaches predominantly rely on model-centric strategies, such as architectural modifications and data scaling, which are costly and offer limited interpretability. Alternative methods leveraging external tools or prompting techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) are often fragmented and lack a unified framework. In this paper, we propose MAS-Algorithm, a systematic multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving inspired by the practices of competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. Our framework decomposes the end-to-end solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among agents. The design emphasizes both rigor and extensibility, allowing it to generalize across diverse problem types.Experimental results on a self-constructed benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple Qwen series models, achieving an average gain of 6.48% in acceptance rate. In contrast, parameter-efficient fine-tuning on the same data yields only a marginal improvement of 0.89%. We further observe a 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with consistent improvements across additional accuracy and efficiency metrics.Beyond performance gains, we conduct comprehensive analyses to better understand the reasoning process within the workflow, including error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors. We further perform customized replacement and ablation studies to explore the upper bound of the framework, showing that individual agents can contribute improvements of up to 27.7%. These results highlight the strong potential of MAS-Algorithm for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning.

</details>


### [402] [HaM-World: Soft-Hamiltonian World Models with Selective Memory for Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05951)
*Haoyun Tang,Haodong Cui,Keyao Xu,Kun Wang,Zhandong Mei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HaM-World (HMW) introduces a structured world model that factorizes the latent into a canonical (q, p) pair plus a context c, integrated with Mamba memory, enabling stable, long-horizon planning and strong OOD robustness in DM Control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the instability of imagined rollouts in model-based planning due to missing history-conditioned Markov completeness and a lack of geometric separation between configuration, momentum, and task semantics.

Method: Decompose latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c. The (q, p) dynamics follow a Hamiltonian-derived vector field with learnable residuals, while c captures dissipative/semantic factors. Use Mamba selective state-space memory as history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. A single latent state is shared across dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM search.

Result: On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World achieves the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations (dynamics shifts, action delay, observation masking), it attains the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift and coherent energy transfer during rollouts, supporting the Soft-Hamiltonian design.

Conclusion: Structured latent design with a Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics interface improves stability and generalization of world-model-based planning, yielding strong in-distribution and OOD performance, and enabling a unified latent interface for dynamics, value, imagination, and search.

Abstract: World models enable model-based planning through learned latent dynamics, but imagined rollouts become unstable as the planning horizon grows or the dynamics distribution shifts. We argue that this instability reflects two missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and geometric organization that separates configuration, momentum, and task semantics. We propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that decomposes the latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c, while using Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. Within this interface, (q, p) evolves through an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This gives the planner a single latent state shared by dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search. On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World reaches the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, HaM-World achieves the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics further show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift, structured energy variation under policy rollouts, and coherent control-induced energy transfer, supporting the intended Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

</details>


### [403] [Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05958)
*Peilin Zhan,Wei Chen,Weilin Chen,Shuyi Pan,Ruichu Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) for Knowledge Tracing to correct selection bias in logged student interactions using a doubly robust estimator that combines a propensity model and an error-imputation model, plus a temporal smoothness regularizer to control variance; demonstrates across real benchmarks that TSDR improves KT backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selection bias from non-random exercise recommendations and student choices contaminates knowledge-tracing training data, causing biased mastery estimates and error accumulation in sequential recommendations.

Method: Introduce a doubly robust estimator that combines a propensity model for the observation mechanism with an error-imputation model for missing/corrupted data. Derive a temporal generalization bound showing variance accumulation threatens training stability. Propose TSDR: jointly optimize KT predictor and imputation model with a temporal smoothness regularizer to reduce variance while preserving DR unbiasedness.
