Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple real-world benchmarks shows that TSDR consistently improves various state-of-the-art KT backbones, validating the effectiveness of principled bias correction and variance control in KT.

Conclusion: DR-based bias correction with temporal smoothness stabilization yields more accurate and stable knowledge traces across models and datasets, highlighting the importance of handling non-random observation processes in KT.

Abstract: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is fundamental to intelligent education systems, yet relies on educational logs that are selectively observed. The non-random nature of exercise recommendations and student choices inevitably induces severe selection bias. Most existing KT methods neglect this issue, training on observed logs using standard empirical risk, which yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations. To address this, we introduce a doubly robust (DR) formulation for KT that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. Beyond unbiasedness, in the sequential setting of KT, we identify that the estimator's performance is compromised by variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, thereby causing training instability and limiting performance. To mitigate this, we derive a generalization bound that explicitly characterizes the impact of estimator variance and identifies temporal smoothness as a key factor in controlling it. Building on these theoretical insights, we propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework. TSDR jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputation model with a smoothness regularizer, effectively reducing variance while preserving the unbiasedness guarantee of DR. Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, underscoring the vital role of principled bias correction in KT.

</details>


### [404] [From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05959)
*Xinghao Wu,Jianwei Niu,Guogang Zhu,Xuefeng Liu,Shaojie Tang,Jiayuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FedSAF introduces structural alignment for heterogeneous federated learning, shifting from coordinate-level to relational, class-structure alignment to handle diverse feature extractors. It outperforms coordinate alignment and prior prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52% on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coordinate alignment couples semantic structure with a shared feature basis, which is suboptimal under model heterogeneity. Heterogeneous feature extractors induce client-specific subspaces; preserving their subspaces while aligning class relations can improve learning.

Method: Propose FedSAF which replaces absolute-coordinate alignment with inter-class relational (structural) alignment of prototypes. This decouples semantic structure from a fixed feature basis, allowing client-specific subspaces to be leveraged while maintaining global class relationships.

Result: Structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings, achieving up to 3.52% improvement over state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structural alignment is more effective for HtFL than coordinate alignment, as it preserves client-specific subspaces while enforcing global inter-class relations, leading to better classification performance.

Abstract: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

</details>


### [405] [TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05963)
*Junkai Li,Yunghwei Lai,Tianyi Zhu,Zheng Long Lee,Weizhi Ma,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TheraAgent introduces an iterative generate-judge-refine framework for treatment-plan generation, bolstered by TheraJudge, achieving state-of-the-art results on HealthBench and strong expert validation against physicians.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs' one-shot generation lacks explicit verification, leading to rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans; there is a need for an agentic, iterative reasoning process with explicit clinical evaluation.

Method: TheraAgent replaces one-shot generation with a loop: generate a draft, judge for quality, then refine. TheraJudge is a treatment-specific evaluator integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Evaluations include HealthBench metrics and expert assessments comparing against physicians.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench in Accuracy and Completeness; expert evaluations show an 86% win rate against physicians with superior Targeting and Harm Control; strong agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations indicates reliability.

Conclusion: An effective, reliable framework that improves safety and quality of treatment-plan generation by mirroring human iterative reasoning and embedding explicit clinical evaluation; demonstrates strong performance and alignment with expert judgment.

Abstract: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline. By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.

</details>


### [406] [BehaviorGuard: Online Backdoor Defense for Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05977)
*Yinbo Yu,Xueyu Yin,Jiadai Wang,Chunwei Tian,Sai Xu,Qi Zhu,Daoqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BehaviorGuard is an online, behavior-based backdoor defense for DRL that detects and mitigates backdoor actions by monitoring action distribution drift, focusing on high-quantile and tail behavior, effective in single- and multi-agent DRL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in DRL threaten policy reliability; existing defenses rely on reward anomalies or fine-tuning, which are brittle and costly; trigger-agnostic, online defenses are needed.

Method: Introduce a drift-based metric on action distributions to identify backdoor behavior; detect and suppress backdoor actions at runtime; applicable to single- and multi-agent DRL; evaluate across diverse benchmarks and backdoor attacks.

Result: BehaviorGuard achieves superior efficacy and efficiency compared to prior methods; robust detection and mitigation across varied attacks with low runtime overhead.

Conclusion: First online, behavior-based backdoor defense for DRL; broad applicability and practical utility; future work to analyze false positives, generalization, and adaptation to new attack types.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Current defenses typically rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model finetuning to remove backdoors. However, complex trigger patterns undermine their robustness, and fine-tuning entails high costs, limiting practical utility. Therefore, we shift defense concerns to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors and propose BehaviorGuard, an online behavior-based backdoor detection and mitigation framework for DRL. Specifically, we find that regardless of attacks, backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions to ensure reliable activation, leaving detectable traces in high-quantile regions and distribution tails, even in the absence of triggers. Based on this, we design a novel metric that captures behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. To our knowledge, this is the first online backdoor defense that counters attacks both in single- and multi-agent DRL. Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

</details>


### [407] [TACT: Mitigating Overthinking and Overacting in Coding Agents via Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05980)
*Yuan Sui,Yulin Chen,Yibo Li,Xue Jiang,Yufei He,Yihong Dong,Xiaoxin He,Tianyu Gao,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TACT detects and mitigates agent drift (overthinking/overacting) in long-horizon LLM-driven software engineering tasks by steering residual activations along two drift axes, improving resolve rates and reducing steps-to-resolve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon language-model agents frequently deteriorate into failure modes (overthinking and overacting) during extended reasoning and tool use; a real-time, region-focused intervention in the model's hidden states is proposed to preserve calibrated behavior.

Method: Label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated; observe that hidden states separate linearly along two drift axes. At test time, project each step’s residual activation onto these axes and pull drifted steps back toward the calibrated region to mitigate drift.

Result: Two drift axes separate calibrated vs. failure modes with AUC ~0.9. TACT outperforms unsteered baselines on SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval. Gains include +5.8 percentage points in average resolve rate on Qwen3.5-27B and +4.8 percentage points on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it, and up to a 26% reduction in steps-to-resolve.

Conclusion: Drift is a steerable direction in the residual stream; TACT provides a viable mechanism to enhance reliability of long-horizon LLM agents via activation steering.

Abstract: When language model agents tackle complex software engineering tasks, they often degrade over long trajectories, which we define as *agent drift*. We focus on two recurring failure modes *overthinking* and *overacting*, i.e., where the agent repeatedly reasons over information it already has, and where it issues tool calls without integrating recent observations or acquiring new evidence. In this paper, we introduce TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering), to detect and mitigate agent drift in the residual stream before it surfaces as a behavioral failure. In specific, we label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find that their hidden states can separate linearly along two *drift axes*, pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode (AUC $\approx$ 0.9). To mitigate agent drift, we project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region. Experiments show that TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by $+5.8$ pp on Qwen3.5-27B and $+4.8$ pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to $26\%$. These gains frame agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream, and position TACT as a viable handle for reliable long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [408] [BioResearcher: Scenario-Guided Multi-Agent for Translational Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05985)
*Remigiusz Kinas,Joanna Krawczyk,Rafał Powalski,Przemysław Pietrzak,Agnieszka Kowalewska,Krzysztof Kolmus,Maciej Sypetkowski,Łukasz Smoliński,Tomasz Jetka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ingenix BioResearcher is a scenario-guided multi-agent system for translational medicine that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to 30+ tools, combines DB access with sandboxed genome analyses, and uses claim-level multi-model reconciliation for editorial assembly; it outperforms baselines on multiple unit, open-ended, and end-to-end benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of general-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented systems which lack auditable, scenario-specific workflows and provenance for heterogeneous biomedical sources.

Method: Proposes Ingenix BioResearcher architecture: scenario-guided query-to-playbook mapping, delegation to specialized subagents across 30+ tools/ML endpoints, mixed structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and claim-level multi-model reconciliation prior to editorial assembly; evaluated on unit-level capabilities, open-ended reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery.

Result: Outperforms baselines with 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass, 0.892 avg); 89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50; top mean 0.758 on BaisBench Scientific Discovery; 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark: 74.7% ±3.3% positive hits; 96.8% ±0.2% negative clears.

Conclusion: Demonstrates broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations, supporting the viability of a scenario-guided multi-agent framework for translational medicine with auditable provenance.

Abstract: Translational medicine turns underspecified development goals into evidence synthesis that must combine literature, trials, patents, and quantitative multi-omics analysis while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. General-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented or multi-agent systems are not built for this: they tend to produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly, and fall short on the auditable, scenario-specific workflows that heterogeneous biomedical sources demand.
  This paper introduces Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
  We evaluate BioResearcher across unit-level capabilities, open-ended biomedical reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery. It leads evaluated baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 average score), achieves strong biomedical benchmark performance (89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and the top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery), and leads on a 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark with the highest positive hit rate (74.7% $\pm$ 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% $\pm$ 0.2%). These results show broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations.

</details>


### [409] [Strat-LLM: Stratified Strategy Alignment for LLM-based Stock Trading with Real-time Multi-Source Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06024)
*Wenliang Huang,Zengyi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Examines how Stratified Strategy Alignment (Strat-LLM) interacts with reasoning style, mode constraints, and model scale to shape autonomous trading performance across market regimes using live data and forward-looking evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges a gap in benchmarks by jointly evaluating architectural reasoning (internal logic) and external strategy alignment under realistic trading conditions, including look-ahead bias elimination and heterogeneous data streams.

Method: Strat-LLM framework deployed in a live-forward setting (2025) integrating sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports. Stress-tests on A-share and U.S. markets assess: (i) mode-dependent utility with Free/Guided/Strict settings; (ii) regime-dependent alignment utility across uptrends vs downtrends; (iii) impact of model scale (e.g., 35B vs 122B) on fidelity under constraints; (iv) common LLM pitfalls like high win-rate traps. Metrics include alignment utility, drawdowns, total returns, and risk anchors.

Result: Key findings: (1) reasoning-heavy models peak in Free Mode; standard models rely on Strict Mode for risk anchoring; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent: Free/Guided capture momentum in uptrends; Strict mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) exhibit best fidelity under strict constraints; ultra-large models (122B) incur an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs tend to pursue high win-rate at the expense of total return, mitigated only by deep reasoning or strict guardrails.

Conclusion: Stratified Strategy Alignment shapes trading performance in an architecture-aware, regime-sensitive manner. Mode selection, model scale, and data integration critically determine risk-adjusted returns; the framework reveals trade-offs and suggests tailored configurations for different market regimes.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving into autonomous trading agents, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency. We propose Strat-LLM, a framework grounded in Stratified Strategy Alignment. Operating in a live-forward setting throughout 2025, it integrates heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports to eliminate look-ahead bias. Extensive stress tests on A-share and U.S. markets reveal: (1) reasoning-heavy models achieve peak utility in Free Mode via internal logic, whereas standard models require Strict Mode as a vital risk anchor; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent, with Free and Guided modes capturing momentum in uptrending markets, while Strict Mode mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints, whereas ultra-large models (122B) suffer an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain a performance premium in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs often fall into a high win-rate trap, optimizing for small gains at the expense of total returns, which can only be mitigated through deep reasoning or strict external guardrails. Project details are available at https://Strat-LLM.github.io.

</details>


### [410] [Pathways to AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06029)
*Gordon Fletcher,Saomai Vu Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Critical, sociotechnical pathway analysis of current generative AI development, comparing frontier proprietary, open-weight, and domain models to identify leverage points and alternative trajectories, with recommendations for AGI-adjacent programs under transparency and wellbeing constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the inevitability of AI progress, link AI development to social-political-economic conditions, and study how different decision points shaped current tools, to inform responsible, transparent governance.

Method: Qualitative, historical-sociotechnical analysis using five guiding questions; pathway mapping, leverage-point identification, cross-trajectory comparison, branching project assessment, and scenario-based policy recommendations.

Result: A structured analytic framework and preliminary mappings of dominant pathways, leverage nodes, alternative trajectories, and drivers across three foundational-model trajectories; identification of potential socio-technical development programs toward AGI-adjacent capability.

Conclusion: Argues that responsible progression toward AGI-adjacent capabilities is plausible within governance structures that enforce transparency, moderation, wellbeing, and sustainable business models; highlights leverage points and policy avenues for future work.

Abstract: Our focus are five related questions that stem from a critical software studies perspective. Underpinning this view is the acknowledged need to avoid assumptions regarding the inevitability of the current situation relating to AI. What we need to see is the closeness of the linkage between current commercial AI development and our prevailing social, political and economic circumstances. This does mean that the perspectives presented here are done so critically and conditionally. Most importantly, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is seen as being problematic both conceptually and definitionally. This conditioning of any view regarding AGI does lead the discussion in specific directions and to certain conclusions regarding the future. However, adopting this perspective enables the work to offer some final recommendations. We set out to ask the following questions, 1. What are the critical pathways that produced the current dominant generative AI tools (capabilities, product forms, adoption patterns)? 2. Which decision points acted as leverage nodes (small changes that had large downstream effects), and which dead ends reveal alternative possibilities that did not become dominant? 3. How do pathways differ across three foundational-model trajectories such as the frontier proprietary models, open-weight models or specific domain and sovereign models? 4. Which alternative projects branched from key leverage nodes, what is their current state, and why did some succeed, stall, fail or become absorbed? 5. Based on this analysis, what socio-technical development programmes could plausibly move toward AGI-adjacent capability while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing and sustainable business models?

</details>


### [411] [Novelty-based Tree-of-Thought Search for LLM Reasoning and Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06040)
*Leon Hamm,Zlatan Ajanovic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a novelty-based pruning mechanism for tree-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, defining novelty as the uniqueness of a new thought relative to the search tree; uses LLM prompts and pre-trained embeddings to estimate novelty; prunes branches to reduce token costs; validated on language-based planning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought, and RL methods improve reasoning but remain brittle and costly; width-based planning ideas suggest novelty-guided search could improve efficiency in language tasks.

Method: Define a novelty metric for each new node by prompting the LLM to assess its uniqueness w.r.t. previously seen nodes and leveraging pre-trained embeddings. Use this metric to prune low-novelty branches in the tree-of-thought search. Despite more prompts per state, overall token cost is reduced by shrinking the tree. Evaluate on language-based planning and general reasoning benchmarks.

Result: In experiments across multiple benchmarks, novelty-guided pruning reduces the search space and total token usage while preserving or improving reasoning performance compared to baselines that do not prune.

Conclusion: Novelty-based pruning is a viable, cost-effective enhancement for tree-of-thought approaches, salvaging efficiency through pruning while maintaining reasoning quality; aligns with width-based search principles in planning.

Abstract: Although advances such as chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought or reinforcement learning have improved the performance of LLMs in reasoning and planning tasks, they are still brittle and have not achieved human-level performance in many domains, and often suffer from high time and token costs. Inspired by the success of width-based search in planning, we explore how the concept of novelty can be transferred to language domains and how it can improve tree-of-thought reasoning. A tree of thoughts relies on building possible "paths" of consecutive ideas or thoughts. These are generated by repeatedly prompting an LLM. In our paper, a measurable concept of novelty is proposed that describes the uniqueness of a new node (thought) in comparison to nodes previously seen in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and making use of embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric can then be used to prune branches and reduce the scope of the search. Although this method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing the overall tree size. This procedure is tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

</details>


### [412] [Visual Fingerprints for LLM Generation Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06054)
*Amal Alnouri,Andreas Hinterreiter,Christina Humer,Furui Cheng,Marc Streit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework to compare LLM generation conditions by treating outputs as distributions of linguistic choices and visualizing distribution fingerprints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture jointly bias outputs and to aid prompt design and model evaluation.

Method: Extract linguistic choices (content, expression, structure) from repeated samples under different generation conditions using NLP pipelines, then represent and visualize their distributions as “visual fingerprints” for direct comparison.

Result: Visual fingerprints reveal distribution-level patterns of LLM behavior across conditions, demonstrated in four usage scenarios beyond what single responses or aggregate metrics show.

Conclusion: Distribution-level fingerprints enable robust comparison of condition-specific tendencies, supporting analysis of how generation conditions shape LLM outputs.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) outputs arise from complex interactions among prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture. We refer to specific configurations of these factors as generation conditions, each of which can bias outputs in various ways. Understanding how different generation conditions shape model behaviors is essential for tasks such as prompt design and model evaluation, yet it remains challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation. We present an approach to visually compare LLM outputs across generation conditions by modeling responses as collections of linguistic choices, including content, expression, and structure. We extract these choices using natural language processing pipelines and represent their distributions across repeated samples. We then visualize these distributions as visual fingerprints, enabling direct, distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies. Through four usage scenarios, we demonstrate how visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

</details>


### [413] [VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06068)
*Keisuke Kamahori,Shihang Li,Simon Peter,Baris Kasikci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VibeServe introduces a multi-agent, end-to-end system that synthesizes bespoke LLM serving stacks for different usage scenarios, achieving competitive standard performance and superior gains in non-standard settings by generation-time specialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM serving stacks are monolithic and heavily hand-tuned, limiting adaptation to diverse workloads, models, and hardware. The paper argues for generation-time specialization via an agentic loop to tailor stacks per scenario.

Method: Propose VibeServe with an outer planning/search loop over system designs and an inner loop that implements candidates, verifies correctness, and benchmarks performance on the target workload; evaluation across standard and six non-standard deployment scenarios.

Result: In standard deployments, VibeServe is competitive with vLLM. In non-standard scenarios, it outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities missed by generic stacks, leveraging model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware optimizations across six scenarios.

Conclusion: Generation-time specialization via an agentic loop is a promising design space for infrastructure software, offering performance gains in niche but important scenarios without sacrificing standard performance; code is available.

Abstract: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

</details>


### [414] [Safety Certification is Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06087)
*Oliver Schön,Licio Romao,Sadegh Soudjani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Kernel-embedding classifier estimates T-step safety directly from trajectory data, avoiding DP recursion; it unifies barrier certificates and robust models and remains horizon-stable and applicable to non-Markovian dynamics, demonstrated on a neural quadrotor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome compounding errors in DP-based safety certification and extend to non-Markovian dynamics; enable reliable long-horizon certificates.

Method: Kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a trajectory-classification problem; directly estimates the T-step safety probability without recursion; shows barrier certificates and robust Markov models arise as special cases.

Result: Direct estimators remain stable with respect to certification horizon and non-Markovian dynamics; DP-based certificates can become unsound; validated in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unifying, horizon-stable approach to safety certification, enabling certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics and suggesting directions for kernel choice and robustness.

Abstract: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

</details>


### [415] [Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06105)
*Jungsuk Oh,Hyeseo Jeon,Hyunjune Ji,Kyongmin Kong,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPEED reduces long-context KV memory by materializing non-anchor prompt KV states only in lower layers while keeping decode-phase tokens full-depth, achieving near-baseline performance with substantial memory savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in decoder-only LMs is costly due to processing long prompts during prefill, caching at all layers, and repeated attention during decoding. Reducing the KV-cache footprint without large performance loss is desirable.

Method: SPEED (Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode) introduces a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy: non-anchor prompt KV states are materialized only in lower layers; prefill tokens in upper layers are removed from the Decode visibility set while decode-phase tokens retain full-depth visibility. A minimal BoS anchor is used. Only about 75% of layers are used for prefill tokens.

Result: In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED with 75% layers for prefill achieves 51.2 average on OLMES-style benchmarks vs 51.4 baseline; TTFT improves by 33%, TPOT by 22%; active KV memory is reduced by 25.0% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics indicate preservation of the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions.

Conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens need not persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth; SPEED offers substantial memory savings with minimal performance loss.

Abstract: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

</details>


### [416] [On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06110)
*Xinglin Wang,Zishen Liu,Shaoxiong Feng,Peiwen Yuan,Yiwei Li,Jiayi Shi,Yueqi Zhang,Chuyi Tan,Ji Zhang,Boyuan Pan,Yao Hu,Kan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MCPP is a lightweight closed-loop planner that optimizes the probability of completing a dependency-structured workflow within a given budget and deadline by simulating executions and replanning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practice, workflows face hard budget and deadline constraints. Prior work that optimizes average efficiency may fail to maximize the chance that the entire workflow finishes on time and within budget.

Method: Formulate as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem. The executor allocates models and parallelizes subtasks that can be executed concurrently, while tracking remaining budget/time. It uses Monte Carlo simulations of workflow executions to estimate the constrained completion probability and replans after observing outcomes.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CodeFlow and ProofFlow shows MCPP consistently improves the constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget–deadline settings.

Conclusion: MCPP provides a practical, closed-loop, simulation-based approach for constrained completion in agentic workflows, enabling more reliable adherence to hard budget/deadline requirements.

Abstract: Agentic systems increasingly solve complex user requests by executing orchestrated workflows, where subtasks are assigned to specialized models or tools and coordinated according to their dependencies. While recent work improves agent efficiency by optimizing the performance--cost--latency frontier, real deployments often impose concrete requirements: a workflow must be completed within a specified budget and before a specified deadline. This shifts the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability that the entire workflow completes successfully under explicit budget and deadline constraints. We study \emph{constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows}. Given a dependency-structured workflow and estimates of success rates and generation lengths for each subtask--model pair, the executor allocates models and parallel samples across simultaneously executable subtasks while managing the remaining budget and time. We formulate this setting as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and propose \emph{Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning} (MCPP), a lightweight closed-loop planner that directly estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes. Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow demonstrate that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget--deadline constraints.

</details>


### [417] [CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06115)
*Zhen Zeng,Leijiang Gu,Feng Li,Jing Yu,Zenglin Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes cross-cultural knowledge insertion for multilingual LLMs; introduces CrossCult-KIBench benchmark and Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) baseline; findings indicate current methods struggle to balance cultural adaptation with preserving non-target behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: English-centric training of MLLMs leads to culturally misaligned or inappropriate outputs in cross-cultural scenarios; there is a need for evaluation benchmarks and methods to adapt models to specific cultures without disturbing behavior in other cultures.

Method: CrossCult-KIBench: 9,800 image-grounded cases spanning 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic; supports single-insert and sequential-insert evaluation. MCKI baseline retrieves cultural knowledge from external memory using frozen MLLM representations and prepends matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable.

Result: Experimental results show current approaches struggle to simultaneously achieve effective cultural adaptation and preservation of base behavior across non-target cultures.

Conclusion: Highlights a key challenge in building culturally-aware MLLMs and establishes CrossCult-KIBench as a resource to drive research toward more culturally adaptive and responsible models.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

</details>


### [418] [Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06123)
*Nguyen Viet Tuan Kiet,Bui Dinh Pham,Dao Van Tung,Tran Cong Dao,Huynh Thi Thanh Binh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Shifts AHD from code-centric bottom-up to knowledge-first top-down search, formalizing a statistical-learning view with a distortion–compression trade-off; demonstrates efficiency, transfer, and generalization gains across combinatorial optimization tasks by learning reusable, interpretable knowledge, often outperforming code-centric methods; combining both strategies yields additional gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs enable automatic heuristic design for combinatorial optimization, but current work emphasizes searching executable programs (bottom-up). The paper argues for a top-down, knowledge-centric paradigm where knowledge is the primary object of search and code merely instantiates and tests it, enabling reuse across problems and trajectories.

Method: Formalizes a statistical-learning view of AHD, introducing a distortion–compression trade-off. Implements this knowledge-first approach in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks and evaluates across combinatorial optimization tasks and related domains.

Result: Knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperfoming code-centric pipelines. Combining knowledge-first and code-centric strategies yields further gains.

Conclusion: Progress in automatic heuristic design should focus on building and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory, enabling reusable knowledge across problems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

</details>


### [419] [P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06124)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes P-Guide, a single-pass latent-guidance method for classifier-free guidance (CFG) in flow-based generation, achieving ~50% latency reduction while preserving fidelity and prompt alignment; under a first-order view it matches CFG by steering from the prior space without velocity extrapolation; explores homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors with mean-variance modeling for adaptive loss and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFG provides high-quality conditional generation but requires dual forward passes per step, incurring high computational cost. A single-pass alternative would reduce latency while preserving performance.

Method: Introduce P-Guide that modulates only the initial latent state to provide guidance; show equivalence to CFG under a first-order approximation by steering via the prior space rather than velocity fields; analyze both homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors and jointly model mean and variance to attenuate loss adaptively and improve robustness; validate with experiments measuring latency and fidelity.

Result: Inference latency decreased by approximately 50% with fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

Conclusion: Single-pass, latent-space guidance (P-Guide) is a viable alternative to CFG in flow matching, offering substantial speedups with comparable quality; heteroscedastic priors further enhance robustness, suggesting broader applicability and potential for real-time conditional generation.

Abstract: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

</details>


### [420] [Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06130)
*Yaorui Shi,Yuxin Chen,Zhengxi Lu,Yuchun Miao,Shugui Liu,Qi GU,Xunliang Cai,Xiang Wang,An Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified policy co-evolves selection, utilization, and distillation of skills via a single task-outcome objective, improving persistent skill libraries over isolated methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches optimize skill selection, usage, or distillation separately, causing partial progress and conflicting dynamics. A unified objective can align capabilities.

Method: Train a single policy that (1) queries the skill library, (2) re-ranks to select a candidate, (3) solves the task conditioned on the chosen skill, (4) distills a new skill from the trajectory; learning driven by a single task-outcome signal with distinct credit frequencies for selection vs distillation.

Result: Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and RL baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop; experiments show co-evolution of capabilities; ablations show removing any credit signal degrades performance.

Conclusion: Integrated co-evolution under a unified objective enables more effective persistent skill libraries and strategy reuse; the approach is validated empirically and supported by ablations.

Abstract: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

</details>


### [421] [Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06154)
*Kossi Amouzouvi,Robert Wardenga,Jens Lehmann,Sahar Vahdati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graphlet-based vocabulary tokens unify heterogeneous knowledge graphs to expose transferable structural invariances for KG foundation models, improving zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction across 51 KGs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge graphs are discrete yet non-Euclidean, lacking a universal token grid. This hinders transferable representations across unseen KGs. Graphlet tokens offer consistent, recurring local patterns to bridge diverse graphs.

Method: Introduce a model-agnostic framework using a graphlet vocabulary (closed/open 2- and 3-paths, and star graphlets) and pattern matching to mine a KG between relations. This yields a token-based representation that can be fed into KG foundation models for link prediction, evaluated in zero-shot inductive and transductive settings across 51 KGs.

Result: Incorporating graphlets into the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KG foundation models in both zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction across a broad set of KGs.

Conclusion: Graphlet-based structural tokens provide robust invariances and enable cross-KG transfer, improving KGFMs’ performance. The approach offers a simple, model-agnostic plug-in to enhance transferability without requiring a universal token set.

Abstract: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

</details>


### [422] [Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06161)
*Shihao Weng,Yang Feng,Xiaofei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based evaluators for agent safety lack policy invariance: verdicts depend on evaluator wording as much as agent behavior. The paper defines policy invariance principles, proposes a stress-test protocol, and reports significant verdict instability and reliability variation among judges, recommending a standardized scoring and reporting framework (Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card) with open release of protocol and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure trustworthy safety judging, evaluators must be robust to changes in evaluation policy text and not conflate agent conduct with evaluator prompting. The work operationalizes this need as policy invariance and provides concrete tests and metrics.

Method: Introduce three testable principles (rubric-semantics invariance, rubric-threshold invariance, ambiguity-aware calibration) and instantiate them in a stress-test protocol using four agent-class judges. Evaluate trajectories from ASSEBench and R-Judge under content-preserving rewrites and threshold shifts. Quantify verdict flips and measure judge reliability (Judge Card).

Result: Found that judges respond to both meaningful normative shifts and meaningless structural rewrites with similar impact; content-preserving rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts; 18-43% of flips occur on unambiguous cases under rewrites. Demonstrated an order-of-magnitude variation in judge reliability not captured by accuracy alone.

Conclusion: Current safety scores conflate agent behavior with evaluator prompting. The Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card reveal substantial reliability gaps and provide a practical auditing framework. The protocol and code are released to enable future benchmarks to audit evaluators rather than trust them by default.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

</details>


### [423] [BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06177)
*Jinge Wu,Hongjian Zhou,Mingde Zeng,Jiayuan Zhu,Junde Wu,Jiazhen Pan,Sean Wu,Honghan Wu,Fenglin Liu,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioMedArena is an open-source toolkit that standardizes evaluation of biomedical deep-research agents, decoupling six evaluation layers to enable fair cross-model benchmarking across 147 benchmarks and 75 tools, and reporting state-of-the-art gains while reducing integration effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the per-paper engineering tax and inconsistent harness/tool registries that hinder fair, scalable comparison and rapid incorporation of new foundation models in biomedical research.

Method: Decouples six evaluation layers—benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring—into independent components; ships 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 functional families; provides 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies (12 backbones); supports adding new models/benchmarks/tools via small provider adapters; includes per-task traces.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA.

Conclusion: BioMedArena enables fair, scalable, and reproducible evaluation of biomedical deep-research agents, reducing the per-paper engineering tax and fostering cross-model comparability; the toolkit, configurations, and traces are publicly available (GitHub).

Abstract: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA. The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

</details>


### [424] [Rethinking Adapter Placement: A Dominant Adaptation Module Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06183)
*Suoxin Zhang,Run He,Di Fang,Xiang Tan,Kaixuan Chen,Huiping Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAGE reveals a single dominant, shallow FFN down-projection where LoRA adapters should be placed; DomLoRA places one adapter there, achieving superior efficiency and performance across multiple tasks and model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify optimal, sparse adapter placement for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, addressing the open question of where to locate a limited number of trainable adapters.

Method: Introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy for each candidate LoRA adapter. Find that energy concentrates on a single shallow FFN down-projection (dominant adaptation module). Propose DomLoRA to place one adapter at this module. Evaluate across four downstream tasks (instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, multi-turn conversation) and multiple model families; show DomLoRA improves over vanilla LoRA and benefits other LoRA variants.

Result: PAGE identifies a dominant adaptation module, enabling a single adapter placement (DomLoRA) that uses ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA trainable parameters yet outperforms vanilla LoRA on average across tasks. The finding is architecture-dependent but task-stable, and the approach generalizes to other LoRA variants.

Conclusion: A practical guideline for parameter-efficient fine-tuning emerges: place adapters at the dominant adaptation module to maximize impact with minimal parameters; this challenges distributed adapter patterns and offers robust gains across diverse tasks and model families.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that places trainable low-rank adapters into frozen pre-trained models. Recent studies show that using fewer LoRA adapters may still maintain or even improve performance, but existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance largely open. To investigate this, we introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy available to each candidate LoRA adapter. Surprisingly, we find that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across two model families and four downstream tasks. We term this module the dominant adaptation module and show that its layer index is architecture-dependent but task-stable. Motivated by this finding, we propose DomLoRA, a placement method that places a single adapter at the dominant adaptation module. With only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters, DomLoRA outperforms it on average across various downstream tasks, including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. This method also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant adaptation module perspective as a practical placement guideline.

</details>


### [425] [Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06185)
*Peizheng Yan,Yu Zhao,Liang Xie,Juntong Qi,Mingming Wang,Erwei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Event-Causal RAG for infinite long-video reasoning using SES event graphs and a dual-store memory to enable efficient semantic and causal retrieval, surpassing clip-based baselines and long-context models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address ultra-long/infinite video understanding where memory and causal inference across long temporal spans are needed, while overcoming O(n^2) self-attention and fragmented clip-level memory in existing methods.

Method: Segment streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represent each as a State-Event-State (SES) graph. Merge graphs into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in a dual-store memory supporting semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. Use bidirectional retrieval to identify relevant event causal chains and provide them with video evidence to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation.

Result: Experiments on long-video benchmarks show consistent improvement over clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, especially for multi-event integration and causal inference across long gaps, with better memory efficiency and streaming robustness.

Conclusion: A memory-augmented, causally aware retrieval framework that scales to infinite streams and improves reasoning over long videos, addressing both memory and computation challenges while enhancing interpretability through explicit event graphs.

Abstract: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

</details>


### [426] [OPSD Compresses What RLVR Teaches: A Post-RL Compaction Stage for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06188)
*Jaehoon Kim,Dongha Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPSD's effectiveness in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning is primarily as a compression mechanism when trained on correct rollouts; training on incorrect rollouts harms accuracy. A separation of correct/incorrect rollout training reveals that hindsight supervision better compress long traces than supply better replacements. Proposed pipeline: train via supervised learning (SFT), then RLVR, then OPSD to maximize efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate why OPSD, successful in other RL contexts, underperforms or behaves differently in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning. Disentangle the effects of hindsight supervision by applying OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollouts.

Method: Apply OPSD separately to two groups of rollouts (correct and incorrect) from thinking-enabled math reasoning traces, measure accuracy and response length, compare to baselines, and assess the value of OPSD as a correction vs compression tool. Propose a post-training pipeline based on findings.

Result: OPSD preserves accuracy and substantially shortens outputs when trained on correct rollouts (compression with maintained accuracy). Training on incorrect rollouts lowers accuracy, indicating less effective correction capabilities in this setting. In long think-enabled traces, OPSD more reliably compresses than corrects.

Conclusion: A revised post-training pipeline is recommended: SFT -> RLVR -> OPSD. Rationale: OPSD is safer and more effective as a compression mechanism for thinking-enabled reasoning, and its corrective benefit is limited; careful selection of rollout groups is crucial.

Abstract: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) has recently emerged as an alternative to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), promising higher accuracy and shorter responses through token-level credit assignment from a self-teacher conditioned on privileged context. However, this promise does not carry over to thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, where reported accuracy gains shrink and sometimes turn negative. We hypothesize that hindsight supervision can specify better token-level alternatives in short thinking-disabled outputs, but in long thinking-enabled traces it more readily identifies redundancy than supplies better replacements. To test this, we applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups, so that compression and correction can be observed in isolation. Our results show that in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, OPSD behaves most reliably as a compression mechanism rather than a correction mechanism: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while substantially shortening responses, whereas training only on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. In light of these findings, we propose a revised post-training pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: SFT then RLVR then OPSD.

</details>


### [427] [Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06191)
*Shivali Dalmia,Ananya Mantravadi,Prasanna Desikan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Zero-shot/few-shot LLMs can detect actionable clinical actions in discharge notes comparable to supervised models for binary tasks, but fall behind on fine-grained multi-label classification; a two-stage prompting framework helps decomposition, and annotation misalignment reveals the need for reasoning-annotated data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate safety-critical clinical action extraction from narrative discharge notes by comparing general-purpose LLMs to task-specific supervised models under privacy constraints, and to understand annotation inconsistencies affecting evaluation.

Method: Propose a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes into explicit actionable tasks via staged prompting. Systematically evaluate zero-shot and few-shot LLMs against BERT-based supervised models on the CLIP discharge-note dataset, focusing on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety, and analyze annotation inconsistencies and reasoning gaps.

Result: LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification. Errors largely arise from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, especially for implicit actions and rigid labeling rules. The findings suggest current performance reflects limitations in clinical reasoning not captured by plain annotations, and that rationale-free labels hinder distinguishing reasoning failures from annotation mismatches.

Conclusion: Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans are labeled, to enable proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

Abstract: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

</details>


### [428] [The Granularity Axis: A Micro-to-Macro Latent Direction for Social Roles in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06196)
*Chonghan Qin,Xiachong Feng,Ziyun Song,Xiaocheng Feng,Jing Xiong,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Granularity of social role representations in LLMs is a dominant, structured latent axis. A Granularity Axis (difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states) aligns with PC1, explains over half the variance, and is causally manipulable via prompt steering; this holds across models (Qwen3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and transfers between them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs encode socially meaningful granularity of roles (from micro-level to macro-level) in their internal representations, and whether this granularity is controllable and transferable across models.

Method: Define a Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. Construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels; collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses; extract role-level hidden states; project onto the axis; evaluate alignment with PC1 (cosine similarity) and variance explained; test stability across layers, prompts, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and subsets; assess cross-model transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; perform activation steering along the axis to modulate response granularity.

Result: The Granularity Axis aligns with PC1 (cosine 0.972) and explains 52.6% of the variance in role representations. Role projections increase monotonically across all five granularity levels and remain stable across layers, prompts, and data splits; the axis transfers to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Activation steering along the axis causally shifts granularity (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct moves from 2.00 to 3.17 on a 5-point macro scale under positive steering). Differences in controllability suggest model-specific default regimes govern steering efficacy.

Conclusion: Social role granularity is a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned LLM behavior, not merely a surface feature; this axis is robust, transferable, and actionable for steering model outputs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are routinely prompted to take on social roles ranging from individuals to institutions, yet it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode the granularity of such roles, from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational, institutional, or national reasoning. We show that they do. We define a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. In Qwen3-8B, this axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of the role representation space at cosine 0.972 and accounts for 52.6% of its variance, indicating that granularity is the dominant geometric axis organizing prompted social roles. We construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels and collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants, then extract role-level hidden states and project them onto the axis. Role projections increase monotonically across all five levels, remain stable across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. The axis is also causally relevant: activation steering along it shifts response granularity in the predicted direction, with Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale under positive steering on prompts that admit local responses. The two models differ in controllability, suggesting that steering depends on each model's default operating regime. Overall, our findings suggest that social role granularity is not merely a stylistic surface feature, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior.

</details>


### [429] [Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06201)
*Ying Gu,Mei Chee Leong,Hui Li Tan,Shangbo Mao,Liyuan Li,Nancy Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces VL-LCM, a GT-free metric for evaluating vision-language logical consistency in MLLMs, revealing that logical consistency trails accuracy but can guide model selection, validation, and justification in novel tasks without ground truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
