A token-histogram baseline achieves higher overall purity, so the contribution of the graph view is complementary rather than dominant: it surfaces structural relationships that token-frequency and decoder-weight views alone do not capture. Cluster assignments are stable across graph-construction hyperparameters and random seeds.

</details>


### [453] [Process Matters more than Output for Distinguishing Humans from Machines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06524)
*Milena Rmus,Mathew D. Hardy,Thomas L. Griffiths,Mayank Agrawal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Process-level cognitive features outperform performance in distinguishing humans from machines; explicit process-level supervision improves mimicry but generalizes poorly across tasks, highlighting process specification as a bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the reliance on output-level indistinguishability (Turing-like) by leveraging cognitive-process features to distinguish humans from agents; aim to understand how process-level signals can be used to detect machine behavior and guide human-like cognition in AI.

Method: Introduce CogCAPTCHA30, a battery of 30 cognitive tasks designed to elicit diagnostic process-level features while keeping performance matched. Compare multiple agents (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro), Centaur (human-decisions fine-tuned LM), and task-specific A-SFT and P-SFT fine-tuning on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct. Evaluate using process-feature classifiers and cross-task transfer to assess generalization of process representations.

Result: Process features yield stronger discrimination (mean AUC around 0.88 under output matching) than performance metrics. Broad fine-tuning on human decisions enhances human-like task processes relative to off-the-shelf models; task-specific process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT) further improves mimicry. However, advantages decline under cross-task transfer when supervised process targets do not generalize. Explicit process supervision helps only with suitable task-specific process representations, making process specification a bottleneck for achieving human-like cognitive processes in machines.

Conclusion: Process-level supervision can improve human behavioral mimicry but is limited by the availability and generalizability of task-specific process representations. To advance human-like cognition in AI, future work should address how to learn or generalize process representations across tasks and environments.

Abstract: Reliable human-machine discrimination is becoming increasingly important as large language models and autonomous agents are deployed in online settings. Existing approaches evaluate whether a system can produce behavior or responses indistinguishable from those of a human, following the emphasis on outputs as a criterion for intelligence proposed by Alan Turing. Cognitive science offers an alternative perspective: evaluating the process by which behavior is produced. To test whether cognitive processes can reliably distinguish humans from machines, we introduce CogCAPTCHA30, a battery of 30 cognitive tasks designed to elicit diagnostic process-level features even when task performance is matched. Across the battery, process-level features provide stronger discriminative signal than performance metrics alone, reliably distinguishing humans from agents even under output matching (mean process-feature classifier AUC = 0.88). To evaluate agentic process differences, we compare off-the-shelf frontier agents (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro), Centaur (a language model fine-tuned on 10.7M human decisions), and two task-specific fine-tuning approaches applied to Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct: action-level supervised fine-tuning (A-SFT) and process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT), which directly optimizes process features. Broad fine-tuning on human decisions improves human-like task processes relative to off-the-shelf agents, while task-specific process-level supervision further improves behavioral mimicry. However, this advantage diminishes under cross-task transfer when supervised process targets do not naturally generalize across tasks. Explicit process-level supervision can improve human behavioral mimicry, but only if appropriate task-specific process representations are available, highlighting process specification as a bottleneck for achieving human-like cognitive processes in machines.

</details>


### [454] [SpatialEpiBench: Benchmarking Spatial Information and Epidemic Priors in Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06530)
*Ruiqi Lyu,Alistair Turcan,Bryan Wilder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new benchmark, SpatialEpiBench, evaluates spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting across 11 datasets with rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics, finding that many adjacency-informed models underperform a simple last-value baseline; identifies three failure modes and provides data/code for further development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no standardized benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting that reflects real-time practice; existing evaluations use naive train-test splits and may not reveal practical obstacles in sparse, noisy, and non-stationary epidemic data.

Method: Curate 11 epidemic datasets; implement standardized rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics; assess adjacency-informed models using epidemic priors; analyze failure modes; release benchmark data, code, and instructions.

Result: Most methods underperform a simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even with priors; three major failure modes identified: poor outbreak anticipation, difficulty handling sparsity/noise, limited utility of geographic adjacency for epidemiology.

Conclusion: SpatialEpiBench provides a challenging, realistic benchmark to stimulate development of operational forecasting models; addressing outbreak anticipation, data sparsity/noise, and meaningful use of adjacency is essential; resources are released to support the community.

Abstract: Accurate epidemic forecasting is crucial for public health response, resource allocation, and outbreak intervention, but remains difficult with sparse, noisy, and highly non-stationary data. Because epidemics unfold across interacting regions, spatiotemporal methods are natural candidates for improving forecasts. Despite growing interest in spatial information, no standardized benchmark exists, and current evaluations often use simple chronological train-test splits that do not reflect real-time forecasting practice. We address this gap with SpatialEpiBench, a challenging benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting in realistic public-health settings. SpatialEpiBench includes 11 epidemic datasets with standardized rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics. We evaluate adjacency-informed forecasting models with widely used epidemic priors that adapt general models to epidemiology, but find that most methods underperform a simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even during outbreaks and with these priors. We identify three major failure modes: (1) poor outbreak anticipation, (2) difficulty handling sparsity and noise, and (3) limited utility of common geographic adjacency for epidemiological spatial information. We release benchmark data, code, and instructions at https://github.com/Rachel-Lyu/SpatialEpiBench to support development of operationally useful epidemic forecasting models.

</details>


### [455] [Ex Ante Evaluation of AI-Induced Idea Diversity Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06540)
*Nafis Saami Azad,Raiyan Abdul Baten*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a human-relative, model-only ex ante framework to benchmark AI-induced diversity crowding in creative outputs, yielding metrics Δ (excess crowding) and ρ (human-relative diversity ratio); finds frontier LLMs show crowding under parity across tasks; suggests design interventions and feasible data requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional evaluation prioritizes individual utility of AI outputs without accounting for population-level diversity loss when many artifacts become similar. There is a need for an ex ante, human-free protocol to estimate crowding risk before deployment.

Method: Model ideas as congestible resources and derive crowding metrics from within-distribution comparisons between model-only generations and matched unaided baselines. Define Δ and ρ to capture excess crowding and relative diversity. Connect to an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs. Demonstrate across short stories, slogans, and alternative-uses tasks using frontier LLMs.

Result: Crowding parity (ρ ≥ 1) is not achieved by three frontier LLMs across tested kernels; estimates stabilize with feasible sample sizes. Generation-protocol variants show targeted design can reduce crowding, indicating diversity collapse is actionable at development time.

Conclusion: Population-aware evaluation of creative AI is feasible ex ante; reducing crowding is achievable via protocol design, enabling safer and more diverse generative systems.

Abstract: Creative AI systems are typically evaluated at the level of individual utility, yet creative outputs are consumed in populations: an idea loses value when many others produce similar ones. This creates an evaluation blind spot, as AI can improve individual outputs while increasing population-level crowding. We introduce a human-relative framework for benchmarking AI-induced human diversity collapse without requiring human-AI interaction data, providing an ex ante protocol to estimate crowding risk from model-only generations and matched unaided human baselines. By modeling ideas as congestible resources, we show that source-level crowding is identifiable from within-distribution comparisons, yielding an excess-crowding coefficient $Δ$ and a human-relative diversity ratio $ρ$. We show that $ρ\ge1$ is the no-excess-crowding parity condition and connect $Δ$ to an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs. Across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks, three frontier LLMs fall below parity across crowding kernels. Estimates stabilize with feasible model-only sample sizes. Importantly, generation-protocol variants show that crowding can be reduced through targeted design, making diversity collapse an actionable, development-time evaluation target for population-aware creative AI.

</details>


### [456] [Improved techniques for fine-tuning flow models via adjoint matching: a deterministic control pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06583)
*Zhengyi Guo,Jiayuan Sheng,David D. Yao,Wenpin Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Deterministic adjoint matching for aligning flow-based generative models to human preferences via an optimal-control over velocity fields; introduces a truncated adjoint scheme for terminal-reward focus, and generalizes regularization beyond KL; shows gains in alignment and diversity on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve human preference alignment for flow-based generative models with stable training, computational efficiency, and flexible trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation.

Method: Formulate alignment as an optimal-control problem over velocity fields in continuous normalizing/flow-based models. Directly regress the control toward a value-gradient-induced target under the current policy. Introduce a truncated adjoint that concentrates computation on the terminal trajectory where reward signals are strongest. Generalize beyond KL-based regularization to allow flexible alignment-distribution trade-offs.

Result: Empirical results show consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics and substantial improvements in diversity and mode preservation on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B.

Conclusion: The framework provides stable training, computational savings via truncation, and flexible alignment with preserved diversity across tested models and datasets.

Abstract: We propose a deterministic adjoint matching framework that formulates human preference alignment for flow-based generative models as an optimal control problem over velocity fields. One can directly regress the control toward a value-gradient-induced target under the current policy, leading to a simple and stable training objective. Building on this perspective, we introduce a truncated adjoint scheme that focuses computation on the terminal portion of the trajectory, where reward-relevant signals concentrate, which yields substantial computational savings while preserving alignment quality. We further generalize the framework beyond standard KL-based regularization, allowing more flexible trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation. Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B demonstrate consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics, along with substantially improved diversity and mode preservation.

</details>


### [457] [NeuroAgent: LLM Agents for Multimodal Neuroimaging Analysis and Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06584)
*Lujia Zhong,Yihao Xia,Jianwei Zhang,Shuo huang,Jiaxin Yue,Mingyang Xia,Yonggang Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeuroAgent automates preprocessing and analysis of multimodal neuroimaging data via a hierarchical, LLM-driven agent architecture, enabling end-to-end, automated pipelines and improved disease classification performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce manual, modality-specific preprocessing and analysis barriers and improve reproducibility across heterogeneous neuroimaging toolchains.

Method: Hierarchical multi-agent Generate-Execute-Validate (GEV) loop; LLM backends; autonomous code generation; runtime error recovery; integrity validation; human-in-the-loop interface; evaluation on ADNI dataset across sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, Tau-PET; ablation across backends.

Result: High intent parsing accuracy (up to 100% for capable models); end-to-end preprocessing correctness up to 84.8% with Qwen3.5-27B; AD classification AUC 0.9518 with four modalities; outperforms single-modality baselines; automated recovery reduces manual intervention.

Conclusion: NeuroAgent reduces manual preprocessing effort and enables end-to-end automated multimodal neuroimaging analysis pipelines.

Abstract: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis often involves complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows that require careful configuration, quality control, and coordination across heterogeneous toolchains. Beyond preprocessing, downstream statistical analysis and disease classification commonly require task-specific code, evaluation protocols, and data-format conventions, creating additional barriers between raw acquisitions and reproducible scientific analysis. We present NeuroAgent, an LLM-driven agentic framework that automates key preprocessing and analysis steps for heterogeneous neuroimaging data, including sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, and PET, and supports interactive downstream analysis through natural-language queries. NeuroAgent employs a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine: agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect and recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity. We evaluate the system on 1,470 subjects pooled across all ADNI phases (CN=1,000, AD=470), where all subjects have sMRI and tabular data, with subsets also having Tau-PET (n=469), fMRI (n=278), and DTI ($n=620$). Pipeline ablation studies across multiple LLM backends show that capable models reach up to 100% intent-parsing accuracy, with the strongest backend (Qwen3.5-27B) reaching 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness. Automated recovery limits manual intervention to edge cases where human review is required via the Human-In-The-Loop interface. For Alzheimer's Disease classification using automatically preprocessed multimodal data, our agent ensemble achieves an AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines. These results show that NeuroAgent can reduce the manual effort required for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines for neuroimaging research.

</details>


### [458] [SkillOS: Learning Skill Curation for Self-Evolving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06614)
*Siru Ouyang,Jun Yan,Yanfei Chen,Rujun Han,Zifeng Wang,Bhavana Dalvi Mishra,Rui Meng,Chun-Liang Li,Yizhu Jiao,Kaiwen Zha,Maohao Shen,Vishy Tirumalashetty,George Lee,Jiawei Han,Tomas Pfister,Chen-Yu Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillOS is an experience-driven RL recipe to learn long-horizon skill curation for self-evolving agents, featuring a frozen skill-executor and a trainable SkillRepo updated by accumulated experience, guided by composite rewards and grouped task streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based agent systems struggle with long-term learning of reusable skills due to indirect/delayed feedback, manual curation bottlenecks, and short-horizon optimization. A learnable, scalable curation policy is needed to enable sustained self-evolution.

Method: A frozen agent executor retrieves/applies skills; a trainable skill curator updates an external SkillRepo from experience. Composite rewards provide learning signals; tasks are grouped by skill dependencies so earlier trajectories update the repo and later related tasks evaluate it. The approach is validated on multi-turn agentic and single-turn reasoning tasks, with cross-domain generalization across executors.

Result: SkillOS outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines in effectiveness and efficiency across task types. The learned curator yields more targeted skill use, and SkillRepo contents evolve into richly structured Markdown files encoding higher-level meta-skills.

Conclusion: Experience-driven skill curation can be learned to drive self-evolving agents, with a reusable curator that generalizes across executors and task domains and that produces interpretable, higher-level skill abstractions.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed to handle streaming tasks, yet they often remain one-off problem solvers that fail to learn from past interactions. Reusable skills distilled from experience provide a natural substrate for self-evolution, where high-quality skill curation serves as the key bottleneck. Existing approaches either rely on manual skill curation, prescribe heuristic skill operations, or train for short-horizon skill operations. However, they still struggle to learn complex long-term curation policies from indirect and delayed feedback. To tackle this challenge, we propose SkillOS, an experience-driven RL training recipe for learning skill curation in self-evolving agents. SkillOS pairs a frozen agent executor that retrieves and applies skills with a trainable skill curator that updates an external SkillRepo from accumulated experience. To provide learning signals for curation, we design composite rewards and train on grouped task streams based on skill-relevant task dependencies, where earlier trajectories update the SkillRepo, and later related tasks evaluate these updates. Across multi-turn agentic tasks and single-turn reasoning tasks, SkillOS consistently outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines in both effectiveness and efficiency, with the learned skill curator generalizing across different executor backbones and task domains. Further analyses show that the learned curator produces more targeted skill use, while the skills in SkillRepo evolve into more richly structured Markdown files that encode higher-level meta-skills over time.

</details>


### [459] [MASPO: Joint Prompt Optimization for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06623)
*Zhexuan Wang,Xuebo Liu,Li Wang,Zifei Shan,Yutong Wang,Zhenxi Song,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MASPO introduces an automatic, iterative framework for prompt optimization in LLM-based multi-agent systems by jointly evaluating prompts across agents to maximize downstream success, using a data-driven evolutionary beam search. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods across 6 tasks (avg. improvement ~2.9) and releases code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Local prompt optimization in multi-agent systems often misaligns with global system goals because agents optimize locally. A system-level, automatic method is needed to bridge local interactions and global outcomes without relying on ground-truth labels.

Method: MASPO employs a joint evaluation mechanism to assess prompts by their ability to facilitate downstream success for successor agents, not just local validity. It uses a data-driven evolutionary beam search to explore the high-dimensional prompt space and iteratively refine prompts across the entire system.

Result: MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods across 6 tasks, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9.

Conclusion: MASPO effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes in LLM-based MAS, enabling automatic, scalable prompt optimization without ground-truth labels; code is released.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based Multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown promise in tackling complex collaborative tasks, where agents are typically orchestrated via role-specific prompts. While the quality of these prompts is pivotal, jointly optimizing them across interacting agents remains a non-trivial challenge, primarily due to the misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals. To address this, we introduce MASPO, a novel framework designed to automatically and iteratively refine prompts across the entire system. A core innovation of MASPO is its joint evaluation mechanism, which assesses prompts not merely by their local validity, but by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents. This effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes without relying on ground-truth labels. Furthermore, MASPO employs a data-driven evolutionary beam search to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional prompt space. Extensive empirical evaluations across 6 diverse tasks demonstrate that MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9. We release our code at https://github.com/wangzx1219/MASPO.

</details>


### [460] [GlazyBench: A Benchmark for Ceramic Glaze Property Prediction and Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06641)
*Ziyu Zhai,Siyou Li,Juexi Shao,Juntao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GlazyBench: a large-scale glaze dataset (23,148 formulations) enabling AI-driven glaze property prediction and image generation; establishes baselines and benchmarks for a new field.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of large-scale, domain-specific datasets hampers AI-assisted glaze design; reducing costly trial-and-error and enabling multimodal AI.

Method: Assembles 23,148 real glaze formulations; two tasks: predict post-firing surface properties from raw materials; generate visual glaze representations from predicted properties; evaluate with traditional ML models and LLMs for properties; evaluate image generation with deep generative and multimodal models; provides standard benchmarks.

Result: Baselines show promise but challenges remain; dataset enables systematic evaluation; highlights gaps in modeling glaze chemistry and rendering.

Conclusion: Paves a new research direction; provides standardized benchmark; will accelerate AI-assisted material design in ceramics.

Abstract: Developing ceramic glazes is a costly, time-consuming process of trial and error due to complex chemistry, placing a significant burden on independent artists. While recent advances in multimodal AI offer a modern solution, the field lacks the large-scale datasets required to train these models. We propose GlazyBench, the first dataset for AI-assisted glaze design. Comprising 23,148 real glaze formulations, GlazyBench supports two primary tasks: predicting post-firing surface properties, such as color and transparency, from raw materials, and generating accurate visual representations of the glaze based on these properties. We establish comprehensive baselines for property prediction using traditional machine learning and large language models, alongside image generation benchmarks using deep generative and large multimodal models. Our experiments demonstrate promising yet challenging results. GlazyBench pioneers a new research direction in AI-assisted material design, providing a standardized benchmark for systematic evaluation.

</details>


### [461] [Can RL Teach Long-Horizon Reasoning to LLMs? Expressiveness Is Key](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06638)
*Tianle Wang,Zhaoyang Wang,Guangchen Lan,Xinpeng Wei,Sipeng Zhang,Guanwen Qiu,Abulhair Saparov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ScaleLogic provides a controllable RL environment to study how training compute scales with reasoning depth and logical expressiveness; reveals a power-law compute scaling with depth with exponent rising with expressiveness; more expressive training yields better downstream transfer and compute efficiency; scaling law holds across RL methods and curricula.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no systematic, scalable environment to study RL scaling for LLM reasoning; need to understand how task difficulty affects training compute and transfer.

Method: Introduce ScaleLogic with two axes: horizon (D) and logic expressiveness (implication-only to full first-order with and, or, not, for all). Evaluate RL training compute T vs D (T ∝ D^γ, γ depends on expressiveness). Measure over multiple RL methods; test curriculum-based training; evaluate on downstream math and general reasoning benchmarks.

Result: Found T ∝ D^γ with R^2 > 0.99; γ increases from 1.04 to 2.60 as expressiveness grows; downstream gains up to +10.66 points and improved transfer efficiency with more expressive training; power-law holds across methods; curriculum improves scaling efficiency.

Conclusion: Training on more expressive logical tasks shapes downstream transfer and scaling behavior; both training quantity and task design matter; curriculum-based strategies can boost scaling efficiency; findings generalize across RL methods.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied to improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet the systematic study of how training scales with task difficulty has been hampered by the lack of controlled, scalable environments. We introduce ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning framework that offers independent control over two axes of difficulty: the depth of the required proof planning (i.e., the horizon) and the expressiveness of the underlying logic. Our proposed framework supports a wide range of logics: from simple implication-only logic ("if-then") towards more expressive first-order reasoning with conjunction ("and"), disjunction ("or"), negation ("not"), and universal quantification ("for all"). Using this framework, we show that the RL training compute $T$ follows a power law with respect to reasoning depth $D$ ($T \propto D^γ$, $R^{2} > 0.99$), and that the scaling exponent $γ$ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness, from $1.04$ to $2.60$. On downstream mathematics and general reasoning benchmarks, more expressive training settings yield both larger performance gains (up to $+10.66$ points) and more compute-efficient transfer compared to less expressive settings, demonstrating that what a model is trained on, not just how much it is trained, shapes downstream transfer. We further show that the power-law relationship holds across multiple RL methods, and curriculum-based training substantially improves scaling efficiency.

</details>


### [462] [AI Co-Mathematician: Accelerating Mathematicians with Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06651)
*Daniel Zheng,Ingrid von Glehn,Yori Zwols,Iuliya Beloshapka,Lars Buesing,Daniel M. Roy,Martin Wattenberg,Bogdan Georgiev,Tatiana Schmidt,Andrew Cowie,Fernanda Viegas,Dimitri Kanevsky,Vineet Kahlon,Hartmut Maennel,Sophia Alj,George Holland,Alex Davies,Pushmeet Kohli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for an interactive AI co-mathematician that supports end-to-end mathematical research via an asynchronous, stateful workspace, achieving notable benchmarks and aiding open problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To accelerate mathematical discovery by modeling collaborative human workflows and managing uncertainty, hypotheses, and literature integration within AI-assisted research.

Method: Build a workbench that orchestrates AI agents for ideation, literature search, computation, theorem proving, and theory building; provides asynchrony, statefulness, hypothesis tracking, and native mathematical artifacts; empirical tests include solving open problems and benchmarking on FrontierMath Tier 4.

Result: Researchers reported solving open problems, discovering new directions, and finding overlooked literature; the system achieved a 48% score on FrontierMath Tier 4, the highest among evaluated AI systems.

Conclusion: The co-mathematician demonstrates a viable, scalable platform for AI-assisted mathematical exploration with strong empirical performance and potential to reshape research workflows, though claims will require replication and broader testing.

Abstract: We introduce the AI co-mathematician, a workbench for mathematicians to interactively leverage AI agents to pursue open-ended research. The AI co-mathematician is optimized to provide holistic support for the exploratory and iterative reality of mathematical workflows, including ideation, literature search, computational exploration, theorem proving and theory building. By providing an asynchronous, stateful workspace that manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, the system mirrors human collaborative workflows. In early tests, the AI co-mathematician helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. Besides demonstrating a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery, the AI co-mathematician also achieves state of the art results on hard problem-solving benchmarks, including scoring 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high score among all AI systems evaluated.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [463] [A GPU-Accelerated Hybrid Method for a Class of Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05208)
*Zhenyu Lei,Jin-Kao Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid metaheuristic for multi-depot VRPs that fuses a learning-driven, diversity-controlled crossover with a multi-depot search guided by a multi-penalty evaluation; enhanced by tensor-based GPU acceleration and a multi-move update scheme, achieving state-of-the-art performance on three MDVRP variants, especially on large instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDVRPs are central to multi-depot logistics but are computationally hard due to combinatorial explosion; achieving scalable, high-quality solutions requires advanced search mechanisms that exploit depot structure and parallel computation.

Method: A two-layered algorithm: (1) a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover to generate diverse high-quality offspring; (2) a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search framework guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function; two depot-focused local search operators to enhance exploration/exploitation in depot-aware contexts; an enhanced GPU-accelerated version employing tensor-based computation and a novel multi-move update strategy.

Result: Computational experiments on benchmark instances across three MDVRP variants show the proposed methods are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, with pronounced advantages for large-scale instances in both solution quality and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The integrated learning-driven crossover with penalty-guided and depot-aware search constitutes an effective, scalable solution framework for MDVRPs, and GPU acceleration with a multi-move strategy further enhances scalability, enabling competitive performance on large problems.

Abstract: Multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRPs) are prevalent in a variety of practical applications. However, they are computationally challenging to solve due to their inherent complexity. This paper proposes an effective hybrid algorithm for a class of MDVRPs. The algorithm integrates a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover and a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search framework guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function. Two dedicated depot-related local search operators are incorporated to further strengthen the search capability in multi-depot settings. To improve computational efficiency and scalability, an enhanced version of the algorithm is developed that uses a tensor-based GPU acceleration combined with a novel multi-move update strategy. Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show that the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, especially for large-scale instances.

</details>


### [464] [Topology-Driven Anti-Entanglement Control for Soft Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05236)
*Haoyang Le,Shengxuan Wang,Mohan Chen,Shuo Feng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Topology-driven MARL (TD-MARL) enhances anti-winding coordination for multi-robot unwinding in constrained environments, outperforming advanced DRL in convergence and entanglement suppression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackling observability challenges and entanglement risk in multi-robot soft-robot coordination under high-density obstacles; need robust, scalable learning with reduced inter-robot communication.

Method: A topology-driven MARL framework with a centralized-critic network that shares topological state among agents, distributed execution to minimize comms, and an integrated topological security layer using topological invariants to mitigate entanglement; evaluated in a realistic simulation environment.

Result: TD-MARL converges faster and achieves stronger anti-winding performance than state-of-the-art DRL baselines in simulation.

Conclusion: TD-MARL offers robust multi-robot unwinding control in constrained settings by leveraging topology awareness and invariants, reducing communication requirements while improving stability and safety against entanglement.

Abstract: In the field of precision manufacturing in complex constrained environments, the role of soft robots is increasingly prominent, and the realization of anti-winding control based on multi-intelligent body reinforcement learning has become a research hotspot. One of the core problems at present is to coordinate multiple robots to complete the unwinding operation in a highly constrained environment. The existing distributed training framework faces some observability challenges in high-density barrier and unstable environments, resulting in poor learning results. This paper proposes a topology-driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (TD-MARL) framework to coordinate multi-robot systems to avoid entanglement. Specifically, the critical network adopts centralized learning, so that each intelligent body can perceive the strategies of other intelligent bodies by sharing the topological state, thus alleviating the training instability caused by complex interactions; eliminating the demand for communication resources between robots through distributed execution, Upgrade system reliability; the integrated topological security layer uses topological invariants to accurately assess and mitigate the risk of entanglement to avoid the strategy from falling into local difficulties. Finally, the full simulation experiments carried out in the real simulation environment show that the method is better than the current advanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method in terms of convergence and anti-winding effect.

</details>


### [465] [DexSim2Real: Foundation Model-Guided Sim-to-Real Transfer for Generalizable Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05241)
*Zijian Zeng,Fei Ding,Huiming Yang,Xianwei Li,Yuhao Liao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSim2Real introduces a unified sim-to-real framework for dexterous manipulation that combines foundation-model-guided domain randomization (FM-DR), tactile-visual cross-attention policy (TVCAP), and a progressive skill curriculum (PSC) to achieve strong real-world performance (78.2% average) and narrow the sim-to-real gap to 8.3%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation is a major bottleneck, with existing approaches relying on manually designed domain randomization or task-specific adaptations that lack generalization across diverse tasks.

Method: Three components: (1) FM-DR uses a vision-language foundation model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based DR methods; (2) TVCAP applies cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion for zero-shot sim-to-real RL; (3) PSC builds an LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks. Evaluation on six manipulation tasks with blinded tests.

Result: Achieves 78.2% average real-world success across six tasks, outperforms DrEureka and DeXtreme, and reduces the sim-to-real performance gap to 8.3%.

Conclusion: DexSim2Real demonstrates that integrating foundation-model guidance, multimodal perception, and curriculum-based task progression yields stronger, more generalizable sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation than prior methods.

Abstract: Sim-to-real transfer remains a critical bottleneck for deploying dexterous manipulation policies learned in simulation to real-world robots. Existing approaches rely on manually designed domain randomization or task-specific adaptation, limiting their generalizability across diverse manipulation scenarios. We present DexSim2Real, an integrated framework that leverages vision-language foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap for dexterous manipulation. Our system combines three components: (1) Foundation Model-Guided Domain Randomization (FM-DR), which uses a vision-language model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches like DrEureka with direct visual feedback; (2) a Tactile-Visual Cross-Attention Policy (TVCAP) that adapts cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion to zero-shot sim-to-real RL; and (3) a Progressive Skill Curriculum (PSC) that builds on LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks. Extensive experiments on six challenging manipulation tasks with blinded evaluation demonstrate that DexSim2Real achieves a 78.2% average real-world success rate, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme while reducing the sim-to-real performance gap to only 8.3%.

</details>


### [466] [Track A*: Fast Visibility-Aware Trajectory Planning for Active Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05338)
*Hanxuan Chen,Kangli Wang,Ji Pei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TA star is an offline, beam-pruned DAG-based trajectory planner for visibility-aware target tracking on a 4D (x, y, z, t) grid; it delivers substantial speedups and robust convergence with near-baseline visibility, at the cost of strict optimality and with limitations in dense vegetation scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline reference trajectories are needed for constructing multi-modal tracking datasets and benchmarking online tracking planners under repeatable conditions.

Method: TA star formulates planning on a discretized four-dimensional spatio-temporal grid using a layered Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search. It employs cross-time obstacle distance caching via a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. A beam-pruned heuristic search balances solution quality with scalability, trading strict optimality for practical performance.

Result: In a 1000-scenario stress test over eight CARLA maps, TA star converges in 45 s using 32 workers. Compared to an unoptimized priority-queue A* baseline (BinaryHeap) with the same inputs and a 5×10^6 expansion cap, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0× and worst-case planning time by 11.8×, while increasing convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 converged subset, TA star changes average visibility by -0.15 percentage points (pp), with no scenario dropping by more than 5 pp. The authors present TA star as a practical offline reference planner under these conditions, and discuss limitations and failure cases for environments such as Town07 with dense vegetation.

Conclusion: TA star provides a robust, scalable offline reference planner with substantial speedups and near-baseline visibility performance, suitable for benchmarking, while acknowledging limitations in dense vegetation and other challenging environments that motivate future improvements.

Abstract: Offline reference trajectories for active target tracking are needed both for building multi-modal tracking datasets and for benchmarking online tracking planners under repeatable conditions. We present Track A star (TA star), an offline search-based trajectory planner that targets the visibility-aware target tracking objective on a discretized four-dimensional spatio-temporal grid (x, y, z, t). TA star combines a layered Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search with three engineering optimizations: cross-time obstacle distance caching against a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. TA star employs a beam-pruned heuristic search on this discrete graph to efficiently find high-quality tracking trajectories. While it trades strict theoretical optimality for practical scalability, our empirical results demonstrate robust, near-baseline visibility performance at a fraction of the computational cost. On a 1000-scenario stress test across eight CARLA Optimized maps, TA star converges on all scenarios and completes in 45 s using 32 workers; on a 248-scenario controlled comparison against an unoptimized priority-queue A star baseline (BinaryHeap implementation) under identical scenario inputs and a 5 x 10^6 expansion cap, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0x and worst-case planning time by 11.8x, while raising convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 baseline-converged subset, TA star changes average visibility by only -0.15 percentage points (pp), with no scenario exceeding a 5 pp drop. We position TA star as a practical offline reference planner under these specific conditions, with limitations and failure cases discussed for environments such as Town07 dense vegetation.

</details>


### [467] [Passive Fault Tolerance through Tension-to-Thrust Feed-Forward: Hybrid Input-to-State Stability for Decentralized Multi-UAV Slung-Load Transport under Abrupt Cable Severance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05339)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Paul Schmitt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A passive, decentralized fault-tolerant controller for multi-UAV slung-loads maintains stability after abrupt cable severance by feeding measured tension directly into altitude thrust, aided by a PD anti-swing cascade and a projection mechanism; it offers a conditional hybrid ISS-style certificate and shows strong fault-recovery performance in simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load systems, addressing limitations of coordination-based or fixed-topology controllers.

Method: A passive architecture routes each vehicle’s measured cable tension into its altitude thrust command (T_i^{ff}=T_i) and uses a surrounding PD anti-swing and projection cascade to preserve local tracking feasibility. The main theoretical contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate combining slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction ρ in (0,1) into a recovery envelope under actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions.

Result: Drake multibody simulations with five vehicles, 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and severance scenarios yield closed-loop RMSE ~0.312–0.328 m and peak sag ~76–95 mm, with recovery within one payload-pendulum period. Turning off the identity inflates cruise error by ~34–39% and peak sag by ~3.6–4.0×, indicating local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism.

Conclusion: Local tension feed-forward is the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the tested decentralized cascade, and the proposed architecture provides a certified-like decentralized recovery pathway for unannounced severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport.

Abstract: Abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport redistributes load and changes the active constraint set, leaving limited time for fault diagnosis and reconfiguration. Existing controllers rely on coordinated force allocation, peer-state exchange, or fixed cable topology, and therefore lack a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced severance. We present a passive architecture that routes each vehicle's measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command, $T_i^{\mathrm{ff}}=T_i$, while a surrounding proportional-derivative, anti-swing, and projection cascade preserves local tracking feasibility. The main contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate that composes a slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction $ρ\in (0,1)$ into an explicit recovery envelope under stated actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions. We validate the controller in Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, a 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and single- and dual-severance schedules: the closed loop attains 0.312-0.328 m RMSE, 76.1-95.2 mm peak sag, and recovery within one payload-pendulum period. Disabling the identity inflates cruise error by 34-39% and peak sag by 3.6x-4.0x, identifying local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the tested decentralized cascade.

</details>


### [468] [Creative Robot Tool Use by Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05411)
*M. Tuluhan Akbulut,Varun Satheesh,Ahmed Jaafar,Alper Ahmetoglu,Shane Parr,Aditya Ganeshan,Shivam Vats,George Konidaris*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use grounded in physics, using VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation in a dynamics model; transfers tool use via keypoint matching with causal features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, creative tool use beyond primary objectives by discovering and grounding causal relationships between tools, tasks, and physical properties.

Method: Use simulated dynamics to discover causality; decouple into VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation through targeted geometric/physical perturbations; classify novel objects by causal features; transfer tool-use skill via keypoint matching conditioned on causal features; reconstruct task dynamics to ground physics.

Result: Baseline comparisons indicate that identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties improves tool selection reliability and strengthens skill keypoint transfer.

Conclusion: Identifying causal features and grounding them in tool physics enhances reliable, transferable tool use for diverse tasks.

Abstract: We propose a causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use where a suitable tool for a task is correctly identified for use beyond its primary objectives. The proposed framework first discovers the causal relationships between the tool and the task by conducting simulated experiments in a dynamics model. We decouple the causal discovery problem into two complementary components: VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric and physical feature perturbations. Then, novel objects are classified based on identified causal features, and the tool use skill is transferred via keypoint matching conditioned on the identified causal features. By reconstructing the task in a dynamics model, our approach grounds tool use in the physics of the problem. We illustrate our approach in reaching a distant object with different sticks, scooping candies from a bowl using diverse items, and using different boxes or crates as stepping platforms to retrieve an object from a high shelf. Our baseline comparisons show that identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties leads to more reliable tool selection and stronger skill keypoint transfer.

</details>


### [469] [Contact-Free Grasp Stability Prediction with In-Hand Time-of-Flight Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05461)
*Kyle DuFrene,Cindy Grimm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors on a robotic gripper, enabling stability classification without grasping. It cycles at 15 Hz and was trained on 2,500 real grasps over 15 objects, with evaluation on 6 unseen objects; achieved ~85.5–86.0% accuracy on validation/test sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce dependence on contact-based, tactile stability checks; speed up grasp planning and improve robustness to sensor noise by predicting stability before executing a grasp.

Method: Place multi-zone ToF sensors on distal gripper links to gather non-contact depth/zone information. Train a classifier on real-world grasps, with data from 15 objects (2,500 grasps) and evaluate on 6 unseen objects (3 validation, 3 test). Achieves 15 Hz prediction cycle.

Result: Classifier achieved 85.5% accuracy on validation objects and 86.0% accuracy on test objects, indicating strong performance for non-contact stability prediction and real-time capability.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of contact-free stability prediction using distributed ToF sensing, offering faster stability judgments and generalization to unseen objects; potential to accelerate grasp planning pipelines.

Abstract: Current approaches to grasp planning for robotics demonstrate high success rates, but degrade with noisy sensors and other factors. Previous works have proposed tactile-based grasp stability classifiers to detect failures, but these approaches rely on making contact and grasping the object to do so. We propose a contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors mounted in the distal links of a gripper. Our method, as it does not require grasping the object to make a prediction, significantly speeds up the stability classification process, cycling at 15 Hz. We collected over 2,500 real-world grasps across 15 objects to train a classifier. Additionally, we conducted grasp attempts over six additional unseen objects, three for validation and model selection, and three for model testing. Our approach demonstrated strong classification performance, with an accuracy of 85.5% on validation and 86.0% on test objects.

</details>


### [470] [Robust $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Controller Design For INDI-Controlled Quadrotor Using Online Parameter Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05483)
*Tom Aantjes,Till M. Blaha,Spilios Theodoulis,Ewoud J. J. Smeur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Robust gain-scheduled attitude control for quadrotor using an INDI inner loop with online parameter identification and H-infinity shaping, achieving good stability margins and acceptable real-world performance in simulation and flight, even with parameter drift; works for actuator time constants under 40 ms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a robust method to tune outer-loop gains for INDI-based inner-loop attitude control under parameter uncertainty, by leveraging online system identification and H∞ closed-loop shaping.

Method: Design of a gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with a feedforward filter; INDI-based inner loop; online identification of system parameters; signal-based H∞ closed-loop shaping for a symmetric quadrotor.

Result: Good stability margins; nonlinear simulations show effective tracking under uncertainty; flight tests with full online parameter identification show acceptable performance even when identified parameters exceed assumed uncertainty; performance comparable to simulations for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

Conclusion: The proposed approach yields a robust gain-scheduled attitude control framework for INDI-based quadrotor control, validated by simulation and flight experiments. It demonstrates tolerance to parameter variations and highlights the importance of online identification and actuator bandwidth for practical robustness.

Abstract: It has recently been shown that all physical parameters of an Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller can be estimated onboard a multirotor within half a second, which is fast enough to do the full identification during a throw in the air. However, a robust method to tune outer loop gains for this feedback-linearizing INDI controller depending on the model parameters is still missing. This work presents the design of a robust gain-scheduled controller for attitude control of quadrotor, using an INDI-based inner loop with online identification of its system parameters. A gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with a feedforward filter is synthesized for a symmetric quadrotor using signal-based $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ closed-loop shaping. The resulting controller exhibits good stability margins, with nonlinear simulations confirming effective tracking performance under uncertainty. Experimental evaluation is also conducted through flight tests with full online parameter identification. Even though the identified parameters during these tests are far outside the defined uncertainty range, acceptable flight performance comparable to simulation results is maintained for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

</details>


### [471] [Real-world Latency Analysis of Vehicular Visible Light Communication with Multiple LED Transmitters and an Event-Based Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05541)
*Ryota Soga,Tsukasa Shimizu,Shintaro Shiba,Quan Kong,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An event-camera-based VLC system for V2X addressing bandwidth, multi-transmitter reception, and latency. It uses positive-event-only mode, a protocol to suppress events while preserving range/FOV, methods to identify multiple transmitters, and demonstrates simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs with end-to-end latency suitable for cooperative perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage the high temporal resolution and low latency of event cameras to enable VLC in V2X while mitigating bandwidth constraints and enabling multi-transmitter reception in dynamic road scenes.

Method: Adopt positive-event-only mode; design a protocol to suppress event generation without sacrificing communication distance or field of view; develop a method to identify and separate signals from multiple LEDs; demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs; evaluate end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios.

Result: Demonstrates simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs and end-to-end latency within cooperative perception requirements; supports event-camera-based VLC as a feasible complement to RF-based V2X.

Conclusion: Event-camera-based VLC shows promise as a low-latency, wide-FOV complement to RF V2X, capable of supporting multi-transmitter reception and maintaining communication distance, with latency suitable for cooperative perception—subject to further validation across diverse conditions.

Abstract: Event cameras offer high temporal resolution, low latency, and wide dynamic range, making them promising receivers for visible light communication (VLC) in vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications. This work presents an event-camera-based VLC system addressing three key challenges: bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter reception, and latency characterization.
  We adopt a positive-event-only mode and design a protocol that suppresses event generation while maintaining communication distance and a wide field of view. We also propose a method to identify multiple transmitters and demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs. Finally, we evaluate end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios and show that the system meets cooperative perception requirements. These results demonstrate that event-camera-based VLC is a feasible complement to existing V2X technologies (e.g., RF).

</details>


### [472] [On the Emergence of Pendular Structure in Multi-Contact Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05707)
*Lingxue Lyu,Zihui Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A centroidal optimization targeting angular-momentum-rate yields LIPM-like pendular forces as the optimal pattern; the pendular rate is set by the SVD of the moment Jacobian and foot-span geometry, matching experiments within 16%. With two-contact (trotting) stances, the friction cone enforces a non-removable lower bound on the angular-momentum-rate and creates a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration. Introducing a task term for nonzero angular-momentum-rate shifts the optimum away from the pendular set in a predictable way. The results align with the ZMP/DCM view, and are demonstrated on a point-mass quadruped and the Unitree Go1 in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and torque-controlled loop); the asymptotic picture has limits for closed-loop behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reveal how the LIPM-like behavior commonly used in legged locomotion emerges from an optimization criterion focused on minimizing the rate of change of angular momentum, and to understand how stance geometry and contact constraints shape the optimal pattern, thereby connecting LIPM, ZMP, and DCM perspectives.

Method: Derive and analyze a small centroidal optimal control problem (OCP) that penalizes the rate of angular momentum. Compare full-rank stance vs N=2 stance. Derive a closed-form expression for a non-smooth kink at a critical horizontal acceleration. Introduce a task term forcing a nonzero angular-momentum-rate and observe the shift. Validate insights through open-loop QP and torque-loop simulations on a point-mass quadruped and Unitree Go1 in MuJoCo.

Result: Key findings include: (1) with full-rank stance, the optimal force pattern converges to a pendular type, with rate governed by the SVD of the moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches experiments within ~16%; (2) with N=2 stance, the friction cone imposes a lower bound on the angular-momentum-rate that cannot be removed by weight tuning, and there is a closed-form, non-smooth kink at a critical horizontal acceleration; (3) adding a task term for nonzero angular-momentum-rate moves the optimum away from the pendular set in a predictable way; (4) all results are consistent with the ZMP/DCM framework; (5) validation on a point-mass quadruped and Unitree Go1 in MuJoCo shows where the asymptotic model ceases to describe the closed-loop behavior.

Conclusion: The analysis reinforces that LIPM-like pendular behavior can arise naturally from an angular-momentum-rate minimization within centroidal optics, and that the classical ZMP/DCM view remains a relevant lens. However, contact constraints (friction, multi-contact stances) and explicit task terms can push the optimal behavior away from pure pendulum dynamics, especially in closed-loop implementations; practical validation on robotic platforms confirms the theory while highlighting limits in the asymptotic regime.

Abstract: LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a modeling choice rather than as something the
  controller's cost actually prefers. This note tries to make that link more explicit. Working from a small centroidal
  OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like. Three things come
  out. With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at a rate determined by the SVD of the
  moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within 16%. With N=2 stance,
  as in trot, the friction cone introduces a lower bound on $\|\dot{H}_G\|$ that no amount of weight tuning fixes; we
  also see a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration that we can write in closed form. Adding
  a task term that asks for a nonzero $\dot{H}_G$ moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable way. None of
  this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture. We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1
  in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we note where the asymptotic story stops being
  a good description of what the closed loop actually does.

</details>


### [473] [MaMi-HOI: Harmonizing Global Kinematics and Local Geometry for Human-Object Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05756)
*Hao Wang,Shiqi Wang,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MaMi-HOI proposes a hierarchical framework for 3D HOI generation that combats geometric forgetting in diffusion models by using: (1) GAPA to re-inject dense object geometry for precise contact (residual snapping) and (2) KHA to align whole-body kinematics with spatial goals, enabling natural motion with precise contact and long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based HOI generation often excels at high-level semantic alignment but loses precise object contact due to depth and feature entanglement—the phenomenon termed geometric forgetting. This degrades manipulation realism and contact fidelity.

Method: MaMi-HOI combines macro-level kinematic fluidity with micro-level spatial precision in a hierarchical framework. GAPA (Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter) injects dense object details to recover precise contact via residual snapping corrections. KHA (Kinematic Harmony Adapter) aligns full-body posture with spatial constraints to preserve naturalness and global dynamics. The approach aims to prevent local over-enforcement from breaking global motion, and to enable long-term, complex trajectories in 3D scenes.

Result: Extensive experiments validate that MaMi-HOI achieves both natural motion and precise object contact, and extends generation to long-term tasks with complex trajectories, bridging global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D environments. Code is released.

Conclusion: A two-tiered architecture—GAPA for local geometry-aware contact refinement and KHA for global kinematic harmony—successfully reconciles macro and micro aspects of HOI generation, mitigating geometric forgetting and enabling robust, realistic 3D interactions.

Abstract: Generating realistic 3D Human-Object Interactions (HOI) is a fundamental task for applications ranging from embodied AI to virtual content creation, which requires harmonizing high-level semantic intent with strict low-level physical constraints. Existing methods excel at semantic alignment, however, they struggle to maintain precise object contact. We reveal a key finding termed \textit{Geometric Forgetting}: as diffusion model depth increases, semantic feature tend to overshadow object geometry feature, causing the model to lose its perception to object geometry. To address this, we propose MaMi-HOI, a hierarchical framework reconciling \textbf{Ma}cro-level kinematic fluidity with \textbf{Mi}cro-level spatial precision. First, to counteract geometric forgetting, we introduce the Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA), which explicitly re-injects dense object details to perform residual snapping corrections for precise contact. Nevertheless, such aggressive local enforcement can disrupt global dynamics, leading to robotic stiffness. In response, we introduce the Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA), which proactively aligns whole-body posture with spatial objectives, ensuring the skeleton actively accommodates constraints without compromising naturalness. Extensive experiments validate that MaMi-HOI simultaneously achieves natural motion and precise contact. Crucially, it extends generation capabilities to long-term tasks with complex trajectories, effectively bridging the gap between global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes. Code is available at https://github.com/DON738110198/MaMi-HOI.git

</details>


### [474] [Resource-Constrained Robotic Planning in the face of Mixed Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05797)
*Yihao Yin,Pian Yu,Andrea Turrini,Zhiming Chi,Yong Li,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes robust strategy synthesis for robots under uncertain dynamics and resource constraints by modeling as CMDPST and combining with LTLf specifications; offers direct unrolling and pruning-based optimization methods, validated on a warehouse network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face quantifiable and unquantifiable uncertainty and strict resource limits; need strategies that maximize task satisfaction probability (LTLf) while never depleting resources; existing methods struggle with nondeterminism and resource constraints.

Method: Introduce Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions (CMDPST) to capture nondeterminism, stochastic/unquantified uncertainty, and consumption; form product with LTLf; propose two synthesis algorithms: (i) direct unrolling-based method, (ii) optimized method using state-space pruning; evaluate on warehouse transportation network.

Result: Two solution methods effectively synthesize robust strategies; the pruning-based method improves efficiency; experiments demonstrate the ability to satisfy LTLf objectives under resource constraints in a warehouse scenario.

Conclusion: CMDPST provides a unified modeling framework for robust, resource-aware control; combining CMDPST with LTLf allows automated strategy synthesis with guarantees on avoiding resource exhaustion; the optimized method offers scalable performance; applicable to robotics with uncertainty and resource limits.

Abstract: Robots operate under significant uncertainty, from quantifiable noise to unquantifiable unknowns, and must account for strict operational constraints, such as limited resources. In this paper, we consider the problem of synthesizing robust strategies to guide a robot's actions in fulfilling a given task, while ensuring the system never exhausts its resources. To solve this problem, we first model the robotic system as a Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions(CMDPST), a unified framework modelling nondeterministic actions, quantifiable and unquantifiable uncertainty, and resource consumption. Then, we combine the CMDPST with the task specification, expressed as a Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf ) formula. Lastly, we address the resource constrained optimal robust strategy synthesis problem, which aims to synthesize a strategy that maximizes the probability of satisfying the LTLf objective without resource exhaustion. Our solution involves two techniques: a direct unrolling-based method and a more efficient, optimized approach that leverages state-space pruning for better performance. Experiments on a warehouse transportation network show the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

</details>


### [475] [A Comparative Study of INDI and NDI with Nonlinear Disturbance Observer for Aerial Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05825)
*Benedetta Rota,Mirko Mizzoni,Amr Afifi,Pieter van Goor,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: INDI shows greater robustness to model mismatch and combined disturbances in simulation, while NDI+NDO matches nominal performance but is more sensitive to non-ideal conditions; offers practical guidance on choosing between incremental and observer-based inversion for fully actuated aerial robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically compare the robustness of two inversion strategies (INDI vs NDI+NDO) for fully actuated aerial robots to inform controller selection under uncertainties and disturbances.

Method: A simulation-based campaign across representative operating scenarios, evaluating tracking performance, robustness, and control effort under parametric variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise.

Result: INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in model-mismatch and combined-stress scenarios; NDI+NDO largely matches nominal performance but is more sensitive under several non-ideal conditions.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental (INDI) and observer-based (NDI+NDO) inversion strategies for aerial robotics, suggesting when each is advantageous and highlighting areas for further validation and tuning.

Abstract: This work presents a simulation-based comparative robustness analysis of Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) and Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion augmented with a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDI+NDO) for fully actuated aerial robots. A systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios is conducted, where we compare tracking performance, robustness, control effort, under parametric variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise. Results show that INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases, while NDI+NDO primarily matches nominal performance but exhibits greater sensitivity under several non-ideal conditions. These findings provide practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental and observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotic applications.

</details>


### [476] [Cycle-resolved Cephalopod-Inspired Pulsed-Jet Robot With High-Volume Expulsion and Drag-Reduced Gliding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05875)
*Yiyuan Zhang,Anye Zhong,Junkai Chen,Wenci Xin,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot uses a rigid-soft origami mantle to achieve large, actively driven body deformation, enabling substantial volume reduction and drag reduction; a cycle-resolved framework decouples expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway to study expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is inherently cycle-based, involving expulsion, passive glide, and mantle refilling. The work aims to (i) implement a robotic platform that mimics this cycle with geometric control, and (ii) establish an experimental framework to analyze how cycle-stage variables govern whole-cycle performance.

Method: Design of a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle combining rigid folding panels with a silicone compliant framework, enabling ~75% cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and ~75.7% drag-area reduction in contracted glide. Integration of mantle-aperture-inspired passive inlet valves for refill. A cycle-resolved experimental framework to independently vary expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway and measure their effects on locomotion.

Result: Peak speed ~0.5 m/s (3.8 body-lengths/s); average speed >0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. High expelled-volume contraction enhances speed; reduced-drag-area gliding is effective across glide durations; passive inlet valves assist refill.

Conclusion: The study delivers a functional cephalopod-like pulsed-jet robot with actively deformable mantles and provides a unified platform to investigate expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics, advancing both bio-inspired propulsion hardware and cycle-resolved locomotion analysis.

Abstract: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is not a single isolated expulsion event, but a coordinated cycle involving jet expulsion, passive gliding, and mantle refilling. Inspired by this cycle-resolved biological strategy, this paper presents a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle that enables large, actively driven, and geometry-guided body deformation. The proposed mantle integrates rigid folding panels with a compliant silicone framework, allowing a 75% effective cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and reducing the projected cross-sectional drag area by approximately 75.7% in the contracted gliding configuration. Using this platform, we formulate a cycle-resolved framework to separately investigate how expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway influence whole-cycle locomotion performance. Experiments show that the robot reaches a peak speed of approximately 0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and an average speed exceeding 0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. The results further demonstrate the roles of high expelled-volume-ratio contraction in speed generation, reduced-drag-area gliding under different glide durations, and mantle-aperture-inspired passive inlet valves in assisting refill. This work provides both a robotic implementation of actively deformable cephalopod-like jet propulsion and a unified experimental platform for studying expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics in pulsed-jet locomotion.

</details>


### [477] [Generating Roadside LiDAR Datasets from Vehicle-Side Datasets via Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05897)
*Yuhan Xia,Runxin Zhao,Hanyang Zhuang,Chunxiang Wang,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-synthesis framework (VRS) converts vehicle-side LiDAR data into labeled roadside LiDAR data via cross-view synthesis, addressing view and data gaps with vehicle point cloud completion and occupancy-based visibility constraints, enabling multi-view data generation and improving roadside 3D object detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR data and the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap hinder robust roadside perception; need scalable data to improve generalization to new viewpoints.

Method: VRS pipeline: (1) vehicle-side LiDAR capture; (2) perform vehicle point cloud completion to recover missing geometry; (3) cross-view LiDAR rendering to synthesize roadside viewpoints; (4) apply occupancy-based visibility constraints to cope with large viewpoint changes; (5) enable flexible multi-view rendering for scalable data generation; (6) train/evaluate roadside 3D detectors on synthesized labels.

Result: Synthesized roadside data effectively supplements real data, reduces limitations of scarce real roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints in 3D object detection experiments.

Conclusion: VRS bridges the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, provides scalable roadside data generation via novel-view synthesis, and enhances robustness of roadside perception systems.

Abstract: Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) require reliable environmental perception to support safe and efficient transportation. With the rapid development of Vehicle-to-everything (V2X), roadside perception has become an effective means to extend sensing coverage and improve traffic safety. However, the scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets poses a major challenge for training high-performance roadside perception models. In this paper, we introduce Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS), a data synthesis framework that generates labeled roadside LiDAR datasets from vehicle-side datasets via LiDAR novel view synthesis. To mitigate the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, VRS employs vehicle point cloud completion to compensate for missing geometry in vehicle-side observations, and introduces an occupancy-based visibility constraint to handle large viewpoint changes during cross-view rendering. The proposed framework enables flexible multi-view rendering for scalable roadside data generation. Extensive experiments on roadside 3D object detection demonstrate that the synthesized data effectively complements real roadside data, mitigates the limitations of limited real-world roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

</details>


### [478] [DexSynRefine: Synthesizing and Refining Human-Object Interaction Motion for Physically Feasible Dexterous Robot Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05925)
*Hyesung Lee,Hyunwoo Jung,Si-Hwan Heo,Sungwook Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSynRefine combines HOI-based trajectory synthesis with a task-space residual RL policy and a dynamics adaptation module to enable sim-to-real dexterous manipulation across five tasks, outperforming kinematic retargeting by 50-70 points and achieving successful real-world transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HOI data offer scalable demonstrations but are sparse and provide only kinematic motion, inconsistent with embodiment and contact-rich dynamics; a framework is needed to synthesize executable references and ground them in real-world physics.

Method: Three coupled components: (1) HOI-MMFP extends motion manifold primitives conditioned on task/object initial states to synthesize coordinated hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI data; (2) a task-space residual RL policy anchors the synthesized reference in physical space while preserving its kinematic structure; (3) a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module enables sim-to-real transfer using proprioceptive history.

Result: Across five dexterous tasks (pick-and-place, tool use, object reorientation), the task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulation and transfers to a real robot on all tasks, with a 50-70 percentage point improvement over kinematic retargeting.

Conclusion: DexSynRefine demonstrates a practical route to scalable, real-world dexterous manipulation by leveraging HOI data to generate executable references and grounding them through learning and dynamics adaptation, achieving reliable sim-to-real transfer across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation from human-object interaction (HOI) data is a scalable alternative to teleoperation, but HOI demonstrations are sparse and provide only kinematic motion that is not directly executable under embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics.
