We present DexSynRefine, a framework with three coupled components: HOI-MMFP, a task- and object-initial-state-conditioned extension of motion manifold primitives that synthesizes coordinated hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations; a task-space residual RL policy that physically grounds the synthesized reference while inheriting its kinematic structure; and a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module that enables sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history. Across five dexterous manipulation tasks spanning pick-and-place, tool use, and object reorientation, our task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulations and transfers to a real robot on all five tasks, improving over kinematic retargeting by 50-70 percentage points.

</details>


### [479] [Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion for Universal Goal-Oriented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05960)
*Zhixuan Shen,Yijie Zeng,Shengxiang Luo,Tianrui Li,Haonan Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based map completion approach (PLMD) for Goal-Oriented Navigation (GON) that fills unobserved BEV regions with obstacle and semantic labels using a DDPM, addressing inconsistent semantic associations and enabling localization in partial maps; claims state-of-the-art on three GON tasks and easy integration with existing semantic-map-based planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In GON, agents must locate a goal in unexplored environments. Self-centered semantic maps suffer when maps are incomplete and when semantic associations are inconsistent between observed and unobserved regions. A robust method is needed to hallucinate plausible, structure-consistent labels for unobserved regions to support localization.

Method: Introduce Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD): a diffusion-based map completion model (DDPM) that predicts obstacle and semantic labels for unseen regions. It leverages structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts and incorporates obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process to reduce inconsistency. Predicted labels fill unobserved areas so the agent can localize the target object. The approach is designed to plug into existing navigation systems that rely on semanticBEV maps.

Result: Extensive experiments show that PLMD expands the region of unknown maps, integrates with existing semantic-map-based navigation strategies, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.

Conclusion: PLMD provides a plug-and-play diffusion-based map completion solution for GON that enables robust localization under partial observability by hallucinating plausible obstacle and semantic labels, while mitigating semantic inconsistency and remaining compatible with current planners.

Abstract: In embodied vision, Goal-Oriented Navigation (GON) requires robots to locate a specific goal within an unexplored environment. The primary challenge of GON arises from the need to construct a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) map to understand the environment while simultaneously localizing an unobserved goal. Existing map-based methods typically employ self-centered semantic maps, often facing challenges such as reliance on complete maps or inconsistent semantic association. To this end, we propose Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), which defines a novel map completion diffusion model based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). PLMD generates obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions through a diffusion-based completion process, thereby enabling goal localization even in partially observed environments. Moreover, it mitigates inconsistent semantic association by leveraging structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts and integrating obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process. By substituting predicted labels for unobserved regions, robots can accurately localize the specified objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMD \textbf{(I)} effectively expands the region of unknown maps, \textbf{(II)} integrates seamlessly into existing navigation strategies that rely on semantic maps, \textbf{(III)} achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.

</details>


### [480] [Accurate Trajectory Tracking with MPCC for Flapping-Wing MAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06042)
*Charbel Toumieh,Jack Zeng,Niel Mistry,Dario Floreano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Compact, differentiable model for bird-scale ornithopters enables real-time MPCC tracking of arc-length trajectories without predefined timing; validated on XFly with 6.5–9 cm mean deviation at up to 3 m/s, ~10× better than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional cascaded controllers treat altitude, speed, and heading independently, causing persistent tracking errors during complex maneuvers. Time-parameterized trajectory tracking requires predefined speed profiles that are not robust for the coupled, aerodynamics-dominated dynamics of bird-scale ornithopters.

Method: Develop a compact, continuously differentiable model capturing dominant couplings in flapping-wing aerodynamics; apply Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) to track arc-length parameterized trajectories and optimize progress online, avoiding predefined timing while keeping real-time computational feasibility.

Result: Experimental validation on the XFly ornithopter with circular and 3D racing trajectories yielded mean trajectory deviations between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, representing an order-of-magnitude improvement (~10×) over prior ornithopter control methods.

Conclusion: MPCC with a compact, differentiable coupled-aerodynamics model enables robust, timing-free trajectory tracking for bird-scale ornithopters, achieving precise autonomous control under challenging maneuvers and outperforming prior approaches.

Abstract: Flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles offer quieter and safer operation than rotary-wing drones, yet achieving precise autonomous control of bird-scale ornithopters remains challenging: lift, airspeed, and turning authority are tightly coupled and governed by only a few control inputs. Conventional cascaded controllers treat altitude, speed, and heading independently, producing persistent tracking errors during complex maneuvers, while time-parameterized trajectory tracking requires predefined speed profiles that existing methods cannot robustly produce for these coupled dynamics. We address both limitations simultaneously with a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) approach that tracks arc-length-parameterized trajectories while optimizing progress online, eliminating the need for predefined timing. However, MPCC requires a dynamical model that captures the coupled aerodynamics without exceeding the computational budget of real-time nonlinear optimization. Here, we propose a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures the dominant couplings of bird-scale ornithopters, enabling real-time predictive control. We validated the method with the XFly ornithopter flying along circular and three-dimensional racing trajectories and achieved a mean deviation from the reference trajectory between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, which represents an almost 10-fold improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

</details>


### [481] [Monitoring autonomous persistent surveillance missions using invariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06062)
*Vladislav Nenchev,Prodromos Sotiriadis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a runtime monitoring framework for persistent surveillance by autonomous robots with a black-box autonomy stack, using offline-computed invariants and a scalable compositional monitor; under independence assumptions, it is sound and complete relative to the full invariant, demonstrated in a labyrinth case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe and reliable runtime monitoring for persistent robotic surveillance when the control stack is opaque and environments are large, by combining offline invariant computation with scalable online verification.

Method: Model the closed-loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter-varying dynamics. Compute an invariant offline. Create a compositional monitor by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariants for each uncertainty region and verify their conjunction online. Prove soundness/completeness under independence assumptions.

Result: The compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant under the stated assumptions and is demonstrated on a real robot conducting persistent surveillance in a labyrinth.

Conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, practically applicable runtime monitoring method for persistent surveillance with black-box autonomy, leveraging offline invariants and decentralized verification; suitable for real-world deployment with typical independence assumptions.

Abstract: This paper studies runtime monitoring for persistent surveillance by autonomous robots when the autonomy stack is a black box. The environment is partitioned into finitely many parts, each carrying an uncertainty state that decreases when observed and increases otherwise. We model the closed loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter varying dynamics and design a monitor based on an invariant computed offline. As this invariant is typically hard to obtain for large to-be-surveyed spaces, we propose a compositional monitor obtained by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariant sets for each uncertainty region, and checking their conjunction online. Under common independence assumptions, the compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant. The approach is applied in a case study with a real robot persistently monitoring a labyrinth, emphasizing its applicability in practice.

</details>


### [482] [VLA-GSE: Boosting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning in VLA with Generalized and Specialized Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06175)
*Yuhua Jiang,Junjie Lu,Xinyao Qin,Xiaoyu Chen,Kaixin Wang,Feifei Gao,Li Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLA-GSE is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for vision-language-action models that uses generalized and specialized experts derived from spectral decomposition of a frozen backbone to improve robotic control with minimal parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of adapting VLA models to robot control: full fine-tuning risks overfitting and catastrophic forgetting; existing PEFT methods underperform on robotic tasks.

Method: Spectrally decompose the frozen backbone and assign leading singular components to generalized (shared) experts and disjoint residual components to specialized (routed) experts; fixed trainable-parameter budget; updates only 2.51% of full model parameters with improved adaptation.

Result: Under the same parameter budget, VLA-GSE consistently outperforms FFT and PEFT baselines; achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus; preserves pre-trained VLM capabilities on multimodal benchmarks similarly to LoRA; improves real-world manipulation under distribution shifts.

Conclusion: VLA-GSE provides an effective, parameter-efficient adaptation mechanism for VLA models in robotics, balancing knowledge preservation with enhanced control performance; code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models inherit rich visual-semantic priors from pre-trained vision-language backbones, but adapting them to robotic control remains challenging. Full fine-tuning (FFT) is prone to overfitting on downstream robotic data and catastrophic forgetting of pretrained vision-language capabilities. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) better preserves pre-trained knowledge, yet existing PEFT methods still struggle to adapt effectively to robot control tasks. To address this gap, we propose VLA-GSE, a parameter-efficient VLA fine-tuning framework that improves control adaptation while retaining PEFT's knowledge preservation advantage. Specifically, VLA-GSE (Generalized and Specialized Experts) is initialized by spectrally decomposing the frozen backbone, assigning leading singular components to generalized experts (shared experts) and disjoint residual components to specialized experts (routed experts). This decomposition improves adaptation capacity under a fixed trainable-parameter budget. Under a comparable parameter budget, VLA-GSE updates only 2.51% of the full model parameters and consistently outperforms strong FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pre-trained VLM capability comparably to LoRA on multimodal understanding benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation success under multiple distribution shifts. Code is available at: https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/VLA-GSE

</details>


### [483] [When to Trust Imagination: Adaptive Action Execution for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06222)
*Rui Wang,Yue Zhang,Jiehong Lin,Kuncheng Luo,Jianan Wang,Zhongrui Wang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive verification for World Action Models (WAMs) that adjusts action horizons based on prediction-observation consistency using FFDC and Mixture-of-Horizon Training, yielding strong robustness-efficiency gains in RoboTwin and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current WAMs predict a fixed number of future actions; when reality diverges from imagined futures, execution becomes inefficient or failures occur. An adaptive mechanism is needed to assess trust in the imagined trajectory and adjust rollout length accordingly.

Method: Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC) as a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate trust in the remaining rollout. Adaptive action chunking emerges from prediction-observation consistency. Mixture-of-Horizon Training improves long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution.

Result: On RoboTwin: reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10%, reduces execution time by 34.02%, and improves success rate by 2.54% over a short-chunk baseline. Real-world experiments: success rate improves by 35%.

Conclusion: Adaptive verification enables a robust-efficiency trade-off for long-horizon WAM execution; FFDC and Mixture-of-Horizon Training yield responsive, efficient planning in contact-rich and difficult phases.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by jointly predicting future visual observations and future actions. However, current WAMs typically execute a fixed number of predicted actions after each model inference, leaving the robot blind to whether the imagined future remains consistent with the actual physical rollout. In this work, we formulate adaptive WAM execution as a future-reality verification problem: the robot should execute longer when the WAM-predicted future remains reliable, and replan earlier when reality deviates from imagination. To this end, we propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate whether the remaining action rollout can still be trusted. FFDC enables adaptive action chunk sizes as an emergent consequence of prediction-observation consistency, preserving the efficiency of long-horizon execution while restoring responsiveness in contact-rich or difficult phases. We further introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training to improve long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution. Experiments on the RoboTwin benchmark and in the real world demonstrate that our method achieves a strong robustness-efficiency trade-off: on RoboTwin, it reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10% and execution time by 34.02%, while improving success rate by 2.54% over the short-chunk baseline; in real-world experiments, it improves success rate by 35%.

</details>


### [484] [RobotEQ: Transitioning from Passive Intelligence to Active Intelligence in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06234)
*Kuofei Fang,Xinyi Che,Haomin Ouyang,Shufan Zhang,Xuehao Wang,Qi Liu,Liyi Liu,Chenqi Zhang,Wenxi Cai,Wenyu Dai,Jinyang Wu,Fan Zhang,Haoyu Chen,Bin He,Zheng Lian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces RobotEQ, the first benchmark for active intelligence in embodied AI, including a dataset (RobotEQ-Data) with 1,900 egocentric images across 10 categories and 56 subcategories, annotated with 5,353 action judgment questions and 1,286 spatial grounding questions; RobotEQ-Bench evaluates models, showing current models struggle with active social compliance, particularly spatial grounding; incorporating external social norm knowledge via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) helps; aims to shift from passive guidance to active social compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots operating in human environments must understand and follow social norms autonomously, not only respond to explicit commands; existing benchmarks emphasize instruction following rather than normative understanding; there is a need to evaluate and improve active social intelligence in embodied AI.

Method: Data construction and annotation of egocentric images; definition of action-judgment and spatial-grounding tasks; development of RobotEQ-Bench; evaluation of state-of-the-art models; exploration of RAG with external norm KBs.

Result: Current models underperform on active intelligence, with notable weakness in spatial grounding; RAG with external norm knowledge improves performance, but gaps remain.

Conclusion: RobotEQ provides a baseline and metrics for active social compliance in embodied AI, encouraging research to move toward normative behavior rather than only following explicit instructions.

Abstract: Embodied AI is a prominent research topic in both academia and industry. Current research centers on completing tasks based on explicit user instructions. However, for robots to integrate into human society, they must understand which actions are permissible and which are prohibited, even without explicit commands. We refer to the user-guided AI as passive intelligence and the unguided AI as active intelligence. This paper introduces RobotEQ, the first benchmark for active intelligence, aiming to assess whether existing models can comprehend and adhere to social norms in embodied scenarios. First, we construct RobotEQ-Data, a dataset consisting of 1,900 egocentric images, spanning 10 representative embodied categories and 56 subcategories. Through extensive manual annotation, we provide 5,353 action judgment questions and 1,286 spatial grounding questions, specifying appropriate robot actions across diverse scenarios. Furthermore, we establish RobotEQ-Bench to evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art models on this task. Experimental results show that current models still fall short in achieving reliable active intelligence, particularly in spatial grounding. Meanwhile, we observe that leveraging RAG techniques to incorporate external social norm knowledge bases can generally enhance performance. This work can facilitate the transition of robotics from user-guided passive manipulation to active social compliance.

</details>


### [485] [CKT-WAM: Parameter-Efficient Context Knowledge Transfer Between World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06247)
*Yuhua Jiang,Yijun Guo,Hongbing Yang,Guojun Lei,Nuo Chen,Yinuo Zhang,Shaoqiang Yan,Bo Lin,Feifei Gao,Biqing Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CKT-WAM offers a parameter-efficient context-based knowledge transfer for World Action Models by injecting teacher knowledge into a student via a compact textual conditioning context, using learnable-query cross-attention compressors and a modular adapter/router framework; it yields strong zero-shot generalization and long-horizon performance with minimal trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable cross-architecture knowledge transfer across heterogeneous WAMs with reduced adaptation cost and without dense hidden-state matching or output imitation, addressing mismatched latent interfaces and rigidity of traditional distillation.

Method: Extract intermediate teacher representations, compress tokens with learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), and transform them through an always-on generalized adapter, a lightweight router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters. The resulting context is appended to the student’s conditioning text embeddings to inject knowledge with minimal architectural changes.

Result: Empirical results show improved zero-shot generalization, achieving 86.1% total success on LIBERO-Plus with only 1.17% trainable parameters, approaching full fine-tuning performance. In real-world tasks, the method attains 83.3% average success across four long-horizon manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: CKT-WAM demonstrates that parameter-efficient, context-based knowledge transfer can effectively transfer teacher WAM knowledge into student WAMs, yielding substantial performance gains with minimal training footprint.

Abstract: World action models (WAMs) provide a powerful generative framework for embodied control, yet transferring knowledge across heterogeneous WAMs remains challenging due to mismatched latent interfaces, high adaptation cost, and the rigidity of conventional distillation objectives. We propose \textbf{CKT-WAM}, a parameter-efficient \textbf{C}ontext \textbf{K}nowledge \textbf{T}ransfer framework that transfers teacher WAM's knowledge into a student WAM through a compact context in the text embedding space, rather than output imitation or dense hidden-state matching. Specifically, CKT-WAM extracts intermediate teacher hidden states, reduces the number of tokens via compressors' learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), and transforms them through an always-on generalized adapter, a lightweight router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters. The resulting context is then appended to the student's conditioning textual embeddings, thereby injecting the transferred knowledge into the student with minimal architectural modification. Experiments show that CKT-WAM consistently improves zero-shot generalization and achieves the best overall performance on LIBERO-Plus, reaching 86.1\% total success rate with only 1.17\% trainable parameters, while approaching full fine-tuning performance. Beyond simulation, CKT-WAM also demonstrates strong real-world long-horizon manipulation ability, achieving the best average success rate of 83.3\% across four multi-step and long-horizon tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/CKT-WAM.

</details>


### [486] [Toward Visually Realistic Simulation: A Benchmark for Evaluating Robot Manipulation in Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06311)
*Yixin Zhu,Zixiong Wang,Jian Yang,Jin Xie,Jingyi Yu,Jiayuan Gu,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VISER is a visually realistic simulation benchmark for robot manipulation that incorporates physically-based rendering materials and lighting, using an MLLM-driven pipeline to generate material-aware assets, achieving a high sim-to-real correlation (r = 0.92).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing simulation benchmarks lack visual realism, causing a large sim-to-real domain gap that undermines reliable evaluation of robot manipulation policies. Lighting and material effects critically influence geometric reasoning and spatial grounding but are underrepresented.

Method: Systematic analysis of lighting and material effects; creation of VISER—a dataset with >1,000 high-fidelity 3D assets with PBR materials; scene construction via curated layouts or generation; automated pipeline using Multi-modal LLMs for material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval; development of diverse evaluation tasks (grasping, placing, long-horizon) for Vision-Language-Action models.

Result: Demonstrates that lighting and material are pivotal for accurate simulation-based evaluation; VISER achieves a strong real-world correlation with an average Pearson coefficient of 0.92 across evaluated policies.

Conclusion: VISER provides a scalable, reproducible benchmark to reduce the sim-to-real visual gap, supported by a pipeline for scalable asset generation and material-aware evaluation tasks, enabling more reliable assessment of VLA robotic manipulation in simulation.

Abstract: Reliable simulation evaluation of robot manipulation policies serves as a high-fidelity proxy for real-world performance. Although existing benchmarks cover a wide range of task categories, they lack visual realism, creating a large domain gap between simulation and reality. This undermines the reliability of simulation-based evaluation in predicting real-world performance. To mitigate the sim-to-real visual gap, we conduct a systematic analysis to isolate the effects of lighting and material. Our results show that these factors play a critical role in geometric reasoning and spatial grounding, yet are largely overlooked in existing benchmarks. Motivated by the analysis, we propose VISER, a visually realistic benchmark for evaluating robot manipulation in simulation. VISER features a high-fidelity dataset of over 1,000 3D assets with physically-based rendering (PBR) materials, along with 3D scenes created from these assets through curated layouts or generation. To this end, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval, enabling scalable generation of physically plausible assets. Building on the high-fidelity 3D asset dataset, we construct diverse evaluation tasks, such as grasping, placing, and long-horizon tasks, enabling scalable and reproducible assessment of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Our benchmark shows a strong correlation between simulation and real-world performance, achieving an average Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92 across different policies.

</details>


### [487] [AssistDLO: Assistive Teleoperation for Deformable Linear Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06323)
*Berk Guler,Simon Manschitz,Kay Pompetzki,Jan Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SA-CBF-based shared autonomy improves DLO teleoperation; naive users gain most; VA benefits experts; adaptive, material-aware assistance is needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulating deformable linear objects (DLOs) is challenging due to their effectively infinite-dimensional configuration space and nonlinear dynamics. Depth uncertainty in teleoperation degrades perception and response; robust, adaptive assistance is required to preserve geometry while easing operator workload.

Method: Proposes AssistDLO: real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). Unlike traditional simple geometric attractors, SA-CBF acts as a geometry-aware funnel that preserves DLO geometry while maintaining operator authority. Evaluation via a bimanual knot-untangling user study (N=22) with ropes of varying length and rigidity.

Result: SA-CBF yields the strongest gains for naive users, raising task success from 71% to 88% and proving effective for stiffer ropes. Experts prefer VA. Highly compliant, long ropes benefit more from visual support than localized action assistance.

Conclusion: Fixed shared-autonomy strategies are insufficient for DLO teleoperation. Adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy (SA-CBF plus VA) is needed to handle operator expertise variations and DLO properties.

Abstract: Manipulating Deformable Linear Objects (DLOs) is challenging in robotics due to their infinite-dimensional configuration space and complex nonlinear dynamics. In teleoperation, depth uncertainty hinders state perception and reaction. AssistDLO addresses this challenge as an assistive teleoperation framework for DLO manipulation that combines real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). While traditional shared autonomy methods often rely on simple geometric attractors and may fail to preserve DLO geometry, SA-CBF acts as a geometry-aware funnel, facilitating precise grasping while preserving the operator's high-level authority. The framework is evaluated in a bimanual knot-untangling user study (N = 22) using ropes with varying length and rigidity. Results show that the effectiveness of the assistance depends strongly on operator expertise and DLO properties. SA-CBF provides the strongest gains for naive users, acting as a skill equalizer that increases task success from 71% to 88%, and is effective for stiffer ropes. Conversely, expert users prefer VA, and highly compliant, long ropes benefit more from visual support than localized action assistance. Ultimately, these findings demonstrate that effective DLO teleoperation cannot rely on a fixed strategy, highlighting the critical need for adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy.

</details>


### [488] [TouchDrive: Electronics-Free Tactile Sensing Interface for Assistive Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06432)
*Jing Xu,Xuezhi Niu,Didem Gurdur Broo,Klas Hjort*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TouchDrive is an electronics-free tactile interface that converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback via a valve-driven, passive loop, enabling user-modulated grasp forces for robust handling of delicate, compliant objects across multiple robotic platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing assistive grasping systems rely on electronic sensing and multi-stage processing, causing higher cost, complexity, and reduced accessibility. A simple, electronics-free tactile interface could improve reliability, affordability, and usability for safe manipulation of fragile items.

Method: A pneumatic, valve-mediated switching mechanism directly transduces contact forces into pneumatic signals, using a normally closed valve, compressed air tank, sensing element, and a haptic actuator. The loop integrates sensing, signal generation, and feedback without electronics, enabling a single passive pathway for tactile feedback across different robots.

Result: Validated on diverse robotic platforms, TouchDrive demonstrated reliable performance and practical applicability in assistive grasping tasks, including handling fruits and everyday items (up to 20 objects), signaling robust and precise manipulation of delicate objects.

Conclusion: TouchDrive offers a cost-effective, electronics-free tactile interface for assistive grasping that reduces system complexity while providing reliable, delicate-object manipulation across multiple platforms.

Abstract: Assistive robotic grasping plays an important role in enabling safe and adaptive manipulation of diverse objects. However, existing systems often rely on electronic sensing and multi-stage processing pipelines, increasing system complexity and reducing accessibility. To address these limitations, we present TouchDrive, a cost-effective, electronics-free tactile sensing interface for assistive grasping. TouchDrive directly converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback through valve-mediated switching, integrating sensing, signal generation, and feedback within a single passive mechanical loop. The system can be employed using a pneumatic normally closed valve, a compressed air tank, sensing element, and haptic feedback actuator without electronics. By delivering tactile cues, TouchDrive empowers users to modulate grasp forces, enabling precise and robust delicate manipulation of compliant and fragile objects. The interface has been validated across diverse robotic platforms, consistently demonstrating reliable performance and practical applicability in assistive grasping tasks, such as handling fruits and everyday items (up to 20 objects).

</details>


### [489] [GA3T: A Ground-Aerial Terrain Traversability Dataset for Heterogeneous Robot Teams in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06478)
*Siwei Cai,Knut Peterson,Quan Tran,Christian Ricks,Dhanush Parthasarathy,Amir Kaidarov,Neil Deshpande,Sukaina Najm,David Han,Lifeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GA3T is a real-world ground-aerial multi-robot perception dataset across unstructured terrains, pairing ground LiDAR/stereo/IMU/GPS with aerial RGB/thermal imagery and GPS, capturing 4 environments (~29 min, 13k synchronized frames), including SAM3-based segments and 8k manual labels; designed to enable cross-view perception, air-ground fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in off-road settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of real-world datasets featuring overlapping multi-modal observations from heterogeneous platforms in unstructured terrain, hindering progress in cross-view, occlusion-aware collaborative perception for off-road environments.

Method: Data collection with a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO II UAV. Ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, and GPS; aerial platform provides RGB imagery, thermal/infrared observations, and GPS. 4 diverse environments were sampled, totaling over 13,000 synchronized frames across ~29 minutes. Early-spring collection allows partial aerial visibility through sparse canopies, enabling occlusion-aware collaboration. Annotations include SAM3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images.

Result: The dataset comprises >13k synchronized frames across ~29 minutes in 4 environments, with multi-modal and cross-view data (ground: LiDAR, stereo, IMU, GPS; air: RGB, thermal, GPS). It supports cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments; distinct from prior datasets focusing on SLAM or simulated driving.

Conclusion: GA3T fills a critical gap by enabling real-world cross-view collaboration research in off-road settings, supporting cross-modal fusion, occlusion-aware perception, and traversability estimation, thereby advancing perception systems for ground-air robot teams.

Abstract: Heterogeneous air-ground robot teams combine complementary sensing modalities, mobility characteristics, and spatial viewpoints that can significantly enhance perception in complex outdoor environments. However, progress in multi-robot collaborative perception has been constrained by the lack of real-world datasets featuring overlapping multi-modal observations from platforms operating in unstructured terrain. We present GA3T (Ground-Aerial Team for Terrain Traversal), a real-world multi-robot collaborative perception dataset collected using a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO~II UAV across diverse unstructured environments, including forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow piles, and grass-covered fields. The ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, and GPS data, while the aerial platform contributes RGB imagery, thermal/infrared observations, and GPS from a complementary overhead viewpoint, allowing for rich cross-modal and cross-view perception. The dataset is collected in 4 unique environments, with over 13,000 synchronized frames across approximately 29 minutes of operation, and includes both SAM~3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images. A unique aspect of the dataset is its early-spring collection period, during which sparse tree canopies allow the aerial robot to partially observe the ground robot and terrain through the trees, allowing for occlusion-aware collaborative perception. Unlike prior multi-robot datasets that focus on SLAM or simulated cooperative driving, GA3T is specifically designed to support research on cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments.

</details>


### [490] [OA-WAM: Object-Addressable World Action Model for Robust Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06481)
*Yushan Liu,Peibo Sun,Shoujie Li,Yifan Xie,Lingfeng Zhang,Xintao Chao,Shiyuan Dong,Fang Chen,Xiao-Ping Zhang,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OA-WAM introduces an object-addressable world model for robotics by partitioning each frame into N object slots plus a robot slot, each with persistent address and dynamic content; cross-slot attention is routed via address-only keys; a world head predicts next-frame slot states and a flow-based action head decodes a 16-step action.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Holistic world representations (images, video tokens, global latents) hinder referential reasoning on specific objects, especially under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context. An object-addressable interface could decouple identity from state for robust manipulation.

Method: Represent each frame as N object slots + 1 robot slot. Each slot has a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector. Slots fuse with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states; an action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same forward pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens.

Result: OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on a seven-axis aggregate. Causal slot-intervention yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87 (vs. at most 0.09 for holistic baselines).

Conclusion: Addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) enhance Vision-Language-Action policies by jointly predicting scene evolution and robot actions, but existing methods usually represent the predicted world as holistic images, video tokens, or global latents. These representations are difficult for an action decoder to address when an instruction refers to a particular object, especially under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context. We propose OA-WAM, an Object-Addressable World Action Model for robust robot manipulation. OA-WAM decomposes each frame into N+1 slot states, with one robot slot and N object slots. Each slot contains a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector, and is fused with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states, while a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same forward pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens. OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), reaches state-of-the-art performance on the most relevant LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on the seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87, versus at most 0.09 for holistic baselines. These results suggest that addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

</details>


### [491] [Lie Group Formulation of Recursive Dynamics Algorithms of Higher Order for Floating-Base Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06498)
*Ahmed Ali,Chiara Gabellieri,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Efficient higher-order time-derivative dynamics for floating-base trees with SE(3) base, using spatial twists; closed-form EOM with passive Coriolis, invariant articulated inertia; demonstrated on 12-DoF aerial manipulator; quadratic scaling with derivative order; outperforming autodiff baseline which scales exponentially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accurate computation of high-order derivatives of dynamics for floating-base robotic trees, enabling advanced control/estimation and planning, with computational efficiency and stability (passivity and inertia invariance).

Method: Extend Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics recursions to compute higher-order derivatives; formulate in spatial twist representation; derive closed-form EOM; prove Coriolis matrix satisfy passivity and that articulated inertia tensor remains constant across time derivatives; apply to 12-DoF aerial manipulator; derive analytic forward/inverse dynamics and first derivatives; perform numerical simulations up to fifth order.

Result: Closed-form EOM, admissible Coriolis matrix with passivity, articulated inertia invariance; analytic expressions for 12-DoF forward/inverse dynamics and first derivatives; simulations validate higher-order derivatives up to fifth order; benchmarks show quadratic scaling with derivative order; AD baseline shows exponential scaling.

Conclusion: The framework provides efficient, stable higher-order dynamic computations for floating-base trees, with practical implications for real-time control and optimization; demonstrated utility on a 12-DoF aerial manipulator and favorable scaling relative to autodiff baselines.

Abstract: In this paper, we describe procedures for computing higher-order time derivatives of the Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms for floating-base trees, where the base configuration evolves on SE(3) and the attached mechanism is an open kinematic tree with configuration on the (n1+n2)-dimensional manifold T^{n1} \times R^{n2}, using spatial representation of twists. After presenting the algorithms, we collect the resulting recursions into closed-form equations of motion, identifying an admissible Coriolis matrix satisfying the passivity property, and showing that the articulated inertia tensor remains unchanged across all time derivatives. We then apply the developed methods to a 12-DoF aerial manipulator to derive analytical expressions for its geometric forward and inverse dynamics along with their first time derivatives whereas the numerical simulations successfully evaluate these dynamics up to fifth order. Finally, to demonstrate their practical utility, we benchmark the proposed extensions and show that, in the considered tests, their computational cost scales quadratically with the derivative order, whereas the automatic-differentiation baseline exhibits exponential scaling.

</details>


### [492] [ReActor: Reinforcement Learning for Physics-Aware Motion Retargeting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06593)
*David Müller,Agon Serifi,Sammy Christen,Ruben Grandia,Espen Knoop,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bilevel optimization framework retargets human-like reference motion to diverse robot morphologies by jointly adapting motions and training a tracking policy, using an approximate gradient to make optimization tractable and sparse semantic correspondences to avoid manual tuning; integrated with physics simulation to produce physically plausible motions for robust imitation learning, validated in simulation and on hardware including a quadruped.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retargeting human kinematic ref motions to robots is difficult due to morphological differences and physical constraints, leading to foot-sliding, collisions, and infeasibility. An integrated, learnable retargeting approach that is physics-aware can improve imitation learning and transfer across morphologies.

Method: A bilevel optimization framework: upper level adapts reference motions to the robot’s morphology; lower level trains a tracking policy via reinforcement learning. An approximate gradient for the upper-level loss makes optimization tractable. The approach uses sparse semantic rigid-body correspondences and a parameterization that preserves motion characteristics across embodiments, eliminating manual tuning. Physics simulation is integrated to ensure physical plausibility during retargeting.

Result: Produces physically plausible retargeted motions and improves downstream imitation learning. Demonstrated in both simulation and hardware, handling challenging morphologies far from human norms, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

Conclusion: Joint optimization of motion retargeting and policy learning with approximate gradients and physics integration yields tractable, generalizable retargeting across diverse morphologies without manual tuning, enabling robust imitation learning in realistic settings.

Abstract: Retargeting human kinematic reference motion onto a robot's morphology remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods often produce physical inconsistencies, such as foot sliding, self-collisions, or dynamically infeasible motions, which hinder downstream imitation learning. We propose a bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to a robot's morphology while training a tracking policy using reinforcement learning. To make the optimization tractable, we derive an approximate gradient for the upper-level loss. Our framework requires only a sparse set of semantic rigid-body correspondences and eliminates the need for manual tuning by identifying optimal values for a parameterization expressive enough to preserve characteristic motion across different embodiments. Moreover, by integrating retargeting directly with physics simulation, we produce physically plausible motions that facilitate robust imitation learning. We validate our method in simulation and on hardware, demonstrating challenging motions for morphologies that differ significantly from a human, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

</details>


### [493] [Cross-Modal Navigation with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06595)
*Shuo Liu,Xinzichen Li,Christopher Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CRONA is a MARL framework for cross-modal navigation using lightweight modality-specialized agents, a centralized multi-modal critic, and auxiliary beliefs, achieving superior performance over single-agent baselines in visual-acoustic navigation; findings highlight when homogeneous versus heterogeneous collaboration is advantageous and the need for richer modalities in large environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust embodied navigation benefits from complementary sensory cues, but high-quality multi-modal data is hard to obtain and monolithic models struggle with large, complex policy spaces. A cross-modal, multi-agent approach offers scalable, flexible deployment while preserving modality strengths.

Method: CRONA is a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning framework for Cross-Modal Navigation. It deploys lightweight modality-specialized agents that collaborate through control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic with access to the global state.

Result: Experiments on visual-acoustic navigation show multi-agent methods significantly outperform single-agent baselines in performance and efficiency. Key findings: (1) homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities suffices for short-range navigation under salient cues; (2) heterogeneous collaboration among agents with complementary modalities is efficient and effective; (3) navigation in large, complex environments benefits from richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

Conclusion: CRONA demonstrates scalable cross-modal navigation via MARL with auxiliary beliefs and a centralized critic. The work provides practical guidance on when to employ homogeneous vs. heterogeneous collaboration and emphasizes the need for richer modalities and larger models for complex environments.

Abstract: Robust embodied navigation relies on complementary sensory cues. However, high-quality and well-aligned multi-modal data is often difficult to obtain in practice. Training a monolithic model is also challenging as rich multi-modal inputs induce complex representations and substantially enlarge the policy space. Cross-modal collaboration among lightweight modality-specialized agents offers a scalable paradigm. It enables flexible deployment and parallel execution, while preserving the strength of each modality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{CRONA}, a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for \textbf{Cro}ss-Modal \textbf{Na}vigation. CRONA improves collaboration by leveraging control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic with global state. Experiments on visual-acoustic navigation tasks show that multi-agent methods significantly improve performance and efficiency over single-agent baselines. We find that homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities is sufficient for short-range navigation under salient cues; heterogeneous collaboration among agents with complementary modalities is generally efficient and effective; and navigation in large, complex environments requires both richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

</details>


### [494] [Multi-Robot Coordination in V2X Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06662)
*John Pravin Arockiasamy,Alexey Vinel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decentralized V2X framework for social robots enabling role-aware awareness and event-driven coordination through RAM/RAS and RMCM/RMCS, validated by a real-world PoC and simulations, with VRU integration and reduced channel load.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safe, scalable collaboration of robots in urban traffic by enabling decentralized, low-latency cooperation, integrating vulnerable road users (VRUs), and aligning with ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination standards.

Method: Introduce robot-centric facility-layer services: Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS), implemented via Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM). These enable role-aware awareness, integrate external VRUs, and support event-driven, low-latency coordination without central infrastructure. A real-world PoC demonstrates deterministic multi-robot coordination using a formal finite-state coordination model between a humanoid and a quadrupedal robot during road crossing; simulations evaluate VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments, assessing channel load and redundancy.

Result: PoC shows deterministic, coordinated robot maneuvers in a road-crossing scenario governed by a formal finite-state model. Simulations indicate RAS-based VRU clustering effectively includes non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant V2X transmissions, thereby lowering channel load.

Conclusion: The proposed RAM/RAS and RMCM/RMCS framework offers a scalable, standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems, enabling decentralized, role-driven coordination in urban traffic.

Abstract: This paper presents a Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication framework that enables decentralized cooperation among social robots operating in complex urban traffic environments. Building on ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination services, the framework introduces two robot-centric facility-layer services: the Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS), realized through the Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM), respectively. RAS enables role-aware, task-oriented robot awareness while integrating externally detected Vulnerable Road Users (VRUs), including non-V2X pedestrians, into cooperative awareness. RMCS supports event-driven, low-latency coordination of robot maneuvers under explicitly established roles, without centralized infrastructure or prior pairing. A real-world proof of concept demonstrates deterministic multi-robot coordination between a humanoid robot and a quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian during a road-crossing scenario, governed by a formally specified finite-state coordination model. Complementary simulations evaluate robot-mediated VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments, showing that RAS-based clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant transmissions from V2X-enabled VRUs, thereby lowering channel load. Together, the proposed services provide a scalable and standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems.

</details>
